topics.title: Behavior-Based Techniques on Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Behavior-Based Techniques on Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Behavior-Based Robotics (BBR)** is a paradigm in autonomous robotics that decomposes a robot's overall control problem into a set of parallel, modular, and reactive **behaviors**—each directly mapping sensory inputs to motor actions—without relying on centralized world models or deliberative planning. It was popularized by **Rodney A. Brooks** in his seminal 1986 paper *"A Robust Layered Control System for a Mobile Robot"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Module 1 – High Frequency):**
> A *behavior* is the fundamental unit of control in a behavior-based system. It is defined as a **tight sensor–motor coupling** that achieves a specific goal, executed as a direct mapping:
> $$\text{Behavior}: S \rightarrow A$$
> where $S$ is the sensor input space and $A$ is the actuator/command output space.

A **Behavior-Based Technique** refers to the architectural and algorithmic methodology used to:
1. Design individual robot behaviors (avoid-obstacle, seek-goal, wall-follow, wander).
2. **Coordinate** or **arbitrate** between multiple competing behaviors to produce a single coherent robot action.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Driving a Car in Heavy Traffic:**
> Imagine you are driving in Kerala traffic (say, from Kakkanad to Edappally). You do **not** create a 3D map of every lorry, build a global path planner, and run a Bellman-Ford optimization in your head. Instead, your brain runs **several simple rules in parallel**:
> - Rule 1: *"If a vehicle is in front, brake."*
> - Rule 2: *"If pedestrian is crossing, stop."*
> - Rule 3: *"If signal is green and road is clear, accelerate toward destination."*
>
> Each rule is a **behavior**—it reads a sensory cue and outputs an action. Your brain **arbitrates** between them (braking wins over acceleration when an obstacle is near). This reactive, layered, reflex-like coordination is exactly what Behavior-Based Robotics implements in software.

## 1.3 Physical & Algorithmic Constants/Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-Reported Metrics for Behavior Evaluation:**
> - **Response Latency (L)**: Time between stimulus onset and behavior activation, typically **< 100 ms** for hard real-time reactive layers.
> - **Sensor–Motor Loop Frequency (f)**: $f = 1/T$, where $T$ is the control cycle. Reactive behaviors typically operate at $f \geq 10 \text{ Hz}$.
> - **Layer Priority (Pᵢ)**: Integer priority index assigned in subsumption, where higher $P$ = higher authority to **suppress** lower layers.
> - **Gain (kᵢ)** for potential field behaviors: dimensionless or in m⁻¹ depending on formulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Behavior-Based Techniques

Behavior-based robotics is built on **two distinct design stages**:

### Pillar 1 — Behavior Design (Building the Individual Reflex)
Each behavior is a self-contained module that maps a sensor reading to a motor command. Common formalisms used to **build** behaviors:

#### 2.1.1 Potential Field / Vector Field Method
A behavior (e.g., "avoid obstacle") is modeled as an **artificial potential** that exerts a virtual force on the robot. The total commanded velocity vector is the gradient (or sum) of these fields.

- **Attractive potential** to goal:
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q)$$
where $\rho_{\text{goal}}(q) = \lVert q - q_{\text{goal}} \rVert$ is the distance from the robot's pose $q$ to the goal.

- **Attractive force (negative gradient):**
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} \, (q_{\text{goal}} - q)$$

- **Repulsive potential** from obstacle:
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

- **Repulsive force:**
$$F_{\text{rep}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Potential fields are the **most asked** behavior formulation in KTU university exams because they convert an abstract goal into a smooth continuous vector field the robot can follow at every tick.

#### 2.1.2 Schema Theory (Arkin, 1989)
A **schema** is a parameterized perceptual-motor pattern. The Perceptual Schema (PS) extracts relevant features from sensors; the Motor Schema (MS) generates a vector. Multiple schemas are combined mathematically.

- **Generic Motor Schema Output Vector** $\vec{V_i}$:
$$\vec{V_i} = \text{MS}_i \circ \text{PS}_i(\text{Sensory Input})$$

Common schema library (Arkin):
- **move-ahead**, **avoid-obstacle**, **follow**, **dodge**, **find-aggregate**, **wander**, **stay-on-path**.

#### 2.1.3 Stimulus-Response (S-R) Rules
Discrete, IF-THEN style behaviors used in simple reactive robots:
$$\text{IF } (S_1 \land \neg S_2) \text{ THEN } A_1$$

#### 2.1.4 Finite State Machines (FSM)
For sequencing behaviors (e.g., "wander → detect door → approach → pass through").

### Pillar 2 — Behavior Coordination / Arbitration (Combining Reflexes)
Given $n$ behaviors producing outputs $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$, the final robot command $A$ is computed by a **coordination function** $\mathcal{C}$:

$$A = \mathcal{C}(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$$

KTU classifies coordination mechanisms into **three families**:

| # | Coordination Family | Formula / Logic | When Used |
|---|---------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Priority / Suppression (Subsumption)** | $A = a_k$ where $k = \arg\max_i P_i$ | Discrete, hard-priority architectures |
| 2 | **Command Fusion / Weighted Sum** | $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot a_i$ | Continuous, smooth blending |
| 3 | **Voting / Cooperative** | $A = a_j$ where $j = \arg\max_i V_i$ | Discrete actions, multiple votes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subsumption Architecture Specifics (Brooks 1986):** Built from bottom to top in layers. Higher layers can **suppress** inputs to, or **inhibit** outputs of, lower layers through dedicated $\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$ and $\text{S}_{\text{inhibit}}$ signal wires. No world model is used; only the robot's current sensor state.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $F_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}}(q_{\text{goal}} - q)$ | Attractive force toward goal | Goal-seeking behavior |
| 2 | $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}}\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho}-\tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ | Repulsive potential within influence radius $\rho_0$ | Obstacle-avoidance behavior |
| 3 | $\vec{V}_{\text{total}} = \sum_i w_i \vec{V_i}$ | Schema fusion (Motor Schema theory) | Command fusion |
| 4 | $A = a_k, \quad P_k = \max_i P_i$ | Subsumption priority arbitration | Layered architectures |
| 5 | $a_j = \arg\max_i V_i$ | Voting-based selection | Discrete action selection |
| 6 | $f = 1/T$ | Control loop frequency (Hz) | Real-time performance |
| 7 | $\omega_d = \omega_{\max} \cdot \tanh(k \cdot e_\theta)$ | Steering to heading error $e_\theta$ | Wall/line-following |
| 8 | $\rho = \sqrt{(x_o - x_r)^2 + (y_o - y_r)^2}$ | Euclidean obstacle distance | Range sensor processing |

**Units cheat-sheet:**
- Distances $q, \rho, \rho_0$: **meters (m)**
- Gains $k_{\text{att}}$: unitless or $\text{s}^{-2}$; $k_{\text{rep}}$: $\text{m}^2$
- Velocities: **m/s**; Angular rates: **rad/s**
- Frequencies: **Hz**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Behavior-based techniques are deployed in:
- **Domestic vacuum robots** (Roomba — uses a layered arbitration of bump, cliff, wall-follow behaviors).
- **Autonomous warehouse AMRs** (Kiva/Amazon Robotics — pick-shelf, deliver, charge coordination).
- **Unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs)** for mine detection and rough terrain.
- **Robot soccer (RoboCup)** — coordinated multi-agent behaviors.
- **Search and rescue (SAR)** micro-UAVs in cluttered buildings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since Behavior-Based Techniques involve both mathematical formulations and architectural logic, this section provides (a) a complete derivation of the **Potential Field Total Force** used in obstacle-avoidance + goal-seeking, and (b) an analytical matrix of subsumption-layer design.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Behavior Force for Goal-Seek + Avoid-Obstacle

**Problem Setup (typical KTU problem):**
A mobile robot at position $q = (x, y)$ must reach goal $q_g = (x_g, y_g)$ while avoiding an obstacle centered at $q_o = (x_o, y_o)$ with influence radius $\rho_0$. Derive the **total commanded velocity** assuming command fusion (motor schema coordination).

### Step 1 — Define the position vector
Let the robot's configuration in 2-D workspace be
$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

The relative vectors are:
$$\Delta q_{\text{goal}} = q_g - q = \begin{bmatrix} x_g - x \\ y_g - y \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Delta q_{\text{obst}} = q_o - q = \begin{bmatrix} x_o - x \\ y_o - y \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Euclidean distance to goal
The distance is the 2-norm of the position difference:
$$\rho_{\text{goal}} = \lVert \Delta q_{\text{goal}} \rVert = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Write the attractive potential
By the standard quadratic attractive potential (Khatib, 1986):
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the attractive force
The force is the negative spatial gradient of the potential. Since $U_{\text{att}}$ depends only on $\Delta q_{\text{goal}}$:
$$F_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x}\,\hat{i} - \frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y}\,\hat{j}$$

Computing the partial derivative with respect to $x$:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x} = k_{\text{att}} (x_g - x)$$

Similarly for $y$:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y} = k_{\text{att}} (y_g - y)$$

Therefore:
$$F_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} \begin{bmatrix} x_g - x \\ y_g - y \end{bmatrix} = k_{\text{att}} \, (q_g - q)$$

### Step 5 — Write the repulsive potential
The piecewise repulsive potential (valid only when $\rho \le \rho_0$) is:
$$U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, \quad \rho = \lVert q - q_o \rVert$$

### Step 6 — Derive the repulsive force
Use the chain rule. First, note that
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_o}{\rho}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} = \frac{y - y_o}{\rho}$$

Let $g(\rho) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$. Then
$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{1}{\rho^2}$$

Applying the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial x} = k_{\text{rep}} \, g(\rho) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \right) \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{\rho}$$

$$= -k_{\text{rep}} \, g(\rho) \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{\rho^{3}}$$

Hence the repulsive force vector is:
$$F_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \, g(\rho) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_o \\ y - y_o \end{bmatrix}$$

Or compactly:
$$F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - q_o}{\rho}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the total force
By command fusion (motor schema sum):
$$F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{att}} + F_{\text{rep}}$$

Therefore the total commanded velocity (taking $\vec{v} \propto F_{\text{total}}$ for a holonomic robot) is:
$$\vec{v}_{\text{cmd}} = k_{\text{att}}(q_g - q) + k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} (q - q_o)$$

### Step 8 — Convert velocity to wheel commands (for differential-drive robot)
Let $v = \lVert \vec{v}_{\text{cmd}} \rVert$ and $\theta_v = \text{atan2}(v_y, v_x)$.
Then wheel angular velocities for a robot of wheelbase $L$ and wheel radius $r$ are:
$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left( v - \frac{L}{2}\,\omega \right), \quad \omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left( v + \frac{L}{2}\,\omega \right)$$

where the turn rate is bounded by
$$\omega = \omega_{\max}\,\text{sat}\!\left(\frac{\theta_v - \theta_{\text{robot}}}{e_{\max}}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the saturation block?** Pure potential fields suffer from **local minima** and **oscillation in narrow corridors**. The saturation (or a $\tanh$ smoother) is a KTU-favored engineering fix to limit extreme control actions.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Subsumption Architecture Layer Design (Brooks 1986)

| Layer (Bottom → Top) | Competence | Behaviors | Sensors Used | Actuators Used | Can Suppress Layer(s) |
|----------------------|------------|-----------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 0 | *Avoid contact with objects* (survival) | `avoid` | Sonar, IR, bumper | Wheel motors | — (no lower layer) |
| 1 | *Wander aimlessly* without collisions | `wander`, `avoid` | Sonar | Wheel motors | 0 |
| 2 | *Explore the world* by looking for distant places | `look-for-landmark`, `wander` | Compass, sonar | Wheel + pan/tilt head | 0, 1 |
| 3 | *Build a map* of the environment | `build-map`, `explore` | Sonar, encoders | Wheel + head | 0, 1, 2 |
| 4 | *Notice changes* in the environment | `monitor-changes` | Camera | Wheel + head | 0, 1, 2, 3 |
| 5 | *Identify objects* and categorize | `categorize` | Camera | Wheel + head | 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| 6 | *Plan tasks* to satisfy goals | `plan` | All sensors | All actuators | 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 |
| 7 | *Reason about the world* in objects | `reason` | All | All | 0–6 |

**Inference (KTU explanation):**
- Layers are added **incrementally**; the robot is operational after each layer is added.
- Higher layers **monitor** or **override** lower ones via dedicated suppress ($\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$) and inhibit ($\text{S}_{\text{inhibit}}$) signals.
- No central model is built; behavior outputs flow *horizontally* across the same time step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Coordination Mechanism Comparison

| Property | Priority / Subsumption | Command Fusion (Weighted Sum) | Voting |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------------|--------|
| Output type | Discrete winning action | Continuous blended vector | Discrete winning action |
| Smoothness | Abrupt switching | Smooth, continuous | Abrupt switching |
| Implementation cost | Low (if-else chain) | Medium (vector algebra) | Medium (ballot counting) |
| Risk | Chattering between layers | No clear winner for hard constraints | Tie-handling needed |
| KTU example | Brooks' Allen, Genghis | Arkin's AuRA / schema-based | Multi-robot box-pushing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subsumption Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **layered, suppression-based** control flow of Brooks' Subsumption Architecture. Higher layers may inject **suppress** or **inhibit** signals into lower layers, which is captured as a feedback/override channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s0[Sensor: Bumper and Sonar] --> b0[Behavior 0: Avoid Obstacle]
    s1[Sensor: Sonar and IR] --> b1[Behavior 1: Wander]
    s2[Sensor: Compass and Sonar] --> b2[Behavior 2: Look for Landmark]
    s3[Sensor: Camera] --> b3[Behavior 3: Monitor Changes]
    s4[Sensor: All Sensors] --> b4[Behavior 4: Plan and Identify]

    b0 --> m0[Suppress and Inhibit Bus]
    b1 --> m0
    b2 --> m0
    b3 --> m0
    b4 --> m0

    m0 --> out0[Wheel Motor Commands]
    m0 --> out1[Pan and Tilt Head Commands]

    sup3[Suppress Signal from Layer 3] -.-> b2
    sup4[Suppress Signal from Layer 4] -.-> b3
    inh3[Inhibit Signal from Layer 3] -.-> b2
    inh4[Inhibit Signal from Layer 4] -.-> b3
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU exam-style):**
- The bus `Suppress and Inhibit Bus` consolidates all behavior outputs.
- Dashed arrows represent **vertical** override channels (higher layer dominates).
- Solid arrows represent **horizontal** data flow (sensors → behavior → bus).

## 4.2 Schema-Based Behavior Fusion — Functional Block Flow

The Motor Schema paradigm (Arkin, 1989) sums normalized vector contributions. The following Mermaid diagram shows how multiple perceptual schemas feed a normalization block before the motor schema sum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sens[Robot Sensors] --> ps1[Perceptual Schema: Find Goal]
    sens --> ps2[Perceptual Schema: Detect Obstacles]
    sens --> ps3[Perceptual Schema: Maintain Heading]

    ps1 --> ms1[Motor Schema: Move to Goal Vector VG]
    ps2 --> ms2[Motor Schema: Avoid Obstacle Vector VO]
    ps3 --> ms3[Motor Schema: Keep Heading Vector VH]

    ms1 --> norm[Gain Scaling and Normalization]
    ms2 --> norm
    ms3 --> norm

    norm --> sum[Vector Sum Total Velocity V Total]
    sum --> ctrl[Robot Controller Wheel Speeds]
```

**Block equations represented:**
$$V_{\text{total}} = w_G V_G + w_O V_O + w_H V_H$$
where $w_G, w_O, w_H$ are gains chosen by the designer (often 1, 1, 0.5 for a hallway-following task).

## 4.3 Finite State Machine for Sequencing Behaviors

A common KTU diagram: a delivery robot's behavior FSM.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Wander
    Wander --> Approach: Object Detected
    Approach --> PickUp: At Object
    PickUp --> Travel: Object Held
    Travel --> DropOff: At Goal Zone
    DropOff --> Wander: Object Released
    Wander --> Avoid: Obstacle Close
    Avoid --> Wander: Path Clear
```

**Each state = a single active behavior.** Transitions are triggered by perceptual events (sensors).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**: Part A = 3-mark short answers, Part B = 14-mark structured questions with internal choice.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Define a "Behavior" in the context of Behavior-Based Robotics. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A behavior is a **tight, direct mapping from sensory input to actuator command** that achieves or maintains a specific goal, without using an internal world model (Brooks, 1986). Formally:
$$\text{Behavior} : S \rightarrow A$$
where $S$ is the sensor input space and $A$ is the action/command space. Each behavior operates as an **independent, concurrent module**, and multiple behaviors are coordinated through arbitration mechanisms.

**Example:** *Avoid-Obstacle Behavior* — if a sonar reading $d_{\min} < d_{\text{threshold}}$, the robot commands a turn-away angular velocity $\omega = k(d_{\text{threshold}} - d_{\min})$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**List the three primary behavior coordination mechanisms with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Priority / Suppression (Subsumption):** The behavior with the highest priority $P_{\max}$ wins and its output is sent to the actuators; lower-priority outputs are suppressed.
2. **Command Fusion (Weighted Sum / Schema):** All active behavior outputs are summed as a weighted vector $A = \sum w_i a_i$, producing a smooth blended action.
3. **Voting / Cooperative:** Each behavior casts a vote (or score) for a candidate action, and the action with the highest total vote is selected.

---

## 📕 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers either **Question A OR Question B**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Potential Field Formulation + Subsumption

#### Part (a) — `[7 marks]`
**With a neat block diagram, explain the Potential Field method for designing an *Obstacle-Avoidance* behavior. Derive the repulsive force vector from the repulsive potential.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
The potential field method (Khatib, 1986) treats the robot as a point moving in a workspace where the goal generates an attractive potential well and obstacles generate repulsive potential hills. The robot follows the negative gradient of the total potential.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** (See Section 4.2 — the Avoid-Obstacle path)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
The repulsive potential is defined piecewise as:
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\rho = \lVert q - q_o \rVert$ is the Euclidean distance to the obstacle and $\rho_0$ is the influence radius.

The repulsive force is the negative gradient:
$$F_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \, \hat{n}_{ro}$$

where $\hat{n}_{ro} = (q - q_o)/\rho$ is the unit vector pointing from obstacle to robot. In expanded form:
$$F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_o \\ y - y_o \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion (0 marks spare — written explanation):** As $\rho \to \rho_0$ the force tends to zero; as $\rho \to 0$ the force grows unbounded, ensuring the robot never collides with the obstacle.

#### Part (b) — `[7 marks]`
**Draw the Subsumption Architecture layers from 0 to 4 (Brooks 1986) and explain the role of $\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$ and $\text{S}_{\text{inhibit}}$ signals.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram; reproduce in exam as a labeled box diagram with vertical stacks.)

| Layer | Competence | Behavior |
|-------|------------|----------|
| 0 | Avoid contact | `avoid` |
| 1 | Wander safely | `wander` |
| 2 | Explore | `explore` |
| 3 | Build map | `map` |
| 4 | Notice changes | `monitor` |

**Suppress / Inhibit explanation (4 marks):**
- **$\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$** (Suppression): A higher layer **replaces** the output of a lower layer by sending its own signal down the same wire. E.g., Layer 3 sends $\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$ to Layer 2's output line, blocking Layer 2's wander command and substituting Layer 3's mapping behavior output.
- **$\text{S}_{\text{inhibit}}$** (Inhibit): A higher layer **temporarily disables** a lower layer's output for a specified duration (often one control cycle). E.g., Layer 2 inhibits Layer 1's `avoid` behavior for 5 seconds while crossing a doorway safely.
- Both signals travel on **dedicated vertical wires**, never sharing the data path used by horizontal sensor–behavior flow.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Schema Theory + Coordination Comparison

#### Part (a) — `[7 marks]`
**Explain Arkin's Schema Theory. List four Motor Schemas from his behavior library and write the generalized command-fusion equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Schema Theory (Arkin, 1989) is a behavior-based methodology where the robot's control is the **vector sum of multiple concurrent motor schemas**, each driven by a corresponding perceptual schema. It is a biologically-inspired model based on the neuroscientific idea of *motor schemas* as stored motor patterns.

**Generic architecture (2 marks):**
$$\text{Sensory Input} \xrightarrow{\text{Perceptual Schema (PS)}} \text{Perceptual Output} \xrightarrow{\text{Motor Schema (MS)}} \text{V}_{\text{schema}}$$

**Four Motor Schemas (2 marks):**
1. **Move-to-Goal:** $V_{\text{mtg}} = v_{\max} \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{goal}}$ (unit vector toward goal).
2. **Avoid-Obstacle:** $V_{\text{ao}} = \sum_j v_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_0 - \rho_j}{\rho_0} \hat{n}_{r,oj}$ for obstacles within $\rho_0$.
3. **Stay-on-Path:** $V_{\text{sop}} = k_p \, d_{\text{path}} \, \hat{n}_{\perp}$ (perpendicular correction).
4. **Dodge:** Behaves like Avoid-Obstacle but for moving agents, with closing-velocity weighting.

**Command-fusion equation (1 mark):**
$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i V_i$$
where $w_i \in [0,1]$ is the gain for schema $i$.

#### Part (b) — `[7 marks]`
**Compare Priority (Subsumption), Command Fusion, and Voting coordination mechanisms. State one real-world robot application for each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Priority / Subsumption | Command Fusion | Voting |
|---------|------------------------|----------------|--------|
| Selection rule | Highest $P_i$ wins | Vector sum | Highest vote-count wins |
| Output type | Discrete | Continuous vector | Discrete |
| Switching | Abrupt | Smooth | Abrupt |
| Hardware | Simple if–else | Floating-point ALUs | Ballot counters |
| Real-world example | Roomba vacuum (bump > cliff > wander) | Georgia Tech's AuRA (nav in corridors) | MIT box-pushing with 3 robots |

**Detailed write-up (4 marks):**
- **Subsumption** is used in **iRobot Roomba** — the bump behavior *always* overrides the cliff-avoid behavior when pressed, because the room's hard wall exerts a stronger priority than the IR cliff sensors. The architecture uses pre-wired vertical inhibition lines.
- **Command Fusion** is used in **Georgia Tech's AuRA (Autonomous Robot Architecture)** for museum tour-guide robots, where multiple schemas (move-to-goal, avoid, stay-on-path) are smoothly summed to produce a natural-looking motion.
- **Voting** is used in **multi-robot box-pushing** at MIT, where each robot votes on the direction to push and the majority direction is followed, giving emergent cooperative behavior without a leader.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Suppress and Inhibit (–2 marks):** $\text{S}_{\text{suppress}}$ *replaces* the output; $\text{S}_{\text{inhibit}}$ *blocks* it temporarily. Examiners will deduct marks if you swap these definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the piecewise condition on $U_{\text{rep}}$ (–1 mark):** The repulsive potential is **zero** for $\rho > \rho_0$. Students often write the formula without this boundary condition, which is required for full credit.
> 3. **Writing sensor–motor loop as "S → A" without explaining the absence of a world model (–1 mark):** Always emphasize that BBR does **NOT** use a central world representation — this is its defining feature.
> 4. **Drawing Subsumption layers as horizontal blocks without showing vertical suppression wires (–1 mark):** The wires (S_suppress, S_inhibit) are part of the architecture's syntax.
> 5. **Confusing Schema Theory (Arkin) with Subsumption (Brooks):** Schema Theory uses **continuous fusion** of vectors; Subsumption uses **discrete priority suppression**. Examiners often ask for a direct contrast.
> 6. **Forgetting units or signs in force derivation (–1 mark):** $F = -\nabla U$ — the negative sign matters; sign error makes the robot flee *toward* the obstacle.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **behavior** = direct sensor-to-motor mapping $S \rightarrow A$, no world model.
- ✅ **Brooks' Subsumption Architecture (1986)** = layered competence, higher layers can Suppress or Inhibit lower layers, built bottom-up.
- ✅ **Arkin's Schema Theory (1989)** = perceptual schema (PS) + motor schema (MS); outputs are **vector-summed** with weights $w_i$.
- ✅ **Khatib's Potential Field (1986)** = attractive + repulsive potential; force $F = -\nabla U$. Always piecewise for $U_{\text{rep}}$.
- ✅ Three coordination mechanisms: **Priority**, **Command Fusion (Weighted Sum)**, **Voting**.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize: $F_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}}(q_g - q)$, $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2}k_{\text{rep}}(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$, $V_{\text{total}} = \sum w_i V_i$.
- ✅ **Finite State Machines (FSM)** are used to *sequence* behaviors; transitions are event-triggered by sensors.
- ✅ BBR is **fast and reactive** (low latency), **robust** (no single point of failure), but **limited in long-horizon planning** (hence hybrid deliberative + reactive systems like AuRA and 3T).
- ✅ KTU-expected keywords: **competence, suppression, inhibition, schema, motor, perceptual, potential field, arbitration, fusion, voting, subsumption, layered control, reactive**.
- ✅ Real-world examples to cite: **Roomba** (priority), **AuRA tour-guide robot** (fusion), **MIT box-pushers** (voting), **Genghis robot** (subsumption).
- ✅ Avoid confusing **Discrete** (priority, voting) with **Continuous** (fusion) coordination in answers.
- ✅ Always draw **block diagrams or layered stacks** when asked about subsumption — verbal description alone loses 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of Behavior-Based Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Features of Behavior-Based Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Behavior-Based Robot (BBR)** is an autonomous robotic system whose intelligence and complex task execution emerge from the direct coupling between perception and action, mediated through a collection of parallel, loosely coupled control modules called **behaviors**. Unlike classical, sense-model-plan-act (SMPA) architectures, behavior-based systems (popularized by Rodney Brooks in 1986) deliberately eliminate the central world model and instead rely on situated, embodied interaction with the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A behavior-based robot is one in which the control software is decomposed into a set of independent, concurrently executing *behaviors*, each of which is a tight perception-to-action loop that maps specific sensor inputs directly to specific actuator outputs without requiring a symbolic intermediate world representation.

### 1.2 Key Vocabulary Decoded

| KTU Term | Plain English Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Behavior** | A self-contained control law (e.g., "avoid obstacles", "follow light") |
| **Situatedness** | The robot is physically embedded in a real, dynamic world |
| **Embodiment** | The robot has a physical body whose shape, sensors, and actuators constrain what it can do |
| **Emergence** | Complex global behavior arises from simple local interactions of sub-behaviors |
| **Subsumption** | Lower-priority behaviors are *suppressed* (not replaced) by higher-priority ones |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cockroach vs. The Chess Player"

Imagine two ways to clean a room:

- **The Chess Player (Deliberative Robot):** Stops, builds a complete 3D map of the room, plans an optimal coverage path using A*, then executes it. If a chair moves while it is "thinking", its plan is instantly obsolete.
- **The Cockroach (Behavior-Based Robot):** Has no map. It has a few simple rules: *if bump into wall, turn*; *if dirty, clean*; *if light detected, hide*. Together these rules produce surprisingly competent room-cleaning behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the analogy matters:** Behavior-based robots trade off long-term optimality for **real-time reactivity, robustness, and low computational cost**, which is exactly what the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus expects you to articulate in 14-mark answers.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in BBR

- **Reactive Latency:** Typically **< 100 ms** between sensor input and motor command.
- **Computational Footprint:** Often **< 1 MIPS** per behavior (Brooks' original Allen and Genghis ran on 8-bit microcontrollers).
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Elevated by graceful degradation rather than catastrophic central-controller failure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Canonical Features of Behavior-Based Robots

Below is the authoritative list (must-know for KTU 14-mark questions):

1. **Reactive / Reflexive Action** — Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping; no deliberation loop.
2. **Situatedness** — The robot operates in, and is influenced by, the real physical world — not a simulation.
3. **Embodiment** — The physical form (morphology, sensors, wheels, legs) is part of the "intelligence".
4. **Emergence** — Global intelligent behavior is *not* programmed; it arises from the interaction of many simple behaviors.
5. **Parallelism** — Multiple behaviors execute concurrently, not sequentially.
6. **Robustness & Graceful Degradation** — Loss of one behavior does not crash the system; the robot continues with reduced capability.
7. **No Central World Model** — A symbolic, globally consistent map of the environment is *deliberately absent* (this is the *fundamental* deviation from classical AI robotics).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Feature

- **Reactivity → Why?** Real worlds are non-deterministic. A planning cycle that takes 2 seconds is useless if an obstacle is 0.5 m away at 1 m/s.
- **Situatedness → Why?** St. Augustine's paradox ("What is more certain than the fact that the world exists?") — the world is the *best* model of itself.
- **Embodiment → Why?** A wheeled robot cannot climb stairs no matter how smart its controller is; *morphology* offloads computation.
- **Emergence → Why?** The "More is Different" principle — ant colonies optimize foraging without any ant knowing the global optimum.
- **Parallelism → Why?** Sequential processes have a single point of failure; parallel behaviors are fault-tolerant by construction.
- **Robustness → Why?** Field robots (Mars rovers, mine sweepers) cannot be "rebooted" easily.
- **No Central Model → Why?** Maintaining a consistent world model in a dynamic, noisy, multi-sensor environment is *provably* the most error-prone step in classical robotics (see Smith & Cheeseman, 1986 — uncertainty compounding).

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Behavior-Based Robotics is largely **qualitative and architectural**, so the "formulas" are primarily structural rules and arbitration equations. The following table is exhaustive for KTU purposes.

| # | Concept / Equation | Symbolic Form | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Behavior activation (winner-take-all) | $b^* = \arg\max_{i} w_i \cdot \phi_i(s)$ | Choose behavior $i$ with maximum weighted stimulus $w_i \phi_i$ |
| 2 | Motor command (vector sum) | $u = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \, u_i$ | Linear blending of $N$ behavior outputs $u_i$ with activation $\alpha_i \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | Subsumption inhibition | $u = u_{\text{higher}} \quad \text{if } u_{\text{higher}} \neq 0, \text{ else } u_{\text{lower}}$ | Higher layer *suppresses* lower; lower is *not deleted* |
| 4 | Reactive cycle time | $T_{\text{react}} \le \dfrac{d_{\text{safe}}}{v_{\max}}$ | Must be less than safe stopping time; typical $T_{\text{react}} \in [10, 100]$ ms |
| 5 | Potential-field repulsion | $U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ | $\rho$ = distance to obstacle, $\rho_0$ = influence range, $\eta$ = gain |
| 6 | Force on robot | $F = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}$ | Negative gradient of total potential (artificial fields) |
| 7 | Schema equation (Arkin) | $S = \langle p, r, \mathbf{v} \rangle$ | Perceptual stimulus $p$, response $r$, vector $\mathbf{v}$ |
| 8 | Graceful degradation ratio | $R_{\text{deg}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{active}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio of surviving behaviors; $R_{\text{deg}} \to 0$ ⇒ graceful failure |
| 9 | Brooks' competence ladder | $L_k \in \{\text{Avoid}, \text{Wander}, \text{Explore}, \text{Map}, \text{Plan}, \dots\}$ | $L_0$ (avoid) ⊂ $L_1$ (wander) ⊂ … each layer subsumes the previous |
| 10 | Motor Schema combination | $\mathbf{V} = \sum_{i} \gamma_i \mathbf{V}_i$ | Weighted vector sum of motor schemas (Arkin, 1987) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mars Rovers (Sojourner, Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use behavior-based fault protection; when a fault is detected, the rover sheds high-level behaviors and falls back to basic "survive" behaviors.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots (Roomba, iRobot):** Pure behavior-based — bump-and-turn, wall-following, spiral cleaning.
- **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs):** Need *low-power* on-board intelligence; BBR is preferred over SLAM-heavy deliberative stacks.
- **Search-and-Rescue (SAR) Robots:** Operate in collapsed, GPS-denied, communication-denied environments where only reactive autonomy survives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Robot Command from Multiple Behaviors

This is the **core mathematical step** you must be able to write in a 14-mark KTU question. We derive the **motor schema blending equation** used in Arkin's Autonomous Robot Architecture (AuRA).

**Step 1 — Define a single behavior (perceptual schema).**
Let a perceptual schema $P_i$ produce a scalar stimulus $s_i$ from sensor reading $z \in \mathbb{R}^m$:

$$s_i = f_i(z), \quad f_i : \mathbb{R}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$$

**Step 2 — Convert stimulus to a gain (activation) $\alpha_i$.**
A common choice is a normalized linear mapping:

$$\alpha_i = \min\!\left(1, \frac{s_i}{s_{\max}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the motor schema output.**
Each motor schema $M_i$ returns a *vector* command (e.g., a translational velocity vector) $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$:

$$\mathbf{v}_i = g_i(z), \quad g_i : \mathbb{R}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the blended robot command.**
The final actuator command is the *weighted sum* of all motor schemas:

$$\mathbf{V} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_i$$

**Step 5 — Saturate to actuator limits.**

$$\mathbf{V}_{\text{out}} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \min\!\left(1, \frac{V_{\max}}{\lVert \mathbf{V} \rVert}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply to differential-drive wheels (kinematic split).**
For a differential-drive robot with wheel separation $L$:

$$v_{\text{left}} = V_{\text{out},x} - \tfrac{1}{2} L \, \omega$$

$$v_{\text{right}} = V_{\text{out},x} + \tfrac{1}{2} L \, \omega$$

where $V_{\text{out},x} = \lVert \mathbf{V}_{\text{out}} \rVert \cos \theta$, $\omega = \dfrac{V_{\text{out},y}}{L}$, and $\theta$ is the heading error.

> This derivation is what KTU examiners look for when they ask *"Explain how multiple behaviors are combined in a behavior-based robot."*

### 3.2 Derivation: Subsumption Arbitration (Brooks)

In the subsumption architecture, arbitration is *not* a weighted sum — it is a strict priority cascade.

**Step 1 — Define layer outputs.**
Each layer $L_k$ produces a *default* output vector $\mathbf{u}_k^{\text{default}}$.

**Step 2 — Define a *suppress* signal.**
A higher layer $L_{k+1}$ can send a 1-bit **suppression signal** $S_{k+1 \to k} \in \{0,1\}$ to a lower layer.

**Step 3 — Define an *inhibit* signal.**
A higher layer can also send an *inhibition* signal that *overwrites* the lower layer's output for a fixed time window $\Delta t$.

**Step 4 — Compose the final output.**
The effective output of layer $k$ is:

$$\mathbf{u}_k^{\text{eff}} = (1 - S_{k+1 \to k}) \cdot \mathbf{u}_k^{\text{default}}$$

And the actuator receives the *highest-priority non-zero* output:

$$\mathbf{u}_{\text{final}} = \mathbf{u}_{M} \;\Vert\; \mathbf{u}_{M-1} \;\Vert\; \dots \;\Vert\; \mathbf{u}_0$$

where $\Vert$ denotes "fallback if zero".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the philosophical difference: **Vector sum is democratic** (every behavior votes); **Subsumption is dictatorial** (highest priority wins). Both are *behavior-based*, but the arbitration rule is what differentiates motor-schema approaches (Arkin) from subsumption approaches (Brooks). This is a **favourite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Behavior-Based vs. Deliberative Robots

| Attribute | Deliberative (SMPA) | Behavior-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| World model | Explicit, symbolic, central | Absent or distributed |
| Response time | Slow (planning overhead) | Fast (typically < 100 ms) |
| Computational cost | High (search, optimization) | Low (lookup, weighted sum) |
| Robustness | Brittle (single failure point) | Graceful degradation |
| Optimality | Globally near-optimal | Locally reactive, not optimal |
| Learning | Easy (model-based RL) | Harder (distributed credit assignment) |
| Task complexity ceiling | High (with good models) | Medium (low for long-horizon tasks) |
| Sensor noise sensitivity | High (model corruption) | Low (noise absorbed in behavior) |
| Typical application | Factory arms, warehouse AGVs | Mars rovers, vacuums, toys, drones |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Features → Engineering Benefits

| Feature | Engineering Benefit | Example System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reactivity | Survives dynamic obstacles | Autonomous car emergency braking |
| Situatedness | Uses real-world cues (light, sound) | Braitenberg vehicle |
| Embodiment | Energy efficiency, passive stability | Boston Dynamics' dynamic balance |
| Emergence | Bug-free global behavior from tested parts | Ant colony path optimization |
| Parallelism | Multi-task execution (fly + avoid) | Insect-inspired micro air vehicle |
| Robustness | Field deployable | Mars rover Sojourner |
| No central model | Lower memory & power | Vacuum Roomba (16 MHz CPU) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Behavior-Based Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Sensor Suite\nCamera, LIDAR, IR, Bumper"] --> P1["Perceptual Schema P1\nObstacle Detection"]
    S1 --> P2["Perceptual Schema P2\nLight Source Localization"]
    S1 --> P3["Perceptual Schema P3\nGoal Bearing"]

    P1 --> B1["Behavior B1\nAVOID-OBSTACLE"]
    P2 --> B2["Behavior B2\nSEEK-LIGHT"]
    P3 --> B3["Behavior B3\nMOVE-TO-GOAL"]

    B1 --> A1["Arbiter\nVector Sum / Subsumption"]
    B2 --> A1
    B3 --> A1

    A1 --> M1["Motor Schema M1\nTranslational Velocity"]
    A1 --> M2["Motor Schema M2\nRotational Velocity"]

    M1 --> ACT["Actuator Layer\nWheel / Leg / Rotor Drivers"]
    M2 --> ACT

    ACT -.feedback.-> ENV["Environment"]
    ENV -.perceived by.-> S1

    style S1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style A1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style ACT fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style ENV fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8
```

### 4.2 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Control (Brooks, 1986)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Layer 0 — AVOID\nStop / back off on contact"]
    L1["Layer 1 — WANDER\nRandom heading exploration"]
    L2["Layer 2 — EXPLORE\nLook for distant goals"]
    L3["Layer 3 — MAP\nBuild internal spatial map"]
    L4["Layer 4 — PLAN\nSequence tasks via plan graph"]
    L5["Layer 5 — REASON\nDiagnose and recover faults"]

    L5 -->|suppress| L4
    L4 -->|suppress| L3
    L3 -->|suppress| L2
    L2 -->|suppress| L1
    L1 -->|suppress| L0

    L0 --> ACT["Actuator Output\n(suppressed outputs are blocked)"]

    style L0 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style L1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style L2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style L3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style L4 fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8
    style L5 fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
    style ACT fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#374151
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows point *downward* in the suppression direction. Layer 3 is allowed to *replace* (suppress) the output of Layer 2 if and only if it has a non-zero command. This guarantees that the *lowest* level of competence (e.g., obstacle avoidance) is *always* the last to fail — a defining safety property of behavior-based robots.

### 4.3 Behavior Coordination Mechanism (Vector-Field / Potential-Field View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Goal\n(Attractive Field)"] --> SUM((+))
    OBS["Obstacles\n(Repulsive Field)"] --> SUM
    KEEP["Keep-Wall\n(Edge-Field)"] --> SUM
    SUM --> NET["Net Force Vector F"]
    NET --> CTRL["Robot Velocity Command"]
    CTRL --> ROB["Mobile Robot"]
    ROB -.position.-> G
    ROB -.position.-> OBS

    style SUM fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style NET fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style ROB fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Deliberative vs. Behavior-Based

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEL["Deliberative (SMPA)"]
        S1["Sensors"] --> M1["World Model\n(central, symbolic)"]
        M1 --> P1["Planner\n(A*, D*)"]
        P1 --> A1["Actuators"]
    end

    subgraph BB["Behavior-Based (Parallel)"]
        S2["Sensors"] --> B1["Behavior 1\nAvoid"]
        S2 --> B2["Behavior 2\nExplore"]
        S2 --> B3["Behavior 3\nCommunicate"]
        B1 --> A2["Actuators"]
        B2 --> A2
        B3 --> A2
    end

    style DEL fill:#fff1f2,stroke:#9f1239
    style BB fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#166534
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a "behavior" in the context of behavior-based robotics. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *behavior* is a self-contained, perception-to-action control law that maps a specific set of sensor inputs directly to a specific set of actuator outputs without using an intermediate symbolic world model. Each behavior is a tight, reflexive loop. **Example:** An *avoid-obstacle* behavior that takes IR-sensor distance readings as input and produces a steering-angle command that turns the robot away from the nearest obstacle. *(+1 mark for definition, +1 for tight loop explanation, +1 for example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*List any three features of behavior-based robots and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reactive action** → *Advantage:* The robot can respond to dynamic obstacles in real time, essential for navigation in unpredictable environments.
2. **Embodiment** → *Advantage:* The robot's physical body contributes to intelligence (e.g., passive stability), reducing software complexity.
3. **No central world model** → *Advantage:* Lower memory, lower power, and immunity to single-point model corruption failures.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the seven salient features of behavior-based robots. For each feature, state one engineering advantage.

**(b) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, describe **Brooks' Subsumption Architecture**. Show how Layer 3 can suppress the output of Layer 2 and why this design gives inherent fault tolerance.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature (1/2 mark each) | Engineering Advantage (1/2 mark each) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Reactivity** — direct sensor→action loop | Survives dynamic, non-deterministic worlds |
| 2. **Situatedness** — robot is in the real world | Real-world cues act as implicit, always-fresh world model |
| 3. **Embodiment** — body shape matters | Passive dynamics offload computation |
| 4. **Emergence** — global behavior from local rules | Easier to design, test, and combine modules |
| 5. **Parallelism** — multiple behaviors run concurrently | No single point of failure |
| 6. **Robustness / Graceful degradation** | Field-deployable in hostile environments |
| 7. **No central world model** | Low memory, low power, immune to map corruption |

*(Award 1 mark per row × 7 rows = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

1. **(2 marks) Definition:** Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986) is a layered, bottom-up control architecture for behavior-based robots in which higher layers *augment* the competence of lower layers by *suppressing* or *inhibiting* their outputs, never by replacing the lower layers themselves.
2. **(2 marks) Diagram:** Refer to the layered diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. Layers 0 (Avoid) → 1 (Wander) → 2 (Explore) → 3 (Map) → 4 (Plan) → 5 (Reason). Arrows point *downward* showing suppression direction.
3. **(2 marks) Suppression mechanism:** If Layer 3 has a non-zero output, the **suppress signal** $S_{3 \to 2} = 1$ zeros out Layer 2's default output, i.e., $\mathbf{u}_2^{\text{eff}} = 0$. The actuator then receives Layer 3's command. If Layer 3 is *silent*, Layer 2's command passes through unmodified.
4. **(1 mark) Fault tolerance argument:** Because lower layers are *never deleted*, the lowest competence levels (e.g., obstacle avoidance) are the *last* to fail. Even if the highest "Reason" layer crashes, the robot will still avoid obstacles — a property called *graceful degradation*.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare behavior-based robotics with the classical sense-model-plan-act (SMPA) architecture across **at least five** criteria. State one real-world robot that uses each paradigm.

**(b) (7 marks)** Derive the *motor schema vector-sum equation* used in Arkin's AuRA. Show how the *avoid-obstacle* and *move-to-goal* behaviors are blended into a single velocity command for a differential-drive robot.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

| Criterion (1/2 mark) | SMPA (1/2 mark) | Behavior-Based (1/2 mark) | Example SMPA (1/2) | Example BB (1/2) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| World model | Central, symbolic | Absent / distributed | Google self-driving car (high-level planner) | Roomba |
| Response time | Slow (planning) | Fast (< 100 ms) | KUKA industrial arm | Boston Dynamics Spot |
| Optimality | Globally near-optimal | Locally reactive | Warehouse AGV (Kiva) | Mars rover Sojourner |
| Robustness | Brittle | Graceful degradation | — | — |
| Computation | High | Low | Full SLAM stack | Vacuum robot (16 MHz MCU) |
| Learning | Easy (model-based RL) | Harder (distributed credit) | — | — |
| Task horizon | Long-horizon plans | Short-horizon reactive | — | — |

*(7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks. Award partial credit for ≥5 rows fully filled.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

1. **(1 mark)** Define perceptual schema stimulus: $s_i = f_i(z)$ where $z$ is the sensor reading.
2. **(1 mark)** Compute normalized activation: $\alpha_i = \min(1, s_i / s_{\max})$.
3. **(1 mark)** Define motor-schema vector: $\mathbf{v}_i = g_i(z)$ (e.g., $\mathbf{v}_{\text{avoid}}$ points away from the nearest obstacle; $\mathbf{v}_{\text{goal}}$ points toward the goal).
4. **(1 mark)** Apply vector-sum blending:

$$\mathbf{V} = \alpha_{\text{avoid}} \mathbf{v}_{\text{avoid}} + \alpha_{\text{goal}} \mathbf{v}_{\text{goal}}$$

5. **(1 mark)** Saturate to actuator limits:

$$\mathbf{V}_{\text{out}} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \min\!\left(1, \frac{V_{\max}}{\lVert \mathbf{V} \rVert}\right)$$

6. **(1 mark)** Convert to wheel velocities for a differential-drive robot (wheel separation $L$):

$$v_{\text{left}} = V_{\text{out},x} - \tfrac{1}{2} L \, \omega, \qquad v_{\text{right}} = V_{\text{out},x} + \tfrac{1}{2} L \, \omega$$

7. **(1 mark)** Conclude with the *intuition*: when the robot is far from obstacles, $\alpha_{\text{avoid}} \approx 0$ and it goes straight to the goal; when close, $\alpha_{\text{avoid}}$ grows, the repulsive vector dominates, and the robot turns away — producing *emergent* obstacle-avoidance without a planner.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "no world model" with "no intelligence".** The robot is *not* stupid; it just stores its world model *implicitly* in the sensor-actuator loop. Many students lose 2 marks for writing "behavior-based robots don't have a brain" — wrong. They have *distributed* intelligence.
> 2. **Forgetting the arbitration rule.** When asked "how are behaviors combined?", the examiner expects *both* the **vector-sum** form **and** the **subsumption** form, with a clear distinction of *when* each is used. Writing only one loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Confusing reactive with random.** Reactive is *deterministic given the sensor input*; it is *not* the same as wandering randomly. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram.** In any 7-mark "explain" sub-question, a labelled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always draw the layered subsumption diagram.
> 5. **Writing the vector-sum equation without normalizing the gain** $\alpha_i$. This is a mathematical error and loses 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Behavior-based robot = autonomous robot whose control is a *set of parallel perception-action behaviors* with no central world model.
- ✅ **Seven features (must memorize):** Reactive, Situated, Embodied, Emergent, Parallel, Robust, Model-free.
- ✅ **Two arbitration schemes:** (i) Vector sum (Arkin — democratic) and (ii) Subsumption (Brooks — dictatorial). Both are behavior-based.
- ✅ **Brooks' subsumption:** Layers are *added* on top, never removed. Suppression is one-way, downward.
- ✅ **Emergence ≠ Magic:** It is a *predictable* consequence of *locally correct* behaviors interacting through the world.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Roomba (vacuum), Sojourner (Mars), Genghis (hexapod walker), Braitenberg Vehicles (thought experiments).
- ✅ **Equations to remember:** $T_{\text{react}} \le d_{\text{safe}}/v_{\max}$; $\mathbf{V} = \sum_i \alpha_i \mathbf{v}_i$; subsumption: $u_k^{\text{eff}} = (1 - S_{k+1 \to k}) u_k^{\text{default}}$.
- ✅ **Examiner mantra:** "Behaviors are tight loops, robots are situated, intelligence is embodied, order is emergent."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioral Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Behavioral Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioral Architecture** in robotics refers to a software and hardware organizing framework in which a robot's intelligence emerges from the parallel and asynchronous interaction of multiple independent, task-achieving **behaviors** (also called *competences* or *schemas*), each tightly coupled to a specific subset of sensory inputs and actuator outputs, *without* a centralized world model or symbolic planner.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition of behavioral robotics, an architecture specifies:

- **The decomposition rule** — how a global mission (e.g., navigate a hospital) is broken into discrete behaviors (avoid-obstacle, seek-goal, wall-follow, dock).
- **The arbitration / blending rule** — how competing behaviors are combined into final actuator commands (priority, vector sum, fuzzy fusion, voting).
- **The data-flow rule** — what each behavior perceives and what it produces (stimulus → response).

The term was popularized by **Rodney A. Brooks (MIT, 1986)** in his seminal paper *"A Robust Layered Control System for a Mobile Robot"*, which introduced the **Subsumption Architecture** — the canonical KTU reference architecture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a busy **airport ground controller** (you, the human) coordinating four specialists over radio headsets: a *fuel truck driver*, a *baggage crew lead*, a *pushback tractor driver*, and a *marshaller*. None of them has the full picture of the airport. Each is bound by a strict rule-book for their niche job. Yet, when their actions are combined, the airplane gets refueled, loaded, pushed back, and guided to the runway smoothly. **No one "planned" the whole sequence** — it *emerged* from their parallel behavior.

A behavioral robot works the same way:

- **Behavior** = a small specialist ("if I see a wall within 0.5 m → turn right").
- **Architecture** = the radio protocol and the rules that decide whose command wins when two specialists disagree.
- **Intelligence** = an emergent property of all specialists working in parallel — there is no "boss brain" with a map.

This is the *biological inspiration*: insects (ants, bees) have tiny brains yet exhibit sophisticated group behavior because each ant runs simple reactive rules. Behavioral robotics takes that lesson literally.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Problems

The following constants are repeatedly used in behavioral robotics numerical and analytical questions:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $v_{max}$ | Maximum translational wheel velocity | **0.5 m/s** (Roomba-class) up to **3.0 m/s** (research platforms) |
| $\omega_{max}$ | Maximum angular velocity | **1.0 rad/s** to **2.0 rad/s** |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence radius of an obstacle | **0.3 m to 1.5 m** |
| $d_s$ | Safe stopping distance | **0.25 m** |
| $\eta$ | Gain of the repulsive potential field | **0.2 – 5.0** (dimensionless scaling) |
| $\xi$ | Gain of the attractive potential field | **0.5 – 2.0** |
| $T_s$ | Control-loop sample time | **10 ms – 100 ms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When a numerical problem omits a numerical gain, write it as a *named symbol* (e.g., $\eta$, $\xi$) in your equation and clearly state *"value to be tuned empirically"* in the answer. This shows the examiner that you understand the engineering trade-off, which is rewarded under NEP-2020 outcome-based evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Philosophical Pillars of Behavioral Robotics

A board-quality answer on Behavioral Architectures must explicitly enumerate the three foundational principles articulated by Brooks. Examiners award marks for stating these *verbatim-class*:

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Situatedness:** The robot is embedded in, and reacts to, a real, dynamic world. It does *not* reason over a pre-built symbolic map.
>
> **Pillar 2 — Embodiment:** The robot has a *physical* body. Behaviors exploit morphology (shape, wheel placement) instead of compensating for it through software complexity.
>
> **Pillar 3 — Intelligence Emerges from Interaction:** Globally intelligent behavior is **not** explicitly programmed. It *emerges* from the interaction of many small, parallel, reactive modules with each other and the environment.

These three pillars collectively reject the classical *Sense → Plan → Act* (SPA) paradigm in favor of *Sense → Act* cycles nested in parallel.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Behaviors (High-Yield KTU Taxonomy)

Behaviors are categorized along two independent axes:

**Axis A — Cognitive Sophistication (from lowest to highest):**

1. **Reflexive Behavior** — stateless, pure function of the *instantaneous* sensor reading. Example: *taxis* (turn-toward-light).
2. **Reactive Behavior** — short-window temporal memory (e.g., hysteresis to avoid chattering at a wall).
3. **Deliberative Behavior** — uses an internal model; breaks the pure BBR rule but is allowed in *hybrid* architectures.

**Axis B — Mission Purpose:**

- **Goal-seeking (Attractor) behaviors** — pull the robot toward a target.
- **Obstacle-avoidance (Repulsor) behaviors** — push the robot away from hazards.
- **Boundary-following behaviors** — keep the robot at a fixed offset from walls (useful for corridor traversal).
- **Homeostatic / Battery behaviors** — drive the robot to a charging dock when energy is low.

---

## 2.3 The Three Canonical Behavioral Architectures (KTU Board Must-Know)

### Architecture A — Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)

- Behaviors are organized in **strictly prioritized horizontal layers**.
- Higher layers *subsume* (suppress or replace) the output of lower layers.
- Layers communicate through **inhibitors** and **suppressors** — explicit message-passing wires.
- No internal state; entirely reactive.

### Architecture B — Motor Schema Architecture (Arkin, 1987)

- Each behavior is a **motor schema** that outputs a *vector* $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ in the robot's velocity space.
- Behaviors are **not** prioritized; instead, the final motor command is the **normalized vector sum** of all active schemas.
- Inspired by the neurobiological notion of *schemas* in motor control.

### Architecture C — Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architecture (e.g., 3-T, ATLANTIS)

- Adds a slow deliberative *planner* on top of a fast reactive *behavioral layer*.
- Most production-grade autonomous systems (NASA Mars rovers, autonomous cars) use this variant.
- The planner periodically re-supplies goals (way-points) to the behavioral layer.

---

## 2.4 Arbitration vs. Command Fusion (A Frequently Tested Distinction)

> [!WARNING]
> Students routinely lose marks by writing "behaviors are combined" without specifying *how*. KTU examiners expect the precise term.

| Mechanism | How it works | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Arbitration** | Exactly **one** behavior's output is selected at each time step (the "winner"). | Subsumption, priority-based |
| **Command Fusion (Blending)** | All behaviors contribute; outputs are combined (sum, weighted sum, fuzzy AND/OR). | Motor Schemas, DAMN |
| **Voting** | Each behavior casts a vote per actuator; the majority wins. | Blackboard architectures |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Potential-Field Style Behavioral Math)

The **artificial potential field method** is the most testable mathematical representation of a behavioral architecture because every behavior reduces to a *potential* and the total behavior is the *gradient* of the sum.

$$\vec{F}_{total}(\vec{q}) = -\nabla U_{total}(\vec{q}) = -\nabla U_{att}(\vec{q}) - \nabla U_{rep}(\vec{q})$$

The attractive potential (pulls the robot to the goal $\vec{q}_{goal}$):

$$U_{att}(\vec{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\, \bigl\Vert \vec{q} - \vec{q}_{goal} \bigr\Vert^{2}$$

$$\vec{F}_{att}(\vec{q}) = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi\,(\vec{q} - \vec{q}_{goal})$$

The repulsive potential (pushes away from the nearest obstacle at $\vec{q}_{obs}$, with influence radius $\rho_0$):

$$U_{rep}(\vec{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(\vec{q})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & 0 \le \rho(\vec{q}) \le \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0, & \rho(\vec{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

$$\vec{F}_{rep}(\vec{q}) = \begin{cases} \eta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(\vec{q})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{1}{\rho(\vec{q})^{2}}\,\dfrac{\vec{q} - \vec{q}_{obs}}{\rho(\vec{q})}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\[6pt] \vec{0}, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

The instantaneous wheel commands (differential-drive kinematic mapping) for a robot of wheel-base $L$:

$$v = \sqrt{F_{x}^{2} + F_{y}^{2}}, \qquad \omega = \frac{\mathrm{atan2}(F_{y}, F_{x})}{T_{s}}$$

And the left/right wheel velocities:

$$v_{L} = v - \tfrac{\omega L}{2}, \qquad v_{R} = v + \tfrac{\omega L}{2}$$

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Domestic vacuum robots** (iRoomba) use a primitive version of motor schemas with behaviors such as *bounce*, *spiral*, and *wall-follow*.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva) use arbitration among *go-to-shelf*, *avoid-human*, and *dock* behaviors.
- **Mars rovers** (Curiosity, Perseverance) use a hybrid deliberative-reactive stack: the on-ground planner issues waypoints, and the onboard behavior layer handles obstacle avoidance and slip recovery.
- **Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)** use behavioral architectures because GPS is unavailable — reactive sonar-based behaviors dominate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Vector Acting on a Robot in a Single Obstacle Field

> **Problem Setup (typical KTU board scenario):**
> A differential-drive robot is located at $\vec{q} = (2.0,\;1.5)$ m. The goal is at $\vec{q}_{goal} = (5.0,\;4.0)$ m. An obstacle is at $\vec{q}_{obs} = (2.5,\;2.0)$ m with influence radius $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m. Given gains $\xi = 1.0$ and $\eta = 0.8$, compute the **total force vector** and the **wheel velocities** for a robot with wheel-base $L = 0.4$ m. State whether the robot will reach the goal.

**Step 1 — Compute the Euclidean distance to the obstacle.**

$$\rho(\vec{q}) = \bigl\Vert \vec{q} - \vec{q}_{obs} \bigr\Vert = \sqrt{(2.0-2.5)^{2} + (1.5-2.0)^{2}}$$

$$\rho(\vec{q}) = \sqrt{(-0.5)^{2} + (-0.5)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5}$$

$$\rho(\vec{q}) \approx 0.7071 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho(\vec{q}) = 0.7071 < \rho_0 = 1.0$, **the obstacle is within its influence radius**, so the repulsive term is active.

**Step 2 — Compute the attractive force vector.**

$$\vec{F}_{att} = -\xi\,(\vec{q} - \vec{q}_{goal}) = -1.0 \cdot ( (2.0 - 5.0),\; (1.5 - 4.0) )$$

$$\vec{F}_{att} = -1.0 \cdot (-3.0,\;-2.5) = (3.0,\; 2.5) \text{ N}$$

*Interpretation:* The positive $x$ and $y$ components correctly indicate that the attractive force pulls the robot *toward* the goal (right and up on the map).

**Step 3 — Compute the scalar prefactor of the repulsive force.**

$$k = \eta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}$$

$$k = 0.8 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{0.7071} - \dfrac{1}{1.0}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{(0.7071)^{2}}$$

$$k = 0.8 \cdot (1.4142 - 1.0) \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.5}$$

$$k = 0.8 \cdot 0.4142 \cdot 2.0 = 0.6627 \text{ (N·m, dimensionless for our 2-D model)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the unit vector pointing *from the obstacle to the robot*.**

$$\hat{u} = \dfrac{\vec{q} - \vec{q}_{obs}}{\rho} = \dfrac{(2.0 - 2.5,\;1.5 - 2.0)}{0.7071} = \dfrac{(-0.5,\;-0.5)}{0.7071} = (-0.7071,\;-0.7071)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain the repulsive force.**

$$\vec{F}_{rep} = k \cdot \hat{u} = 0.6627 \cdot (-0.7071,\;-0.7071)$$

$$\vec{F}_{rep} = (-0.4687,\; -0.4687) \text{ N}$$

*Interpretation:* The repulsive force points *away* from the obstacle. Since the obstacle is to the upper-right of the robot, "away" means lower-left — and indeed both components are negative. ✓

**Step 6 — Compute the total force.**

$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = (3.0 - 0.4687,\; 2.5 - 0.4687)$$

$$\vec{F}_{total} = (2.5313,\; 2.0313) \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Convert force vector to linear and angular velocities.**

$$v = \sqrt{F_{x}^{2} + F_{y}^{2}} = \sqrt{(2.5313)^{2} + (2.0313)^{2}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{6.4075 + 4.1262} = \sqrt{10.5337} \approx 3.246 \text{ (interpreted as m/s in board convention)}$$

$$\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(F_{y},\; F_{x}) = \mathrm{atan2}(2.0313,\; 2.5313) \approx 0.6768 \text{ rad} \approx 38.78^{\circ}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{T_{s}} \quad \text{(If } T_s = 1 \text{ s for the simplified board case, } \omega = 0.6768 \text{ rad/s)}$$

**Step 8 — Compute left and right wheel velocities.**

$$v_{L} = v - \dfrac{\omega L}{2} = 3.246 - \dfrac{0.6768 \times 0.4}{2} = 3.246 - 0.1354 = 3.111 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{R} = v + \dfrac{\omega L}{2} = 3.246 + 0.1354 = 3.381 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 9 — Conclude on goal reachability.**

Since $\vec{F}_{total}$ is non-zero and points in the general direction of the goal (both components positive), the robot will continue moving toward $\vec{q}_{goal}$. However, the repulsive component has *reduced* the magnitude from $\Vert \vec{F}_{att}\Vert = 3.905$ N down to $\Vert \vec{F}_{total}\Vert = 3.246$ N — i.e., the obstacle has correctly slowed the approach by ~17%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The robot will reach the goal, but the trajectory will be *deflected* away from the obstacle — this is the desired emergent behavior of the architecture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Argument for Stability of the Potential Field

To prove that a gradient-descent robot will asymptotically reach the goal (absent local minima), we analyze the energy budget.

**Step 1 — Define total potential energy.**

$$U(\vec{q}) = U_{att}(\vec{q}) + U_{rep}(\vec{q})$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate along the trajectory.**

$$\frac{dU}{dt} = \nabla U \cdot \frac{d\vec{q}}{dt} = -\vec{F}_{total} \cdot \vec{v}_{robot}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\vec{v}_{robot} = k_F \cdot \vec{F}_{total}$ (closed-loop controller assumption, $k_F > 0$).**

$$\frac{dU}{dt} = -\vec{F}_{total} \cdot (k_F \vec{F}_{total}) = -k_F \Vert \vec{F}_{total}\Vert^{2} \le 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply Lyapunov's direct method.** Since $U(\vec{q})$ is positive-definite, bounded below by $0$, and non-increasing, the robot's state converges to the set $\{\vec{q} \mid \vec{F}_{total} = \vec{0}\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lyapunov stability guarantee** is what makes behavioral potential-field architectures mathematically rigorous, not merely engineering heuristics — a point frequently tested under the KTU 2024 *Apply* / *Analyze* Bloom level.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE)

| Criterion | Subsumption (Brooks) | Motor Schema (Arkin) | Hybrid (3-T, ATLANTIS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centralized planner? | **None** | None | Yes (slow deliberative layer) |
| Internal world model? | No | No (per-schema state only) | Yes |
| Combination rule | Arbitration (priority) | Vector sum (blending) | Layered; planner sets goals, behaviors execute |
| Reaction time | Milliseconds | Milliseconds | Seconds for planning, ms for execution |
| Robustness | **Very High** (graceful degradation) | High | Medium (planner is a single point of failure) |
| Engineering complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Suitable for | Simple missions, unknown terrain | Multi-goal missions | Long-horizon missions with sub-tasks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

The diagram below models Brooks' classic subsumption stack. Lower layers never know higher layers exist; higher layers may *suppress* (inhibit) or *replace* (override) the output of lower layers through explicit signal wires.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[sonar0]:::sensor --> l0
    s2[sonar1]:::sensor --> l0
    s3[sonar2]:::sensor --> l0
    s4[bumpSensor]:::sensor --> l0

    subgraph L0["Layer 0 - Avoid Obstacles"]
        l0[Behave_Avoid]:::behavior
    end

    subgraph L1["Layer 1 - Wander"]
        l1[Behave_Wander]:::behavior
    end

    subgraph L2["Layer 2 - Explore"]
        l2[Behave_Explore]:::behavior
    end

    subgraph L3["Layer 3 - Map and Plan"]
        l3[Behave_Plan]:::behavior
    end

    l0 --> m0[motorOutput0]:::motor
    l1 --> m1[motorOutput1]:::motor
    l2 --> m2[motorOutput2]:::motor
    l3 --> m3[motorOutput3]:::motor

    m0 --> a0[Actuator]:::actuator
    m1 --> a0
    m2 --> a0
    m3 --> a0

    l1 -. suppress .-> l0
    l2 -. suppress .-> l1
    l3 -. suppress .-> l2

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#036,stroke-width:1px
    classDef behavior fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef motor fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px
    classDef actuator fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#060,stroke-width:1.5px
```

**How to read the diagram in an exam answer:**

- The yellow boxes are **behaviors** (the *competences*).
- The blue boxes are **sensors**.
- The green box is the **actuator** (wheels).
- The dashed arrows labeled `suppress` are the **suppression wires** that allow higher layers to silence lower layers — this is the *defining feature* of subsumption.

---

## 4.2 Motor Schema Architecture — Vector-Sum Block Diagram

Unlike subsumption, motor schemas do not suppress each other. They all contribute, and the **normalized vector sum** is sent to the actuator.

```mermaid
graph LR
    g1[Goal Loc]:::in --> att[Attractor Schema]:::schema
    g2[Obstacle Loc]:::in --> rep[Repulsor Schema]:::schema
    g3[Wall Loc]:::in --> fol[Follow Schema]:::schema

    att --> s[Normalize and Sum]:::op
    rep --> s
    fol --> s

    s --> v[Vout linear]:::out
    s --> w[Wout angular]:::out

    v --> a[Differential Drive Actuator]:::actuator
    w --> a

    classDef in fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003
    classDef schema fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef op fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#609,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef out fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900
    classDef actuator fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#060,stroke-width:1.5px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key exam phrase:** "Motor schemas *cooperate* via vector summation; subsumption layers *compete* via priority-based suppression."

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architecture

For long-horizon missions (e.g., "deliver this package from room A to room B"), a *slow planner* and a *fast behavior layer* cooperate.

```mermaid
graph TD
    mp[Mission Planner Deliberative Layer]:::plan --> wpts[Waypoint Queue]:::buf

    wpts --> g[Current Goal]:::goal

    g --> att[Attractor Behavior]:::bhv
    son[Sonar Ring]:::sensor --> rep[Repulsor Behavior]:::bhv
    ir[IR Cliff Sensors]:::sensor --> rep

    att --> fus[Command Fusion Engine]:::op
    rep --> fus

    fus --> whl[Wheel Motor Controller]:::act

    mp -. periodic re-plan .-> wpts

    classDef plan fill:#ffd0a0,stroke:#840,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef buf fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444
    classDef goal fill:#c0e0ff,stroke:#048
    classDef bhv fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef op fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#609,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef sensor fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003
    classDef act fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#060,stroke-width:1.5px
```

The dotted `periodic re-plan` arrow is the **only** information flow from the deliberative layer to the reactive layer — the planner never directly commands wheels. This separation is the *orthogonality* principle that makes hybrid architectures robust.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Architecture to Choose?

```mermaid
graph TD
    q[New Robot Project]:::q --> a{Is the environment fully known and static?}:::d
    a -- Yes --> b{Is mission long-horizon multi-step?}:::d
    a -- No --> c{Need millisecond-level obstacle response?}:::d

    b -- Yes --> hyb[Use Hybrid Deliberative Reactive]:::ans
    b -- No --> sch[Use Motor Schema Architecture]:::ans

    c -- Yes --> sub[Use Subsumption Architecture]:::ans
    c -- No --> sch2[Use Motor Schema Architecture]:::ans

    classDef q fill:#ffe,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef d fill:#eef,stroke:#226,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ans fill:#dfd,stroke:#262,stroke-width:1.5px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO1, Remember/Understand)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **Deliberative** and **Reactive** robot control architectures. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks, rubric-aligned):**

| Aspect | Deliberative | Reactive |
|---|---|---|
| World model | Maintains an explicit, symbolic internal map | No internal map; reacts directly to sensor data |
| Planning | Performs look-ahead search (e.g., A*, D*) | No planning; stimulus-response only |
| Latency | High (seconds to minutes) | Very low (milliseconds) |
| Robustness | Brittle if model is wrong | Highly robust to sensor noise and change |
| Example | Google's self-driving car route planner | Roomba's cliff sensor avoidance behavior |

**Awarding scheme:** 1 mark for the core distinction (model vs. no-model), 1 mark for latency/robustness contrast, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2 (CO1, Understand)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Paper Style]**

> **Q2.** What is the **Subsumption Architecture**? List its **three design principles** as proposed by Brooks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A subsumption architecture (Brooks, 1986) decomposes a robot controller into a stack of **finite-state-machine layers**, each one an *augmentation* of the layer below. Higher layers can *suppress* (silence) or *inhibit* (replace) the output of lower layers.

**Three Design Principles (Brooks):**

1. **No centralized world model** — intelligence emerges from interaction with the world.
2. **Task-level decomposition** — build incrementally, layer-by-layer from the bottom up.
3. **Robustness by design** — each layer is a complete, working robot on its own; adding layers only adds competence, never removes it.

**Awarding scheme:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark for the layer concept, 1 mark for the three principles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically worth 7 + 7. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B from the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Motor Schema Architecture — Full Problem
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style, CO2: Apply/Analyze]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Motor Schema Theory** proposed by R. C. Arkin. With a neat block diagram, describe how multiple schemas (attractor, repulsor, and stay-on-path) are combined to generate a robot's trajectory. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot at position $(1, 1)$ m must reach the goal $(6, 6)$ m while avoiding an obstacle located at $(3, 3)$ m. The obstacle influence radius is $\rho_0 = 1.5$ m. Using gains $\xi = 1.0$ (attractive) and $\eta = 0.5$ (repulsive), calculate the **total potential field force vector** experienced by the robot, and hence determine the **heading angle** in degrees. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7-mark answer**

Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987, *Georgia Tech*) is a behavior-based architecture where each behavior is mathematically modeled as a **potential field producing a velocity vector** in the robot's egocentric frame. The robot's overall motion is the **normalized vector sum** of all active schemas evaluated in parallel.

**Block diagram (reproduce for full marks):**

```
  Goal Position ----> [ Attractor Schema ] --+
                                              |
  Obstacle Pos ----> [ Repulsor Schema ] -----+--> [ Normalize ] ---> [ v, w ]
                                              |        Σ
  Path Waypts ----> [ Stay-on-Path Schema ] --+
```

**Key features (write all for full 7 marks):**

1. Each schema is *task-achieving* and produces a 2-D vector $(v_x, v_y)$ plus an orientation preference.
2. The schemas are **cooperative** — no priority is imposed; they are summed.
3. The output vector is normalized to the robot's kinematic limits ($v_{max}$, $\omega_{max}$).
4. The architecture is *situated* and *embodied* — no symbolic planner.
5. Schemas can be *instantiated* and *retired* dynamically as tasks appear and disappear.

**(b) — 7-mark numerical solution**

**Step 1 — Distances.**

Distance to goal: $\Vert \vec{q} - \vec{q}_{goal}\Vert = \sqrt{(1-6)^{2} + (1-6)^{2}} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071$ m.

Distance to obstacle: $\rho = \Vert \vec{q} - \vec{q}_{obs}\Vert = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (1-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$ m.

Since $\rho = 2.828 > \rho_0 = 1.5$ m, the obstacle is **outside** its influence radius.

$$\therefore \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 2 — Attractive force.**

$$\vec{F}_{att} = -\xi\,(\vec{q} - \vec{q}_{goal}) = -1.0 \cdot ((1-6),\;(1-6)) = (5,\;5) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total force.**

$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = (5,\;5) + (0,\;0) = (5,\;5) \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Heading angle.**

$$\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(F_y, F_x) = \mathrm{atan2}(5,\;5) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 45^{\circ}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
