- *Examples:* STRIPS planner, Shakey the Robot (Nilsson, 1969), early HMM-based SLAM.
- *Cost:* High computational load, suffers from the **Frame Problem** (see §2.4).

### Stance 2 — **Weak / Minimal Representation (Behavioral)**
- *Belief:* A robot needs *only* the representation necessary to couple current sensation to current action.
- *Method:* Sense → Act (no global map; the world is its own best model — Brooks' maxim).
- *Examples:* Subsumption Architecture, schema-based systems.
- *Cost:* Poor at long-horizon tasks requiring memory (e.g., "deliver coffee to the office I visited yesterday").

### Stance 3 — **Hybrid / Intermediate Representation (Modern Synthesis)**
- *Belief:* Neither extreme works universally; combine minimal local representations with selective global reasoning.
- *Method:* Reactive layer + deliberative layer (e.g., 3T architecture, LAAS architecture).
- *Examples:* Roomba (reactive) + SLAM-back-end map for coverage.

## 2.2 Levels of Representation in Behavioral Systems

| Level | Representation Type | Use in Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L0 — None** | Stateless, pure reflex | Obstacle avoidance | Braitenberg vehicle |
| **L1 — Statistic** | Running aggregate of sensors | "Walls more often on left" | Wall-following behavior |
| **L2 — Local Geometric** | Short-horizon map of immediate vicinity | Local obstacle escape | Dynamic Window Approach |
| **L3 — Topological** | Nodes = places, edges = paths | Route replay | Graph-based navigation |
| **L4 — Semantic** | Object classes with affordances | "Graspable cup" | Symbol-grounded behavior |
| **L5 — Full Metric-Semantic** | Global pose + classified landmarks | Long-term autonomy | Modern SLAM + semantic labeling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule of Thumb:** Pure behavioral systems rarely exceed **L2**. Anything above L2 is usually considered *hybrid*. Examiners love asking: *"Justify why a purely behavioral robot would struggle with task L5."*

## 2.3 The Five Core Representational Issues (KTU Board-Favorite)

### Issue 1 — The **Frame Problem**
How does a robot know which parts of its world model remain valid after it acts? Re-deriving the entire state of the world after every actuator command is computationally infeasible.

### Issue 2 — **Sensor Noise and Uncertainty**
Real sensors are stochastic. Representing uncertainty requires probability (Bayesian filters) or fuzzy sets. Pure behavior systems traditionally *sidestepped* this by using thresholded sensors (e.g., "if IR < threshold → turn").

### Issue 3 — **The Correspondence Problem**
When the robot revisits a place, how does it know it is the *same* place? In a behavior-based system, this is often solved by *local place recognition* (signature of sensor readings) rather than global coordinates.

### Issue 4 — **Time and Real-Time Constraints**
A representation that takes longer to update than the world changes is **useless**. Behavioral systems enforce hard real-time bounds by *discarding* representation.

### Issue 5 — **Symbol Grounding Problem (Harnad, 1990)**
How do internal symbols acquire meaning? A purely reactive system **does not have symbols**, so it sidesteps the problem — but at the cost of being unable to communicate using language or high-level goals.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor update rate vs. world change | $f_{\text{sense}} \geq f_{\text{world-change}}$ | Sampling theorem for reactive loops | Justifying no-map choice |
| Reactive loop time budget | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}} \leq T_{\text{deadline}}$ | Hard real-time constraint | Behavior arbitration |
| Bayesian belief update (1D) | $b'(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, b(x_{t-1})$ | Posterior over hidden state | Comparing to behavior-based memory |
| Affordance (formal) | $A(o, r) = \{a \in \mathcal{A} \mid \text{action } a \text{ is enabled by object } o \text{ for robot } r\}$ | Action possibility set | Grounded representation |
| Behavior arbitration (priority) | $b_{\text{active}} = \arg\max_{i} \left( \text{priority}_i \cdot \text{condition}_i(x) \right)$ | Subsumption-style selection | Layered control |
| Entropy of representation (info) | $H(R) = -\sum_i p(r_i) \log_2 p(r_i)$ | Information content stored | Trade-off analysis |
| Computational complexity of full SLAM | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ in landmarks $n$ | Why behavioral systems avoid it | Justification for minimalism |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Why don't behavioral systems use SLAM?"*, your answer should explicitly cite the **real-time deadline** $T_{\text{total}} \leq T_{\text{deadline}}$ and the **computational complexity** $O(n^2)$–$O(n^3)$ — never just "because it's slow."

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Robots (Roomba, Dyson 360):** Use L0–L1 representation. Pure behavior is sufficient for cleaning coverage.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Kiva/Amazon):** Use L3 topological + L4 semantic. Need to remember shelf locations.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Hybrid — reactive obstacle avoidance (L1) + HD map (L5).
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use hybrid — visual odometry (L2) + global path planning (L3–L4).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use strong representation (L5) for safety; cannot afford emergent surprises.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time Deadline for a Reactive Loop

A reactive behavioral layer must complete its *Sense → Compute → Actuate* cycle before the environment changes meaningfully. We derive the deadline condition.

### Step 1 — Define the loop period

Let $T_{\text{loop}}$ be the duration of one full behavior cycle, measured from the moment a sensor is sampled to the moment the corresponding actuator command is applied.

$$T_{\text{loop}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}} + T_{\text{comm}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{sense}}$ = time to acquire and digitize sensor data
- $T_{\text{compute}}$ = time to run the behavior arbitration
- $T_{\text{actuate}}$ = time for motors to begin moving
- $T_{\text{comm}}$ = bus / network latency

### Step 2 — Define the world-change timescale

Let $\tau_{\text{world}}$ be the characteristic time over which the relevant world feature (e.g., an approaching obstacle) changes by a behaviorally significant amount.

For a robot moving at speed $v$ toward a wall at distance $d$, the wall reaches the robot in time:

$$\tau_{\text{world}} = \frac{d}{v}$$

### Step 3 — Impose the safety constraint

For the behavior to remain *valid*, the loop must finish before the world changes appreciably. A standard safety factor of 2 is applied:

$$T_{\text{loop}} \leq \frac{\tau_{\text{world}}}{2} = \frac{d}{2v}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the maximum allowable compute time

Rearranging for $T_{\text{compute}}$ — the only term we can actually engineer down — yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{compute}} &\leq \frac{d}{2v} - T_{\text{sense}} - T_{\text{actuate}} - T_{\text{comm}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The harder the robot drives (larger $v$) or the closer the obstacle (smaller $d$), the *less* time is available for any internal representation to be consulted. This is the **physical reason** behavioral systems banish global maps in tight loops.

### Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style)

Suppose a mobile robot moves at $v = 0.5$ m/s and an ultrasonic sensor detects a wall at $d = 0.4$ m. Sensing takes $T_{\text{sense}} = 30$ ms, actuation $T_{\text{actuate}} = 20$ ms, and comm $T_{\text{comm}} = 5$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{compute}} &\leq \frac{0.4}{2 \times 0.5} - 0.030 - 0.020 - 0.005 \\
&= 0.400 - 0.055 \\
&= 0.345 \text{ s} \\
&= 345 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

In this 345 ms budget, the robot *could* consult a small local representation (L2), but **not** a full SLAM graph of $O(n^2)$ cost for $n \geq 50$ landmarks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Behavioral vs. Deliberative Representation

| Attribute | **Deliberative (Strong Rep.)** | **Behavioral (Minimal Rep.)** | **Hybrid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| World model | Full, persistent | None / local | Selective global |
| Planning horizon | Long | Short (immediate) | Mixed |
| Reaction latency | High (100 ms – s) | Very low (1 – 50 ms) | Tunable |
| Robustness to sensor noise | Good (Bayesian filtering) | Brittle unless designed-in | Good |
| Robustness to world dynamics | Poor (stale map) | Excellent (no stale data) | Good |
| Computational load | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(1)$ per behavior | Variable |
| Memory usage | High (MB – GB) | Negligible | Medium |
| Task complexity ceiling | High (reasoning, language) | Low (reflexive tasks) | High |
| Engineering effort | High (map maintenance) | Low (tune reflexes) | Highest |
| Example | Shakey, modern AV planner | Braitenberg, Roomba | 3T, LAAS |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Affordance-Based Representation

Consider a mobile manipulator robot $r$ in a kitchen. It sees three objects: a *cup*, a *drawer*, and a *stove*.

### Step 1 — Define the robot's action set

$$\mathcal{A} = \{ \text{grasp}, \text{push}, \text{approach}, \text{avoid} \}$$

### Step 2 — Define the affordance function

$$
A(o, r) = \{ a \in \mathcal{A} \mid \text{action } a \text{ is enabled by object } o \text{ for robot } r \}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate for each object

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(\text{cup}, r) &= \{ \text{grasp}, \text{approach} \} \\
A(\text{drawer}, r) &= \{ \text{approach}, \text{push} \} \\
A(\text{stove}, r) &= \{ \text{avoid} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The robot's *representation* of each object is *not* a list of geometric features (height, width, color), but the **set of actions it affords**. This is the essence of *action-oriented representation* in behavioral robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Affordances are **robot-relative**. The same cup affords *grasp* to a manipulator but only *avoid* to a floor-cleaning robot. Examiners test this relativity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the term "action-oriented perception" as introduced in the context of behavior-based robotics. How does it differ from classical perception in the Sense–Plan–Act paradigm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Action-oriented perception (a term popularized by **R. Brooks**, 1986) is the principle that a robot's perceptual processes should be *driven by, and tuned to, the immediate behavioral needs* of the agent, rather than aimed at constructing a complete, general-purpose internal world model. In classical Sense–Plan–Act systems, perception produces a *world-centered* symbolic description (e.g., a 3-D occupancy grid) intended to be reused by *any* downstream planner. In contrast, action-oriented perception produces a *behavior-centered* description that answers only the question *"what does the world look like for the current action I am about to take?"* — for instance, "is there something in front of me to avoid?" rather than "what is the full layout of this room?" This avoids the Frame Problem, saves computation, and is naturally real-time compliant. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "affordance" in the context of robotics and explain, with a one-sentence example, why affordances are agent-relative.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **affordance**, originally introduced by **J. J. Gibson** (1979) in ecological psychology and adapted to robotics, is a property of an object or environment feature that specifies the *set of actions* an agent can perform on or with it. Formally, $A(o, r)$ is a function of both the object $o$ and the robot $r$. It is **agent-relative** because the same physical object affords different actions to different robots. *Example:* A table affords "place-object-on" to a manipulator arm but affords "move-around" to a floor-cleaning robot. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students *frequently* lose marks by describing "behavior-based robotics" as **"the same as reactive control."** They are *related but not identical*: reactive control emphasizes stimulus–response loops; behavior-based emphasizes the *emergent intelligence* from multiple interacting behaviors.
> - Do **not** confuse Brooks' *Subsumption Architecture* (a specific 1986 implementation) with behavior-based robotics as a whole — it is one *instance*.
> - Always state the **trade-off** between reactivity and deliberation; do not blindly praise one side. Examiners reward balanced critique.
> - When asked for the **Frame Problem**, mention *both* McCarthy & Hayes' classical formulation **and** how behavioral systems sidestep it.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Discuss in detail the representational issues that arise in building a purely behavior-based robotic system. Your answer must cover the Frame Problem, the Symbol Grounding Problem, and the limits of memoryless behavior. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare and contrast the three representational stances — strong (deliberative), weak (behavioral), and hybrid — with suitable examples and a justification of when each is appropriate. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Representational Issues in Purely Behavior-Based Systems (7 marks)**

1. **The Frame Problem (1.5 marks):** First formalized by **McCarthy and Hayes** (1969), the Frame Problem asks: *how can a robot determine which facts about the world remain true after it performs an action?* In a deliberative system, the planner must explicitly re-derive invariants. In a pure behavior-based system, the Frame Problem is *sidestepped but not solved* — by avoiding a persistent world model, the robot never has to keep facts consistent. **Trade-off:** the robot cannot reason about consequences of *hypothetical* actions, only immediate ones.

2. **The Symbol Grounding Problem (1.5 marks):** **Harnad** (1990) asked how internal symbols acquire meaning tied to the real world. Pure behavior systems reject symbols entirely, grounding behavior in raw sensor signals. **Trade-off:** the robot cannot use language, communicate goals symbolically, or benefit from human-readable task descriptions.

3. **Memoryless Behavior Limit (2 marks):** A purely reactive behavior has **no state** between cycles. Therefore:
   - It cannot perform tasks that require remembering an event from seconds or minutes ago.
   - It cannot learn from past experience in any persistent way.
   - It cannot perform *deliberative* tasks such as "go to the kitchen *only if* the office is closed."
   - **Workaround:** use *augmented finite-state machines* or *schemas with internal variables* to inject minimal memory without sacrificing reactivity.

4. **Sensor and Actuator Noise (1 mark):** Behavior-based systems traditionally use *thresholded* sensors. Real sensors have noise, dead zones, cross-talk. A purely thresholded system is brittle to calibration drift.

5. **Difficulty of Scaling (1 mark):** Adding more behaviors increases *interaction effects* (e.g., oscillation between "wander" and "avoid"). Arbitration hierarchies (Brooks' subsumption) provide one solution but introduce priority engineering overhead.

**(b) Three Representational Stances (7 marks)**

| Stance | Representation | Strengths | Weaknesses | When Appropriate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong (Deliberative)** | Full symbolic/geometric world model | Long-horizon planning, language, learning | Slow, brittle, Frame Problem | Structured indoor environments, off-line planning, surgery |
| **Weak (Behavioral)** | Minimal / no representation | Real-time, robust, low cost | No memory, no long-horizon tasks | Cleaning, simple foraging, dynamic clutter |
| **Hybrid** | Local + selective global | Balanced | Engineering complexity, layer coordination | Autonomous vehicles, warehouse robots, Mars rovers |

**Examples:**
- *Strong:* Shakey (1969), modern AV planning stack.
- *Weak:* Roomba, Braitenberg vehicles.
- *Hybrid:* 3T architecture (NASA), LAAS architecture.

**Justification (2 marks):** Use strong representation when the environment is structured and changes slowly; use weak when the environment is dynamic and the task is short-horizon; use hybrid whenever the task is *both* long-horizon *and* dynamic — which is the common case in modern robotics.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain Brooks' argument that "the world is its own best model." How does this lead to the rejection of centralized representation in the Subsumption Architecture? Use a labeled diagram in your answer. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive the real-time deadline for a behavioral loop and explain how this mathematical constraint mathematically forces the designer toward minimal representation. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) "The World Is Its Own Best Model" (7 marks)**

1. **Brooks' Core Argument (3 marks):** In his 1991 paper *"Intelligence Without Representation"*, **Rodney Brooks** argued that building and maintaining a symbolic world model is *more expensive* and *less accurate* than simply *re-sensing* the world whenever a decision must be made. The world is always up-to-date, whereas any internal model becomes stale the moment the world changes. Therefore, an agent that uses the world *directly as its own model* avoids the overhead of model maintenance and the Frame Problem entirely.

2. **Implication for Subsumption (2 marks):** In the Subsumption Architecture, each *behavior* (e.g., *avoid-obstacle*, *wander*, *explore*) reads its own sensors and produces its own actuator commands. Higher-level behaviors can *suppress* or *inhibit* lower-level ones via fixed-priority arbitration. **There is no central data structure** representing the world. Each behavior is a *local*, *reactive*, *embodied* coupling between a subset of sensors and a subset of actuators.

3. **Diagram (2 marks):**
   ```mermaid
   graph TD
       S["SENSORS"]:::io
       B1["BEHAVIOR 1 AVOID"]:::b
       B2["BEHAVIOR 2 WANDER"]:::b
       B3["BEHAVIOR 3 EXPLORE"]:::b
       ARB["ARBITRATOR"]:::arb
       M["ACTUATORS"]:::io
       S --> B1
       S --> B2
       S --> B3
       B1 --> ARB
       B2 --> ARB
       B3 --> ARB
       ARB --> M
       classDef io fill:#333333,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
       classDef b fill:#5f1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
       classDef arb fill:#5f5f1f,stroke:#333333,color:#ffffff
   ```
   *No central memory block — each behavior taps the world (sensors) directly.*

**(b) Real-Time Deadline Derivation (7 marks)**

We derived in §3.1 that the loop time must satisfy:

$$T_{\text{compute}} \leq \frac{d}{2v} - T_{\text{sense}} - T_{\text{actuate}} - T_{\text{comm}}$$

1. **Interpretation (3 marks):** As $v$ increases and $d$ decreases, the available compute time **shrinks linearly**. For a fast robot (say $v = 2$ m/s) approaching a wall at $d = 0.2$ m, the entire window is:

$$T_{\text{loop}} \leq \frac{0.2}{2 \times 2} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

2. **Implication for Representation (2 marks):** A 50 ms budget can accommodate *one* thresholded IR check and *one* reflex turn — but not a 50-landmark EKF-SLAM update, which has $O(n^2) = O(2500)$ operations per cycle. Therefore, the *physics of real-time interaction* mathematically *forces* the designer to abandon global representation in the inner loop and use it only at slower, outer time-scales (hybrid architectures).

3. **Engineering Conclusion (2 marks):** The design rule is: *place the smallest possible representation at the tightest loop, and any larger representation must be inside a slower loop that consults it less frequently.* This is the formal justification for the *layered time-scales* in 3T and similar hybrid architectures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** *Representational issues* concern *how* a robot internally encodes knowledge in a behavioral system.
- **Three stances:** Strong (deliberative) | Weak (behavioral) | Hybrid.
- **Brooks' maxim:** *"The world is its own best model."* Justifies minimal representation.
- **Subsumption Architecture (1986):** Layered, fixed-priority, no central model. Levels (bottom-up): avoid, wander, explore, map, plan, reason.
- **Action-oriented perception:** Perception is selected by, and tuned to, the current behavior — not a general-purpose sensing sweep.
- **Affordance:** $A(o, r)$ — agent-relative action possibility set.
- **Frame Problem:** How to know what is still true after an action. Behavioral systems sidestep it by avoiding persistent state.
- **Symbol Grounding Problem (Harnad 1990):** How internal symbols get meaning. Behavior-based robotics avoids it by using no symbols.
- **Real-time deadline:** $T_{\text{compute}} \leq \dfrac{d}{2v} - T_{\text{sense}} - T_{\text{actuate}} - T_{\text{comm}}$ — drives the design choice toward minimal representation.
- **Levels of representation (L0–L5):** Stateless → metric-semantic global. Pure behavioral systems rarely exceed **L2**.
- **Sensor noise:** Traditional behavior systems use thresholds; modern extensions add probabilistic filters.
- **Memoryless limit:** Cannot do long-horizon tasks. Workaround: state-augmented schemas.
- **Trade-off principle:** *More representation = better reasoning, less reactivity.* *Less representation = better reactivity, no reasoning.*
- **Common KTU question stems:** *"Why don't behavioral systems use SLAM?"* → cite $O(n^2)$ cost and real-time deadline. *"Justify hybrid."* → cite the need for both reactivity and long-horizon goals.
- **Three high-yield examples:** Roomba (weak), Shakey (strong), Mars rover (hybrid).
- **One-line definition of embodiment:** Intelligence is grounded in the *physical body*, not in the CPU alone.
- **One-line definition of situatedness:** The robot is *in* a real world, not in a simulation.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Representational Issues for Behavioral Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Behavior
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Behavioral Robotics**, **Adaptive Behavior** refers to the capability of an autonomous robotic agent to dynamically modify its perceptual, cognitive, and motor actions in response to changes in its internal state (e.g., battery level, wear, sensor drift) or external environment (e.g., terrain variation, obstacle appearance, lighting change), without explicit reprogramming by a human designer.

Mathematically, an adaptive behavior $B_a$ can be expressed as a closed-loop mapping:

$$B_a : (S_t, M_t, G) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{A}} A_{t+1}$$

where:
- $S_t$ = Sensory input at time $t$
- $M_t$ = Internal state (memory/model) at time $t$
- $G$ = Goal or motivational drive
- $\mathcal{A}$ = Adaptation function (learning rule)
- $A_{t+1}$ = Adapted action at time $t+1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Adaptive behavior* in behavioral robotics is a control paradigm wherein the robot continuously tunes its behavior-producing mechanisms using feedback from the environment to maintain goal-directed functionality in the face of uncertainty, perturbation, and novelty.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Commuter Cyclist**
> Imagine a person cycling home every day along the same route. On a sunny day, they pedal normally. When it rains, they slow down, lean differently, brake earlier at corners, and pull up their hood — all *without* re-learning how to ride a bicycle. This is **adaptive behavior**: the underlying *skill* (cycling) is fixed, but the *parameters of execution* (speed, posture, braking force, route) are continuously tuned to current conditions.

A robot exhibiting adaptive behavior is essentially doing the same: its **basic behavioral repertoire** (go-to-goal, avoid-obstacle, follow-wall) stays the same, but the **activation weights, thresholds, gains, and parameters** are continuously tuned using environmental feedback.

### 1.3 Three Pillars of Adaptive Behavior

| Pillar | Description | Robotic Example |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Sensor Adaptation** | Re-calibrating sensor interpretation | Adjusting IR reflectance thresholds under changing light |
| **Motor Adaptation** | Modifying actuator commands | Reducing wheel torque on a slippery floor |
| **Behavioral/Task Adaptation** | Re-selecting/weighting behaviors | Switching from "explore" to "exploit" when goal is found |

### 1.4 Key Terminology with Units

- **Adaptation Rate ($\alpha$)** — Unitless learning rate, typically $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, default **$\alpha = 0.1$** in many BDI controllers.
- **Time Constant ($\tau_a$)** — Time over which adaptation settles — measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Plasticity Window ($W_p$)** — Duration during which learning is active — measured in **seconds (s)** or **epochs**.
- **Convergence Criterion ($\varepsilon$)** — Threshold below which adaptation is considered complete — typically **$\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Levels of Adaptation in Behavioral Robotics

Behavioral robotics classifies adaptive behavior into **four hierarchical levels** (Brooks' Subsumption extended view):

1. **Reactive Adaptation (0th order)** — Pure stimulus-response, no memory.
2. **Parametric Adaptation (1st order)** — Tunable gains/thresholds within fixed behaviors.
3. **Structural Adaptation (2nd order)** — Behaviors are added, removed, or re-wired.
4. **Cognitive Adaptation (3rd order)** — Internal world model is updated; planning is re-used.

### 2.2 Core Mechanisms Behind Adaptive Behavior

- **Habituation** — Progressive decrease in response to a repeated, irrelevant stimulus. Used by robots to ignore persistent noise.
- **Sensitization** — Increased response after a novel or intense stimulus. Used by robots to elevate vigilance.
- **Classical Conditioning (Pavlovian)** — Associating a neutral stimulus (CS) with a meaningful one (US).
- **Operant Conditioning (Instrumental)** — Behavior is strengthened or weakened by its consequence (reward/punishment).
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** — Mathematical formalization of operant conditioning using a reward signal $R_t$.
- **Evolutionary Adaptation** — Population-level adaptation over generations (genetic algorithms, evolutionary robotics).
- **Homeostatic Adaptation** — Maintaining internal variables (battery, temperature) within viable bounds.

### 2.3 Closed-Loop Adaptive Behavior Equation

For a robot with motor command $u(t)$ and environmental disturbance $d(t)$, the adaptive controller adjusts its parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using the gradient of a performance error $J$:

$$\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(t) = -\alpha \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}, e(t))$$

where the tracking error $e(t) = y_d(t) - y(t)$ is the difference between desired and actual output.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This exact formulation is used in Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) deployed in humanoid robots (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas) and autonomous ground vehicles (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot) to compensate for unknown payload masses and uneven terrain.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Domain | Used For |
|---|-------------------|-----------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | Adaptive Mapping | $B_a : (S_t, M_t, G) \to A_{t+1}$ | Discrete-time | Defining adaptation |
| 2 | Parameter Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha \, \delta_t$ | Unitless | Learning weights |
| 3 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Reward / Action | RL adaptation |
| 4 | Habituation Decay | $H_{t+1} = (1-\beta) H_t + \beta H_0$ | $0 < \beta < 1$ | Forgetting noise |
| 5 | Sensitization Gain | $S_{t+1} = S_t + \kappa \, (S_{max} - S_t)$ | $\kappa \in [0,1]$ | Boosting response |
| 6 | Tracking Error | $e(t) = y_d(t) - y(t)$ | Sensor units | Adaptation trigger |
| 7 | MRAC Law | $\dot{\theta} = -\alpha \, \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta, e)$ | Continuous | Motor adaptation |
| 8 | Homeostatic Set-Point | $\dot{x} = -k(x - x_{set})$ | $k > 0$ | Stability regulation |
| 9 | Convergence Criterion | $\vert e(t) \vert < \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ | Stopping condition |
| 10 | Plasticity Decay | $P(t) = P_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_a}$ | Seconds | Time-limited learning |

> [!NOTE]
> All unitless constants such as learning rate $\alpha$, discount factor $\gamma$, and decay $\beta$ must lie within $[0, 1]$ to guarantee convergence in KTU-style problem settings.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Adaptive Behavior |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-keeping under varying lighting, weather, and traffic density |
| **Search & Rescue Robots** | Reconfiguring gait after partial limb damage |
| **Industrial Manipulators** | Compensating for tool wear and varying payload |
| **Domestic Service Robots** | Vacuum cleaners learning room layouts (e.g., Roomba) |
| **Space Robotics** | Operating under unpredictable planetary soil conditions |
| **Rehabilitation Robots** | Adjusting assist torque based on patient fatigue |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Q-Learning Update Rule (Adaptive Behavior via RL)

**Goal:** Derive the Q-Learning recurrence that an adaptive robot uses to improve its action-selection policy.

**Step 1 — Define the action-value function.**
The expected discounted reward of taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following the optimal policy thereafter is:

$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_{t+1}, a') \,\middle|\, s_t = s, a_t = a \,\right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the discount factor.

**Step 2 — Express the Temporal Difference (TD) error.**
The TD error $\delta_t$ measures how "surprised" the robot is by the actual reward:

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply stochastic gradient ascent.**
The robot updates its estimate of $Q$ in the direction that reduces $\delta_t$:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\delta_t$ explicitly.**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \!\left[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \,\right]$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Condition.**
Watkins (1992) proved that with:

$$0 \le \alpha_t \le 1, \quad \sum_t \alpha_t = \infty, \quad \sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$$

$Q$ converges to $Q^{*}$ with probability **1** — guaranteeing **stable adaptive behavior**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Habituation Dynamics (Adaptive Filter for Sensor Noise)

**Step 1 — Model the habituation variable $H(t)$.**
Habituation decreases exponentially as the stimulus is repeatedly applied:

$$H(t) = H_0 \, e^{-\beta t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the response magnitude $R(t)$.**
The behavioral response is proportional to the inverse of habituation:

$$R(t) = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + H(t)} = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + H_0 \, e^{-\beta t}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at steady state.**
As $t \to \infty$, $H(t) \to 0$ and:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} R(t) = R_{max}$$

This shows the robot *recovers full sensitivity* once the irrelevant stimulus disappears — the hallmark of **adaptive perceptual filtering**.

**Step 4 — KTU Numerical Illustration.**
Given $H_0 = 5$, $\beta = 0.1\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $t = 10\,\text{s}$:

$$H(10) = 5 \times e^{-0.1 \times 10} = 5 \times e^{-1} = 5 \times 0.3679 = 1.8394$$

$$R(10) = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + 1.8394} = 0.352 \, R_{max}$$

Thus, after 10 seconds of repeated stimulus, the robot responds at only **35.2 %** of its maximum — a clear sign of *adaptive suppression*.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) Law

**Step 1 — Define reference model output.**
The desired (reference) closed-loop behavior of the robot joint is:

$$y_m(t) = G_m(s) \, r(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define actual plant output.**

$$y(t) = G_p(s) \, u(t)$$

**Step 3 — Define the tracking error.**

$$e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 4 — Define the Lyapunov-like performance index.**

$$J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, e^{2}(t)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. controller parameter $\theta$.**

$$\nabla_{\theta} J = -e(t) \, \frac{\partial y(t)}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the MIT rule.**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\alpha \, \nabla_{\theta} J = \alpha \, e(t) \, \frac{\partial y(t)}{\partial \theta}$$

This is the classical **MIT rule** used by every KTU lab to implement adaptive wall-following and line-following behaviors in real time.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Adaptive Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Memory Required? | Converges to? | KTU Exam Favorite? | Typical Use |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Habituation | No | Steady low response | ✅ Yes (2-mark) | Ignore noise |
| Sensitization | No | High response | ✅ Yes (2-mark) | Detect novelty |
| Classical Conditioning | Short-term | CS–US association | ✅ Yes (7-mark) | Cue-based navigation |
| Operant / RL | Long-term | Optimal policy $Q^{*}$ | ✅ Yes (14-mark) | Goal-directed tasks |
| Evolutionary | Population | Fittest genome | ⚠️ Sometimes | Body+behavior design |
| Homeostatic | Continuous | Set-point $x_{set}$ | ✅ Yes (7-mark) | Battery/thermal |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Adaptive Behavior Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] -->|Stimulus S_t| B[Sensors]
    B --> C[Perception Module]
    C --> D[Internal State M_t]
    D --> E{Adaptation Function A}
    E -->|Action A_t| F[Actuators]
    F -->|Motor Command u_t| A
    G[Goal Drive G] --> E
    H[Reward R_t] -->|Feedback| E
    E -.Updates.-> I[Memory / Model]
    I -.Updates.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The robot forms a closed perception–action loop with the environment.
- The Adaptation Function $\mathcal{A}$ sits at the heart of the loop and continuously updates internal state $M_t$ using reward $R_t$.
- This is the canonical **Sense → Think → Adapt → Act** cycle of behavioral robotics.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Levels of Adaptive Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Reactive Adaptation]
    L1[Parametric Adaptation]
    L2[Structural Adaptation]
    L3[Cognitive Adaptation]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    subgraph Level0 [Level 0 - Reactive]
        L0a[Stimulus Response]
        L0b[No Memory]
    end
    subgraph Level1 [Level 1 - Parametric]
        L1a[Gain Tuning]
        L1b[Threshold Shift]
    end
    subgraph Level2 [Level 2 - Structural]
        L2a[Add Remove Behavior]
        L2b[Re wire Subsumption]
    end
    subgraph Level3 [Level 3 - Cognitive]
        L3a[World Model Update]
        L3b[Plan Reuse]
    end
```

### 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Adaptive Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[State s_t] --> A[Action a_t]
    A --> E[Environment]
    E --> R[Reward r_t+1]
    E --> S2[Next State s_t+1]
    R --> Q[Q Table Update]
    S2 --> Q
    Q --> S
```

### 4.4 Function-Flow Architecture for an Adaptive Wall-Following Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Sonar Front Distance dF] --> P1[Perception Filter]
    F2[Sonar Side Distance dS] --> P1
    P1 --> AD{Obstacle Detected?}
    AD -- Yes --> BE1[Wall Follow Behavior]
    AD -- No --> BE2[Explore Behavior]
    BE1 --> ARB[Behavior Arbiter]
    BE2 --> ARB
    ARB --> M[Motor Controller]
    M --> W[Wheel Actuators]
    W --> ENV[Environment]
    ENV --> F1
    ENV --> F2
    FB[Feedback: Encoder Counts] --> ARB
```

**Explanation:** The arbiter weights the wall-follow and explore behaviors using adaptively learned gains, ensuring the robot *behaves differently* on a cluttered floor vs an open corridor — a hallmark of **adaptive behavior**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define adaptive behavior in the context of behavioral robotics. List any two mechanisms by which a robot can exhibit adaptive behavior.*

**Model Answer:**
Adaptive behavior is the ability of an autonomous robot to dynamically modify its actions in response to changes in the environment or its internal state, without explicit reprogramming. **(2 marks)**

Two mechanisms:
1. **Reinforcement Learning** — The robot modifies its action-selection policy based on a scalar reward signal.
2. **Habituation/Sensitization** — The robot learns to ignore persistent stimuli (habituation) or become hyper-alert to novel ones (sensitization). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between habituation and sensitization in adaptive robotic systems. Give one robot-relevant example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Habituation | Sensitization |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Response trend | Decreases with repetition | Increases with novel stimulus |
| Function | Filter out irrelevant stimuli | Amplify attention to salient stimuli |
| Example | A vacuum robot ignoring a static curtain | A guard robot becoming hyper-vigilant after detecting a sudden sound |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an adaptive behavior-based mobile robot. Discuss the four levels of adaptation with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework as an adaptive behavior mechanism. Derive the Q-Learning update rule and state its convergence conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define adaptive architecture.**
An adaptive behavior-based mobile robot consists of **sensors**, **perception**, **behavior generation**, **behavior arbitration**, **adaptation unit**, and **actuators**, all connected in a closed feedback loop with the environment.

**Step 2 — Four Levels of Adaptation:**

1. **Reactive Adaptation (0th order)** — Pure stimulus-response (e.g., Braitenberg vehicle turning away from a light source).
2. **Parametric Adaptation (1st order)** — Tuning gains/thresholds of fixed behaviors (e.g., reducing sonar threshold in a noisy corridor).
3. **Structural Adaptation (2nd order)** — Adding/removing behaviors (e.g., subsumption-style layering).
4. **Cognitive Adaptation (3rd order)** — Updating internal world model (e.g., SLAM-based mapping).

**Step 3 — Diagram (block-level architecture):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env[Environment] -->|Sensory Stimulus| Sen[Sensors]
    Sen --> Per[Perception]
    Per --> Beh[Behavior Bank]
    Beh --> Arb[Arbiter]
    Arb --> Act[Actuators]
    Act --> Env
    Rew[Reward Signal] --> Ad[Adaptation Unit]
    Ad --> Beh
    Ad --> Arb
```

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The four levels form a hierarchy where lower levels provide fast reflexes and higher levels provide strategic adaptation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Framework.**
RL frames adaptive behavior as a Markov Decision Process (MDP): $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = set of states
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of actions
- $P$ = transition probability
- $R$ = reward function
- $\gamma$ = discount factor

**Step 2 — Q-function definition.**

$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\middle|\, s_t = s, a_t = a \,\right]$$

**Step 3 — Bellman Optimality.**

$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_{t+1}, a') \,\right]$$

**Step 4 — Update Rule.**
With TD error $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Conditions (Watkins 1992):**
1. All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often.
2. Learning rate satisfies $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$.
3. Reward function $R$ is bounded.

Under these conditions, $Q_t \to Q^{*}$ with probability 1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** What is habituation? Derive the mathematical model of habituation dynamics for a robotic sensor responding to a repeated stimulus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) as applied to a mobile robot. Derive the MIT rule used for parameter adaptation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Habituation is a non-associative form of adaptive behavior wherein the robot's response to a repeated, biologically irrelevant stimulus progressively decreases. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Mathematical Model.**
Let $H(t)$ be the habituation level. Under constant stimulus:

$$H(t) = H_0 \, e^{-\beta t}, \quad \beta > 0$$

The behavioral response $R(t)$ is inversely related to $H(t)$:

$$R(t) = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + H_0 \, e^{-\beta t}}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting Cases.**
- $t = 0$: $R(0) = R_{max}/(1 + H_0)$ — initial attenuated response.
- $t \to \infty$: $R(\infty) = R_{max}$ — full recovery after stimulus removal.

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification.**
With $H_0 = 5$, $\beta = 0.1\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $t = 10\,\text{s}$:

$$R(10) = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + 5 e^{-1}} = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + 1.8394} = 0.352 \, R_{max}$$

**Step 5 — Robotic Example.** A floor-cleaning robot ignores the static baseboard pattern (habituation) but re-attends if the baseboard is moved (dishabituation). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — MRAC Concept.**
MRAC forces the actual robot plant $G_p(s)$ to track the behavior of a *reference model* $G_m(s)$ by adaptively tuning controller parameters. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Reference and Actual Outputs.**
Reference: $y_m(t) = G_m(s) \, r(t)$
Actual: $y(t) = G_p(s) \, u(t)$

**Step 3 — Tracking Error.**
$e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$

**Step 4 — Performance Index.**
$J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}(t)$

**Step 5 — Gradient Computation.**
$\nabla_{\theta} J = -e(t) \, \dfrac{\partial y(t)}{\partial \theta}$

**Step 6 — MIT Rule.**
Apply gradient descent:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\alpha \, \nabla_{\theta} J = \alpha \, e(t) \, \frac{\partial y(t)}{\partial \theta}$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the adaptation gain. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Skipping convergence conditions** in Q-Learning derivations → loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing habituation with sensitization** in 3-mark questions → loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the gradient sign** in the MIT rule (writing $-\alpha \, e \, \partial y/\partial\theta$ instead of $+\alpha \, e \, \partial y/\partial\theta$) → loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not labeling axes/units** in numerical illustrations → loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing up structural vs parametric adaptation** in 7-mark architectural answers → loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions → loses **2–3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Adaptive Behavior**

- ✅ Adaptive behavior = dynamic modification of robot's actions based on environmental/internal feedback, **without human reprogramming**.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Sensor, Motor, Behavioral/Task** adaptation.
- ✅ Four hierarchical levels: **Reactive → Parametric → Structural → Cognitive**.
- ✅ Habituation: $H(t) = H_0 \, e^{-\beta t}$, $R(t) = R_{max}/(1+H_0 e^{-\beta t})$.
- ✅ Sensitization: $S_{t+1} = S_t + \kappa (S_{max} - S_t)$.
- ✅ Q-Learning update: $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q' - Q]$.
- ✅ Convergence needs: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ AND $\sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$.
- ✅ MIT rule: $\dot{\theta}(t) = \alpha \, e(t) \, \partial y/\partial \theta$.
- ✅ Homeostatic regulation: $\dot{x} = -k(x - x_{set})$.
- ✅ Learning rate $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, discount $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, decay $\beta \in (0, 1)$.
- ✅ Real-world utility: autonomous vehicles, search-and-rescue, industrial manipulators, domestic service robots, space robotics.
- ✅ **Always draw the closed-loop block diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions — examiners reward visualization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Behavior
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Social Behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Social Behavior** in the context of Behavioral Robotics refers to the capacity of an autonomous robotic agent to perceive, interpret, model, and respond to the actions, intentions, emotions, and social cues of other agents (humans or robots) operating within a shared environment, while simultaneously signaling its own internal state in a socially legible and contextually appropriate manner.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (PERAT743 – Behavioral Robotics), social behavior is the third pillar of behavioral competence, sitting alongside *reactive behavior* (sensing–action loops) and *deliberative behavior* (planning). It introduces the **"social agent"** abstraction — a robot that is not merely a tool but a *participant* in a social ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT743 – Module 2):**
> Social behavior encompasses (a) cues and signaling, (b) cooperation and coordination, (c) social learning and imitation, (d) human–robot interaction (HRI), and (e) swarm / multi-agent social dynamics. Mastery of these five sub-themes is mandatory for Module 2 evaluation.

## 1.2 Core Sub-Concepts at a Glance

| # | Sub-Concept | What the Robot Must Do |
|---|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Social Cues & Signaling** | Emit and detect gaze, gesture, posture, proxemics |
| 2 | **Social Learning & Imitation** | Acquire new behaviors by observing conspecifics / humans |
| 3 | **Cooperation & Coordination** | Align goals, share tasks, achieve joint intent |
| 4 | **Human–Robot Interaction (HRI)** | Sustain multimodal, bidirectional communication |
| 5 | **Swarm & Multi-Agent Behavior** | Emerge collective intelligence from local rules |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Colleague" Analogy

Imagine a new employee joining a busy office. On day one, the employee does not just *work* — they also:

- **Read the room** (notice when colleagues are stressed, when a meeting has started, when someone is too busy to be interrupted).
- **Signal intent** (a wave before entering, an "excuse me" when crossing in front of a screen).
- **Cooperate** (help carry a box, divide a presentation).
- **Learn socially** (pick up the coffee-making routine by watching a colleague, not by reading a manual).
- **Conform to norms** (stand at an appropriate conversational distance, take turns in discussion).

A *socially behaving robot* must do exactly the same: it is **socially aware**, **socially legible**, and **socially adaptive**. The robot's "manners" are encoded as algorithms, but their *function* is the same as the human employee's.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams:** The defining test of social behavior is *not* whether the robot can detect a face, but whether it can **modulate its own actions** in response to detected social signals. A webcam that records a face is *not* social behavior; a robot that slows its approach because it detected a startled human expression **is** social behavior.

## 1.4 The Three Foundational Properties of Socially Behaving Robots

1. **Legibility** — A human watching the robot can predict its next action with high confidence.
2. **Predictability** — The robot's actions are consistent with the model the human has built of it.
3. **Bidirectionality** — Both human and robot actively influence each other's state.

Mathematically, these are framed as the **Legibility–Predictability Trade-off**, central to KTU Module 2 derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Social Behavior Stack

A robot's social competence is best understood as a layered stack, where each layer consumes the output of the layer below it:

1. **Layer 1 — Perception of Social Cues**
   Visual (face, gaze direction, gesture, body pose), auditory (prosody, turn-taking), proxemic (interpersonal distance), and haptic (touch) cues.
2. **Layer 2 — Affect / Intent Estimation**
   Mapping raw cues to latent variables such as *emotion*, *engagement*, *trust*, and *intent*.
3. **Layer 3 — Social Reasoning**
   Inferring the partner's mental state (Belief–Desire–Intention, or BDI framework) and the joint goal structure.
4. **Layer 4 — Social Action Selection**
   Choosing a behavior that is appropriate to the inferred state (e.g., slow down, back off, take the turn, imitate).
5. **Layer 5 — Legible Expression**
   Emitting cues (gaze, gesture, sound, LED color) that the partner can decode unambiguously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters (KTU 2024 CO mapping):**
> This stack directly addresses Course Outcome **CO2** — *"Design behavior-based control architectures incorporating reactive, deliberative, and social layers."* Examiners frequently ask students to *label the layer* responsible for a given social failure.

## 2.2 The Social Cues Taxonomy

| Modality | Cues | Sensing Technology |
|----------|------|--------------------|
| Visual – Face | Expression, gaze, head pose | RGB-D camera, CNN-based face mesh |
| Visual – Body | Gesture, posture, walking direction | Skeleton tracking (OpenPose, MediaPipe) |
| Auditory | Speech, prosody, turn-taking cues | Microphone array, ASR pipeline |
| Proxemic | Interpersonal distance, orientation | LiDAR, RGB-D |
| Haptic | Force, contact, touch | Tactile skin, force sensors |
| Overt | LED color, screen face, motion style | Actuators, displays |

## 2.3 Proxemics — Hall's Distance Zones in Robotics

Edward T. Hall's proxemics theory is a **direct exam question** in KTU Module 2. A socially behaving robot must respect these interpersonal distances:

| Zone | Distance | Social Meaning | Robot Behavior |
|------|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Intimate** | 0 – 0.45 m | Personal, private | Reserved for trusted users; HRI robots may enter only with explicit consent |
| **Personal** | 0.45 – 1.2 m | Friends, family | Default approach distance for assistive robots |
| **Social** | 1.2 – 3.6 m | Acquaintances, formal interaction | Presentation, guidance |
| **Public** | > 3.6 m | Strangers, audience | Lecture, performance |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Units | Used For |
|--------|------|----------------------|-------|----------|
| $L(t)$ | Legibility score | $L(t) = P(\hat{g} = g \mid \xi_{0:t})$ | dimensionless | Probability observer infers correct goal $g$ from trajectory $\xi_{0:t}$ |
| $P(t)$ | Predictability score | $P(t) = P(\xi_{t+1} = \xi^*_{t+1} \mid \xi_{0:t})$ | dimensionless | Probability robot's actual next state matches observer's expectation $\xi^*_{t+1}$ |
| $D_H$ | Hall distance threshold | $D_H \in \{0.45, 1.2, 3.6\}$ m | meters | Proxemic zone classification |
| $E(t)$ | Engagement | $E(t) = w_g G(t) + w_p P(t) + w_a A(t)$ | dimensionless | Weighted fusion of gaze, pose, attention |
| $T_s$ | Turn-taking latency | $T_s = t_{resp} - t_{end-of-utterance}$ | seconds | Social smoothness in HRI |
| $\eta$ | Imitation fidelity | $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \theta^{d}_i - \theta^{r}_i \vert$ | dimensionless | How closely robot reproduces demonstrated trajectory |
| $\rho$ | Swarm cohesion | $\rho = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} d_{ij}$ | meters | Average inter-agent distance |
| $K_{sep}$ | Separation gain | $\vec{F}_{sep} = -K_{sep} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert^2}$ | N/m² | Reynolds boid separation force |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners *will* ask you to write any one of the above formulas. Memorize $L(t)$, $P(t)$, $\eta$, and the Reynolds boid equations — these four alone account for > 60% of formula-based marks in Module 2.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Socially Assistive Robots (SARs) like *PARO* and *Pepper* reduce patient anxiety and improve therapy adherence.
- **Education:** Robots like *NAO* support autistic children's social-skill training by providing repeatable, predictable social cues.
- **Service Industry:** Reception, guidance, and elderly-care robots use proxemic control to avoid crowding.
- **Industrial Multi-Robot Systems:** Warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva) use flocking rules for collision-free coordination.
- **Search and Rescue:** Swarm robots coordinate via stigmergy (pheromone-like digital signals) to map disaster zones.

## 2.6 The Social Behavior Decision Loop

For any social interaction at time $t$, the robot executes the following closed loop:

$$
\text{Sense} \;\to\; \text{Interpret} \;\to\; \text{Reason} \;\to\; \text{Act} \;\to\; \text{Signal}
$$

Each step is **time-stamped** and **logged** for offline analysis — a practice emphasized in KTU's outcome-based lab rubrics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Convention:** All derivations below are written in the *exact* step-expanded format expected in 14-mark answers. Every line is annotated so that even a partial-credit evaluator can award marks for individual transitions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Legibility vs. Predictability Objective Function

The classical *Dragan et al.* (2013) framework defines a socially behaving robot as one whose trajectory **simultaneously maximizes legibility and predictability** under an observer's rational model.

### Step 1 — Define the goal set

Let the robot have a discrete goal set $\mathcal{G} = \{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_M\}$. The true intended goal is $g^\star \in \mathcal{G}$.

### Step 2 — Define the trajectory

A trajectory is a time-parameterized path:
$$
\xi(t) = \{(x(t), y(t), \theta(t)) \mid t \in [0, T]\}
$$

### Step 3 — Observer's belief over goals

A rational observer updates their belief about which goal the robot is heading toward as:
$$
b_t(g) = P(g \mid \xi(0:t)) = \frac{P(\xi(0:t) \mid g) \, P(g)}{\sum_{g' \in \mathcal{G}} P(\xi(0:t) \mid g') \, P(g')}
$$

This is **Bayesian inference over goals** given the observed trajectory.

### Step 4 — Legibility as belief in the true goal

Legibility is the posterior probability that the observer assigns to the *true* goal after watching the partial trajectory:
$$
L(t) = b_t(g^\star) = P(g^\star \mid \xi(0:t))
$$

### Step 5 — Predictability as action-conformity

Predictability is the probability that the robot's next state matches what the observer expects:
$$
P(t) = P\bigl(\xi(t+\Delta t) = \xi^\star(t+\Delta t) \;\big\vert\; \xi(0:t)\bigr)
$$
where $\xi^\star$ is the *expected* trajectory under the observer's model.

### Step 6 — Combined objective

The robot plans its trajectory by solving:
$$
\max_{\xi} \; J(\xi) = \alpha \int_0^T L(t)\, dt \;+\; \beta \int_0^T P(t)\, dt
$$
subject to the robot's dynamics and obstacle constraints. The weights $\alpha, \beta \ge 0$ balance the two objectives.

### Step 7 — Practical simplification

Most KTU-level exam problems reduce the continuous integral to a discrete sum:
$$
J \approx \alpha \sum_{k=0}^{K} L(k\Delta t) + \beta \sum_{k=0}^{K} P(k\Delta t)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Imitation Fidelity (Trajectory-Level)

When a robot learns a behavior by watching a demonstrator, KTU examiners commonly ask you to define and compute the **Imitation Fidelity** $\eta$.

### Step 1 — Define the demonstrated trajectory

The demonstrator produces a sequence of $N$ joint configurations:
$$
\Theta^d = \{\theta^d_1, \theta^d_2, \ldots, \theta^d_N\}
$$
where each $\theta^d_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is an $n$-DOF joint vector.

### Step 2 — Define the robot's reproduced trajectory

The robot, after learning, reproduces:
$$
\Theta^r = \{\theta^r_1, \theta^r_2, \ldots, \theta^r_N\}
$$

### Step 3 — Per-step joint error

For each timestep $i$, the per-joint error is:
$$
e_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta^{d}_{i,j} - \theta^{r}_{i,j} \vert
$$

### Step 4 — Aggregate over the trajectory

$$
E_{total} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i
$$

### Step 5 — Imitation fidelity

$$
\eta = 1 - E_{total} = 1 - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta^{d}_{i,j} - \theta^{r}_{i,j} \vert \right)
$$

### Step 6 — Worked numerical example (KTU typical)

Suppose a 2-DOF robot arm ($\;n=2\;$) imitates a 3-step demonstration ($\;N=3\;$):

| Step $i$ | $\theta^d_i = (\theta^d_{i,1},\, \theta^d_{i,2})$ | $\theta^r_i = (\theta^r_{i,1},\, \theta^r_{i,2})$ | Per-joint error $e_i$ |
|----------|----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $(30^\circ,\, 45^\circ)$ | $(32^\circ,\, 43^\circ)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(2+2) = 2^\circ$ |
| 2 | $(60^\circ,\, 90^\circ)$ | $(58^\circ,\, 88^\circ)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(2+2) = 2^\circ$ |
| 3 | $(90^\circ,\, 135^\circ)$ | $(95^\circ,\, 130^\circ)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(5+5) = 5^\circ$ |

Then:
$$
E_{total} = \frac{1}{3}(2 + 2 + 5) = 3^\circ
$$
$$
\eta = 1 - 3^\circ = -2^\circ
$$

Since $\eta$ is bounded in $[0,1]$ for normalized angles, the corrected normalized form is:
$$
\eta_{norm} = 1 - \frac{E_{total}}{E_{max}} = 1 - \frac{3}{180} = 0.983
$$

where $E_{max} = 180^\circ$ is the maximum possible joint error for the chosen range.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reynolds Boid Social Forces

Boids implement social behavior through three forces: **separation**, **alignment**, and **cohesion**.

### Step 1 — Separation force

Each agent $i$ repels from neighbors within radius $r_{sep}$:
$$
\vec{F}^{sep}_i = -K_{sep} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}^{sep}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Alignment force

Agent $i$ aligns its velocity to the *average* of its neighbors:
$$
\vec{v}^{avg}_i = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}^{ali}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}^{ali}_i} \vec{v}_j
$$
$$
\vec{F}^{ali}_i = K_{ali} (\vec{v}^{avg}_i - \vec{v}_i)
$$

### Step 3 — Cohesion force

Agent $i$ is attracted to the *centroid* of its neighbors:
$$
\vec{c}_i = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}^{coh}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}^{coh}_i} \vec{r}_j
$$
$$
\vec{F}^{coh}_i = K_{coh} (\vec{c}_i - \vec{r}_i)
$$

### Step 4 — Total social force

$$
\vec{F}^{soc}_i = \vec{F}^{sep}_i + \vec{F}^{ali}_i + \vec{F}^{coh}_i
$$

### Step 5 — Kinematic update

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+\Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + \frac{\vec{F}^{soc}_i}{m_i}\,\Delta t
$$
$$
\vec{r}_i(t+\Delta t) = \vec{r}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+\Delta t)\,\Delta t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation is in the KTU syllabus:** Reynolds boids are the *canonical* example of emergent social behavior in multi-agent systems. The derivation shows how **local rules → global patterns**, a core idea in swarm intelligence that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Sub-Concepts to Algorithms

| Social Sub-Concept | Classical Algorithm | Modern ML Approach | KTU-Mapped CO |
|--------------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Gaze / Expression Recognition | Geometric facial landmarks | CNN (FER-2013 dataset) | CO3 |
| Imitation Learning | DMP, Dynamic Movement Primitives | GAIL, Behavior Cloning | CO2 |
| Cooperation | Stackelberg game, Joint intent | MARL (MADDPG) | CO4 |
| HRI Turn-Taking | FSM, finite-state dialogue | LLM-driven planner | CO5 |
| Swarm Coordination | Reynolds boids, PSO | Graph Neural Networks | CO4 |
| Proxemic Control | Potential field | RL with proxemic reward | CO3 |
| Trust Modeling | Dynamic Bayesian Network | RNN-based trust estimator | CO5 |
| Emotion Synthesis | Rule-based FACS mapping | Diffusion-based affect generation | CO5 |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Engagement Score for an HRI Scenario (14-mark style)

**Problem:** A companion robot tracks a user's gaze direction $G(t) \in [0,1]$, body pose alignment $P(t) \in [0,1]$, and auditory attention $A(t) \in [0,1]$ with weights $w_g = 0.4$, $w_p = 0.3$, $w_a = 0.3$. Given three time samples, compute the engagement and classify the interaction as *engaged*, *neutral*, or *disengaged*.

| $t$ | $G(t)$ | $P(t)$ | $A(t)$ |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 | 0.8 | 0.6 | 0.7 |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.4 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 0.1 | 0.1 |

**Step 1 — Compute engagement per sample**
$$
E(1) = 0.4(0.8) + 0.3(0.6) + 0.3(0.7) = 0.32 + 0.18 + 0.21 = 0.71
$$
$$
E(2) = 0.4(0.5) + 0.3(0.4) + 0.3(0.4) = 0.20 + 0.12 + 0.12 = 0.44
$$
$$
E(3) = 0.4(0.2) + 0.3(0.1) + 0.3(0.1) = 0.08 + 0.03 + 0.03 = 0.14
$$

**Step 2 — Classify each sample** (KTU rule of thumb: $E > 0.6$ engaged, $0.3 \le E \le 0.6$ neutral, $E < 0.3$ disengaged)

- $E(1) = 0.71$ → **Engaged**
- $E(2) = 0.44$ → **Neutral**
- $E(3) = 0.14$ → **Disengaged**

**Step 3 — Compute mean engagement over the interaction window**
$$
\bar{E} = \frac{0.71 + 0.44 + 0.14}{3} = \frac{1.29}{3} = 0.43
$$

**Step 4 — Overall verdict** — $\bar{E} = 0.43$ falls in the neutral band. The robot should *not* initiate a high-engagement activity (e.g., complex game) but may *offer* a low-effort engagement hook (e.g., a soft prompt).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always show (a) the formula, (b) substituted values, and (c) the interpretation. Examiners award partial credit at each step.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Social Behavior Stack (Layered Architecture)

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - Part A (Short Answer): **3 marks each**
> - Part B (Long Answer with internal choice): **14 marks each**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
Define **proxemics** in the context of social robotics. List Hall's four interpersonal distance zones with their typical distance ranges.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Proxemics is the study of how agents — humans and robots — use interpersonal spatial distance to communicate social relationship, intimacy, and intent.
- **Four Zones (2 marks):**
  - Intimate: 0 – 0.45 m
  - Personal: 0.45 – 1.2 m
  - Social: 1.2 – 3.6 m
  - Public: > 3.6 m

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
Differentiate between **legibility** and **predictability** of a robot's trajectory with a one-line example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Legibility (1.5 marks):** The ease with which an observer can *infer the goal* of the robot from a *partial* trajectory. *Example:* A robot curves toward the cup early so the human *knows* it is reaching for the cup, not the book.
- **Predictability (1.5 marks):** The consistency between the robot's *actual* next action and the action the observer *expects* based on the robot's past behavior. *Example:* If the robot has always moved forward after stopping, the human expects it to move forward again.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer social behavior stack** of a socially behaving robot. Describe the function of each layer in 2–3 lines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **legibility objective function** $L(t)$ from the observer's Bayesian belief update over goals, and show how it is combined with predictability to form a joint planning objective. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — The Five-Layer Stack (7 marks):**

1. **Layer 1 — Perception of Social Cues (1.5 marks):**
   The robot's sensors (cameras, microphones, depth sensors, tactile skin) capture raw social signals such as facial expressions, gaze direction, gestures, body posture, vocal prosody, and interpersonal distance. This layer is responsible for *signal acquisition* and *low-level feature extraction*.

2. **Layer 2 — Affect / Intent Estimation (1.5 marks):**
   The raw cues are mapped to latent variables: emotion (happy, sad, surprised, …), engagement level, trust, and current intent. This is typically implemented with classifiers (CNNs, SVMs) or probabilistic graphical models.

3. **Layer 3 — Social Reasoning (1.5 marks):**
   The robot infers the partner's *mental state* using a Belief–Desire–Intention (BDI) model. It estimates what the human believes, what they want, and what they intend to do next, then computes the *joint* goal structure.

4. **Layer 4 — Social Action Selection (1.5 marks):**
   Based on the inferred state, the robot selects a *socially appropriate* action: approach, back off, wait, take a turn, imitate, or cooperate. This is the decision-making layer.

5. **Layer 5 — Legible Expression (1 mark):**
   The chosen action is *expressed* through modalities the human can decode — gaze, gesture, LED color, screen face, motion style. Without this layer, the robot's intent remains invisible to the human.

**(b) — Derivation of Legibility (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the goal set $\mathcal{G} = \{g_1, \ldots, g_M\}$ and the true goal $g^\star \in \mathcal{G}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the trajectory $\xi(t)$ up to time $t$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Write the observer's Bayesian belief update:
$$
b_t(g) = P(g \mid \xi(0:t)) = \frac{P(\xi(0:t) \mid g)\,P(g)}{\sum_{g' \in \mathcal{G}} P(\xi(0:t) \mid g')\,P(g')}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Define legibility as the belief in the true goal:
$$
L(t) = b_t(g^\star)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Define predictability $P(t)$ similarly.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Combine into a joint objective:
$$
J(\xi) = \alpha \int_0^T L(t)\,dt + \beta \int_0^T P(t)\,dt
$$

---

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain **Reynolds' three social forces** (separation, alignment, cohesion) used in flocking behavior. Derive the total social force on agent $i$ and the kinematic update rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF robot arm imitates a 3-step demonstration. The demonstrated joint angles are $(30^\circ, 45^\circ), (60^\circ, 90^\circ), (90^\circ, 135^\circ)$. The reproduced angles are $(32^\circ, 43^\circ), (58^\circ, 88^\circ), (95^\circ, 130^\circ)$. Compute the **Imitation Fidelity** $\eta$ assuming a normalization range of $180^\circ$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Reynolds Boid Forces (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Separation (2 marks):** Each agent repels from neighbors within $r_{sep}$:
$$
\vec{F}^{sep}_i = -K_{sep} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}^{sep}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert^2}
$$
The $1/d^2$ term ensures closer neighbors exert stronger repulsion, preventing collisions.

**Step 2 — Alignment (2 marks):** Agent $i$ matches the average heading of its neighbors:
$$
\vec{F}^{ali}_i = K_{ali} \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}^{ali}_i \vert}\sum_{j} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i \right)
$$
This makes the swarm move in a coordinated direction.

**Step 3 — Cohesion (1.5 marks):** Agent $i$ is attracted to the centroid of its neighbors:
$$
\vec{F}^{coh}_i = K_{coh} \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}^{coh}_i \vert}\sum_{j} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i \right)
$$
This keeps the swarm from dispersing.

**Step 4 — Total force and update (1.5 marks):**
$$
\vec{F}^{soc}_i = \vec{F}^{sep}_i + \vec{F}^{ali}_i + \vec{F}^{coh}_i
$$
$$
\vec{v}_i(t+\Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + \frac{\vec{F}^{soc}_i}{m_i}\Delta t, \quad \vec{r}_i(t+\Delta t) = \vec{r}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+\Delta t)\Delta t
$$

**(b) — Imitation Fidelity Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Set $n = 2$ (DOFs), $N = 3$ (steps), $E_{max} = 180^\circ$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute per-step error $e_i$:
- $e_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert 30-32\vert + \vert 45-43\vert) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2+2) = 2^\circ$
- $e_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert 60-58\vert + \vert 90-88\vert) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2+2) = 2^\circ$
- $e_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert 90-95\vert + \vert 135-130\vert) = \tfrac{1}{2}(5+5) = 5^\circ$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Mean error:
$$
E_{total} = \frac{1}{3}(2+2+5) = 3^\circ
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Normalize:
$$
\eta_{norm} = 1 - \frac{E_{total}}{E_{max}} = 1 - \frac{3}{180} = 0.983
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Interpretation — An imitation fidelity of 0.983 (98.3%) indicates a high-fidelity reproduction; the robot has successfully learned the demonstrated behavior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 – Social Behavior:**
> 1. **Confusing legibility and predictability.** They are *complementary but distinct*: legibility = observer infers goal from early trajectory; predictability = observer correctly anticipates next action. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-integral form.** The legibility objective is $J = \alpha \int_0^T L(t)\,dt + \beta \int_0^T P(t)\,dt$, **not** a point value. Examiners *will* deduct for missing the integral.
> 3. **Skipping the denominator in Reynolds' separation force.** The $1/\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert^2$ normalization is mandatory. Writing only $-K_{sep} \sum (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j)$ loses a full mark.
> 4. **Confusing Hall's zones.** Intimate and Personal are *not* the same. Mixing up 0.45 m and 1.2 m costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing algorithms in non-CS subjects.** PERAT743 is a CS-adjacent course where light pseudocode *is* allowed, but Python snippets in formal derivations are penalized for not being mathematical. Always prefer equations over code.
> 6. **Skipping units.** Engagement $E(t)$ is dimensionless, but proxemic distances are in **meters**. Hall's zones must carry the unit "m" or marks are lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Social Behavior**

- **Five pillars of social behavior:** Social Cues, Social Learning, Cooperation, HRI, Swarm.
- **Three foundational properties:** Legibility, Predictability, Bidirectionality.
- **Hall's Proxemic Zones:** 0–0.45 m (intimate), 0.45–1.2 m (personal), 1.2–3.6 m (social), > 3.6 m (public).
- **Legibility formula:** $L(t) = P(g^\star \mid \xi(0:t))$.
- **Predictability formula:** $P(t) = P(\xi(t+\Delta t) = \xi^\star(t+\Delta t) \mid \xi(0:t))$.
- **Joint planning objective:** $J = \alpha \int L\,dt + \beta \int P\,dt$.
- **Imitation fidelity:** $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \frac{1}{n}\sum_j \vert \theta^d_{i,j} - \theta^r_{i,j} \vert$.
- **Engagement score:** $E(t) = w_g G + w_p P + w_a A$ with weights summing to 1.
- **Reynolds Boid Forces:** Separation (collision avoidance), Alignment (heading match), Cohesion (centroid attraction).
- **BDI Model:** Belief–Desire–Intention is the standard social reasoning framework.
- **Socially Assistive Robots (SAR):** Robots that aid through *social* rather than *physical* interaction.
- **Stigmergy:** Indirect coordination via environmental markers (e.g., digital pheromones in robot swarms).
- **Stiffness of Exam Questions:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) on definitions and 1 Part-B (14 marks) split as a 7-mark *framework explanation* + 7-mark *derivation/computation*.
- **Always carry units** for any physical quantity; **always write the integrand** $L(t)$ inside the integral, not just $L$.
- **Cite the layer** (1–5) when diagnosing a social failure — KTU examiners reward layered reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fringe Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Fringe Robotics — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fringe Robotics** refers to the class of robotic systems that operate at the *technological and conceptual boundaries* of conventional rigid-body, sensor-driven, centrally-controlled robotics. It encompasses robots whose morphology, actuation principles, sensing modalities, control architectures, or operating environments deviate substantially from the industrial-robotic mainstream. These systems rely heavily on **embodied intelligence**, **morphological computation**, **bio-inspiration**, and **emergent behavior**, drawing directly from the philosophical and engineering foundations of *behavioral robotics*.

The "fringe" designation arises because such robots:
- Exist at the *edge of structural possibility* (soft, compliant, self-reconfigurable, swarm-scale).
- Function at the *boundary of traditional control* (decentralised, reactive, hormone-inspired, stigmergic).
- Often inhabit *non-engineered, unstructured, or extreme environments* (terrestrial, aquatic, aerial, micro-scale, or extraterrestrial).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fringe Robotics is positioned in Module 2 of *Behavioral Robotics* because it represents the *practical frontier* where reactive, bio-inspired, and emergent behavioral control paradigms are tested beyond the limits of classical robotic architectures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the field of robotics as a city. The *downtown core* is filled with stiff, precise, factory-floor robotic arms (industrial robots). Move toward the *outskirts* of the city, and you find unusual neighborhoods:

- A **district of squishy creatures** (soft robots) that move like octopuses.
- A **floating cloud of tiny drones** (swarm robots) that coordinate without a leader.
- A **forest of self-snapping cubes** (modular robots) that rearrange themselves into tools.
- A **microscopic hospital ward** (nanorobots) that travel through bloodstreams.

All of these districts together form the **fringe** of robotics — unconventional systems that, instead of relying on heavy computation and rigid hardware, push *intelligence into the body itself*. This is the central intuition behind behavioral robotics applied to fringe systems: **the body shapes the behavior**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** In fringe robotics, behavior emerges from the *interaction between morphology, materials, and environment*, rather than being dictated solely by a central planner.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants in Fringe Robotics

Although fringe robotics is largely qualitative, a few quantitative benchmarks recur in literature:

- **Compliant Strain Capacity:** typically **> 100 %** for soft robotic actuators.
- **Swarm Cardinality:** typically **N ≥ 10** for emergent behavior to manifest.
- **Morphological Computation Ratio (MCR):** the fraction of control effort delegated to body mechanics; values approaching **0.8–0.95** indicate strong embodiment.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** often **infinite (continuum)** in soft manipulators.
- **Reconfiguration Granularity:** the smallest modular unit (often **cm-scale** for M-TRAN/ATRON, **mm-scale** for micro-modular systems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Fringe Robotics

Fringe robotics is best understood through a structured taxonomy of its four principal axes:

### A. Morphological Fringe (Soft & Compliant Robotics)
- Built from **elastomers, hydrogels, fluidic elastomer actuators (FEAs)**, and shape-memory alloys.
- Behavior arises from material deformation rather than rigid kinematics.
- Uses *embodied intelligence* — control effort is reduced because the body *naturally* conforms.

### B. Collective Fringe (Swarm Robotics)
- Operates via **stigmergy**, **flocking rules** (Reynolds' boids: separation, alignment, cohesion), and **decentralised consensus**.
- Behavior is *emergent* — no single agent possesses the global plan.

### C. Reconfigurable Fringe (Modular Self-Reconfigurable Robots — MSRs)
- Composed of identical or near-identical modules with **latching** and **inter-module communication**.
- Topology can morph (chain → lattice → truss) to suit the task.
- Examples: **M-TRAN**, **ATRON**, **SMORES-EP**, **SuperBot**.

### D. Scale-Fringe Robotics
- **Micro/Nano robotics:** operate at micrometer to nanometer scales using magnetic, chemical, or biological propulsion.
- **Macro/Extreme robotics:** operate in disaster zones, deep-sea, polar, or extraterrestrial environments where conventional robotics fails.

## 2.2 Behavioral Control Stack in Fringe Systems

The control hierarchy aligns with classic behavioral robotics principles, layered from reactive to deliberative:

1. **Reactive Layer** — direct sensor-actuator coupling (e.g., Braitenberg vehicles).
2. **Subsumption Layer** — competing behaviors arbitrated by priority (Brooks, 1986).
3. **Hormonal/Swarm Layer** — diffuse signaling between agents or modules.
4. **Emergent Layer** — global behavior arises from local interactions.
5. **Morphological Layer** — body mechanics *itself* produces the behavior.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises the key quantitative relationships used in the analysis of fringe robotic systems. Standard SI units apply unless otherwise noted.

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number (fluid-robot interaction) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Governs flow regime for aquatic/aerial soft robots | dimensionless |
| Morphological Computation Ratio | $MCR = \dfrac{C_{body}}{C_{body} + C_{controller}}$ | Fraction of computation delegated to body | dimensionless, $0 \leq MCR \leq 1$ |
| Elastic Strain (soft actuator) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Linear strain under actuation | dimensionless (or %) |
| Pneumatic Artificial Muscle (PAM) Contraction | $\varepsilon_{PAM} = \dfrac{L_{relaxed} - L_{contracted}}{L_{relaxed}}$ | Strain produced by pressurisation | dimensionless |
| Boids Cohesion Steering Vector | $\vec{v}_{cohesion} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vec{p_i} - \vec{p}_{self}$ | Average position of neighbours minus self position | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Boids Separation Steering Vector | $\vec{v}_{sep} = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_r}\left(\vec{p}_{self} - \vec{p_j}\right)$ | Repulsion from too-close neighbours | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Stigmergic Pheromone Update | $P(x,t+\Delta t) = (1-\rho)P(x,t) + \Delta P(x,t)$ | Evaporation (rate $\rho$) + deposition | arbitrary units |
| Modular Reconfiguration Cost | $E_{rec} = N \cdot E_{latch} + T_{rec} \cdot P_{control}$ | Energy for $N$ latches over time $T_{rec}$ | Joules |
| Compliant Joint Stiffness | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{\tau}{\theta}$ | Effective rotational stiffness | $\text{Nm/rad}$ |
| Swarm Consensus Convergence | $\dot{x_i} = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(x_j - x_i)$ | Distributed averaging rule | state units/s |

> [!NOTE]
