<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of an Inspection Mission

An inspection mission under the behavioral paradigm is decomposed into **four orthogonal layers** of operation:

### Layer 1 — Perceptual Front-End
- The robot's sensors sample the environment at a fixed rate $f_s$ (Hz).
- Each sensor has a **footprint** $\mathcal{F}$ — the area on the target surface covered by one sample.
- Raw data is filtered, segmented, and fed as **percepts** to Layer 2.

### Layer 2 — Behavioral Layer (Core of Module 3)
A collection of independent, concurrently active behavior modules, each of the form:

$$ \mathbf{a}_i = \mathcal{B}_i(\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{s}_i) $$

where $\mathbf{a}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the output actuation vector, $\mathbf{p}_i$ is the percept input vector, and $\mathbf{s}_i$ is the internal state vector of behavior $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "behavioral" and not "planned"?**
> In subsumption-style or motor-schema architectures, each $\mathcal{B}_i$ is a small, fast, often stateless function. The global mission emerges from the **vector summation / arbitration** of all $\mathbf{a}_i$ — this is the *Why* behind the high robustness of behavioral inspection: failure of one behavior does not catastrophically halt the mission.

### Layer 3 — Arbitration / Fusion Mechanism
The output of the behavioral layer is combined by an **arbiter** $\mathcal{A}$ to produce the final motor command:

$$ \mathbf{u} = \mathcal{A}(\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{a}_2, \dots, \mathbf{a}_n) $$

Common arbiters include:
- **Priority-based subsumption** (Brooks, 1986) — higher-priority behaviors suppress lower ones.
- **Weighted vector summation** (Arkin, motor schemas) — outputs are linearly combined with weights $w_i$.
- **Fuzzy fusion** — each behavior outputs a fuzzy membership; defuzzification yields $\mathbf{u}$.
- **Voting / Null-space** — Davenport et al. — used in multi-robot inspection.

### Layer 4 — Mission Manager (Deliberative Overlay)
A slower, deliberative layer that tracks coverage, re-allocates regions, and decides *mission complete / abort*.

## 2.2 Sensor Footprint Geometry

For a downward-facing camera at altitude $h$ with horizontal field-of-view $\theta_h$ and vertical field-of-view $\theta_v$:

$$ A_{\text{foot}} = h^2 \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_h}{2}\right) \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_v}{2}\right) \cdot 4 $$

For a side-facing 2D range sensor (e.g., scanning sonar) at standoff distance $d$ with angular aperture $\alpha$ and range resolution $\Delta r$:

$$ A_{\text{foot,2D}} \approx d \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta r $$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every line is a likely KTU ESE formula question.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coverage Ratio** | $C_r = \dfrac{A_{\text{inspected}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | $A_{\text{inspected}}$ = area observed; $A_{\text{total}}$ = target area | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Camera Footprint (nadir)** | $A_{\text{foot}} = 4h^2 \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_h}{2}\right) \tan\!\!\left(\frac{\theta_v}{2}\right)$ | $h$ = altitude; $\theta_h,\theta_v$ = FOV | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | **Overlap Ratio** | $o_v = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta x}{L_h}$ | $\Delta x$ = forward step; $L_h$ = footprint length | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Lawn-Mower Track Spacing** | $y_{sep} = L_w (1 - o_v)$ | $L_w$ = footprint width | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | **Sensor Range Constraint** | $h \le h_{\max} = \dfrac{r_{\max} \tan(\theta_v/2)}{\text{res}_{px}}$ | $r_{\max}$ = required ground resolution | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | **Detection Probability (SNR model)** | $P_d = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_s - \mu_n}{\sigma_n} - \Phi^{-1}(P_{fa})\right)$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF; $\mu_s,\mu_n$ = signal/noise means | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Time on Task** | $T_m = \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{v \cdot w_e}$ | $v$ = platform speed; $w_e$ = effective swath | $\text{s}$ |
| 8 | **Energy Model** | $E = P_{\text{cruise}} \cdot T_m + P_{\text{hover}} \cdot T_{\text{hover}}$ | $P_{\text{cruise}}, P_{\text{hover}}$ = power states | $\text{J}$ or $\text{Wh}$ |
| 9 | **Behavior Arbitration (sum)** | $\mathbf{u} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \mathbf{a}_i$ | $w_i$ = behavioral gain; $\sum w_i = 1$ | $\text{m/s}$, $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 10 | **Multi-Robot Coverage (parallel)** | $T_m^{(N)} = \dfrac{T_m^{(1)}}{N \cdot \eta}$ | $N$ = robots; $\eta \in (0,1]$ = coordination efficiency | $\text{s}$ |
| 11 | **Odometer Constraint** | $\ell_{\text{path}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{w_e (1 - o_v)}$ | total path length for full coverage | $\text{m}$ |
| 12 | **Field-of-View Half-Angle** | $\alpha_{1/2} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_{\text{FOV}}}{2h}\right)$ | $d_{\text{FOV}}$ = footprint diameter | $\text{rad}$ |

> **Exam Tip:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ in a markdown table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ in your answer sheets to avoid LaTeX rendering failures in PDF conversion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inspection is one of the **highest commercial-impact applications** of behavioral robotics:

- **Aerial:** Skydio, DJI Matrice, Percepto — power-line, solar-farm, cell-tower inspection.
- **Marine:** Hugin AUVs, Sailbuoy — pipeline and harbor inspections.
- **Ground:** Spot (Boston Dynamics), ANYmal — industrial plant inspection.
- **Indoor:** Fetch, Locus — warehouse aisle inspection.
- **Space:** NASA CADRE (multi-rover lunar surface inspection) — uses exactly the behavior-coordination paradigm taught in this Module.

The engineering value lies in **robustness to uncertainty** — the very property that makes behavior-based control preferable to pure SLAM-and-plan pipelines when the environment is partially known, GPS-denied, or hazardous.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No step is skipped.** Every transition is shown explicitly. Use this section as your *worked-example* reference for numerical problems.

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Altitude for a Specified Ground-Resolution

**Given:** A downward camera with pixel count $N_x \times N_y$, vertical field-of-view $\theta_v$, and a required ground sampling distance (GSD) of $\text{res}_{px}$ metres-per-pixel. **Find:** maximum permissible flight altitude $h_{\max}$.

**Step 1 — Express the angular subtense of a single pixel.**

The vertical FOV is $\theta_v$, distributed across $N_y$ pixels. The angular subtense per pixel is:

$$ \delta_{px} = \frac{\theta_v}{N_y} $$

**Step 2 — Relate the angular subtense to the linear GSD on the ground at altitude $h$.**

Using the small-angle pinhole geometry, the linear extent on the ground corresponding to one pixel is:

$$ \text{GSD}(h) = h \cdot \tan(\delta_{px}) \approx h \cdot \delta_{px} \quad \text{(small-angle approx.)} $$

**Step 3 — Impose the resolution constraint.**

We require $\text{GSD}(h) \le \text{res}_{px}$ to detect a feature of size $r_{\max}$ reliably:

$$ h \cdot \delta_{px} \le \text{res}_{px} $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h$.**

$$ h \le \frac{\text{res}_{px}}{\delta_{px}} = \frac{\text{res}_{px} \cdot N_y}{\theta_v} $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\,h_{\max} = \frac{\text{res}_{px} \cdot N_y}{\theta_v}\,} $$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** $N_y = 3000$ px, $\theta_v = 60^\circ = \pi/3$ rad, $\text{res}_{px} = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m.

$$ h_{\max} = \frac{0.005 \times 3000}{\pi/3} = \frac{15}{1.0472} \approx 14.32 \ \text{m} $$

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Lawn-Mower Track Spacing

**Given:** A rectangular inspection area of width $W$ (perpendicular to the tracks), footprint width $L_w$, and forward step $\Delta x$ with longitudinal overlap $o_v$. **Find:** track separation $y_{sep}$ that ensures full coverage with minimum path length.

**Step 1 — Express the effective swath on the ground.**

With overlap, the *new* ground illuminated per pass is reduced:

$$ w_e = L_w (1 - o_v) $$

**Step 2 — Count the number of tracks needed.**

$$ N_{\text{tracks}} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{w_e} \right\rceil $$

**Step 3 — Express the track separation directly.**

$$ \boxed{\,y_{sep} = L_w (1 - o_v)\,} $$

**Step 4 — Total path length.**

For a rectangular region of length $L$:

$$ \ell_{\text{path}} = N_{\text{tracks}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{tracks}} - 1) \cdot y_{sep} $$

The second term accounts for the U-turns between tracks. At platform speed $v$:

$$ T_m = \frac{\ell_{\text{path}}}{v} $$

> **Worked Numerical Example:** $W = 50$ m, $L = 100$ m, $L_w = 4$ m, $o_v = 0.2$.
> $$ w_e = 4 \times (1 - 0.2) = 3.2 \ \text{m}, \quad N_{\text{tracks}} = \lceil 50 / 3.2 \rceil = 16 $$
> $$ \ell_{\text{path}} = 16 \times 100 + 15 \times 3.2 = 1600 + 48 = 1648 \ \text{m} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Behavior Arbitration Output (Motor Schemas)

**Given:** Three competing behaviors — *Go-To-Waypoint* $\mathcal{B}_1$, *Avoid-Obstacle* $\mathcal{B}_2$, *Follow-Surface* $\mathcal{B}_3$ — with output vectors $\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{a}_2, \mathbf{a}_3 \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and scalar gains $g_1, g_2, g_3$.

**Step 1 — Compute the raw behavior vectors.**

For a unicycle robot with heading $\psi$:

$$ \mathbf{a}_1 = g_1 \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\psi_d - \psi) \\ \sin(\psi_d - \psi) \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ \mathbf{a}_2 = g_2 \frac{\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}_o}{\lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}_o \rVert} \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} $$

$$ \mathbf{a}_3 = g_3 \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\psi_s - \psi) \\ \sin(\psi_s - \psi) \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 2 — Sum the vectors (motor-schema arbitration).**

$$ \mathbf{u}_{\text{raw}} = \mathbf{a}_1 + \mathbf{a}_2 + \mathbf{a}_3 $$

**Step 3 — Normalize to extract a heading command and a speed command.**

$$ \psi_{\text{cmd}} = \text{atan2}(u_y, u_x), \quad v_{\text{cmd}} = v_{\max} \cdot \min(1, \lVert \mathbf{u}_{\text{raw}} \rVert) $$

**Step 4 — Optional subsumption override.** If an obstacle is closer than threshold $d_{\text{safe}}$, the *Avoid* behavior *replaces* the summed output (Brooks, 1986):

$$ \mathbf{u} = \begin{cases} \mathbf{a}_2, & \text{if } d < d_{\text{safe}} \\ \mathbf{a}_1 + \mathbf{a}_3, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Behavior Selection by Mission Phase

| Mission Phase | Dominant Behavior | Supporting Behavior | Suppressed Behavior | Arbiter Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transit to Region** | Go-To-Waypoint | Avoid-Obstacle | Track-Anomaly | Weighted sum |
| **Coverage (Lawn-Mower)** | Follow-Lane | Avoid-Obstacle | Track-Anomaly | Weighted sum |
| **Anomaly Encountered** | Track-Anomaly | Avoid-Obstacle | Follow-Lane | Subsumption (override) |
| **Surface Following (Wall)** | Follow-Surface | Maintain-Standoff | Go-To-Waypoint | Subsumption |
| **Return to Base** | Go-To-Home | Avoid-Obstacle | All others | Subsumption |
| **Multi-Robot Hand-off** | Rendezvous | Avoid-Collision | Track-Anomaly | Voting |

## 3.5 Worked Multi-Robot Coverage Problem

**Problem:** Two UAVs must inspect a $200 \times 200$ m solar farm. Each UAV has a camera footprint of $10 \times 7$ m at operating altitude. Required overlap is $30\%$. The coordination efficiency is $\eta = 0.85$. Compute (a) the effective swath per UAV, (b) the number of tracks each UAV flies, and (c) the expected mission time at $v = 4$ m/s.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Effective swath.**

$$ w_e = 7 \times (1 - 0.30) = 4.9 \ \text{m} $$

**Part (b) — Tracks per UAV.** The two UAVs split the $200$ m width equally, so each covers $W = 100$ m.

$$ N_{\text{tracks}} = \left\lceil \frac{100}{4.9} \right\rceil = 21 \ \text{tracks/UAV} $$

**Part (c) — Mission time.** Path length per UAV (length $L = 200$ m):

$$ \ell_1 = 21 \times 200 + 20 \times 4.9 = 4200 + 98 = 4298 \ \text{m} $$

Single-UAV time: $T_1 = 4298 / 4 = 1074.5$ s. With two UAVs and efficiency $\eta$:

$$ T_2 = \frac{T_1}{2 \times 0.85} = \frac{1074.5}{1.7} \approx 632 \ \text{s} \approx 10.5 \ \text{min} $$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.4 Inspection Mission State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Transit: GPS lock + plan loaded
    Transit --> Coverage: Waypoint reached
    Coverage --> AnomalyTrack: Salient feature detected
    AnomalyTrack --> Coverage: Feature logged
    Coverage --> Handoff: Region complete
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE patterns** (End-Semester Evaluation). Mark distribution and answer depth follow the official PERAT743 pattern.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]
Define *behavioral inspection* in robotics. Differentiate it from classical deliberative path planning.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Behavioral inspection is a paradigm in which a robot executes an inspection mission by coordinating a set of **independent, reactive behavior modules** (e.g., surface-following, obstacle-avoidance, anomaly-tracking) whose outputs are combined by an arbiter to produce motion commands. Unlike **deliberative path planning**, which requires a globally consistent world model and computes a complete trajectory *a-priori*, behavioral inspection is **reactive, model-light, and robust to uncertainty** — the robot's trajectory emerges from local sensor feedback and behavior arbitration. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for contrast in model dependence, 1 for robustness/emergence distinction.)

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]
List any three canonical behaviors used in a bridge-inspection UAV and state the sensor input that activates each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Follow-Surface** — activated by downward LiDAR / altimeter reading; aligns UAV normal to the deck.
2. **Avoid-Obstacle** — activated by stereo-camera or proximity sensor detecting a parapet, cable, or worker.
3. **Track-Anomaly** — activated by an RGB/IR camera detecting a salient crack, spalling, or hotspot.
(3 marks: 1 mark per behavior-sensor pair, 0.5 for the activation logic.)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE convention, **answer ANY ONE** of the following. Both questions are of equal difficulty and map to the same CO and Bloom level (Apply / Analyse).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered behavior-based architecture** for a ship-hull inspection AUV. Identify at least four behaviors and describe the arbiter that fuses them.

**(b)** A downward camera has $N_x \times N_y = 4000 \times 3000$ pixels, vertical FOV $\theta_v = 70^\circ$, and must achieve a ground resolution of $4$ mm/pixel to detect $1$ cm cracks. Compute the maximum operating altitude and the corresponding footprint area. If the AUV must inspect a $120$ m $\times$ $80$ m hull section at speed $v = 1.5$ m/s with $20\%$ forward overlap, find the mission time.

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Layered Architecture (7 marks)**

| Marks | Component | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Block diagram** | (Reference Fig. 4.1 / 4.2 above — present the same in your answer sheet) |
| 1.5 | **Perceptual layer** | Sonar, downward camera, depth sensor; provides $\mathbf{p}_i$ to behaviors |
| 0.5 each × 4 = 2 | **Four behaviors** | (1) *Follow-Hull* (depth-sensor driven), (2) *Maintain-Standoff* (sonar ranging), (3) *Avoid-Obstacle* (forward sonar), (4) *Track-Anomaly* (camera-based defect detector) |
| 1.5 | **Arbiter** | Subsumption — *Avoid* > *Track* > *Follow* > *Maintain*. On obstacle approach, *Avoid* overrides all others (Brooks, 1986). |
| 0.5 | **Mission manager** | Maintains coverage map, logs anomalies, hands off to next lane |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Maximum altitude (3 marks).**

$$ h_{\max} = \frac{\text{res}_{px} \cdot N_y}{\theta_v} = \frac{0.004 \times 3000}{(70 \times \pi / 180)} = \frac{12}{1.222} = 9.82 \ \text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Footprint area (2 marks).**

First compute horizontal FOV. Assuming square pixels and the same $\theta_h = 70^\circ$ (equal FOV camera):

$$ A_{\text{foot}} = 4 h^2 \tan(\theta/2)^2 = 4 \times 9.82^2 \times \tan(35^\circ)^2 = 4 \times 96.43 \times 0.4903 = 189.1 \ \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Mission time (2 marks).**

Effective swath $w_e = L_w (1 - o_v) = \sqrt{189.1} \times 0.8 = 13.75 \times 0.8 = 11.0$ m. Number of lanes: $\lceil 80 / 11.0 \rceil = 8$ lanes. Path length:

$$ \ell = 8 \times 120 + 7 \times 11.0 = 960 + 77 = 1037 \ \text{m} $$

$$ T_m = 1037 / 1.5 \approx 691 \ \text{s} \approx 11.5 \ \text{min} $$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]

**(a)** Compare the **subsumption architecture** and the **motor-schema architecture** as two alternative arbiters for a behavior-based inspection robot. Which is more suitable for *multi-robot inspection*, and why?

**(b)** Two ground robots must cooperatively inspect a $90$ m $\times$ $60$ m warehouse floor. Each robot has a circular field-of-view of diameter $4$ m, moves at $0.8$ m/s, and requires $25\%$ overlap. The coordination efficiency is $\eta = 0.9$. Compute (i) the effective swath, (ii) the number of tracks per robot, and (iii) the expected mission time. If the coverage ratio must exceed $0.95$, is the design acceptable?

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Architectural Comparison (7 marks)**

| Marks | Comparison Axis | Subsumption (Brooks) | Motor Schema (Arkin) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Combination rule** | Discrete override (winner-takes-all at a layer) | Continuous vector summation with gains |
| 1 | **Output nature** | Discrete (one behavior's output is used) | Continuous (smooth blending) |
| 1 | **Design effort** | Lower — discrete layer hierarchy | Higher — tuning of $w_i$ gains |
| 1 | **Smoothness** | Lower (jittery on layer switch) | Higher (smooth trajectories) |
| 1 | **Multi-robot suitability** | Less suited — winner-takes-all is brittle when robots must negotiate | **More suited** — vector fields are additive and naturally support inter-robot repulsive fields |
| 1 | **Tunability** | Hard (binary enable/disable) | Soft (continuous $w_i$) |
| 1 | **Verdict** | Better for *single-robot, hard real-time* missions | **Better for multi-robot inspection** due to additive force-field formulation |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Effective swath (2 marks).** For a circular FOV, $L_w = 4$ m, and the *effective* footprint is approximated as a rectangle of width 4 m:

$$ w_e = 4 \times (1 - 0.25) = 3.0 \ \text{m} $$

**(ii) Tracks per robot (2 marks).** Each robot takes half the warehouse width, so $W = 45$ m:

$$ N_{\text{tracks}} = \lceil 45 / 3.0 \rceil = 15 \ \text{tracks/robot} $$

**(iii) Mission time (3 marks).** Path length per robot (length $L = 60$ m):

$$ \ell_1 = 15 \times 60 + 14 \times 3.0 = 900 + 42 = 942 \ \text{m} $$

Single-robot time: $T_1 = 942 / 0.8 = 1177.5$ s. With two robots and $\eta = 0.9$:

$$ T_2 = \frac{1177.5}{2 \times 0.9} = \frac{1177.5}{1.8} \approx 654 \ \text{s} \approx 10.9 \ \text{min} $$

**Coverage check:** With $25\%$ overlap the design is theoretically $100\%$ covered (overlap guarantees re-imaging, not gap), so $C_r \to 1.0 > 0.95$ — **the design is acceptable**. If $o_v$ were reduced below $\sim 10\%$, the GPS drift of ground robots would cause $C_r < 0.95$ and the design would be rejected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting radians vs. degrees** in $h_{\max} = \text{res}_{px} \cdot N_y / \theta_v$. Always convert $\theta_v$ to **radians** before substituting. A 60° treated as 60 rad gives a wildly wrong answer (~3% of correct). Lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing overlap ratio definitions.** $o_v$ is *longitudinal* (forward) overlap. Lateral overlap is separately denoted $o_l$. Mixing them up inverts your swath and inflates the number of tracks. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using $\lceil \rceil$ incorrectly** — students often use round-down and end up with under-coverage at the last lane. Always justify using $\lceil W / w_e \rceil$ explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring the U-turn distance** in total path length. The transition between lanes is non-negligible for small $L$. Lose **1 mark** if you only compute $N_{\text{tracks}} \times L$.
> 5. **In multi-robot problems, dividing by $N$ without $\eta$.** Real coordination has overhead — your "perfect" time is a lower bound. Lose **1 mark** for omitting $\eta$.
> 6. **Writing $\lvert x \rvert$ with `|` in markdown tables** — this crashes KTU's online portal renderer. Use `\lvert` and `\rvert` in your LaTeX answer sheets.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Behavioral inspection** = reactive, model-light, multi-behaviour coordination. *Not* a global planner.
- ✅ **Four-layer model** = Sensors → Perceptual Front-End → Behavior Layer → Arbiter → Actuator. Add a Mission Manager as a deliberative overlay.
- ✅ **Three canonical arbiters** = (1) Subsumption (Brooks — override), (2) Motor Schemas (Arkin — vector sum), (3) Fuzzy / Voting.
- ✅ **Essential metrics** = $C_r$ (coverage ratio), $P_d$ (detection), $P_{fa}$ (false alarm), $T_m$ (mission time), $E$ (energy).
- ✅ **Camera footprint** = $4 h^2 \tan(\theta_h/2)\tan(\theta_v/2)$. **Altitude for resolution** = $h_{\max} = \text{res}_{px} \cdot N_y / \theta_v$ (with $\theta_v$ in **radians**).
- ✅ **Track spacing** = $y_{sep} = L_w (1 - o_v)$. **Number of tracks** = $\lceil W / w_e \rceil$.
- ✅ **Total path length** = $N_{\text{tracks}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{tracks}} - 1) \cdot y_{sep}$ (do **not** forget the U-turn term).
- ✅ **Multi-robot time** = $T_m^{(N)} = T_m^{(1)} / (N \eta)$, where $\eta \in (0, 1]$ is the coordination efficiency.
- ✅ **Detection probability (SNR model)**: $P_d = \Phi\bigl((\mu_s - \mu_n)/\sigma_n - \Phi^{-1}(P_{fa})\bigr)$ — use the standard-normal CDF.
- ✅ **Behavior selection by phase** = Transit → *Go-To-Waypoint*; Coverage → *Follow-Lane*; Anomaly → *Track-Anomaly*; Emergency → *Avoid-Obstacle*; Multi-robot → *Rendezvous*.
- ✅ **Real-world platforms** = Spot, ANYmal (ground); Skydio, DJI (aerial); Hugin, Sailbuoy (marine); CADRE (space).
- ✅ **Module-3 syllabus outcome (CO3)** = Design a behavior-coordinated inspection system, justify arbitration choice, and quantitatively evaluate mission performance.

> **End of Module 3 — Inspection Notes (PERAT743, KTU 2024 Scheme).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Welding (Behavioral Robotics Context):** A behavior-based robotic manipulation paradigm in which an autonomous or semi-autonomous manipulator executes arc-welding, spot-welding, or laser-welding tasks by decomposing the high-level *weld task* into a layered set of reactive and deliberative behaviors — such as **seam tracking**, **torch pose regulation**, **adaptive heat-input control**, **arc-length stabilization**, and **fault-recovery reflexes** — coordinated through a hybrid reactive/deliberative control architecture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for PERAT743 (Behavioral Robotics), *Welding* is treated as a **canonical industrial testbed** for studying how behavior-based control, subsumption architectures, and multi-sensor fusion can be deployed in a real manufacturing cell. The welding robot is not viewed as a single monolithic program but as a **society of interacting behaviors**, each subscribing to or suppressing others based on real-time sensory inputs.

### 1.2 Subsystems of a Behavioral Welding Robot

| Sub-system | Behavioral Role |
|---|---|
| Arc sensor (voltage/current feedback) | Reactive behavior — arc-length regulation |
| Vision / laser seam tracker | Reactive behavior — seam tracking & cross-seam correction |
| Force/torque sensor | Reactive behavior — contact detection & standoff control |
| Path planner (offline CAD/CAM) | Deliberative behavior — global trajectory generation |
| Supervisor (state machine) | Meta-behavior — mode arbitration (SEARCH → TRACK → WELD → RETRACT) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Skier on a Mountain":** Imagine you are learning to ski down a snowy slope. You do **not** memorize every snowflake. Instead, your brain runs *behaviors*: keep your knees bent, lean downhill, avoid the tree, slow down at the bump. Each behavior reacts to one specific stimulus. A welding robot works exactly the same way. One behavior keeps the arc length constant (like keeping ski knees bent), another keeps the torch on the seam (like avoiding the tree), and a higher-level planner tells them all when to start and stop (like the trail map). **No single behavior knows everything — together they produce a perfect weld.**

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Arc voltage (typical GMAW):** $V_{arc} \approx 18\text{–}32\ \text{V}$
- **Welding current (GMAW):** $I \approx 80\text{–}350\ \text{A}$
- **Wire feed speed:** $WFS \approx 2\text{–}15\ \text{m/min}$
- **Travel speed:** $TS \approx 0.3\text{–}1.5\ \text{m/min}$
- **Heat input per unit length:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \times 1000}\ \text{kJ/mm}$ where $\eta$ is **arc efficiency** (GMAW $\eta \approx 0.80$)
- **Standoff distance (CTWD):** $d_{st} \approx 10\text{–}25\ \text{mm}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Behavior-Based Architecture (Three-Layer Model)

Behavioral robotics decomposes the welding task into **three interacting layers**, inspired by Brooks' subsumption architecture:

**Layer 1 — Reactive (Reflex) Behaviors (Sub-symbolic, ~1 kHz loop)**
- **Arc-length regulator:** measures arc voltage $V_{arc}$ and adjusts wire feed speed $WFS$ such that the actual arc length tracks the reference. Implemented as a fast PI/PID loop with sampling rate $f_s \geq 1\ \text{kHz}$.
- **Seam tracker:** reads lateral deviation $e_{lat}$ from a laser line scanner and pushes the TCP sideways to keep $e_{lat} \rightarrow 0$.
- **Touch-sensing / arc-blow reflex:** detects current spike on contact and retracts.

**Layer 2 — Deliberative (Sequencer) Behaviors**
- Reads the offline-programmed weld path from the **CAD-to-path** translator.
- Schedules sub-behaviors: SEARCH → IGNITE → TRAVEL → CRATER-FILL → RETRACT.
- Performs **look-ahead** adaptive current ramping to compensate for thermal accumulation.

**Layer 3 — Meta (Supervisory) Behavior**
- Communicates with the cell PLC, interlocks with fixtures, handles faults, and publishes welding logs to the MES (Manufacturing Execution System).

## 2.2 Why the "Behavior" Decomposition Works for Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Welding is a **multi-timescale** problem: arc dynamics happen at $10^{-3}\ \text{s}$, seam geometry changes occur at $10^{-1}\ \text{s}$, and the job schedule operates at $10^{2}\ \text{s}$. Behavior-based control lets each timescale be handled by its own tightly-scoped behavior, avoiding the brittleness of one monolithic controller.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbol | Standard Form | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat input per unit length | $HI$ | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I}{TS \times 1000}$ | $\text{kJ/mm}$ |
| 2 | Arc-length PI control law | $u(t)$ | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $\text{V}$ |
| 3 | Lateral seam error (image plane) | $e_{lat}$ | $e_{lat} = f \cdot \tan\theta - x_{ref}$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| 4 | Wire-feed-rate to current relation | $I$ | $I = k_w \cdot WFS$ | $\text{A}$ |
| 5 | Touch-sensing threshold | $\Delta V$ | $\Delta V = V_{open} - V_{contact}$ | $\text{V}$ |
| 6 | Through-arc seam tracking gain | $K_{ta}$ | $K_{ta} = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial h}$ | $\text{A/mm}$ |
| 7 | Travelling-coordinate transform | $\vec{p}_s$ | $\vec{p}_s = R(\psi)^T (\vec{p}_w - \vec{p}_{seam})$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| 8 | Standoff distance (CTWD) | $d_{st}$ | $d_{st} = \dfrac{V_{arc}}{E_{column}}$ | $\text{mm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $f$ is the camera focal length, $\theta$ is the pixel offset angle, $E_{column}$ is the electric field in the arc column, $R(\psi)$ is the rotation matrix aligning world frame to the seam frame, $\vec{p}_{seam}$ is the seam reference point, and $\vec{p}_w$ is the world-frame TCP position. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in place of plain pipes to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Automotive body-in-white (BIW):** spot-welding robots run > 6,000 spots per vehicle; behavior-based arc-blow recovery reduces rework by ~30 %.
- **Shipbuilding / heavy fabrication:** adaptive seam tracking tolerates $\pm 5\ \text{mm}$ plate distortion without re-teaching.
- **Aerospace thin-gauge welding:** ultra-low-heat-input behaviors prevent burn-through on aluminium alloys.
- **Collaborative welding cobots:** force-based behaviors allow human-robot shared welding of large structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat-Input Formula

The **heat input per unit length** $HI$ is one of the most examined quantities in robotic welding because it determines penetration, distortion, and HAZ (heat-affected zone) width. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of energy.**
Electrical power delivered to the arc is $P = V \cdot I$.

**Step 2 — Energy deposited per unit length.**
A small length of weld $dx$ is traversed in time $dt = dx / TS$. Energy deposited over that length is:
$$dE = P \cdot \dfrac{dx}{TS} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{TS}\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Introduce arc efficiency.**
Not all electrical energy enters the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ does; the rest is lost as spatter, radiation, and fume. So the *useful* heat input is:
$$\dfrac{dE_{useful}}{dx} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I}{TS}$$

**Step 4 — Convert units to $\text{kJ/mm}$.**
Since $V$ is in volts, $I$ in amps, $TS$ in $\text{mm/min}$, we multiply by $60/10^6 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ to convert $(\text{V}\cdot\text{A}\cdot\text{min})/\text{mm}$ into $\text{kJ/mm}$:
$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I}{TS \times 1000}\ \text{kJ/mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **1000** (not $10^6$) is correct because $TS$ is conventionally expressed in $\text{mm/min}$ (not $\text{m/s}$). KTU examiners frequently mark this wrong — verify your units.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Through-Arc Seam-Tracking Control Law

Through-arc tracking uses the **welding current itself** as a probe: as the torch moves laterally relative to the seam, the contact-tip-to-work distance changes, modulating arc current. Let us derive the controller.

**Step 1 — Lateral deviation variable.**
Define the lateral offset $h$ as the horizontal distance between the torch axis and the seam centerline.

**Step 2 — Static current vs. height characteristic.**
Empirically (and from first-order plasma physics), $I(h)$ is monotonic:
$$I(h) = I_0 + K_{ta} \cdot h$$

where $K_{ta} = \partial I / \partial h$ has units $\text{A/mm}$.

**Step 3 — Define the error signal.**
The reference current $I_{ref}$ corresponds to the desired lateral alignment. The error is:
$$e(t) = I_{ref} - I(t) = I_{ref} - \bigl(I_0 + K_{ta}\, h(t)\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Convert error to correction velocity.**
A proportional-corrective lateral velocity $v_{lat}$ is commanded as:
$$v_{lat}(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Solve the closed-loop dynamics.**
Substituting $e(t)$:
$$\dot{h}(t) = -v_{lat}(t) = -K_p\bigl(I_{ref} - I_0 - K_{ta}\, h(t)\bigr) - K_i \int_0^t \bigl(I_{ref} - I_0 - K_{ta}\, h(\tau)\bigr) d\tau$$

Letting $\tilde{I} = I_{ref} - I_0$ and taking the derivative to obtain a closed ODE:
$$\ddot{h}(t) + K_p K_{ta}\, \dot{h}(t) + K_i K_{ta}\, h(t) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Characteristic equation and stability.**
The characteristic polynomial $s^2 + K_p K_{ta}\, s + K_i K_{ta} = 0$ has roots:
$$s_{1,2} = \dfrac{-K_p K_{ta} \pm \sqrt{(K_p K_{ta})^2 - 4 K_i K_{ta}}}{2}$$

For asymptotic stability, we require $K_p > 0$, $K_i > 0$, and discriminant $\geq 0$ for real-root overdamped response (preferred for welding, to avoid oscillation across the seam).

**Convergence condition (final, exam-ready form):**
$$K_i \leq \dfrac{(K_p)^2 \, K_{ta}}{4}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Arc-Length Control from Standoff

The arc column behaves as an approximately constant-resistance plasma. Ohm's law gives:
$$V_{arc} = E_{column} \cdot d_{st}$$

Therefore the standoff distance is:
$$d_{st} = \dfrac{V_{arc}}{E_{column}}$$

If the measured arc voltage drifts upward, the standoff has grown; the wire-feed behavior must advance the wire to reduce it. The closed-loop wire-feed command $WFS_{cmd}$ is:
$$WFS_{cmd}(t) = WFS_{nom} + K_v \bigl(V_{ref} - V_{arc}(t)\bigr)$$

which can be rearranged to show that the steady-state arc voltage converges to $V_{ref}$ provided the loop gain $K_v \cdot \partial V_{arc}/\partial WFS$ is positive.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> A GMAW robotic cell uses $\eta = 0.80$, $V = 28\ \text{V}$, $I = 220\ \text{A}$, and $TS = 0.6\ \text{m/min}$. Compute the heat input.

Convert travel speed: $TS = 0.6\ \text{m/min} = 600\ \text{mm/min}$.

$$HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 220}{600 \times 1000} = \dfrac{4928}{600000} \approx 0.00821\ \text{kJ/mm}$$

$$HI \approx 8.21\ \text{J/mm}$$

This falls in the **low-to-medium** heat-input range, suitable for 3 mm structural steel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Behavior-Based Welding Control Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["META SUPERVISOR PLC MES INTERFACE"]
    nodeB["DELIBERATIVE PLANNER CAD TO PATH TRANSLATOR"]
    nodeC["REACTIVE SEAM TRACKER LASER VISION"]
    nodeD["REACTIVE ARC LENGTH REGULATOR V AND I SENSORS"]
    nodeE["REACTIVE TORCH POSE FORCE TORQUE SENSOR"]
    nodeF["ROBOT MANIPULATOR 6 DOF ARM"]
    nodeG["WELDING POWER SOURCE GMAW GTaw"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above obeys the Mermaid compilation safeguards: every node ID is purely alphanumeric (for example, `nodeA`, `nodeB`), and every quoted label is plain uppercase alphanumeric text with no embedded markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Behavioral State Machine for a Single Weld Pass

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateIDLE["STATE IDLE ROBOT AT HOME"]
    stateSEARCH["STATE SEARCH SCAN FOR SEAM"]
    stateIGNITE["STATE IGNITE ARC STRIKE"]
    stateTRACK["STATE TRACK SEAM FOLLOW"]
    stateCRATER["STATE CRATER FILL RAMP DOWN"]
    stateRECOVER["STATE RECOVER ARC BLOW FAULT"]
    stateDONE["STATE DONE RETRACT AND LOG"]

    stateIDLE --> stateSEARCH : START SIGNAL
    stateSEARCH --> stateIGNITE : SEAM FOUND
    stateIGNITE --> stateTRACK : ARC STABLE
    stateTRACK --> stateCRATER : END OF PATH
    stateTRACK --> stateRECOVER : FAULT DETECTED
    stateRECOVER --> stateTRACK : FAULT CLEARED
    stateCRATER --> stateDONE : ARC OFF
```

## 4.3 Sensor-Fusion Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sn1["LASER SCANNER 2D PROFILE"]
    sn2["ARC VOLTAGE DIVIDER"]
    sn3["HALL EFFECT CURRENT SENSOR"]
    sn4["6 AXIS F T SENSOR AT FLANGE"]
    fus["SENSOR FUSION KALMAN FILTER"]
    beh["BEHAVIOR ARBITRATOR SUBSUMPTION"]
    out["TORCH VELOCITY AND WFS COMMAND"]

    sn1 --> fus
    sn2 --> fus
    sn3 --> fus
    sn4 --> fus
    fus --> beh
    beh --> out
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**Q:** *Define "behavior-based control" in the context of robotic welding. Name TWO reactive behaviors used in an arc-welding robot.*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Behavior-based control decomposes a complex welding task into multiple small, independent, sensor-driven modules called *behaviors*, each running in parallel and reacting to its own stimulus. Unlike a single monolithic controller, behaviors cooperate and compete through subsumption or arbitration, producing robust weld quality even under part variability. Two reactive behaviors are: **(1) arc-length regulator** (uses arc-voltage feedback to keep wire feed and torch height stable), and **(2) lateral seam tracker** (uses through-arc current variation or a laser scanner to keep the torch aligned with the joint centerline). *(1 mark definition + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark for two behaviors.)*

### Question A2

**Q:** *State the heat-input formula for arc welding and explain the significance of arc efficiency $\eta$.*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The heat input per unit length is $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{TS \times 1000}$ $\text{kJ/mm}$. The arc efficiency $\eta$ accounts for the fraction of electrical energy that is actually transferred into the workpiece as heat; the rest is lost to spatter, radiation, convection, and fume. For GMAW, $\eta \approx 0.80$; for GTAW, $\eta \approx 0.60$; for SAW, $\eta \approx 0.95$. Higher $\eta$ implies more penetration per joule. *(1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation of $\eta$ + 1 mark numeric values.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer behavior-based architecture** for a robotic arc-welding cell. List the sensors and behaviors at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer characteristic** of the through-arc seam-tracking behavior and state the stability condition on $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_{ta}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The architecture consists of three interacting layers:

1. **Reactive Layer (Sub-symbolic, $\sim 1\ \text{kHz}$ loop):**
   - Arc-length regulator using voltage feedback.
   - Lateral seam tracker using through-arc current or laser scanner.
   - Touch-sensing reflex for seam-finding.
   - Force-based standoff regulator using F/T sensor.
2. **Deliberative Layer (Symbolic, $\sim 10\ \text{Hz}$ loop):**
   - Path sequencer translating CAD/CAM weld paths into robot trajectories.
   - Heat-input ramping behavior for thermal accumulation compensation.
3. **Meta-Supervisory Layer (Cell-level, $\sim 1\ \text{Hz}$):**
   - PLC / MES interface, interlocks, fault logging, mode arbitration.

| Layer | Loop Rate | Sensors | Example Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactive | 1 kHz | Arc V/I, F/T, laser | Seam tracking |
| Deliberative | 10 Hz | Encoders, path DB | Path sequencing |
| Meta | 1 Hz | PLC I/O, cell signals | Mode arbitration |

*(1 mark diagram + 1 mark per layer description + 1 mark table.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Starting from $I(h) = I_0 + K_{ta} h$, error $e(t) = I_{ref} - I_0 - K_{ta} h(t)$, and corrective velocity $v_{lat} = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt$, with $\dot{h} = -v_{lat}$:

$$\ddot{h}(t) + K_p K_{ta}\, \dot{h}(t) + K_i K_{ta}\, h(t) = 0$$

Characteristic equation: $s^2 + K_p K_{ta}\, s + K_i K_{ta} = 0$.

Roots:
$$s_{1,2} = \dfrac{-K_p K_{ta} \pm \sqrt{(K_p K_{ta})^2 - 4 K_i K_{ta}}}{2}$$

For overdamped, asymptotically stable tracking:
$$K_p > 0,\quad K_i > 0,\quad K_i \leq \dfrac{(K_p)^2 K_{ta}}{4}$$

*(2 marks derivation of ODE, 2 marks characteristic equation, 2 marks roots, 1 mark stability condition.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain **seam-finding** and **touch-sensing** behaviors used to localize the start of a weld. Differentiate between the two. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic GMAW cell operates at $V = 30\ \text{V}$, $I = 250\ \text{A}$, $TS = 0.5\ \text{m/min}$, $\eta = 0.85$. Compute the heat input per unit length in $\text{kJ/mm}$ and $\text{J/mm}$, and comment on its suitability for a 4 mm low-carbon-steel plate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Seam-finding behavior:** A search behavior where the robot TCP performs a programmed zig-zag or spiral pattern until a **joint** is detected — typically via arc-ignition (an arc struck off the plate produces a voltage spike when the joint gap opens) or via laser scanner reading a depth jump. Once found, the manipulator orients to the seam. *(3 marks.)*

**Touch-sensing behavior:** A contact-based behavior where the wire or a dedicated probe is driven downward until contact with the workpiece is detected via current flow (wire) or force threshold (probe). The TCP height at contact defines the workpiece surface plane. *(2 marks.)*

**Differentiation:** Seam-finding identifies the *joint line* (lateral feature), while touch-sensing identifies the *workpiece surface* (vertical feature). Seam-finding needs an arc or vision; touch-sensing needs only conductivity. *(2 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Step 1 — Convert units: $TS = 0.5\ \text{m/min} = 500\ \text{mm/min}$.

Step 2 — Apply formula:
$$HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{TS \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 30 \times 250}{500 \times 1000} = \dfrac{6375}{500000} = 0.01275\ \text{kJ/mm}$$

Step 3 — Convert:
$$HI = 12.75\ \text{J/mm}$$

Step 4 — Suitability comment: For 4 mm low-carbon steel, the typical recommended heat input range is $1.0\text{–}1.5\ \text{kJ/mm}$ at the joint, but expressed per unit length on a single-pass butt weld, $8\text{–}15\ \text{J/mm}$ is normal. Our value of $12.75\ \text{J/mm}$ lies in the **acceptable medium range**, predicting good penetration without excessive distortion. *(1 mark setup, 2 marks formula, 1 mark conversion, 1 mark numerical answer, 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit error on $TS$:** Writing $TS = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$ instead of $0.5\ \text{m/min}$. The denominator must be in $\text{mm/min}$, i.e., $TS \times 1000$.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta$:** The heat-input formula *always* includes arc efficiency. Omitting it costs a full mark in most KTU scripts.
> 3. **Confusing seam-finding with touch-sensing:** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Seam-finding = *lateral* feature detection; touch-sensing = *vertical* feature detection.
> 4. **Stability without sign check:** Writing only $K_i \leq (K_p)^2 K_{ta}/4$ without stating $K_p > 0$ and $K_i > 0$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mermaid syntax errors in answers:** If a sketch is asked, drawn diagrams with unlabelled axes also lose marks. Always annotate sensors, signals, and direction of flow.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Welding in behavioral robotics** = layered control: reactive + deliberative + meta.
- **Heat input** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{TS \times 1000}$ — watch units, always include $\eta$.
- **Arc efficiency** $\eta$ depends on process: GMAW 0.80, GTAW 0.60, SAW 0.95.
- **Reactive behaviors:** arc-length regulator, seam tracker, touch-sensing, standoff control.
- **Deliberative behaviors:** path sequencer, heat-input ramping.
- **Meta behaviors:** PLC/MES interface, mode arbitration, fault logging.
- **Through-arc tracking** exploits $I(h) = I_0 + K_{ta} h$ — characteristic ODE:
$$\ddot{h} + K_p K_{ta}\, \dot{h} + K_i K_{ta}\, h = 0$$
- **Stability:** $K_p > 0$, $K_i > 0$, $K_i \leq (K_p)^2 K_{ta}/4$.
- **Seam-finding** = lateral joint detection; **Touch-sensing** = vertical surface detection.
- **Typical GMAW parameters:** $V = 18\text{–}32\ \text{V}$, $I = 80\text{–}350\ \text{A}$, $TS = 0.3\text{–}1.5\ \text{m/min}$.
- **Sensors used:** arc V/I divider, Hall-effect current sensor, laser seam tracker, 6-axis F/T sensor, through-arc sensing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spray Painting and Undersea Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spray Painting and Undersea Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spray Painting Robot — Formal Definition

A **Spray Painting Robot** is a computer-controlled, multi-axis articulated manipulator equipped with a paint atomizer (spray gun) as its end-effector, programmed to traverse a predefined trajectory over a workpiece to deposit a uniform coating of paint, varnish, lacquer, or similar fluid medium. In the context of **Behavioral Robotics**, the spray painting task is treated as a **compliant motion control** problem in which the robot must maintain a constant tool-center-point (TCP) velocity, a fixed standoff distance, and a prescribed spray-gun orientation (often normal to the local surface) while physically interacting with the workpiece environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Spray painting is studied as a classical example of a *continuous-path, surface-tracking, force/compliance* robotic application, where the behavior is governed by trajectory generation algorithms and adaptive end-effector control rather than discrete pick-and-place logic.

**Key Performance Metrics Used in KTU-style Problems:**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Standoff distance | **150 – 250 mm** | $d_s$ |
| TCP speed | **0.5 – 1.5 m/s** | $v_{tcp}$ |
| Spray fan angle | **20° – 60°** | $\theta_f$ |
| Trigger ON/OFF deadband | **± 5 mm** from edge | $\Delta e$ |
| Air pressure | **2 – 4 bar** | $P_a$ |
| Fluid flow rate | **100 – 600 ml/min** | $Q_f$ |

## 1.2 Undersea Robot — Formal Definition

An **Undersea Robot** (commonly known as an **ROV — Remotely Operated Vehicle**, or an **AUV — Autonomous Underwater Vehicle**) is a tethered or untethered robotic platform engineered to operate in fully submerged marine environments. From a behavioral-robotics standpoint, the undersea robot is the canonical test-bed for studying **subsumption architecture**, **reactive behavior layers**, and **sonar-based perceptual feedback**, because the underwater medium (1) absorbs electromagnetic radiation rapidly, (2) introduces high non-linear drag, and (3) makes GPS unavailable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Per KTU Module 3 (Behavioral Robotics framework):**
> - **ROV** — Tethered, human-supervised, behavior is *tele-operated with reactive override* layers.
> - **AUV** — Untethered, fully autonomous, behavior is a *stack of reflexive subsumption layers* (e.g., avoid-obstacle → follow-contour → survey).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain-English Mental Model)

### 🖌️ Spray Painting Analogy — *"The Robotic House-Painter"*
Imagine you hire an expert human painter to spray-coat an entire car body. He doesn't memorize a rigid set of (x, y, z) points — instead, he **watches the surface** as he moves, keeps the gun **perpendicular to the panel at a fixed distance**, overlaps each pass by **~50 %** to avoid streaks, and **pulls the trigger only over the panel** (not the air around it). A spray-painting robot does exactly the same thing, but replaces his eyes with a calibrated CAD model / laser scanner, his hand with a 6-DOF arm, and his brain with a trajectory generator + compliance controller.

### 🌊 Undersea Robot Analogy — *"The Robot Fish with a Brain of Layers"*
Picture a robotic submarine navigating a coral reef in total darkness (because light fades within 10 m of seawater). It cannot rely on cameras or GPS. Instead, it behaves like a fish whose brain is built in **layers of reflexes**: the lowest layer says *"if anything is within 1 m, veer away"*; the middle layer says *"if I detect a current, align with it to save energy"*; the top layer says *"follow the pre-planned survey track"*. Lower layers *override* higher ones when needed — this is precisely **Brooks' Subsumption Architecture** (1986), and the undersea robot is the textbook example used to validate it.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU Puts These Two Topics Together in Module 3:**
> They illustrate the *two extremes* of behavioral robotics — one where the robot must **interact with a structured, near-rigid environment** (spray painting on a car door), and one where the robot must **survive an unstructured, dynamic, hostile environment** (the deep ocean). Together they demonstrate that *behavior* in robotics is fundamentally about **sensor → decision → actuator loops** that adapt to the local situation.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spray Painting Robot — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Kinematic Chain & Tool Configuration

A typical spray-painting cell consists of:

1. **Manipulator (6-DOF articulated arm)** — e.g., FANUC Paint Mate 200iA, ABB IRB 580.
2. **End-effector (spray gun + atomizer + air cap + fluid nozzle).**
3. **Workpiece positioner / rotary table** (often a 7th axis — a 1-DOF turntable).
4. **Controller** running a Continuous-Path (CP) interpolator, not the usual Point-to-Point (PTP) controller used in pick-and-place.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CP vs PTP — The Core Distinction Examiners Love:**
> - *PTP (Point-to-Point)* — used in spot welding, pick-and-place; trajectory between points is not controlled.
> - *CP (Continuous Path)* — used in spray painting, arc welding, gluing; trajectory **between** points is mathematically defined and the TCP must move through every interpolated waypoint at a controlled velocity. This is what makes spray painting a behavioral/continuous-control problem.

### 2.1.2 The Five Behavioral Sub-Tasks of a Spray-Painting Robot

| # | Sub-Task | Behavior Type | Sensor Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Approach** workpiece | PTP rapid move | None (off-line) |
| 2 | **Trigger ON** at edge | Discrete event | Edge-detection via CAD |
| 3 | **Traverse** surface at constant $v_{tcp}$ & $d_s$ | Continuous compliance | Force/torque wrist sensor |
| 4 | **Trigger OFF** at opposite edge | Discrete event | Edge-detection via CAD |
| 5 | **Retract** to next stroke | PTP rapid move | None (off-line) |

The **trigger-ON/OFF deadband** $\Delta e$ ensures the gun sprays only on the panel — this is the simplest "behavior" — a *Bang-Bang controller* on position error.

### 2.1.3 Coating Uniformity — The Overlap Equation

For uniform film thickness, adjacent spray passes must overlap by a fixed percentage. If the **spray fan diameter at the standoff distance** is $D$ and the **centerline spacing** between successive passes is $s$, the **overlap ratio** is:

$$
\text{Overlap} \, (\%) = \left(1 - \frac{s}{D}\right) \times 100
$$

> Typical industrial value: **50 % overlap** ⇒ $s = D/2$.

### 2.1.4 Standoff-Distance Error Compensation

A wrist-mounted **force/torque sensor** measures the small contact force $F_c$ between the gun shroud and the panel (used in *air-blast* or *electrostatic* systems). The compliance controller corrects the standoff distance via:

$$
\Delta d_s(t) = K_p \cdot (F_c(t) - F_{ref}) + K_d \cdot \frac{dF_c}{dt}
$$

where $F_{ref}$ is the desired reference contact force (≈ **0.5 – 2.0 N**) and $K_p$, $K_d$ are the proportional and derivative gains.

### 2.1.5 Paint Flow Rate & Film Thickness

The deposited wet-film thickness $h_w$ (in micrometers) is related to the volumetric flow rate $Q_f$ and the TCP speed $v_{tcp}$ by **mass conservation per unit width of the strip painted**:

$$
h_w = \frac{Q_f}{v_{tcp} \cdot w_{strip}} \times 10^3 \;\; (\mu m)
$$

where $w_{strip}$ is the effective width of one painted strip (≈ $D \cdot \cos\alpha$, $\alpha$ = tilt of gun from surface normal).

## 2.2 Undersea Robot — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 The Marine Environment as a Behavioral Constraint

| Property of seawater | Behavioral consequence for robot design |
|---|---|
| **Electromagnetic absorption** (light fades in ~10 m) | Cannot use cameras beyond very shallow depths → must use **sonar** |
| **No GPS signal** | Must use **inertial navigation (INS)** + **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** + acoustic transponders (LBL/USBL) |
| **High density** ($\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) | Buoyancy becomes a primary control axis |
| **Non-linear drag** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | Speed control is non-trivial; small changes in $v$ cause large changes in drag |
| **Currents (0.1 – 1.5 m/s)** | Robot must *behave against* an external disturbance |
| **Corrosion, pressure** (≈ 1 atm per 10 m depth) | Pressure-rated housings required |

### 2.2.2 Subsumption Architecture Applied to Undersea Robots

Rodney Brooks' **Subsumption Architecture** (1986, MIT AI Lab) was famously validated on undersea robots such as **Allen** (a 3-layer AUV that swam through a tank avoiding sonar-detected obstacles). The architecture organizes behavior into **horizontal layers** of competence:

$$
\mathcal{B} = \{\, \mathcal{L}_0, \mathcal{L}_1, \mathcal{L}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{L}_n \,\}
$$

- Each layer $\mathcal{L}_i$ is an **augmented finite state machine (AFSM)**.
- Higher layers **subsume** (override) the outputs of lower layers via *suppression* and *inhibition* signals.
- There is **no central world model** — each layer reads its own sensors and produces its own actuator commands.

For a typical undersea robot, the layers are:

| Layer | Behavior | Sensor input | Actuator output |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{L}_0$ | **Avoid collision** | Forward-looking sonar | Thruster heading |
| $\mathcal{L}_1$ | **Follow contour / wall** | Side-scan sonar | Lateral thrust |
| $\mathcal{L}_2$ | **Maintain altitude** | Downward-looking altimeter | Vertical thruster |
| $\mathcal{L}_3$ | **Survey track following** | INS + DVL | All thrusters |
| $\mathcal{L}_4$ | **Surface / return-home** | GPS (only at surface) | Buoyancy engine |

### 2.2.3 Sonar Equation (High-Yield KTU Formula)

The **Active Sonar Equation** governs the maximum range at which an undersea robot can detect an obstacle:

$$
\text{SNR} = \text{SL} - 2\text{TL} + \text{TS} - (\text{NL} - \text{DI})
$$

where

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **SL** | Source Level (transmitted intensity) | dB re 1 μPa @ 1 m |
| **TL** | Transmission Loss (one-way) | dB |
| **TS** | Target Strength (reflectivity of obstacle) | dB |
| **NL** | Noise Level (ambient + self-noise) | dB |
| **DI** | Directivity Index of receiving array | dB |
| **SNR** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio at receiver | dB |

For **spreading loss only** (no absorption), $TL = 20\log_{10} R$ (spherical spreading). With absorption coefficient $\alpha$ (dB/km), the more general form is:

$$
TL = 20\log_{10} R + \alpha R
$$

where $R$ is range in km.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Domain | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spray painting | $\text{Overlap}\,\% = (1 - s/D)\times 100$ | $s$ = pass spacing, $D$ = fan diameter (m) | Industrial norm: 50 % |
| 2 | Spray painting | $h_w = \dfrac{Q_f}{v_{tcp}\cdot w_{strip}}\times 10^3$ | $\mu m$ | Wet film thickness |
| 3 | Spray painting | $\Delta d_s = K_p(F_c - F_{ref}) + K_d\,\dot F_c$ | m | Compliance control |
| 4 | Spray painting | $F_c = m_{gun}\,g + F_{air-blast}$ | N | Static balance of gun |
| 5 | Undersea | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | N | Quadratic drag |
| 6 | Undersea | $F_b = \rho V g$ | N | Buoyancy force |
| 7 | Undersea | $\text{SNR} = SL - 2TL + TS - (NL - DI)$ | dB | Active sonar equation |
| 8 | Undersea | $TL = 20\log_{10} R + \alpha R$ | dB, $R$ in km | Transmission loss |
| 9 | Undersea | $P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Pa | Hydrostatic pressure |
| 10 | Undersea | $\tau = I\ddot\theta + b\dot\theta + k\theta$ | N·m | Thruster dynamics (DC motor) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall (Table Pipe-Symbol):** When writing any of these formulas inside an answer booklet, **never** use the ASCII pipe `|` for absolute value or "such-that". Write $|x|$ as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$, and "such that" as $:$ or $\ni$. This avoids ambiguity in valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spray-painting robots** deliver 30–50 % material savings (less overspray), 2× higher first-pass yield, and zero exposure of human workers to isocyanate-laden paints — mandatory in automotive OEM paint shops (Toyota, Tesla, Maruti).
- **Undersea robots** enable offshore oil & gas inspection (subsea Christmas trees),海底光缆 laying, mine countermeasures (NATO naval forces), deep-sea archaeology (Titanic mapping at 3,800 m depth), and polar research (under-ice AUVs beneath Antarctic shelves).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wet-Film Thickness from Paint Flow

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A spray-painting robot deposits paint at a volumetric flow rate of $Q_f = 300 \text{ ml/min}$ while moving the gun at $v_{tcp} = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$ along a strip of width $w_{strip} = 50 \text{ mm}$. Compute the wet-film thickness $h_w$ in micrometers.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify units and convert to SI.**
The paint flow rate is given in ml/min, but SI requires $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$
Q_f = 300 \, \frac{\text{ml}}{\text{min}} = 300 \times \frac{10^{-6} \text{ m}^3}{60 \text{ s}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the strip width in metres.**

$$
w_{strip} = 50 \text{ mm} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.05 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the mass-conservation formula.**

The volume of paint deposited per second over a unit-length strip equals $Q_f$, and this volume must fill a rectangular cross-section of width $w_{strip}$ and thickness $h_w$, swept forward at $v_{tcp}$:

$$
Q_f = h_w \cdot w_{strip} \cdot v_{tcp}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $h_w$.**

$$
h_w = \frac{Q_f}{w_{strip} \cdot v_{tcp}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the SI values.**

$$
h_w = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05 \times 0.8} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.04} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to micrometers (the industry standard unit for coating thickness).**

$$
h_w = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \times 10^6 \, \frac{\mu m}{\text{m}} = 125 \, \mu m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU credit:** A wet-film thickness of $125 \,\mu m$ corresponds to a dry-film thickness (after solvent evaporation) of approximately $50$–$60 \,\mu m$ for a typical 2-component polyurethane (50 % volume solids). This lies in the standard automotive top-coat range of $40$–$60 \,\mu m$, confirming the design is **physically consistent**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spray Pass Overlap

**Problem:** The spray fan at the chosen standoff distance has a diameter $D = 120 \text{ mm}$. To achieve a 50 % overlap, find the centerline spacing $s$ between successive passes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Start from the overlap definition.**

$$
\text{Overlap} \, (\%) = \left(1 - \frac{s}{D}\right) \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical target (50 %).**

$$
50 = \left(1 - \frac{s}{D}\right) \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by 100.**

$$
0.5 = 1 - \frac{s}{D}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $s/D$.**

$$
\frac{s}{D} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $s$ using $D = 120 \text{ mm}$.**

$$
s = 0.5 \times 120 \text{ mm} = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Marking Note:** Students often confuse $D$ (fan diameter) with $R$ (fan radius). For a 50 % overlap you must use the *full diameter*, otherwise the answer is **wrong by a factor of 2**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Active Sonar Range for an Undersea Robot

**Problem:** An ROV emits a sonar ping with source level $SL = 200 \text{ dB re } 1\mu\text{Pa·m}$. The target (a submerged pipeline) has target strength $TS = 10 \text{ dB}$. The ambient noise level is $NL = 70 \text{ dB}$, the receiving array has directivity $DI = 20 \text{ dB}$, and the absorption coefficient in the operating frequency band is $\alpha = 0.04 \text{ dB/km}$. The minimum detectable SNR is $SNR_{min} = 10 \text{ dB}$. Find the maximum detection range $R$ (in km), assuming spherical spreading.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the active sonar equation.**

$$
\text{SNR} = \text{SL} - 2\text{TL} + \text{TS} - (\text{NL} - \text{DI})
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the transmission-loss expression (spherical spreading + absorption).**

$$
\text{TL} = 20\log_{10} R + \alpha R \quad ; \quad R \text{ in km}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute everything into the sonar equation and set SNR = SNR_min.**

$$
10 = 200 - 2(20\log_{10} R + 0.04\,R) + 10 - (70 - 20)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant terms on the right-hand side.**

$$
10 = 200 - 40\log_{10} R - 0.08\,R + 10 - 50
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the constant terms $200 + 10 - 50 = 160$.**

$$
10 = 160 - 40\log_{10} R - 0.08\,R
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the unknowns.**

$$
40\log_{10} R + 0.08\,R = 160 - 10 = 150
$$

**Step 7 — Divide by 40 to get a single log equation in standard form.**

$$
\log_{10} R + 0.002\,R = 3.75
$$

**Step 8 — Solve by iteration (Lambert-W style trial-and-error is acceptable in KTU exam).**

Try $R = 1$ km:
LHS = $\log_{10}(1) + 0.002(1) = 0 + 0.002 = 0.002$ → too small.

Try $R = 1000$ km (nonsensical physically, but shows divergence).
LHS = $3 + 2 = 5$ → too large.

The physically relevant solution lies between 1 km and 10 km. Try $R = 5$ km:
LHS = $\log_{10}(5) + 0.002(5) = 0.699 + 0.010 = 0.709$ → too small.

Try $R = 1000$ km — clearly too large, so solution is between 5 and 1000 km. Try $R = 2000$ km → LHS = $3.301 + 4 = 7.301$ → too large.

The term $0.002\,R$ grows linearly while $\log_{10} R$ grows logarithmically. So the absorption term dominates and pushes the answer down. Let us retry with a more realistic range.

Try $R = 2$ km:
LHS = $\log_{10}(2) + 0.002(2) = 0.301 + 0.004 = 0.305$ → too small.

Try $R = 1000$ km — physically meaningless, suggesting the absorption term must be very small, or the SNR target is too demanding.

**Re-examination of the constant terms:**
The constant 150 has a strong log component. Let $x = \log_{10} R$, so $R = 10^x$. Then:

$$
40x + 0.08 \cdot 10^x = 150
$$

If $x = 3$ ($R = 1000$ km): $40(3) + 0.08(1000) = 120 + 80 = 200$ → too large.
If $x = 2$ ($R = 100$ km): $40(2) + 0.08(100) = 80 + 8 = 88$ → too small.
If $x = 2.5$ ($R = 316$ km): $40(2.5) + 0.08(316) = 100 + 25.3 = 125.3$ → still too small.
If $x = 2.7$ ($R = 501$ km): $40(2.7) + 0.08(501) = 108 + 40.1 = 148.1$ → very close.
If $x = 2.72$ ($R = 525$ km): $40(2.72) + 0.08(525) = 108.8 + 42 = 150.8$ → excellent.

So $R \approx 520$ km. This is **physically absurd** for a typical ROV-mounted sonar — it indicates the chosen $SL$ (200 dB) is enormous (warship/submarine level), and the absorption coefficient is for very low frequency. For a practical ROV (SL ≈ 180 dB, α ≈ 0.5 dB/km at 100 kHz), the detection range collapses to **a few hundred metres**, which is the correct order of magnitude.

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Point:** KTU examiners do **not** require numerically realistic values; they require **the correct algebraic manipulation and dimensional consistency**. The "absurd" result above is acceptable if you clearly state the underlying assumption (very low frequency, very high power). State the limitation in your final answer for full credit.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drag Force on an Undersea Robot

**Problem:** A cylindrical undersea robot of diameter $d = 0.4 \text{ m}$ and length $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$ moves at $v = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$ through seawater ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Take $C_d = 0.8$. Find the drag force.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the frontal (cross-sectional) area.**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the quadratic-drag equation.**

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
F_d = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.8 \times 0.1257 \times (1.2)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute step-wise.**

$$
F_d = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.8 \times 0.1257 \times 1.44
$$

**Step 4a — Multiply $0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4$.**

$$
F_d = 0.4 \times 1025 \times 0.1257 \times 1.44
$$

**Step 4b — Multiply $0.4 \times 1.44 = 0.576$.**

$$
F_d = 0.576 \times 1025 \times 0.1257
$$

**Step 4c — Multiply $0.576 \times 1025 = 590.4$.**

$$
F_d = 590.4 \times 0.1257 = 74.2 \text{ N}
$$

> **Result:** $F_d \approx 74.2 \text{ N}$. This is the thrust the robot must continuously supply to maintain 1.2 m/s — a key parameter for battery sizing.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Behavior Summary

| Behavioral Aspect | Spray-Painting Robot | Undersea Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary environment** | Structured factory floor | Unstructured 3-D marine medium |
| **Pose of end-effector / body** | Fixed TCP orientation, surface-normal | Free 6-DOF body, current-perturbed |
| **Dominant sensor modality** | CAD model + force/torque | Sonar (active/passive) + INS + DVL |
| **Path type** | Continuous path (CP) | Reactive / subsumption-based |
| **Control paradigm** | Impedance / compliance control | Behavior-based layered control |
| **Disturbance** | Panel curvature, tolerance | Currents, waves, drag |
| **Safety mode** | E-stop + software limit on $F_c$ | Auto-surface on low battery |
| **Feedback latency** | ms | 100 ms – 2 s (acoustic) |
| **Failure cost** | Repaint (cheap) | Loss of vehicle (expensive) |
| **KTU Bloom level** | Apply / Analyze | Design / Evaluate |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Spray-Painting Robot Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAM Trajectory Generator] --> B1[CP Interpolator]
    B1 --> C1[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C1 --> D1[Joint Servo Controllers]
    D1 --> E1[6-DOF Articulated Arm]
    E1 --> F1[TCP Spraying Motion]
    F1 --> G1[Workpiece Panel]
    G1 -.Force/Torque Feedback.-> H1[F/T Wrist Sensor]
    H1 --> I1[Compliance Controller]
    I1 --> C1
    A1 --> J1[Edge Detector]
    J1 --> K1[Trigger ON OFF Signal]
    K1 --> E1
    L1[Operator HMI] --> A1
    M1[Paint Flow Regulator] --> F1
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- The **forward path** (top → bottom) generates the spraying motion.
- The **two feedback loops** (dotted arrows) implement the *behavior* of the robot: one for surface-contact compliance, one for trigger activation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Undersea Robot Subsumption Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L4["Layer 4 Surface or Return Home"] --> M1[Behavior Arbitration Bus]
    L3["Layer 3 Survey Track Following"] --> M1
    L2["Layer 2 Maintain Altitude"] --> M1
    L1["Layer 1 Follow Contour"] --> M1
    L0["Layer 0 Avoid Obstacle"] --> M1
    M1 --> THRUSTER[Thruster Command Vector]
    S0[Forward Sonar] --> L0
    S1[Side Scan Sonar] --> L1
    S2[Downward Altimeter] --> L2
    S3[INS plus DVL] --> L3
    S4[Surface GPS] --> L4
    L0 -.Suppression.-> L1
    L1 -.Suppression.-> L2
    L2 -.Suppression.-> L3
    L3 -.Suppression.-> L4
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- All layers **read their own sensors** (left side) — there is **no central world model**.
- All layers **write to the arbitration bus**, which produces the final thruster command.
- The **dotted "Suppression" arrows** show that *lower* (more primitive) layers can override higher ones — this is the essence of **subsumption**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Comparison of Both Robots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPR["Spray Painting Robot"]
        SP1[CAD Model Input] --> SP2[Trajectory Generator]
        SP2 --> SP3[CP Interpolator]
        SP3 --> SP4[Arm Joints]
        SP4 --> SP5[Spray Gun]
    end
    subgraph UND["Undersea Robot"]
        US1[Sonar and INS] --> US2[Behavior Layer Stack]
        US2 --> US3[Thruster Drivers]
        US3 --> US4[Vehicle Body]
    end
    SP5 -.Uniform Paint Film.-> OUT1[Output: Coated Workpiece]
    US4 -.Path Tracking.-> OUT2[Output: Survey Map]
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **left side** of the figure shows that spray painting is *model-driven* (starts from CAD), while the undersea robot is *sensor-driven* (starts from sonar).
- The **right side** contrasts the *output*: a physical coated product vs. an information product (a survey map).
- This is the **fundamental divide** in behavioral robotics: behavior = **f(prior model, sensor stream, goal)** — and the relative weight of each term differs sharply between these two applications.

## 4.4 Schematic — Spray-Painting Cell Layout (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OPER[Human Operator] --> HMI[Paint HMI Terminal]
    HMI --> CTRL[Robot Controller]
    CTRL --> ARM[6-DOF Arm + Spray Gun]
    ARM --> PANEL[Car Body Panel on Turntable]
    PANEL --> FT[F/T Wrist Sensor]
    FT --> CTRL
    TURN[Turntable Drive 7th Axis] --> PANEL
    CTRL --> TURN
    VENT[Ventilation Booth] -.Exhausts Overspray.-> ARM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "7th axis"?** A 1-DOF rotary turntable is mechanically simple but kinematically adds one more axis to the controller's interpolation problem — turning an essentially 5-DOF painting task into a coordinated 6+1 DOF continuous-path problem.
