The robot should interact with the world directly rather than building elaborate internal world models. ### 2.1.2 Layered Decomposition The architecture is organized into **horizontal layers**, each implementing a complete **perception → action** loop. Lower layers handle immediate survival; higher layers add more abstract, goal-directed competence. | **Layer** | **Competence** | **Example Behavior** | |---|---|---| | **Level 0** | Avoid contact with objects (survival) | Force-field obstacle avoidance | | **Level 1** | Wander aimlessly without hitting things | Random walk with collision avoidance | | **Level 2** | Explore the world by noticing places | Path integration, landmark detection | | **Level 3** | Build a map of the environment and plan routes | Topological mapping | | **Level 4** | Notice changes in the environment | Object permanence tracking | | **Level 5** | Reason about goals and identify objects | Symbolic task planning | | **Level 6** | Formulate and execute plans | Multi-step mission execution | | **Level 7** | Reason about other agents' intentions | Social reasoning | ### 2.1.3 Communication Primitives The subsumption architecture uses a strict, minimal set of communication primitives, all unidirectional: - **Suppression (Inhibition):** A higher layer can **completely replace** the output of a lower layer for a specific signal. Used for emergencies. - **Inhibition (Default Inhibition):** A higher layer's signal **blocks** a lower layer's signal from reaching the actuator. Lighter touch than suppression. - **Output Injection / Replacement:** Selective substitution of one signal value with another. > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Point:** Subsumption is a **hard-switching** architecture. There is **no averaging or fuzzy blending** — outputs are either passed through or replaced. This guarantees real-time execution at the cost of behavioral smoothness. ### 2.1.4 Operational Step-by-Step For any robot control cycle at time $t$: **Step 1:** Every layer independently computes its desired actuator command from its sensors. **Step 2:** A signal propagation passes upward from Layer 0 to Layer N. Each higher layer may inject signals into lower layers. **Step 3:** Suppression and inhibition messages modify the signal flow according to the layered priority table. **Step 4:** The final actuator command is produced by the lowest layer (Level 0) after all modifications have been applied. **Step 5:** The cycle repeats at the next sensor sampling instant. ## 2.2 Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987) ### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation Ronald Arkin proposed Motor Schemas as a **biologically inspired**, **mathematically rigorous** alternative to subsumption. Each behavior is a **potential field** distributed across the workspace, and the final motor command is the **vector sum** (or weighted sum) of all active schema outputs. Unlike subsumption's discrete switching, Motor Schemas use **continuous, mathematical fusion**, producing **smooth trajectories** even when multiple behaviors conflict. ### 2.2.2 The Five Canonical Motor Schemas | **Schema** | **Symbol** | **Output** | **Purpose** | |---|---|---|---| | **Move-to-goal** | $\mathbf{V}_{goal}$ | Velocity vector toward target | Goal-directed navigation | | **Avoid-static-obstacle** | $\mathbf{V}_{avoid}$ | Repulsive velocity | Static obstacle avoidance | | **Avoid-moving-obstacle** | $\mathbf{V}_{dyn}$ | Time-to-collision-based repulsion | Dynamic obstacle avoidance | | **Stay-on-path** | $\mathbf{V}_{path}$ | Path-following attractive force | Corridor/path following | | **Noise / Wander** | $\mathbf{V}_{rand}$ | Random walk vector | Exploration | ### 2.2.3 Potential Field Mathematics For a robot at position $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$ with goal at $\mathbf{p}_g$ and obstacle at $\mathbf{p}_o$: **Attractive potential** (pulls robot to goal): $$U_{att}(\mathbf{p}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{att} \cdot \left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_g \right\|^2$$ **Repulsive potential** (pushes robot away from obstacle, only when within influence radius $\rho_0$): $$U_{rep}(\mathbf{p}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{\left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o \right\|} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o \right\| \leq \rho_0 \\[8pt] 0 & \text{if } \left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o \right\| > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$ The total potential is the sum: $$U_{total}(\mathbf{p}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{p}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep,i}(\mathbf{p})$$ The force (negative gradient) gives the motor command: $$\mathbf{V}_{cmd} = -\nabla U_{total}(\mathbf{p}) = -\left( \dfrac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial y} \right)$$ ### 2.2.4 Schema Fusion Equation For $N$ active schemas, each with gain $w_i$ and output $\mathbf{V}_i$: $$\mathbf{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \mathbf{V}_i$$ **KTU-High-Yield Fact:** The gains $w_i$ can be **dynamic** — they may depend on context (e.g., increase $w_{avoid}$ near obstacles, increase $w_{goal}$ when far from obstacles). This is called **gain scheduling** or **adaptive weighting**. ## 2.3 Other Structures (Comparison) ### 2.3.1 ALLIANCE Architecture (Parker, 1996) A multi-robot structure using **motivational behaviors**. Each robot maintains an internal "motivation" level for each task. A robot takes on a task when its motivation exceeds a threshold; otherwise it suppresses itself. Motivation dynamics: $$m_i(t+1) = m_i(t) + \alpha \cdot \text{sensory\_input}_i - \beta \cdot \text{impatience}_i$$ ### 2.3.2 Reactive Action Packages (RAPs, Firby, 1989) A structure that combines **discrete task reasoning** with **reactive behaviors**. Each RAP is a small, finite-state machine that selects appropriate behaviors based on context. ### 2.3.3 Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive (3-Layer Architecture) Combines a **slow deliberative planner**, a **mid-level sequencer**, and a **fast reactive control loop**. Common in modern commercial robotics (e.g., NASA CLARAty, NASREM). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Where Used** | |---|---|---|---| | $U_{att}$ | Attractive potential energy | Joules (or normalized units) | Motor Schemas | | $U_{rep}$ | Repulsive potential energy | Joules (or normalized units) | Motor Schemas | | $k_{att}$ | Attractive gain constant | $\text{N}/\text{m}$ or unitless | Motor Schemas | | $k_{rep}$ | Repulsive gain constant | $\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^2$ or unitless | Motor Schemas | | $\rho_0$ | Obstacle influence radius | meters (m) | Motor Schemas | | $\mathbf{p}$ | Robot position vector $(x, y)$ | meters (m) | All architectures | | $\mathbf{V}_{total}$ | Final motor velocity command | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ | Motor Schemas | | $w_i$ | Behavior blending weight | dimensionless $[0, 1]$ | Motor Schemas | | $m_i(t)$ | Motivation level at time $t$ | unitless | ALLIANCE | | $\alpha, \beta$ | Motivation update rates | unitless | ALLIANCE | | $\left\| \cdot \right\|$ | Euclidean vector norm | unit-dependent | All architectures | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of the Repulsive Potential Field This is a classic **high-frequency KTU Part B question**. We will derive the repulsive force vector from the scalar repulsive potential. ### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption Let the robot's position be $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$ and let an obstacle be located at $\mathbf{p}_o = (x_o, y_o)$. The squared distance between them is: $$d^2 = \left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o \right\|^2 = (x - x_o)^2 + (y - y_o)^2$$ ### 3.1.2 Repulsive Potential Definition (for $d \leq \rho_0$) By convention from Arkin (1987), the repulsive potential is: $$U_{rep}(d) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$$ ### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Repulsive Force (x-component) Using the chain rule, the force in the x-direction is: $$F_x = -\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = -\frac{d U_{rep}}{d d} \cdot \frac{\partial d}{\partial x}$$ **Step A — Compute $\frac{d U_{rep}}{d d}$:** Let $u = \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot u^2$. $$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d d} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot 2u \cdot \frac{d u}{d d} = k_{rep} \cdot u \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{d^2} \right)$$ $$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d d} = -k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^2}$$ **Step B — Compute $\frac{\partial d}{\partial x}$:** Since $d = \sqrt{(x-x_o)^2 + (y-y_o)^2}$: $$\frac{\partial d}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_o}{d}$$ **Step C — Combine:** $$F_x = -\left[ -k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} \right] \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{d}$$ $$\boxed{F_x = k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^3} \cdot (x - x_o)}$$ ### 3.1.4 Symmetric y-component (by symmetry of the problem) $$\boxed{F_y = k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^3} \cdot (y - y_o)}$$ ### 3.1.5 Compact Vector Form $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^3} \cdot (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o)$$ This force points **away from the obstacle** (along $\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o$) and grows rapidly as $d \to 0$ (the $1/d^3$ singularity is exactly what makes the robot "panic" near obstacles). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Trick:** The $1/d^3$ in the numerator of the repulsive force comes from the $1/d^2$ inside the squared potential expansion. Students commonly forget the chain rule inside the chain rule and lose 3 marks. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark Part B):** A robot is at position $\mathbf{p} = (2, 3)$ meters. A goal is at $\mathbf{p}_g = (8, 6)$. A single obstacle is at $\mathbf{p}_o = (4, 3)$ with $\rho_0 = 2$ m. Given $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 5$, compute the total force on the robot. ### 3.2.1 Compute the Attractive Force $$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att} \cdot (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_g)$$ $$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -1 \cdot ((2, 3) - (8, 6)) = -1 \cdot (-6, -3) = (6, 3)$$ $$\left\| \mathbf{F}_{att} \right\| = \sqrt{6^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.708 \text{ N}$$ ### 3.2.2 Compute the Distance to the Obstacle $$d = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (3-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0} = 2 \text{ m}$$ Since $d = 2 \leq \rho_0 = 2$, the repulsive potential is active (boundary case). ### 3.2.3 Compute the Repulsive Force Using the derived formula: $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^3} \cdot (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o)$$ $$\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = 0$$ $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 5 \cdot 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \cdot (2-4, 3-3) = (0, 0)$$ This makes physical sense: at exactly $d = \rho_0$, the obstacle is just at the edge of influence, so it exerts **zero repulsive force**. ### 3.2.4 Total Force $$\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = (6, 3) + (0, 0) = (6, 3)$$ $$\left\| \mathbf{F}_{total} \right\| = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.708 \text{ N}$$ The robot will accelerate at this vector direction. **Result: The robot moves toward the goal at the maximum influence distance from the obstacle.** ## 3.3 Derivation: Subsumption Priority Resolution Table For a 3-layer subsumption architecture (avoid, wander, explore), the resolution follows a strict priority order. For each output signal $S$: $$S_{final} = \begin{cases} S_{avoid} & \text{if } \text{Inhibit}_{avoid} = \text{TRUE} \\ S_{wander} & \text{else if } \text{Inhibit}_{wander} = \text{TRUE} \\ S_{explore} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This is **non-commutative**, in contrast with Motor Schemas which are commutative due to vector addition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Commutativity Difference (FREQUENTLY TESTED):** > - Subsumption: $A \oplus B \neq B \oplus A$ (order matters) > - Motor Schemas: $\mathbf{V}_A + \mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_B + \mathbf{V}_A$ (order does not matter) --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Block Diagram This Mermaid diagram renders the **physical block-level flow** of Brooks' Subsumption Architecture, showing how suppression and inhibition signals flow from higher layers downward to override lower layers. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid the reserved keyword issue. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph S0["Level 0 SURVIVAL LAYER"] A1["Level 0 Module - Avoid Contact"] A2["Force Field Obstacle Detector"] A1 --- A2 end subgraph S1["Level 1 NAVIGATION LAYER"] B1["Level 1 Module - Wander"] B2["Random Walk Generator"] B1 --- B2 end subgraph S2["Level 2 EXPLORATION LAYER"] C1["Level 2 Module - Explore"] C2["Landmark Recognition"] C1 --- C2 end subgraph S3["Level 3 PLANNING LAYER"] D1["Level 3 Module - Map and Plan"] D2["Topological Mapper"] D1 --- D2 end A1 -- Normal Output --> M1["Signal Merger 0"] B1 -- Normal Output --> M2["Signal Merger 1"] C1 -- Normal Output --> M3["Signal Merger 2"] D1 -- Normal Output --> M4["Signal Merger 3"] A2 -- Sensor Data --> A1 B2 -- Sensor Data --> B1 C2 -- Sensor Data --> C1 D2 -- Sensor Data --> D1 D1 -- Suppression Signal --> M1 D1 -- Inhibition Signal --> M2 C1 -- Suppression Signal --> M2 C1 -- Inhibition Signal --> M3 B1 -- Suppression Signal --> M3 B1 -- Inhibition Signal --> M4 M1 -- Final Actuator Command 0 --> ACT["Robot Actuators Wheels Motors Gripper"] M2 -- Final Actuator Command 1 --> ACT M3 -- Final Actuator Command 2 --> ACT M4 -- Final Actuator Command 3 --> ACT ACT -- World Interaction --> ENV["Physical World Sensors and Obstacles"] ENV -- Sensor Feedback --> A2 ENV -- Sensor Feedback --> B2 ENV -- Sensor Feedback --> C2 ENV -- Sensor Feedback --> D2 ``` **Interpretation for the student:** - Sensors in the world feed **all four layers in parallel**. - Each layer computes its desired action independently. - Higher layers send **suppression and inhibition signals DOWN** to override lower layers. - The **signal mergers** (M1, M2, M3, M4) act as AND/OR gates that arbitrate the final command. ## 4.2 Motor Schema Architecture — Potential Field Summation Diagram This Mermaid diagram renders the **vector summation** of motor schemas. Each schema produces a candidate velocity vector; the central summation node computes the final command. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SCH["Active Motor Schemas"] SG["Schema MOVE TO GOAL
V_goal attractive vector"] SA["Schema AVOID STATIC
V_avoid repulsive vector"] SD["Schema AVOID DYNAMIC
V_dynamic repulsive vector"] SP["Schema STAY ON PATH
V_path attractive vector"] end GAIN["Gain Weighting Block
w1, w2, w3, w4 dynamic"] SUM["Vector Summation Node
V_total = SUM of w_i V_i"] NORM["Normalization Stage
V_cmd = V_total divided by max magnitude"] ACT["Robot Actuator Velocity Command"] WORLD["Robot Workspace and Obstacles"] SENSE["Sensors Sonar Lidar Camera"] FB["Feedback Loop"] SG --> GAIN SA --> GAIN SD --> GAIN SP --> GAIN GAIN --> SUM SUM --> NORM NORM --> ACT ACT --> WORLD WORLD --> SENSE SENSE --> FB FB --> SG FB --> SA FB --> SD FB --> SP ``` **Interpretation:** - All schemas run **continuously and in parallel**. - Each schema is multiplied by its current **weight $w_i$**. - The **vector sum** is computed at the central node. - The output is normalized to prevent unbounded speeds. - The robot acts on the world; new sensory data closes the loop. ## 4.3 Comparative Structural Diagram — Subsumption vs Motor Schemas ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["Robot Control Cycle Begins"]) SENSOR["Read Sensors Sonar Lidar Camera IMU"] START --> SENSOR SENSOR --> SSUB["Subsumption Path
Layer 0 to Layer N
Independent Computation"] SENSOR --> SMOT["Motor Schema Path
All Schemas Active in Parallel"] SSUB --> SLAYER["Each Layer Computes Action"] SLAYER --> SUPINH["Apply Suppression and Inhibition
from Higher Layers"] SUPINH --> SFINAL["Final Action from Lowest Active Layer"] SMOT --> SEACH["Each Schema Computes Velocity Vector"] SEACH --> SWEIGHT["Apply Dynamic Weights w_i"] SWEIGHT --> SVEC["Vector Sum V_total"] SVEC --> SNORM["Normalize V_cmd"] SFINAL --> ACTUATOR["Robot Actuators"] SNORM --> ACTUATOR ACTUATOR --> END(["Next Control Cycle"]) ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)] **Define Subsumption Architecture. List its key features.** **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** The Subsumption Architecture, proposed by **Rodney Brooks in 1986**, is a layered, behavior-based control architecture for mobile robots that decomposes robot intelligence into **horizontal layers of competence**. Each layer independently implements a complete perception–action loop, and higher layers can suppress or inhibit the outputs of lower layers to override them when necessary. **Key Features (3 marks broken down):** 1. **Bottom-up layered design** with incremental build-up from survival behaviors to higher reasoning. 2. **Task-level abstraction** without an explicit world model — the world acts as its own model. 3. **Suppression and inhibition** as the sole inter-layer communication primitives; no centralized planner. --- ### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)] **Compare Motor Schema Theory with Subsumption Architecture in terms of behavior fusion.** **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** | **Criterion** | **Subsumption** | **Motor Schemas** | |---|---|---| | **Fusion Mechanism** | Hard switching (suppression/inhibition) | Soft summation (vector addition) | | **Output** | Discrete priority winner | Continuous weighted sum | | **Smoothness** | Can be jerky at switches | Inherently smooth | | **Mathematical Basis** | Finite-state, boolean | Vector fields, potential functions | In Subsumption, only one behavior wins per actuator per cycle (winner-takes-all), while Motor Schemas **sum all active behaviors continuously**, producing smoother trajectories at the cost of higher computational load. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern] **A.** With neat diagrams and equations, explain the **Subsumption Architecture** proposed by Rodney Brooks. Discuss its layered organization, communication primitives, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **B.** Derive the **repulsive force equation** for the Motor Schema obstacle-avoidance behavior. Use it to compute the repulsive force on a robot at position $(3, 4)$ from an obstacle at $(5, 4)$ with $\rho_0 = 3$ m and $k_{rep} = 10$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for A (Subsumption Architecture) — 7 Marks** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** The Subsumption Architecture was introduced by **Rodney Brooks (MIT, 1986)** as an alternative to the classical Sense–Plan–Act paradigm. It decomposes robot control into **parallel, layered competence modules**. **2. Layered Organization (2 marks):** The architecture has **8 layers (Level 0 to Level 7)**, ordered from most basic survival to most abstract reasoning: - Level 0: Avoid contact with objects - Level 1: Wander without collisions - Level 2: Explore and identify places - Level 3: Build maps and plan routes - Level 4: Notice changes in the environment - Level 5: Reason about goals and objects - Level 6: Plan and execute task sequences - Level 7: Reason about other agents **3. Communication Primitives (2 marks):** - **Suppression:** A higher layer's signal completely replaces a lower layer's signal. - **Inhibition:** A higher layer's signal blocks a lower layer's signal from reaching the actuator. - **No shared memory, no global world model, no centralized planner.** **4. Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):** | **Advantages** | **Limitations** | |---|---| | Real-time guaranteed | No global planning, gets stuck | | Robust to sensor noise | Hard to add high-level reasoning | | Incremental development | Jerky transitions between layers | | Low computational cost | Poor for long-horizon tasks | --- #### **Model Solution for B (Repulsive Force Derivation) — 7 Marks** **Given:** $\mathbf{p} = (3, 4)$, $\mathbf{p}_o = (5, 4)$, $\rho_0 = 3$ m, $k_{rep} = 10$. **Step 1: Compute distance (1 mark):** $$d = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + (4-4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0} = 2 \text{ m}$$ Since $d = 2 \leq \rho_0 = 3$, repulsive potential is active. **Step 2: Compute scalar factor (1 mark):** $$\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{3 - 2}{6} = \frac{1}{6}$$ **Step 3: Apply repulsive force equation (2 marks):** $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^3} \cdot (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o)$$ $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 10 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{8} \cdot (3-5, 4-4)$$ $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \frac{10}{48} \cdot (-2, 0) = \frac{5}{24} \cdot (-2, 0) = \left( -\frac{10}{24}, 0 \right) = \left( -\frac{5}{12}, 0 \right)$$ **Step 4: Magnitude and direction (2 marks):** $$\left\| \mathbf{F}_{rep} \right\| = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{5}{12}\right)^2 + 0^2} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.417 \text{ N}$$ The force points in the **negative x-direction**, i.e., **away from the obstacle at $(5, 4)$**, which is physically correct. **Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark):** The robot experiences a repulsive force of approximately **0.417 N** pushing it away from the obstacle along the negative x-axis. --- ### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern] **A.** Explain the **Motor Schema Theory** in detail. Describe the five canonical schemas with their vector equations and the potential field mathematical framework. **(7 marks)** **B.** Discuss the **ALLIANCE architecture** for multi-robot systems. Derive the motivation update equation and explain how robots select tasks based on motivation thresholds. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for A (Motor Schema Theory) — 7 Marks** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** Ronald Arkin (Georgia Tech, 1987) introduced Motor Schema Theory as a **biologically inspired, mathematically rigorous** alternative to subsumption. Behaviors are represented as **potential fields** distributed in the robot's workspace. **2. The Five Canonical Schemas (2 marks):** | **Schema** | **Vector Output** | |---|---| | Move-to-goal | Attractive force toward $\mathbf{p}_g$ | | Avoid-static-obstacle | Repulsive force from $\mathbf{p}_o$ | | Avoid-moving-obstacle | Repulsive force based on time-to-collision | | Stay-on-path | Attractive force toward path corridor | | Noise/Wander | Random exploratory force | **3. Potential Field Equations (2 marks):** **Attractive:** $$U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_g \right\|^2$$ **Repulsive:** $$U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\left\| \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_o \right\|} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 \text{ if } d \leq \rho_0$$ **Total force:** $$\mathbf{F}_{total} = -\nabla U_{total}$$ **4. Schema Fusion (2 marks):** Multiple schemas combine via weighted vector sum: $$\mathbf{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \mathbf{V}_i$$ Gains $w_i \in [0, 1]$ can be **dynamic**, scheduled by context (e.g., higher $w_{avoid}$ near obstacles). --- #### **Model Solution for B (ALLIANCE Architecture) — 7 Marks** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** ALLIANCE, proposed by **Lynne Parker (1996)**, is a multi-robot behavior-based architecture that uses **motivational behaviors** to dynamically allocate tasks among cooperating robots. **2. Motivation Update Equation (2 marks):** For robot $R_k$ and task $T_i$, the motivation level $m_{k,i}$ evolves as: $$m_{k,i}(t+1) = m_{k,i}(t) + \alpha \cdot \text{sensory\_feedback}_i - \beta \cdot \text{impatience}_i$$ where: - $\alpha$ is the **achieving impatience** rate (increases motivation when task is not being accomplished). - $\beta$ is the **suffering impatience** rate (decreases motivation when robot struggles to do the task). **3. Threshold-Based Task Selection (2 marks):** The robot selects task $T_i$ if: $$m_{k,i}(t) > \theta_{i} \quad \text{(threshold)}$$ The robot **suppresses itself** from task $T_i$ when: $$m_{k,i}(t) < \theta_{i} - \epsilon$$ This produces **fault-tolerant** behavior: if a robot fails, its motivation drops, and another robot with higher motivation takes over. **4. Communication and Fault Tolerance (2 marks):** - Robots broadcast their current task and motivation levels. - A robot suppresses itself on detecting another robot performing its task. - This enables **graceful degradation** and **automatic load balancing**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse suppression with inhibition** in subsumption. Suppression replaces the signal entirely; inhibition only blocks. This distinction is worth **2 marks** if asked. > 2. **Always check whether $d \leq \rho_0$** before computing repulsive force. Many students compute $U_{rep}$ for all distances, leading to incorrect results. > 3. **Motor Schemas are commutative (sum) but Subsumption is NOT commutative (priority-based).** This is a **5-mark question** in every KTU paper — practice it well. > 4. **Forgetting the chain rule in $\frac{d U_{rep}}{d d} \cdot \frac{\partial d}{\partial x}$** loses 3 marks instantly. > 5. **In ALLIANCE, motivation updates continuously** — do not treat it as discrete-step only. State the differential form for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Structures** - **Two pillars of the module:** Subsumption (Brooks) and Motor Schemas (Arkin). Know both thoroughly. - **Subsumption uses layered priority + suppression/inhibition.** Motor Schemas use vector fields + summation. - **8 layers of subsumption:** Level 0 (survival) to Level 7 (social reasoning). - **Attractive potential** is parabolic (quadratic in distance to goal). **Repulsive potential** is bounded inverse (only active within $\rho_0$). - **Repulsive force** points away from the obstacle and **grows as $1/d^3$** near the obstacle. - **Schema fusion equation:** $\mathbf{V}_{total} = \sum w_i \mathbf{V}_i$, with weights in $[0, 1]$. - **Commutativity:** Motor Schemas are commutative; Subsumption is not. - **ALLIANCE** uses motivational thresholds ($\theta_i$) for multi-robot task allocation. - **RAPs (Firby)** use finite-state machines for reactive task selection. - **Hybrid architectures** combine deliberative planners with reactive control. - **Local minima problem:** Pure potential field methods can get stuck — this is a classic **5-mark KTU question**. - **Common parameters:** $k_{att}, k_{rep}, \rho_0, w_i$ — always state their units in derivations. - **Real-time guarantee:** Subsumption has hard real-time; Motor Schemas are smooth but may not be hard real-time. > [!NOTE] > **One-Line Mantra for KTU Viva:** > *"Subsumption is a strict parent; Motor Schemas are a democratic committee."* ---

topics.title: VAL Language Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** VAL Language Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is VAL? — Formal Definition

**VAL (Val Assembly Language)** — also marketed by Unimation Inc. as **VAL-II** in its later commercial form — is a high-level, **interpreted, robot-oriented programming language** designed specifically for the control of **Unimation PUMA** (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly) series of industrial robotic manipulators and similar early-generation servo-controlled robots. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT743 – Module 4):**
> VAL is the canonical *behavioral-level robot programming language* in which a programmer *describes the desired task* (motion trajectories, gripper actions, sensor events, logical branching) rather than the low-level joint-torque commands. The compiler/interpreter translates these declarative statements into real-time joint servo set-points for the robot controller (typically a **DEC LSI-11** or **Unimation Controller Mark II** hardware in legacy systems).

VAL is broadly classified as a **point-to-point (PTP) and continuous-path (CP) language** that supports:
- **World-to-Tool** and **Tool-to-World** coordinate transformations.
- **Joint interpolated (MOVE)** and **Straight-line interpolated (MOVES / MOVET)** trajectories.
- **Sequential control flow** with branching, looping, and sub-routine calls.
- **Real-time I/O** integration with binary sensors/actuators.
- **Teach-pendant synchronization** with the on-line program editor.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Chef" Mental Model

Imagine you are teaching a **head chef** how to prepare a recipe in an unfamiliar kitchen:

| Human Chef Analogy | VAL Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You describe the kitchen layout first. | You **define the world coordinate frame** using `SET` and `HERE` commands. |
| You say "lift the spoon, move to the bowl, pour." | You use `MOVE` (joint motion) or `MOVES` (straight-line) commands. |
| You pause when the buzzer rings (sensor). | You use `WAIT` and `SIGNAL` for I/O. |
| You repeat "stir 10 times." | You use `FOR ... ENDFOR` or `WHILE ... DO`. |
| You hand the recipe to the apprentice. | You `STORE` and `EXECUTE` the program. |

In short, **VAL is the "recipe language" you speak to the robot**, and the controller is the "kitchen staff" that obediently executes every line you type, line by line, in the exact sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VAL Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:** Module 4 of PERAT743 tests the student's ability to **write short VAL programs**, **predict trajectory types** (joint vs. straight-line), and **diagnose command syntax errors** that typically appear in 14-mark Part-B questions. Mastery of VAL command semantics is therefore a *high-weight* examination skill.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Architectural Context

- **1975** — Victor Scheinman and Unimation released the original **VAL** for the **PUMA 200/500** series.
- **1983** — **VAL-II** introduced enhanced features: structured programming, math library, and faster interpreter.
- **Controller Platform:** Originally ran on the **DEC LSI-11/03** microprocessor; later the **Mark II** and **Mark III** controllers used Motorola 68000-series processors.
- **Successors:** VAL-II evolved into **Unimate's V+** language, which in turn influenced **Adept's V+, ABB's RAPID, and KUKA's KRL** in spirit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse **VAL** (Unimation) with **AML** (IBM's robot language for the 7565) and **AL** (Stanford's Assembly Language). In KTU answers, *always* state **"VAL / VAL-II by Unimation for PUMA"** in the first line to lock in context marks.

---

## 1.4 VAL Program Structure — A Bird's-Eye View

Every VAL program is a *flat* ASCII-text file consisting of:

```
.PROGRAM  <program_name>          ← Program declaration
   <declarations>                 ← LOCATION, SPEED, etc.
   <executable statements>        ← MOVE, MOVES, SIGNAL ...
.END                              ← End of program marker
```

Program execution is **sequential** by default, and line numbers (e.g., `10`, `20`, `30`) are optional but recommended for branching targets.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology Students MUST Memorize

- **LOCATION** — A named, stored Cartesian or joint configuration (e.g., `HOME`, `PICKUP`).
- **TRANSFORMATION** — A vector defining position and orientation, e.g., `X=400, Y=0, Z=300, O=0, A=90, T=180`.
- **SPEED** — A scalar in the range **\[1, 100\]** (percentage of maximum joint velocity).
- **NULL** — A short pause of approximately **10 ms** for signal settling.
- **COARSE / FINE** — Position-tracking tolerance bands; **FINE** ≈ 0.1 mm, **COARSE** ≈ 5–10 mm.
- **LEFTY / RIGHTY** — Arm-configuration flags for the PUMA's elbow-up/elbow-down solutions.

---

## 1.6 The Six Major Command Categories in VAL

| # | Category | Example Commands | Purpose |
|---|----------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Motion Commands** | `MOVE`, `MOVES`, `MOVET`, `APPRO`, `DEPART`, `DRAW` | Generate robot trajectories |
| 2 | **Hand / Gripper Commands** | `OPENI`, `CLOSI`, `OPEN`, `CLOSE`, `RELAX` | Control end-effector |
| 3 | **Configuration & Speed** | `SPEED`, `COARSE`, `FINE`, `LEFTY`, `RIGHTY`, `UP`, `DOWN`, `INVERSE` | Set motion parameters |
| 4 | **World & Location Definition** | `SET`, `HERE`, `WHERE`, `POINT` | Define coordinate frames |
| 5 | **Program Flow Control** | `GOTO`, `IF ... THEN`, `FOR ... ENDFOR`, `WHILE ... DO`, `REACT` | Branching and looping |
| 6 | **I/O & Synchronization** | `SIGNAL`, `WAIT`, `BITS`, `REACT`, `DELAY`, `PAUSE`, `ABORT` | Sensor and actuator interface |

This taxonomy forms the **mental spine** of every KTU Part-B answer on this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motion Commands — The Heart of VAL

### 2.1.1 `MOVE <location>` — Joint-Interpolated Motion
- The robot **simultaneously moves all six joints** from the current configuration to the target location.
- Each joint accelerates, cruises, and decelerates with its own **trapezoidal velocity profile**.
- The path in **Cartesian space is NOT guaranteed to be straight** — it is the *projection* of synchronized joint motion.
- **Use case:** Free-space gross motion where path accuracy is irrelevant (e.g., "go from home to above the part").

### 2.1.2 `MOVES <location>` — Straight-Line Interpolated Motion
- The end-effector traces a **true geometric straight line** in Cartesian (X, Y, Z) space from current pose to target.
- Orientation is *linearly interpolated* via the equivalent-angle method to avoid quaternion interpolation singularities.
- Internally, the controller solves the **inverse kinematics** at high frequency (typically every 28 ms on legacy hardware).
- **Use case:** Glue dispensing, welding, precise insertion tasks.

### 2.1.3 `MOVET <location>, <delta>` — Straight-Line With Tool Offset
- Performs a straight-line motion while the **tool coordinate frame** remains *aligned* with the world axes.
- `<delta>` is a small offset (X, Y, Z) that the tool tip travels relative to its nominal orientation.
- This is critical for **tool-relative approach maneuvers** in assembly.

### 2.1.4 `APPRO <location>, <distance>` — Approach Command
- Moves the tool tip to a point **`<distance>` mm short of `<location>`** along the approach vector.
- Default approach vector is the **world Z-axis** (Z+).
- **Typical idiom:** `APPRO PART, 50` → stop 50 mm above the part.

### 2.1.5 `DEPART <distance>` — Depart Command
- Moves the tool tip **`<distance>` mm away from the current location** along the world Z-axis.
- Always paired with `APPRO` to "retract" safely before any joint motion.

### 2.1.6 `DRAW <dx>, <dy>, <dz>` — Relative Straight-Line Move
- Relative Cartesian displacement from the **current tool tip position**.
- Note: `DRAW` uses tool-frame coordinates, whereas `MOVET` uses world coordinates.

### 2.1.7 `DRIVE <joint>, <value>, <speed>` — Single-Joint Move
- Moves **only one specified joint** by `<value>` degrees at `<speed>` % of max.
- Used for manual homing, calibration, and joint-limit tests.

---

## 2.2 Hand / Gripper Commands

| Command | Syntax | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `OPENI` | `OPENI` | Open gripper **immediately**, no wait for completion. |
| `CLOSI` | `CLOSI` | Close gripper **immediately** (full stroke). |
| `OPEN` | `OPEN <distance>` | Open gripper to specified aperture (mm). |
| `CLOSE` | `CLOSE <distance>` | Close gripper to specified aperture (mm). |
| `RELAX` | `RELAX` | De-energize gripper motors (part drop). |
| `GRASP` | `GRASP <force>` | Grasp with controlled force (VAL-II only). |

> [!NOTE]
> The **trailing "I"** in `OPENI` and `CLOSI` is the **"Immediate" flag** — the controller does **not** wait for the gripper to *physically* finish moving before advancing to the next line. This is essential in high-speed pick-and-place loops.

---

## 2.3 Program Flow Control — Structured Programming in VAL

### 2.3.1 Branching
```
IF <condition> THEN
    <statements>
ELSE
    <statements>
ENDIF
```

Conditions may use **numeric variables** (e.g., `IF I < 10 THEN`) or **binary I/O bits** (e.g., `IF SIG(101) THEN`).

### 2.3.2 Looping
```
WHILE <condition> DO
    <statements>
ENDWHILE

FOR <var> = <start> TO <end> STEP <step>
    <statements>
ENDFOR
```

### 2.3.3 Unconditional Jump
```
GOTO <label>     ← jumps to a numbered line
```

### 2.3.4 Subroutine
```
CALL <program_name>
```

### 2.3.5 Reactive Control — `REACT` (VAL-II's Crown Jewel)
- Allows **asynchronous, sensor-triggered program flow** without polling.
- Example: `REACT 1, SIG(102), 50` — if digital input 102 goes HIGH, jump to line 50 within 28 ms.
- This is the **direct ancestor of modern robot event handlers**.

---

## 2.4 I/O and Synchronization Commands

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `SIGNAL <port>, <state>` | Set a digital output HIGH or LOW. |
| `WAIT SIG(<port>)` | Block until a digital input becomes TRUE. |
| `BITS <port>, <mask>, <value>` | Manipulate individual bits of an 8-/16-bit port. |
| `DELAY <ms>` | Programmed delay in milliseconds. |
| `PAUSE` | Halt execution; user must type `CONTINUE` to resume. |
| `ABORT` | Kill the running program and return to monitor. |
| `PROCEED` | Resume from a `PAUSE`. |
| `STEP` | Execute **one statement** then pause (debug aid). |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important study artifact** for KTU Part-B questions on VAL. Commit it to memory.

| Concept | Equation / Syntax | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian Transformation | $\mathbf{T} = [x, y, z, \theta, \alpha, \phi]$ | x,y,z in **mm**; angles in **degrees** | VAL uses **Euler Z-Y-Z (roll-pitch-yaw)** convention (O, A, T) |
| Joint-velocity command | $\dot{q}_i = \dfrac{v_{\max,i} \cdot \text{SPEED}}{100}$ | degrees/second | Linear scaling |
| Straight-line distance check | $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ | mm | Used to compute `APPRO` offsets |
| Trapezoidal joint profile — distance per phase | $s_{\text{accel}} = \tfrac{1}{2} a t_a^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{\max} t_a$ | mm | Standard triangular velocity profile |
| Inverse Kinematics solution count (PUMA) | up to **8** solutions (2 shoulder × 2 elbow × 2 wrist) | dimensionless | `LEFTY` / `RIGHTY` selects elbow branch |
| Equivalent angle interpolation | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \dfrac{t}{T} (\theta_f - \theta_0)$ | radians | Used in `MOVES` orientation blend |
| VAL speed range | $\text{SPEED} \in [1, 100]$ | % of rated | Cannot exceed 100 |
| COARSE / FINE tolerance | $\varepsilon_{\text{COARSE}} \approx 5\text{–}10\ \text{mm}$; $\varepsilon_{\text{FINE}} \approx 0.1\ \text{mm}$ | mm | Settling band around target |
| Sampling period (legacy) | $T_s = 28\ \text{ms}$ | ms | DEC LSI-11 controller period |
| Maximum DOF | $n = 6$ (PUMA 560) | joints | Revolute joints only |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why VAL Concepts Survive in Modern Robotics

| Legacy VAL Feature | Modern Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `MOVE` (joint interpolated) | ABB `MoveJ`, KUKA `PTP` |
| `MOVES` (straight-line) | ABB `MoveL`, KUKA `LIN` |
| `APPRO` / `DEPART` | Standard "approach pose" in ROS MoveIt |
| `REACT` (sensor interrupt) | ROS 2 Callbacks, Behavioral Trees in `BehaviorTree.CPP` |
| Teach-pendant waypoints | ROS Movelt "teach mode" |
| LEFTY/RIGHTY config | `ik_solver.set_arm_side()` in MoveIt |
| `SPEED` percentage | `velocity_scaling` factor in ROS |

> [!NOTE]
> This is why KTU examiners love to ask *"Differentiate MOVE and MOVES"* or *"Explain the use of APPRO/DEPART in an assembly task."* — because these concepts are **universal** in industrial robotics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **canonical KTU 2024 Part-B answer components**. The full step-by-step walkthrough is intentional — examiners award partial marks for every intermediate line, so **never skip an algebraic step**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Straight-Line Distance for `APPRO` Command Validation

**Scenario:** The robot must approach a part located at `PICKUP = (X=500, Y=200, Z=150, O=0, A=90, T=0)`. From its current location `CURR = (X=400, Y=200, Z=300, O=0, A=90, T=0)`, the programmer writes `APPRO PICKUP, 75`. Show that this is geometrically consistent.

### Step 1 — Identify the difference vector in world coordinates

$$
\Delta \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{PICKUP}} - \mathbf{p}_{\text{CURR}}
$$

$$
\Delta \mathbf{p} = (500 - 400,\ 200 - 200,\ 150 - 300)
$$

$$
\Delta \mathbf{p} = (100,\ 0,\ -150)\ \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* We subtract the current pose coordinates from the target pose coordinates component-wise, keeping orientation `(O, A, T)` unchanged because `APPRO` uses an *axis-aligned* approach.

### Step 2 — Compute the Euclidean norm of the displacement

$$
d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{100^2 + 0^2 + (-150)^2}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{10{,}000 + 22{,}500}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{32{,}500} \approx 180.28\ \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **total straight-line distance** the end-effector would travel if the programmer had used `MOVES PICKUP` instead of `APPRO PICKUP, 75`.

### Step 3 — Decompose the displacement along the world Z-axis

$$
\Delta z = -150\ \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* The robot must descend by 150 mm in the world Z direction to reach the part surface. The negative sign means the tool moves *downward*.

### Step 4 — Compute the approach-point coordinates

The `APPRO` command moves the tool to a point that is **75 mm above the target along the world Z-axis**. Therefore the new Z-coordinate is:

$$
z_{\text{APPRO}} = z_{\text{PICKUP}} + 75 = 150 + 75 = 225\ \text{mm}
$$

And the X, Y coordinates remain at the target's X, Y:

$$
(x_{\text{APPRO}}, y_{\text{APPRO}}, z_{\text{APPRO}}) = (500, 200, 225)\ \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* `APPRO` always adds the offset to the **target's** coordinates, not to the current coordinates. This is a common student pitfall.

### Step 5 — Verify distance from `CURR` to `APPRO` point

$$
d_{\text{APPRO}} = \sqrt{(500-400)^2 + (200-200)^2 + (225-300)^2}
$$

$$
d_{\text{APPRO}} = \sqrt{10{,}000 + 0 + 5{,}625}
$$

$$
d_{\text{APPRO}} = \sqrt{15{,}625} = 125\ \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* A 125 mm straight-line trajectory is well within the PUMA 560's reachable workspace and reasonable for a standard pick-and-place cycle.

### Step 6 — Conclusion

The command `APPRO PICKUP, 75` moves the end-effector from the current location `CURR` to the point $(500, 200, 225)$ mm using a straight-line interpolated trajectory of length 125 mm. The robot then pauses (waiting for `MOVE PICKUP` or `CLOSI` to complete the actual grasp).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent-Angle Interpolation for `MOVES` Orientation

**Problem:** The end-effector must rotate from an initial orientation $(O_0, A_0, T_0) = (0°, 90°, 0°)$ to a final orientation $(O_f, A_f, T_f) = (45°, 90°, 0°)$ over $T = 2$ seconds. Compute the orientation at $t = 1\ \text{s}$ assuming **linear equivalent-angle interpolation**.

### Step 1 — Convert Euler angles to equivalent axis-angle representation

For small rotations, the equivalent rotation angle about a unit axis $\hat{k} = [k_x, k_y, k_z]$ is:

$$
\theta_{\text{eq}} = 2 \arccos\left(\cos\tfrac{O_f-O_0}{2}\cos\tfrac{A_f-A_0}{2}\cos\tfrac{T_f-T_0}{2} + \sin\tfrac{O_f-O_0}{2}\sin\tfrac{A_f-A_0}{2}\sin\tfrac{T_f-T_0}{2}\right)
$$

*Explanation:* This quaternion-based formula avoids the gimbal-lock singularities of Euler interpolation. VAL's internal `MOVES` planner uses exactly this.

### Step 2 — Simplify for our specific case

Since $A$ and $T$ are constant, only $O$ changes by $\Delta O = 45°$. The equivalent angle reduces to:

$$
\theta_{\text{eq}} = 2 \arcsin\!\left(\left|\sin\tfrac{\Delta O}{2}\right|\right) = 2 \arcsin\!\left(\sin 22.5°\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{eq}} = 45°
$$

*Explanation:* When two of the three Euler angles are constant, the equivalent angle equals the *single varying angle's delta*. This is the simplest case.

### Step 3 — Linear interpolation law

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \dfrac{t}{T}\,\theta_{\text{eq}}
$$

*Explanation:* VAL's interpolator parameterizes the trajectory by *time* (not arc length) when the speed is set as a percentage.

### Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 1\ \text{s}$

$$
\theta(1) = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}\,(45°) = 22.5°
$$

*Explanation:* At the half-way point, the end-effector has rotated by exactly half the total equivalent angle, giving an orientation of $(O, A, T) = (22.5°, 90°, 0°)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics Reachability Test for a `MOVE` Command

**Problem:** A PUMA 560 is commanded to `MOVE REACH = (X=900, Y=0, Z=0, O=0, A=0, T=0)`. Given link lengths $a_2 = 432\ \text{mm}$ (shoulder-to-elbow) and $d_4 = 433\ \text{mm}$ (elbow-to-wrist), determine whether the point is reachable.

### Step 1 — Compute the radial distance from the base to the wrist center

For a point directly along the X-axis at the world origin height:

$$
r = \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2} = 900\ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the reachability inequality

A 6-DOF PUMA can reach a wrist-center point if and only if:

$$
\left\vert a_2 - d_4 \right\vert \le r \le a_2 + d_4
$$

*Explanation:* The reachable workspace is an annulus of inner radius $\vert a_2 - d_4 \vert$ and outer radius $a_2 + d_4$.

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
\vert 432 - 433 \vert = 1\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
432 + 433 = 865\ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare

$$
1\ \text{mm} \le 900\ \text{mm} \le 865\ \text{mm}\ \ \ \ \ ??
$$

The inequality **fails** at the upper bound: $900 > 865$. Therefore the point is **unreachable**.

### Step 5 — Interpretation in VAL context

If this command is executed, the VAL controller will:
1. Compute inverse kinematics for the target.
2. Detect a **kinematic singularity / out-of-reach error** (typical error code: `E-15` "Location out of reach").
3. **Abort the motion** and return a controller error message to the terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners *love* asking "What happens if the target in a `MOVE` command is unreachable?" — your answer should mention both the **kinematic inequality** and the **error code behavior**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing Motion Commands

| Situation | Recommended VAL Command | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Free-space, no obstacles, fastest motion | `MOVE` | Joint-interpolated, minimum cycle time |
| Glue/weld seam, path accuracy critical | `MOVES` | Straight-line path in Cartesian space |
| Tool-tip offset required along an arc | `MOVET` | Tool-frame relative straight-line |
| Approaching a part for grasping | `APPRO loc, d` | Stops short of part to allow fine alignment |
| Retracting from a grasped part | `DEPART d` | Vertical pull-back before free motion |
| Drawing a 2D shape on a flat surface | `DRAW dx, dy, dz` | Tool-frame relative displacements |
| Manual joint calibration / homing | `DRIVE j, θ, v` | Single-joint control |
| Sensor-triggered interrupt | `REACT n, sig, label` | Asynchronous event handling |
| Synchronization with conveyor | `WAIT SIG(port)` | Block until hardware signal |
| Conditional pick / place | `IF ... THEN ... ENDIF` | Sensor-based branching |
| Repetitive pick / place cycles | `WHILE ... ENDWHILE` or `FOR ... ENDFOR` | Loop constructs |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — A Full VAL Pick-and-Place Program

Below is the **canonical KTU 14-mark answer program** for "write a VAL program to pick a part from `BIN` and place it on `CONVEYOR`." Notice how every line is annotated for examination clarity.

```
.PROGRAM  pick_place
  SPEED = 50                 ; Set global speed to 50% of rated
  OPENI                      ; Open gripper immediately
  APPRO BIN, 100             ; Move to a point 100 mm above BIN
  SPEED = 20                 ; Slow down for precise insertion
  MOVE BIN                   ; Descend straight into BIN
  CLOSE                      ; Close gripper with default force
  DEPART 100                 ; Retract 100 mm vertically
  SPEED = 80                 ; Speed up for free motion
  APPRO CONVEYOR, 50         ; Approach 50 mm above CONVEYOR
  MOVE CONVEYOR              ; Lower onto CONVEYOR
  OPENI                      ; Release part
  DEPART 50                  ; Retract
.END
```

### Line-by-line Justification (for the 14-mark answer)

| Line | Marks | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `.PROGRAM pick_place` | 1 | Program declaration. |
| `SPEED = 50` | 1 | Default speed assignment (percentage of rated). |
| `OPENI` | 1 | Immediate gripper open — no waiting, so next motion can start. |
| `APPRO BIN, 100` | 2 | Joint-interpolated motion to a point 100 mm above BIN along world Z+. |
| `SPEED = 20` | 1 | Speed reduction for precise insertion. |
| `MOVE BIN` | 1 | Joint-interpolated descent into BIN. |
| `CLOSE` | 1 | Default-force gripper closure; VAL blocks until motion is complete. |
| `DEPART 100` | 1 | Vertical pull-back from BIN. |
| `SPEED = 80` | 1 | Speed up for free-space motion. |
| `APPRO CONVEYOR, 50` | 2 | Approach CONVEYOR with 50 mm clearance. |
| `MOVE CONVEYOR` | 1 | Descend to place part. |
| `OPENI` | 1 | Release part. |
| `DEPART 50` | 1 | Final retract. |
| `.END` | 1 | Program end marker. |

**Total: 14 marks** — this exact mapping shows why full, annotated programs earn top scores.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **functional architecture** of a VAL program and its **command taxonomy**. These diagrams are *examination-ready* — students may reproduce them verbatim for full marks on 14-mark descriptive questions.

## 4.1 Top-Level VAL Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types EXECUTE program_name] --> B[VAL Monitor loads program]
    B --> C[Interpreter parses line 1]
    C --> D{Command type?}
    D -->|Motion| E[Send joint setpoints to servo loop]
    D -->|Hand| F[Drive gripper actuator]
    D -->|I/O| G[Set/Clear digital ports]
    D -->|Flow| H[Update program counter]
    E --> I[Wait 28 ms for servo cycle]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{More lines?}
    J -->|Yes| C
    J -->|No| K[Program exits cleanly]
    E --> L[REACT handler queue]
    L --> I
```

*Explanation:* The diagram shows the **interpreter loop** that runs every 28 ms on legacy hardware. The `REACT` handler queue is checked *asynchronously* in parallel with the main loop, mimicking the real-time architecture of modern controllers.

## 4.2 VAL Command Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[VAL Command Set] --> M[Motion Commands]
    Root --> H[Hand Commands]
    Root --> C[Config and Speed]
    Root --> W[World Definition]
    Root --> P[Program Flow]
    Root --> IO[I/O and Sync]

    M --> M1[MOVE - joint interp]
    M --> M2[MOVES - straight line]
    M --> M3[MOVET - tool frame]
    M --> M4[APPRO]
    M --> M5[DEPART]
    M --> M6[DRAW]
    M --> M7[DRIVE - single joint]

    H --> H1[OPENI]
    H --> H2[CLOSI]
    H --> H3[OPEN distance]
    H --> H4[CLOSE distance]
    H --> H5[RELAX]

    C --> C1[SPEED n]
    C --> C2[COARSE]
    C --> C3[FINE]
    C --> C4[LEFTY / RIGHTY]
    C --> C5[UP / DOWN]
    C --> C6[INVERSE]

    W --> W1[SET frame]
    W --> W2[HERE]
    W --> W3[WHERE]

    P --> P1[IF THEN ELSE ENDIF]
    P --> P2[WHILE DO ENDWHILE]
    P --> P3[FOR TO STEP ENDFOR]
    P --> P4[GOTO label]
    P --> P5[CALL subprogram]

    IO --> IO1[SIGNAL]
    IO --> IO2[WAIT SIG]
    IO --> IO3[BITS]
    IO --> IO4[DELAY]
    IO --> IO5[REACT]
    IO --> IO6[PAUSE / ABORT / PROCEED]
```

*Explanation:* This taxonomy diagram is the **single most-reproduced** image in KTU Module 4 answers. Memorize the six categories and their member commands.

## 4.3 Robot Motion Path Comparison: `MOVE` vs `MOVES`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Start point A] -->|MOVE: joint-interp| B1[End point B]
    A1 -->|MOVES: straight-line| B2[End point B]
    B1 -.->|Path: arc-like in Cartesian| C1[Path passes over obstacle]
    B2 -.->|Path: pure geometric line| C2[Path collides with obstacle]
```

*Explanation:* The same two endpoints generate *different* Cartesian paths under `MOVE` and `MOVES`. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question: *"When would you choose MOVE over MOVES, and vice versa? Justify with a sketch."*

## 4.4 Pick-and-Place Sequence Diagram (VAL Program Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: HOME] --> S1[APPRO BIN 100]
    S1 --> S2[MOVE BIN]
    S2 --> S3[CLOSE gripper]
    S3 --> S4[DEPART 100]
    S4 --> S5[APPRO CONVEYOR 50]
    S5 --> S6[MOVE CONVEYOR]
    S6 --> S7[OPENI release]
    S7 --> S8[DEPART 50]
    S8 --> S9[Return HOME]
    S9 --> S10{Repeat?}
    S10 -->|Yes| S1
    S10 -->|No| S11[End]
```

*Explanation:* This state diagram is the **canonical picture** students should draw for "Write a VAL program for a pick-and-place task." It demonstrates the APPR–MOVE–DEPART idiom in full cycle.

## 4.5 VAL Controller Hardware Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    User[VAL Program ASCII file] --> Mon[Monitor / Editor]
    Mon --> Interp[Interpreter VALII]
    Interp -->|Motion setpoints| Servo[Servo Loop 28 ms]
    Interp -->|Gripper signals| Hand[Hand Controller]
    Interp -->|Binary I/O| IO[DIO Ports 1 to 16]
    Interp -->|Reactive triggers| ReactQ[REACT Queue]
    ReactQ --> Servo
    Servo --> Amp[PWM Amplifiers]
    Amp --> Motor[DC Servo Motors]
    Motor --> Enc[Encoders]
    Enc --> Servo
    Interp --> Teach[Teach Pendant]
```

*Explanation:* The interpreter runs in *software*, the servo loop runs in *hardware* with a fixed 28 ms period, and the `REACT` queue is a *hardware-interrupt-driven* parallel channel. This is the classical architecture of the Unimation Mark II controller.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test *recall and short comprehension*. Each answer should be **3–4 lines** with a direct definition plus a distinguishing feature.

### **Q1. Differentiate between `MOVE` and `MOVES` commands in VAL.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `MOVE` executes a **joint-interpolated** trajectory: all six joints move simultaneously from current to target configuration, but the *Cartesian path* is not guaranteed to be straight. It is the **fastest** motion type and is used in free-space gross motion.
- `MOVES` executes a **straight-line-interpolated** trajectory in Cartesian (X, Y, Z) space. The end-effector traces a true geometric line, with orientation linearly interpolated. It is used when **path accuracy** is critical (e.g., welding, gluing).
- **Key difference:** `MOVE` = joint space path; `MOVES` = Cartesian straight line.

---

### **Q2. Explain the purpose of the `APPRO` and `DEPART` commands in VAL.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `APPRO location, distance` moves the tool tip to a point **`<distance>` mm short of `<location>`** along the world Z-axis, allowing a safe gross approach without collision.
- `DEPART distance` moves the tool tip **`<distance>` mm away** from the current location along world Z, enabling a safe vertical retract before any joint-interpolated motion.
- Together they form the **canonical APPR–MOVE–DEPART idiom** for pick-and-place programming, separating *coarse approach*, *precise contact*, and *safe retract* phases.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, the Part B question paper offers an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full, step-by-step model solutions are given for **both** alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain in detail the various **motion commands** available in VAL. Compare joint-interpolated and straight-line motion with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of Motion Commands (3 marks):**

VAL supports the following motion commands:

| Command | Motion Type | Coordinate Frame | Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOVE` | Joint-interpolated | Joint space | Joint-synchronized arc |
| `MOVES` | Straight-line | Cartesian world | True geometric line |
| `MOVET` | Straight-line with tool offset | Tool frame | Geometric line + tool frame |
| `APPRO` | Straight-line (axis-aligned) | World | Vertical approach |
| `DEPART` | Straight-line (axis-aligned) | World | Vertical retract |
| `DRAW` | Relative straight-line | Tool frame | Relative geometric line |
| `DRIVE` | Single joint | Joint space | One-joint rotation |

**2. Joint-Interpolated Motion — `MOVE` (2 marks):**
- The controller computes the joint-space trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t) = [q_1(t), q_2(t), \ldots, q_6(t)]$ such that all six joints arrive at the target configuration simultaneously.
- Each joint follows a **trapezoidal velocity profile** with its own $v_{\max,i}$.
- The Cartesian path is the *projection* of this joint motion — it is **not** a straight line.
- **Example:** `MOVE HOME` — fastest motion back to the home configuration.

**3. Straight-Line Motion — `MOVES` (2 marks):**
- The controller solves inverse kinematics at every interpolation cycle (28 ms) to maintain a true geometric straight line in Cartesian space.
- Orientation is interpolated using the **equivalent-angle method** to avoid gimbal lock.
- **Example:** `MOVES WELD_START` — moves the welding tip along a precise straight path.

---

#### (b) Write a **complete VAL program** for a robot to pick a part from location `BIN` and place it on `CONVEYOR`. Explain each line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (annotated program, see Section 3.5 for full line-by-line table):**

```
.PROGRAM  pick_place
  SPEED = 50
  OPENI
  APPRO BIN, 100
  SPEED = 20
  MOVE BIN
  CLOSE
  DEPART 100
  SPEED = 80
  APPRO CONVEYOR, 50
  MOVE CONVEYOR
  OPENI
  DEPART 50
.END
```

**Explanation (7 marks, mapped to lines):**
1. `.PROGRAM` declaration — names the program.
2. `SPEED = 50` — default speed.
3. `OPENI` — open gripper immediately.
4. `APPRO BIN, 100` — gross approach to 100 mm above BIN.
5. `SPEED = 20` — slow down for precision.
6. `MOVE BIN` — descend to grasp.
7. `CLOSE` — close gripper.
8. `DEPART 100` — retract safely.
9. `SPEED = 80` — speed up for free motion.
10. `APPRO CONVEYOR, 50` — approach CONVEYOR.
11. `MOVE CONVEYOR` — descend to place.
12. `OPENI` — release part.
13. `DEPART 50` — final retract.
14. `.END` — termination.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the **program flow control** and **I/O synchronization** commands in VAL with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Program Flow Commands (3.5 marks):**

VAL provides structured programming constructs:

- **Conditional branching:**
```
IF SIG(101) THEN
    MOVE BIN
ELSE
    MOVE REJECT_BIN
ENDIF
```
This checks digital input 101; if HIGH, picks the part, otherwise moves to the reject bin.

- **Looping with WHILE:**
```
WHILE I < 10 DO
    APPRO BIN, 50
    MOVE BIN
    CLOSE
    DEPART 50
    I = I + 1
ENDWHILE
```
Repeats pick operation 10 times.

- **Looping with FOR:**
```
FOR COUNT = 1 TO 5
    SIGNAL 1, ON
    DELAY 1000
    SIGNAL 1, OFF
    DELAY 500
ENDFOR
```
Blinks an output port 5 times with controlled timing.

- **GOTO and CALL:**
```
GOTO 100     ; jump to line 100
CALL cleanup ; execute sub-program 'cleanup'
```

**2. I/O and Synchronization Commands (3.5 marks):**

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `SIGNAL` | Set digital output | `SIGNAL 2, ON` |
| `WAIT` | Block on digital input | `WAIT SIG(101)` |
| `BITS` | Bitwise I/O manipulation | `BITS 1, 7, 1` — set bit 7 of port 1 |
| `DELAY` | Time delay (ms) | `DELAY 500` |
| `REACT` | Asynchronous event trigger | `REACT 1, SIG(102), 200` |
| `PAUSE` | Manual halt | Operator presses pause |
| `PROCEED` | Resume from pause | Operator types `PROCEED` |
| `ABORT` | Kill program | Used in error recovery |

`REACT` is the most powerful — it allows **sensor-interrupt-driven** control without polling, and is the conceptual ancestor of modern **behavior trees** used in ROS 2 and `BehaviorTree.CPP`.

---

#### (b) Explain the **REACT** command in detail. Write a VAL program that uses `REACT` to interrupt a welding seam and divert the robot to a `SAFE` location when a temperature sensor signals an overheat. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. `REACT` Command Syntax and Semantics (3 marks):**

```
REACT <number>, <signal_expression>, <label>
```

- The controller **continuously monitors** the signal expression in the background (every 28 ms servo cycle).
- When the expression becomes TRUE, the current motion is **aborted** and execution jumps to `<label>` within one interpolation cycle.
- Up to 8 `REACT` triggers can be active simultaneously (REACT 1 through REACT 8).
- `REACT <number>, OFF` disables the trigger.

**2. The Welding Interlock Program (4 marks):**

```
.PROGRAM  weld_with_interlock
  REACT 1, SIG(201), 100         ; If temp sensor 201 trips, go to line 100
  SPEED = 60
  APPRO WELD_START, 50
  MOVE WELD_START
  SIGNAL 3, ON                   ; Turn welding torch ON
  FOR I = 1 TO 50
    DRAW 5, 0, 0                 ; Weld a 5 mm segment along X
  ENDFOR
  SIGNAL 3, OFF                  ; Torch OFF
  DEPART 50
  GOTO 200                       ; Skip to end

100  ; OVERHEAT HANDLER LABEL
  SIGNAL 3, OFF                  ; Emergency torch shut-off
  SIGNAL 4, ON                   ; Activate alarm
  MOVE SAFE                      ; Retreat to safe location
  SIGNAL 4, OFF
  REACT 1, OFF                   ; Disarm the REACT trigger

200  ; NORMAL COMPLETION
.END
```

**Explanation:**
- `REACT 1` continuously polls input 201; if the overheat signal goes HIGH, control transfers to line 100.
- The handler **shuts off the torch**, **sounds an alarm**, **retreats to SAFE**, and **disarms** the trigger to prevent re-triggering.
- After recovery, line 200 is reached; if no overheat occurred, `GOTO 200` is executed directly.
- This program demonstrates the **asynchronous, interrupt-driven** capability of VAL-II that modern industrial controllers emulate using PLC scan cycles or ROS 2 callbacks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on VAL questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up `MOVE` and `MOVES`** — stating one when the answer requires the other. Always state the *command type* (joint vs straight line) explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the "I" in `OPENI` / `CLOSI`** — students often write `OPEN` and `CLOSE` (which require timing parameters) when the *immediate* version is correct.
> 3. **Wrong direction in `DEPART`** — it moves *away* from the current location, not toward the target.
> 4. **SPEED range errors** — `SPEED` must be in `[1, 100]`; writing `SPEED = 150` is a syntax error.
> 5. **Omitting the `.END` marker** — a program without `.END` causes the interpreter to read the next program as a continuation.
> 6. **Confusing `APPRO` offset direction** — it adds to the *target's* Z-coordinate, not the *current* Z-coordinate.
> 7. **Forgetting to disable `REACT` triggers** — leaving an active `REACT` after the handler finishes causes spurious re-triggering.
> 8. **Single-line answers for 14-mark questions** — VAL programs must include **speed changes, configuration selection, and full annotations** to score above 10/14.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — VAL Language Commands**

- **VAL** = robot programming language for Unimation **PUMA** manipulators; original in 1975, **VAL-II** in 1983.
- Six command categories: **Motion, Hand, Config/Speed, World, Program Flow, I/O.**
- **`MOVE`** = joint-interpolated; **`MOVES`** = straight-line Cartesian; **`MOVET`** = tool-frame straight-line.
- **`APPRO loc, d`** = approach `<d>` mm above target along world Z; **`DEPART d`** = retract `<d>` mm along world Z.
- **`DRAW dx,dy,dz`** = relative straight-line in tool frame; **`DRIVE j,θ,v`** = single-joint manual move.
- **`OPENI` / `CLOSI`** = immediate gripper actuation (no wait); **`OPEN d` / `CLOSE d`** = aperture-controlled with wait.
- **`SPEED`** in `[1, 100]` %; **`COARSE`** = ~5–10 mm tolerance; **`FINE`** = ~0.1 mm tolerance.
- **`LEFTY` / `RIGHTY`** = elbow configuration flag (PUMA has up to 8 IK solutions).
- **Flow control:** `IF…THEN…ELSE…ENDIF`, `WHILE…DO…ENDWHILE`, `FOR…TO…STEP…ENDFOR`, `GOTO`, `CALL`.
- **`REACT n, sig, label`** = asynchronous, sensor-triggered interrupt (the crown jewel of VAL-II).
- **I/O:** `SIGNAL`, `WAIT`, `BITS`, `DELAY`, `PAUSE`, `PROCEED`, `ABORT`, `STEP`.
- **World definition:** `SET` (frame), `HERE` (capture current), `WHERE` (display).
- **Controller cycle period** = **28 ms** on legacy DEC LSI-11; modern controllers use 1–4 ms.
- **PUMA reachable workspace:** annulus with inner radius $\vert a_2 - d_4 \vert$ and outer radius $a_2 + d_4$.
- **Program structure:** `.PROGRAM name` … `.END` with optional line numbers.
- **Modern descendants:** ABB `MoveJ`/`MoveL` ≈ `MOVE`/`MOVES`; ROS 2 callbacks ≈ `REACT`.

**End of Module 4 — VAL Language Commands Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Motion Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Behavioral Robotics (PERAT743)**, **Motion Control** refers to the systematic generation, coordination, and regulation of actuator commands (joint torques, wheel velocities, end-effector poses) such that a robot physically realizes the trajectories dictated by its behavioral architecture. It is the **lowest computational layer** in the behavior hierarchy — translating abstract, reactive, or deliberative decisions (e.g., "approach the goal," "avoid the obstacle") into **continuous, time-indexed control signals** that move the robot through space. Mathematically, motion control solves the closed-loop problem: $$u(t) = \mathcal{K}\bigl(e(t), \dot{e}(t), \ddot{e}(t), \ldots\bigr)$$ where $u(t)$ is the actuator command, $e(t) = q_{d}(t) - q(t)$ is the tracking error between the **desired state** $q_{d}(t)$ and the **measured state** $q(t)$, and $\mathcal{K}$ is the **control law** (e.g., PID, computed-torque, sliding-mode). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim essence):** > Motion Control encompasses the design of controllers that regulate a robot's position, velocity, acceleration, and force to follow desired trajectories in **joint space** or **Cartesian (operational) space**, while satisfying stability, accuracy, and smoothness constraints imposed by the behavioral task specification. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — Driving a Manual Car on a Curved Highway** > > Imagine you are driving toward a flyover exit. Your **brain's behavioral layer** decides *where* to go (plan: "take the next exit"). Your **eyes** continuously sense the lane markings and the gap from the curb. Your **hands and feet** execute the **motion control**: turning the steering wheel a few degrees, modulating accelerator pressure, lightly tapping the brake. > > - The **behavior** is the high-level goal: *exit the highway*. > - The **trajectory** is the smooth path the car must follow on the road. > - The **controller** is your hands and feet correcting deviations between the car's actual motion and the desired trajectory. > - If a pothole suddenly appears, you instinctively yank the wheel — that is a **feedback response**. > > Now replace *you* with a robot, *brain* with the behavior-based planner, *eyes* with encoders/cameras, and *hands/feet* with motors. That is **motion control in behavioral robotics**. ## 1.3 Why Motion Control Matters in Behavioral Robotics | Behavioral Need | Motion Control Requirement | |---|---| | Reactive obstacle avoidance | Fast, bounded velocity & acceleration changes | | Smooth human-robot interaction | Impedance/admittance control, low jerk | | Goal-directed navigation | Trajectory tracking with global convergence | | Multi-robot coordination | Synchronized motion primitives | | Manipulation & grasping | Hybrid position/force control | ## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Standards) - **Position (m, rad)** — linear or angular displacement - **Velocity (m/s, rad/s)** — first time-derivative of position - **Acceleration (m/s², rad/s²)** — second time-derivative of position - **Jerk (m/s³, rad/s³)** — third time-derivative; governs motion smoothness - **Torque/Force (N·m, N)** — actuator command output - **Sampling period $T_s$ (s)** — discrete controller update interval - **Bandwidth $\omega_b$ (rad/s)** — frequency at which closed-loop gain drops by **−3 dB** - **Steady-state error $e_{ss}$** — residual error as $t \to \infty$ - **Settling time $t_s$ (s)** — time to enter and remain within **2%** of the desired value - **Overshoot $M_p$ (%)** — peak excursion beyond the target # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Motion Control Strategies Motion controllers are organized by **what variable is being regulated** and **where the regulation occurs in the loop**. ### A. By Controlled Variable 1. **Position Control** — regulate $q(t) \to q_d$ 2. **Velocity Control** — regulate $\dot{q}(t) \to \dot{q}_d$ 3. **Force/Torque Control** — regulate $\tau(t) \to \tau_d$ (used in assembly, polishing) 4. **Impedance Control** — regulate the dynamic relationship $F = Z(q - q_d)$, where $Z$ is a desired mechanical impedance (mass-spring-damper) 5. **Hybrid Position/Force Control** — partition task space into position-controlled and force-controlled subspaces (Raibert & Craig, 1981) ### B. By Control Architecture 1. **Open-loop (Feedforward)** — no feedback; used when model is highly accurate 2. **Closed-loop (Feedback)** — uses sensor measurement; robust to disturbances 3. **Feedforward + Feedback** — combines model-based prediction with sensor correction 4. **Computed-Torque Control** — feedback-linearizes nonlinear dynamics 5. **Adaptive Control** — adjusts gains online as plant parameters drift 6. **Sliding-Mode Control** — robust to matched uncertainties ## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Motion Control ### Pillar I — Trajectory Generation A trajectory is a **time-parameterized path**. Two canonical forms: **i. Point-to-Point (PTP):** Only initial and final poses are specified. Joint space interpolation uses polynomials (cubic, quintic). **ii. Continuous Path (CP):** Path is specified through a sequence of via-points; the controller must pass through each one. ### Pillar II — Feedback Law The standard **PID controller** in continuous time: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \, \dot{e}(t)$$ where $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains. ### Pillar III — Actuation & Drive Converts the control signal $u(t)$ into physical motion using DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic actuators, or pneumatic muscles (PAMs/PEAs). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical) | # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tracking error | $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ | rad or m | | 2 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | N·m or N | | 3 | Closed-loop characteristic polynomial (2nd order) | $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$ | Roots determine stability | | 4 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/I}$ | rad/s | | 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{KI}}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Settling time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | s | | 7 | Peak overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | fraction | | 8 | Rise time | $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | s | | 9 | Steady-state error — step input | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | with P-only | | 10 | Cubic polynomial trajectory (joint angle) | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Continuous velocity at endpoints | | 11 | Quintic polynomial trajectory | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Continuous acceleration; smooth jerk | | 12 | Trapezoidal velocity profile | $v(t) = \begin{cases} a t & 0 \le t < t_a \\ v_{max} & t_a \le t < t_d \\ v_{max} - a(t-t_d) & t_d \le t \le T \end{cases}$ | Three phases: accel, cruise, decel | | 13 | Robot dynamics (Euler-Lagrange) | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$ | $M$ inertia, $C$ Coriolis, $G$ gravity, $F$ friction | | 14 | Computed-torque control | $\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Feedback-linearizes the plant | | 15 | Impedance control law | $\tau = J^T(q)\bigl[M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)\bigr]$ | $M_d,B_d,K_d$ desired mass/damping/stiffness | | 16 | Stability (Routh-Hurwitz for 2nd order) | $\omega_n^2 > 0$ and $\zeta > 0$ | Both coefficients must be positive | | 17 | Discrete PID (velocity form) | $\Delta u_k = K_p(e_k-e_{k-1}) + K_i T_s e_k + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1}+e_{k-2})$ | Used in digital controllers | | 18 | Sampling theorem | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Hz; preserve signal information | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Questions on PID tuning ($\zeta$, $\omega_n$, $t_s$, $M_p$) and polynomial trajectory derivation are the most frequently asked. Master the **quintic polynomial** — it has 6 coefficients matching 6 boundary conditions (position, velocity, acceleration at both ends). ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Industrial manipulators** → computed-torque + PID for high-precision assembly. - **Mobile robots (TurtleBot, Roomba)** → differential-drive velocity control. - **Quadrupeds (Spot, ANYmal)** → Model Predictive Control (MPC) layered over whole-body impedance. - **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** → admittance control to translate surgeon hand motion. - **Humanoids** → zero-moment-point (ZMP) based motion control with reactive balance layer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cubic Polynomial Trajectory **Problem.** Compute a smooth joint trajectory $q(t)$ that starts at $q_0$ with velocity $\dot{q}_0$ at $t=0$, and ends at $q_f$ with velocity $\dot{q}_f$ at $t = T$. Assume zero initial and final acceleration. **Step 1 — General polynomial form.** Since we have four boundary conditions, we require a cubic polynomial: $$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$ **Step 2 — First time-derivative (velocity).** $$\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$ **Step 3 — Second time-derivative (acceleration).** $$\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.** $$ \begin{aligned} q(0) = q_0 &\;\Rightarrow\; a_0 = q_0 \\ \dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_0 &\;\Rightarrow\; a_1 = \dot{q}_0 \\ q(T) = q_f &\;\Rightarrow\; a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f \\ \dot{q}(T) = \dot{q}_f &\;\Rightarrow\; a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = \dot{q}_f \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Solve the 2×2 linear system for $a_2$ and $a_3$.** Substituting $a_0$ and $a_1$ into the third equation: $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 + \dot{q}_0 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 &= q_f \\ a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 &= q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T \end{aligned} $$ From the fourth equation: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{q}_0 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 &= \dot{q}_f \\ 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 &= \dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0 \end{aligned} $$ Solve by elimination. Multiply the third equation by 2 and the fourth by $T$: $$ \begin{aligned} 2 a_2 T^2 + 2 a_3 T^3 &= 2(q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T) \\ 2 a_2 T^2 + 3 a_3 T^3 &= T(\dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0) \end{aligned} $$ Subtract the first from the second: $$ \begin{aligned} a_3 T^3 &= T(\dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0) - 2(q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T) \\ a_3 &= \dfrac{T(\dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0) - 2(q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T)}{T^3} \end{aligned} $$ Simplify the numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= T\dot{q}_f - T\dot{q}_0 - 2q_f + 2q_0 + 2T\dot{q}_0 \\ &= T\dot{q}_f + T\dot{q}_0 + 2q_0 - 2q_f \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$a_3 = \dfrac{2(q_0 - q_f) + T(\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)}{T^3}$$ Back-substitute into $a_2 T^2 = (q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T) - a_3 T^3$: $$a_2 = \dfrac{q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 T - a_3 T^3}{T^2}$$ Expanding and simplifying yields the canonical form: $$\boxed{a_2 = \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0) - T(2\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)}{T^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Final compact trajectory equation.** $$q(t) = q_0 + \dot{q}_0\,t + \left[\dfrac{3(q_f-q_0) - T(2\dot{q}_0+\dot{q}_f)}{T^2}\right] t^2 + \left[\dfrac{2(q_0-q_f) + T(\dot{q}_0+\dot{q}_f)}{T^3}\right] t^3$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PID Gains from Second-Order Specification **Problem.** A single-joint robotic link has transfer function (motor + link): $$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(Js + b)}$$ Design a **PD controller** $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$ such that the closed-loop system has natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. **Step 1 — Closed-loop transfer function.** $$T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \dfrac{K(K_p + K_d s)}{Js^2 + bs + K(K_p + K_d s)}$$ **Step 2 — Expand denominator.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= Js^2 + bs + K K_p + K K_d s \\ &= Js^2 + (b + K K_d) s + K K_p \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Normalize and compare with standard form.** Dividing by $J$: $$s^2 + \dfrac{(b + K K_d)}{J} s + \dfrac{K K_p}{J}$$ Compare with standard $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_n^2 &= \dfrac{K K_p}{J} \\ 2\zeta\omega_n &= \dfrac{b + K K_d}{J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $K_p$ and $K_d$.** $$\boxed{K_p = \dfrac{J \omega_n^2}{K}, \qquad K_d = \dfrac{2 J \zeta \omega_n - b}{K}}$$ **Step 5 — Stability condition.** Require $K_d \ge 0$ to guarantee passive derivative action: $$2 J \zeta \omega_n \ge b$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation.** Increasing $\omega_n$ makes the system faster but demands higher gains. Increasing $\zeta$ (toward 1) reduces overshoot but slows response. KTU questions typically give $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$, then ask for $K_p$ and $K_d$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Computed-Torque Control Linearization **Step 1 — Nonlinear robot dynamics.** $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$ **Step 2 — Define the control law.** $$\tau = M(q)\,\alpha + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$ where $\alpha$ is an auxiliary control input. **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = M(q)\,\alpha + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$ Cancelling $C\dot{q}$ and $G(q)$ from both sides: $$M(q)\ddot{q} = M(q)\,\alpha$$ Since $M(q)$ is positive-definite, $M(q)^{-1}$ exists: $$\ddot{q} = \alpha$$ **Step 4 — The nonlinear plant is now a pure double integrator.** Choose a PD law: $$\alpha = \ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$$ Substituting $e = q_d - q$: $$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_p (q_d - q) + K_d (\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})$$ Rearranging: $$\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$ This is a **linear homogeneous second-order ODE**, whose stability is governed entirely by $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$. **Step 5 — Closed-loop poles.** $$s_{1,2} = \dfrac{-K_d \pm \sqrt{K_d^2 - 4 K_p}}{2}$$ For **critical damping**, $K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p}$, giving a repeated real pole at $s = -\sqrt{K_p}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > Computed-torque control is the **gold-standard benchmark** for manipulator motion control and frequently appears in KTU Part B questions. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Controllers | Property | P | PD | PID | Computed-Torque | Impedance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Reduces steady-state error?
