| No (step → $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p}$) | No | **Yes** (eliminates step error) | Yes | Yes | | Handles nonlinearities? | No | No | No | **Yes** | Partial | | Requires dynamics model? | No | No | No | **Yes** | Partial | | Safe for contact tasks? | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Poor | **Excellent** | | Computational cost | Low | Low | Low | **High** | Moderate | | Robust to disturbances? | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Low (without outer loop) | **High** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Motion Control Loop — Block Diagram The figure below illustrates the **canonical feedback motion-control loop** used in behavioral robotics. The behavior layer issues a **desired trajectory** $q_d(t)$; the controller compares it with the **measured state** $q(t)$ from sensors and drives the actuators. ```mermaid flowchart LR beh["Behavior Layer
Subsumption or FSM"] --> trajgen["Trajectory Generator
Cubic / Quintic"] trajgen --> qd["Desired Trajectory qd of t"] qd --> sum1(("Sum: + -")) sens["Sensors
Encoder IMU Camera"] --> q["Measured q of t"] q --> sum1 sum1 --> err["Error e = qd - q"] err --> ctrl["Controller
PID or Computed Torque"] ctrl --> u["Command u = torque or voltage"] u --> plant["Robot Plant
M q double dot + C q dot + G"] plant --> q plant --> phys["Physical World
Motion Force"] phys --> sens style beh fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 style trajgen fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style ctrl fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style plant fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 style sens fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000 style phys fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Computed-Torque Control Architecture The figure decomposes the computed-torque controller into its **inverse-dynamics block** and the **outer linear servo loop**. ```mermaid flowchart TD qd["qd desired"] --> sumA(("+")) q["q measured"] --> sumA sumA --> e["e = qd - q"] qd2["qd dot"] --> sumB(("+")) qdot["q dot measured"] --> sumB sumB --> ed["e dot"] e --> kp["Kp gain"] ed --> kd["Kd gain"] kp --> sumC(("+")) kd --> sumC sumC --> alpha["alpha = qd double dot + Kp e + Kd e dot"] alpha --> invdyn["Inverse Dynamics
M q alpha + C q dot + G"] invdyn --> tau["Torque tau"] tau --> robot["Robot M q double dot + C q dot + G"] robot --> q robot --> qdot style invdyn fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style robot fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 style alpha fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Motion Control Decision Flow — From Behavior to Actuation This **functional architecture flow** maps how a high-level behavioral decision propagates down through the motion control stack to the actuator. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stimulus or Goal
Sensor Event or Plan"] --> B["Behavior Arbitration
Subsumption or Priority"] B --> C["Motion Primitive Selection
approach avoid retreat"] C --> D["Task Space Specification
Cartesian Pose Twist"] D --> E{"Kinematic Layer"} E --> F["Inverse Kinematics
q dot = J inverse x dot"] E --> G["Trajectory Generation
Cubic or Quintic"] F --> H["Joint Space Reference qd"] G --> H H --> I["Closed Loop Controller
PID Computed Torque"] I --> J["Actuator Drivers
PWM Voltage Current"] J --> K["Physical Motion"] K --> L["Sensor Feedback
Encoder IMU Force"] L --> I style A fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000 style B fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style E fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style I fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style J fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 PID Controller — Internal Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR r["Reference r of t"] --> sum(("+ -")) y["Output y of t"] --> sum sum --> e["Error e of t"] e --> P["Proportional Kp"] e --> I["Integral Ki integral"] e --> D["Derivative Kd d by dt"] P --> sumU(("Sum")) I --> sumU D --> sumU sumU --> u["Control u of t"] u --> plant["Plant G of s"] plant --> y style P fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style I fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Differentiate between joint-space and Cartesian-space motion control. Mention one application where each is preferred.** **Model Answer:** In **joint-space motion control**, the reference trajectory $q_d(t)$ is specified directly in the robot's intrinsic joint coordinates, and each joint is independently servoed using its own controller. The mapping to Cartesian coordinates is performed implicitly through forward kinematics only for monitoring. This approach is computationally cheap and avoids singularities inside the joint limits. In **Cartesian-space (operational-space) motion control**, the desired trajectory is specified as a time-indexed pose $X_d(t)$ in the base frame. The controller must compute inverse kinematics continuously to obtain joint references, then servo the joints. This is essential when the task is naturally defined in Cartesian terms. - **Joint-space preferred:** point-to-point moves between waypoints in cluttered cells of a CNC machine. - **Cartesian-space preferred:** straight-line welding or laser-cutting where the tool tip must trace a precise geometric path. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Explain the role of the integral term in a PID motion controller. Why is pure derivative action avoided in practical implementations?** **Model Answer:** The **integral term** $K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau$ accumulates past tracking errors. It guarantees **zero steady-state error** for step disturbances and constant biases (e.g., gravity on a vertical arm), because the integrator grows until the error is fully cancelled. This is critical in motion control where residual offsets corrupt task accuracy. **Pure derivative action is avoided** because: 1. The derivative of a noisy sensor signal amplifies high-frequency noise — it is **not implementable** in continuous form. 2. A sudden set-point change produces an **impulse-like derivative spike** (derivative kick), which can damage actuators. 3. Practically, a filtered derivative $K_d \cdot \dfrac{s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ or a discrete backward-difference is used instead. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme requires **internal choice**: two full questions (A and B) covering the same module area. Either may appear in the question paper. --- ### **Question A — Computed-Torque Motion Control** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **(a)** Derive the computed-torque control law for an $n$-DOF manipulator whose dynamics is given by $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Show that the closed-loop error dynamics reduces to a linear second-order ODE. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A single-joint DC motor driven link has parameters $J = 0.01\,\text{kg·m}^2$, $b = 0.1\,\text{N·m·s/rad}$, $K = 1\,\text{N·m/V}$. Design a PD controller such that the closed-loop response has natural frequency $\omega_n = 10\,\text{rad/s}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.707$. Compute $K_p$, $K_d$, settling time, and percent overshoot. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Write the robot dynamics.** $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$ where $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ is the symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix, $C$ captures Coriolis and centripetal effects, and $G$ is the gravity vector. **Step 2 — Define the computed-torque control law.** $$\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$ where $e = q_d - q$ and $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$. **Step 3 — Substitute the control law into the dynamics.** $$ \begin{aligned} M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) &= M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) \\ M(q)\ddot{q} &= M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\bigr] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $M(q)$ (which is invertible).** $$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_p(q_d - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange as error dynamics.** $$\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} + K_p(q_d - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) = 0$$ $$\boxed{\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0}$$ This is a **linear, decoupled, second-order ODE**. Stability is guaranteed when $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$. With $K_d^2 \ge 4K_p$, the response is critically damped or overdamped. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Computed-torque feedback-linearizes the nonlinear plant, allowing independent tuning of each joint using classical linear control theory — the essence of motion control in behavioral robotics. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $J = 0.01$ kg·m², $b = 0.1$ N·m·s/rad, $K = 1$ N·m/V, $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.707$. **Step 1 — Compute $K_p$.** $$K_p = \dfrac{J \omega_n^2}{K} = \dfrac{0.01 \times 10^2}{1} = \dfrac{0.01 \times 100}{1} = 1.0 \;\text{V/rad}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $K_d$.** $$K_d = \dfrac{2 J \zeta \omega_n - b}{K} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.01 \times 0.707 \times 10 - 0.1}{1}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} K_d &= \dfrac{0.1414 - 0.1}{1} \\ K_d &= 0.0414 \;\text{V·s/rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Settling time (2% criterion).** $$t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.707 \times 10} = \dfrac{4}{7.07} \approx 0.566\;\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Percent overshoot.** $$M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%$$ $$\sqrt{1 - 0.707^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.5} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.707$$ $$M_p = e^{-\pi \times 0.707 / 0.707} = e^{-\pi} = 0.0432 = 4.32\%$$ **Final Results:** $$\boxed{K_p = 1.0,\quad K_d = 0.0414,\quad t_s \approx 0.566\;\text{s},\quad M_p \approx 4.32\%}$$ --- ### **Question B — Trajectory Generation & Impedance Control** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** A robotic joint must move from $q_0 = 0.5$ rad to $q_f = 2.0$ rad in $T = 4$ s, starting and ending at rest ($\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$). Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory $q(t)$ and compute $q$, $\dot{q}$, $\ddot{q}$ at $t = 2$ s. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Explain the principle of **impedance control**. Compare it with pure position control and pure force control. Why is impedance control particularly suited to behavioral robots that physically interact with humans? **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Substitute boundary conditions into the cubic polynomial coefficients.** With $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$, $q_0 = 0.5$, $q_f = 2.0$, $T = 4$: $$a_0 = q_0 = 0.5, \qquad a_1 = \dot{q}_0 = 0$$ $$a_2 = \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0) - T(2\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)}{T^2} = \dfrac{3(2.0 - 0.5) - 4(0)}{16} = \dfrac{4.5}{16} = 0.28125$$ $$a_3 = \dfrac{2(q_0 - q_f) + T(\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)}{T^3} = \dfrac{2(0.5 - 2.0)}{64} = \dfrac{-3.0}{64} = -0.046875$$ **Step 2 — Trajectory equation.** $$q(t) = 0.5 + 0.28125\,t^2 - 0.046875\,t^3$$ **Step 3 — Velocity and acceleration.** $$\dot{q}(t) = 0.5625\,t - 0.140625\,t^2$$ $$\ddot{q}(t) = 0.5625 - 0.28125\,t$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 2$ s.** $$ \begin{aligned} q(2) &= 0.5 + 0.28125 \times 4 - 0.046875 \times 8 \\ &= 0.5 + 1.125 - 0.375 = 1.25 \;\text{rad} \\ \dot{q}(2) &= 0.5625 \times 2 - 0.140625 \times 4 \\ &= 1.125 - 0.5625 = 0.5625 \;\text{rad/s} \\ \ddot{q}(2) &= 0.5625 - 0.28125 \times 2 \\ &= 0.5625 - 0.5625 = 0 \;\text{rad/s}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** At the midpoint $t = 2$ s, the joint is exactly halfway through its **position** (1.25 rad vs. midpoint of 1.25 rad ✓) and the **acceleration is zero**, as expected at the inflection of a symmetric cubic. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Impedance control**, introduced by Neville Hogan (1985), does **not** command either position or force directly. Instead, it regulates the **dynamic relationship** between the deviation from a desired trajectory and the contact force: $$F - F_d = M_d \ddot{x} + B_d \dot{x} + K_d (x - x_d)$$ The robot behaves like a **mass-spring-damper** with tunable inertia $M_d$, damping $B_d$, and stiffness $K_d$. **Comparison:** | Aspect | Position Control | Force Control | Impedance Control | |---|---|---|---| | Regulates | Position $x$ | Force $F$ | Relationship $F \leftrightarrow x$ | | Behavior in free space | Perfect tracking | Unstable (no constraint) | Behaves like position control | | Behavior in rigid contact | Large impact forces | Perfect force tracking | Compliant, force-limited | | Stability with stiff environment | Brittle | Brittle | Robust | | Suitable for human interaction | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | **Why impedance control for behavioral robots interacting with humans:** 1. **Safety** — by adjusting $K_d$ (stiffness) low, the robot yields on contact instead of fighting the human. 2. **Natural dynamics** — the robot can be programmed to feel "heavy" or "light," enabling intuitive physical collaboration (e.g., handing over a cup). 3. **Energy efficiency** — in locomotion, impedance control enables compliant ground contact and energy recycling (spring-loaded actuators). 4. **Compatibility with behavioral architectures** — high-level behaviors specify desired motions; the impedance layer ensures those motions gracefully defer to physical constraints. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Sign error in cubic coefficients** — re-check whether you used $q_0 - q_f$ or $q_f - q_0$. KTU valuation strictly deducts 1 mark for each wrong sign. > 2. **Forgetting units** in $K_p$ and $K_d$ derivations. Always write V/rad and V·s/rad explicitly. > 3. **Mis-stating $M_p$ formula** — students often write $e^{-\pi\zeta}$ instead of $e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$. The denominator matters. > 4. **In computed-torque derivation**, do NOT drop the $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ and $G(q)$ terms on both sides — they must appear on the right-hand side of the control law for cancellation to work. > 5. **Conflating impedance with admittance control.** Impedance maps displacement to force; admittance maps force to displacement. KTU expects this distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Motion control is the lowest layer** of the behavioral hierarchy — it makes the robot *actually move*. - **Tracking error** $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ is the central quantity. - **PID** is the workhorse; its three terms handle present, past, and future errors respectively. - **Cubic polynomials** enforce continuity of position and velocity; **quintic polynomials** additionally enforce acceleration continuity (preferred for smooth, low-jerk motion). - **Trapezoidal velocity profiles** are favored in industrial CNCs for their computational simplicity. - **Computed-torque control** feedback-linearizes nonlinear manipulator dynamics into $n$ independent double integrators. - **Impedance control** regulates the *relationship* between force and motion, making it the safest choice for human-robot interaction. - **Stability of 2nd-order closed loop** requires $\omega_n^2 > 0$ and $\zeta > 0$ — equivalently $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$. - **Settling time** is inversely proportional to $\zeta\omega_n$; **overshoot** increases as $\zeta \to 0$. - **Derivative action** is always filtered in practice to avoid noise amplification and derivative kick. - **Nyquist sampling theorem**: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ for digital controllers. - **Joint-space control** is decoupled and fast; **Cartesian-space control** is task-natural but needs continuous inverse kinematics. - **Behavioral robotics** uses motion control not just for accuracy but also for **compliance, reactivity, and safety** during unexpected interactions. - **Exam-favorite derivations**: cubic polynomial, PD gains from $\zeta$ & $\omega_n$, computed-torque linearization, and PID characteristic equation.

topics.title: Hand Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hand Control

# Hand Control in Behavioral Robotics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hand Control** in behavioral robotics refers to the closed-loop, sensorimotor coordination of a robotic end-effector (gripper, multi-fingered hand, or anthropomorphic manipulator) to achieve stable, purposeful interaction with the environment. In the context of behavior-based robotics, hand control is decomposed into a set of concurrent **reactive behaviors** (e.g., reach, pre-shape, grasp, lift, slip-recovery) coordinated through a behavioral arbitration scheme (subsumption, motor schemas, or fuzzy arbitration) rather than a single monolithic trajectory planner.

The discipline spans four tightly coupled sub-problems:

1. **Kinematic Hand Modeling** — defining the linkage, joint, and tendon architecture.
2. **Grasp Synthesis** — selecting contact points and finger configurations for a target object.
3. **Force/Impedance Regulation** — modulating the contact wrench via position, force, or hybrid control.
4. **Reactive Behavioral Layer** — triggering reflexes (e.g., slip reflex, release reflex) from tactile and proprioceptive feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course treats hand control as a *behavior*, not as a stand-alone manipulator. This means a grasp is the emergent output of multiple competing motor programs arbitrated by the behavioral architecture, not the result of a single pre-computed trajectory.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Waiter" Metaphor

Imagine a waiter holding a wine glass in a crowded, dimly-lit room. The waiter does **not** run a complete Newtonian simulation of the glass's dynamics in their head. Instead, they use a *suite of simple rules*:

- **Reach Rule:** Move your hand toward the glass (coarse position control).
- **Pre-Shape Rule:** Open your fingers *just enough* based on visual size estimate.
- **Curl Rule:** Close fingers until you feel contact (force threshold trigger).
- **Slip-Reflex:** If you feel the glass sliding, tighten immediately (tactile reflex).
- **Lift Rule:** Once a stable grip is confirmed, apply upward force.

This stack of simple, reactive rules — operating in parallel and arbitrated by priority — is exactly what a *behavioral* robotic hand does. No single behavior is "smart," but the *competition and cooperation* among them produces robust, adaptive manipulation.

### Key Physical & Standard Metrics

The following standard performance indices recur in KTU examination questions on hand control:

- **Grasp Stability Margin (GSM):** Ratio of disturbance wrench to friction cone margin.
- **Form Closure:** Geometric condition where contacts immobilize the object (frictionless).
- **Force Closure:** Equilibrium achievable within friction cones at contacts.
- **Dextrous Workspace:** Volume in which a finger tip can reach with full manipulability.
- **Friction Coefficient (μ):** Dimensionless ratio; typical values: **μ ≈ 0.3 – 0.6** for rubber fingertips on glass/metal.
- **Pinch Force Threshold:** Typical minimum ≈ **0.5 N** to **2 N** for stable prehension.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Organization of Hand Control Behaviors

In KTU's behavioral framework, hand control is a *layered* controller. The standard layered architecture (bottom-up) is:

- **Layer 0 (Reflexes):** Slip detection, contact detection, thermal cutoff.
- **Layer 1 (Reactive):** Pre-shape, close, lift, compliant hold.
- **Layer 2 (Tactical):** Grasp-type selection (power vs. precision) based on object affordance.
- **Layer 3 (Strategic):** Task-level goal (e.g., "screw the lid").

Each higher layer can **suppress (subsume)** the output of a lower layer when active.

### 2.2 Grasp Taxonomy (Cutkosky / Napier)

The two classical taxonomic axes are:

**A. Napier's Power vs. Precision Grasp:**

- **Power Grasp:** Object held by flexed fingers + palm; high stability, low dexterity. Used for hammers, knives.
- **Precision Grasp:** Object held by finger pads (pinch); high dexterity, lower force. Used for writing, electronics.

**B. Cutkosky's 16-Grasp Taxonomy:** A hierarchical tree descending from *power* to *precision*, including intermediate grasps (e.g., *hook*, *spherical*, *cylindrical*, *tripod*, *lateral pinch*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A behavior-based hand controller maps detected *object affordances* (size, weight, slipperiness) onto one of these grasp types using a rule-based decision table. This is the *Tactical Layer* of the hand-control behavior stack.

### 2.3 Hand Kinematics — Forward & Inverse

For a multi-fingered hand with **n** fingers and **m** joints per finger, the **Hand Jacobian** $J_h \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times nm}$ maps joint velocities $\dot{q}$ to the object's twist (linear + angular velocity) in the palm frame:

$$v = J_h(q)\,\dot{q}$$

Where $v \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ is the **object twist**, and $q \in \mathbb{R}^{nm}$ is the joint vector.

The inverse velocity problem:

$$\dot{q} = J_h^{+}(q)\,v + (I - J_h^{+}J_h)\,\dot{q}_{0}$$

The second term is the **null-space motion** $N(q)\dot{q}_0$, which is the *internal finger motion* that does NOT move the object — critical for regrasping and manipulation-in-hand.

### 2.4 Force Closure Condition (Mathieu & Salisbury)

A grasp is in **force closure** if there exists a strictly positive set of contact wrenches whose convex hull contains the origin of wrench space. Equivalent test (for $m$ contact points with friction cones $\mathcal{F}_i$):

$$0 \in \text{int}\!\left(\text{conv}\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{m}\mathcal{F}_i\right)\right)$$

For **frictional point contacts** with friction coefficient $\mu$ and inward normal $n_i$, the friction cone is a pyramidal cone of half-angle $\alpha = \arctan(\mu)$.

### 2.5 Impedance Control of the Hand

Impedance control (Hogan, 1985) regulates the *mechanical admittance* between finger position error and contact force:

$$M\,\ddot{x} + B\,\dot{x} + K\,(x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$$

Where $M$, $B$, $K$ are the desired mass, damping, and stiffness matrices. In behavioral robotics, **$K$** is modulated per behavior (stiff during slip, compliant during insertion).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|--------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Object twist $v = J_h(q)\dot{q}$ | Hand Jacobian | $J_h \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times nm}$ |
| 2 | Null-space motion $N(q) = I - J_h^{+}J_h$ | Internal finger motion | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Friction cone half-angle $\alpha = \arctan(\mu)$ | Friction limit | $\alpha \in [16^\circ, 31^\circ]$ |
| 4 | Impedance law $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x-x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$ | Mass-Damper-Spring | $K$ in **N/m**, $B$ in **Ns/m** |
| 5 | Force closure: $0 \in \text{int}(\text{conv}(\mathcal{F}_i))$ | Grasp stability test | Boolean (yes/no) |
| 6 | Slip reflex trigger $\vert F_t / F_n \vert > \mu_s$ | Static friction law | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | Grasp quality $Q = \min_i d_i$, $d_i$ = min distance to friction-cone boundary | Grasp optimality | **N** (force units) |
| 8 | Coulomb friction $\vert F_t \vert \leq \mu \vert F_n \vert$ | Tangential force limit | **N** |
| 9 | Subsumption arbitration: $u = u_{\text{high}} \ \text{if} \ \text{active}, \ \text{else} \ u_{\text{low}}$ | Layer priority | Discrete |
| 10 | Damped least-squares $\dot{q} = J^T(JJ^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} v$ | Robust inverse | $\lambda$ damping factor |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer scripts, always state the **units** alongside any numerical value. Examiners allocate a dedicated mark for correct dimensional analysis.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hand-control algorithms power:
- **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci):** Precision pinch with tremor cancellation.
- **Warehouse automation (Amazon Picking Challenge):** Mixed grasp-type selection in clutter.
- **Prosthetic limbs (DEKA LUKE Arm):** Behavior-switched grips triggered by EMG.
- **Space robotics (Robonaut 2):** Power grasps for EVA tools with 14-DOF hand.
- **Agricultural robotics:** Compliant grip on irregular produce (apples, mushrooms).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Damped Least-Squares Inverse Hand Jacobian

We want $\dot{q}$ such that $J_h \dot{q} = v$, avoiding singularities. Minimizing:

$$\min_{\dot{q}} \frac{1}{2}\,\Vert J_h \dot{q} - v \Vert^{2} + \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\Vert \dot{q} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the cost function.** The first term penalizes tracking error; the second term penalizes large joint velocities (regularization).

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\dot{q}$ and set to zero (first-order optimality):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \dot{q}}\left[\frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T}J_h^{T}J_h\dot{q} - \dot{q}^{T}J_h^{T}v + \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T}\lambda^{2}I\,\dot{q}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Collect the gradient terms:**

$$J_h^{T}J_h\,\dot{q} - J_h^{T}v + \lambda^{2}I\,\dot{q} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $\dot{q}$:**

$$\left(J_h^{T}J_h + \lambda^{2}I\right)\dot{q} = J_h^{T}v$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the Damped Least-Squares (DLS) solution:**

$$\dot{q} = \left(J_h^{T}J_h + \lambda^{2}I\right)^{-1}J_h^{T}v$$

**Step 6 — Apply the matrix-inversion identity** $(A^{-1} + B^{-1})^{-1} = B(A+B)^{-1}A$ (with $A = J_h^{T}J_h$, $B = \lambda^{-2}I$ is messy, so we keep the compact form). An equivalent often-used form is:

$$\dot{q} = J_h^{T}\left(J_h J_h^{T} + \lambda^{2}I\right)^{-1} v$$

**Step 7 — Interpret:** When the manipulability index $\sigma_{\min}(J_h)$ is large, $\lambda$ is small and we recover the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse. Near a singularity, $\lambda$ dominates and $\dot{q}$ remains bounded, preventing wild joint velocities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is the DLS preferred over the pure pseudo-inverse for hand control?"* The model answer: *Because the hand Jacobian loses rank at joint limits and at the edge of the workspace, the pseudo-inverse $\dot{q}=J^{+}v$ diverges. DLS trades off exact tracking for bounded control effort, essential when the hand is in contact with a rigid object.*

### 3.2 Derivation: Force-Closure Sufficient Condition (Liu & Arimoto, 1998)

For a planar grasp with $m=4$ frictionless point contacts at positions $p_i = (x_i, y_i)$, force closure is achieved if and only if the contact normals span all directions, equivalently:

$$\text{rank}\!\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ x_1 & x_2 & x_3 & x_4 \\ y_1 & y_2 & y_3 & y_4 \end{pmatrix} = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \in \text{int}(\text{conv}(p_i))$$

**Step 1 — Construct the grasp matrix** $G \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$ whose columns are $(1, x_i, y_i)^T$. This is a wrench basis for planar wrenches $(f_x, f_y, \tau)$ in 2D.

**Step 2 — A wrench $w = G c$** is generated by a contact-force vector $c = (c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4)$ with $c_i \geq 0$ (frictionless unilateral contact).

**Step 3 — Force closure** requires that for any external disturbance $w_{\text{ext}}$, we can find $c \geq 0$ such that $Gc = -w_{\text{ext}}$. This holds iff the columns of $G$ positively span $\mathbb{R}^{3}$, i.e., $0 \in \text{int}(\text{conv}(\text{columns of }G))$.

**Step 4 — Geometric reading:** The 4 contact points must (i) not be collinear, and (ii) the convex hull of the 4 contact points must contain the center of mass.

**Step 5 — Extension to 3D** uses the 6×m wrench basis with columns $(n_i, p_i \times n_i)^T$.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests this in 2D form. Memorize the *three conditions*: non-collinearity, convex-hull containment, and rank = 3.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior-Triggered Hand Control

| Sensor Input | Trigger Condition | Behavior Activated | Control Law | Output |
|--------------|------------------|--------------------|-------------|--------|
| Vision (size, shape) | Object detected | **Pre-shape** | Open fingers to $q_{\text{pre}}$ | Joint positions |
| Tactile (FSR $> 0.1\,$N) | Contact | **Curl & Stop** | $q \rightarrow q_{\text{closed}}$ until $F>F_{\text{th}}$ | Joint torques |
| Tactile (slip sensor) | $\vert \dot{x}_{tactile} \vert > \delta$ | **Reflex Tighten** | $\tau_{\text{extra}} = K_p (F_{\text{set}} - F)$ | Joint torque |
| Force/torque (wrist) | $F_z > F_{\text{weight}}$ | **Lift** | $v_{ee,z} = v_{\text{lift}}$ | End-effector velocity |
| EMG (prosthetic) | "Open" intent | **Release** | $q \rightarrow q_{\text{open}}$ | Joint positions |
| Object slip detected | Persistent slip | **Regrasp** | Open → pre-shape → re-curl | Sequence |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked to "design a behavior-based controller for a 3-finger hand," students often forget to specify the **arbitration rule** (e.g., subsumption priority, voting, or fuzzy fusion). Always state explicitly which behavior overrides which.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Behavior-Based Hand Control Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSORY INPUT LAYER"]
        cam["Camera RGB-D"]
        tac["Tactile Array FSR"]
        slip["Slip Sensor PVDF"]
        emg["EMG / Intent"]
        ft["Wrist F-T Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph L3["STRATEGIC LAYER - Task Planner"]
        task["Task: Pick-and-Place"]
        graspSel["Grasp Type Selector Power vs Precision"]
    end

    subgraph L2["TACTICAL LAYER - Grasp Synthesis"]
        pre["Pre-shape Behavior"]
        contact["Contact Detection"]
        close["Curl Behavior"]
    end

    subgraph L1["REACTIVE LAYER - Reflexes"]
        slipRef["Slip Reflex"]
        comp["Compliance Modulation"]
        liftB["Lift Behavior"]
    end

    subgraph L0["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        joints["Finger Joint Motors"]
        fingers["3-Finger Hand"]
    end

    cam --> task
    emg --> task
    task --> graspSel
    graspSel --> pre
    tac --> contact
    pre --> close
    contact --> close
    close --> slipRef
    slip --> slipRef
    slipRef --> comp
    comp --> liftB
    ft --> liftB
    liftB --> joints
    joints --> fingers
    fingers -.feedback.-> tac
    fingers -.feedback.-> slip
```

### 4.2 State Machine — Grasp Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Approach
    Approach --> PreShape : Object in workspace
    PreShape --> Contact : Fingers reach aperture
    Contact --> GraspClosed : Force exceeds Fth
    GraspClosed --> Lift : Stable grip confirmed
    Lift --> Hold : Object elevated
    Hold --> Hold : Force within band
    Hold --> Regrasp : Slip detected
    Regrasp --> PreShape : Cycle restart
    Hold --> Transport : Task signal MOVE
    Transport --> Place : Reached destination
    Place --> Release : Open signal
    Release --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Subsumption Arbitration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b0["L0 Slip Reflex"]
    b1["L1 Compliance Mod"]
    b2["L2 Grasp Selection"]
    b3["L3 Task Planner"]
    mux{"Arbiter Priority 3 gt 2 gt 1 gt 0"}
    out["Motor Command tau"]
    b0 --> mux
    b1 --> mux
    b2 --> mux
    b3 --> mux
    mux --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a true subsumption architecture (Brooks, 1986), higher layers can **inject** signals that *inhibit* (⊣) the outputs of lower layers. The arbiter selects the highest-priority active signal. This is the canonical behavioral-robotics hand controller and is a KTU-favorite question.

### 4.4 Force-Closure Wrench Cone Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WC["WRENCH SPACE 6D - Conceptual 2D Slice"]
        origin["Origin 0"]
        c1["Cone from Finger 1"]
        c2["Cone from Finger 2"]
        c3["Cone from Finger 3"]
        c4["Cone from Finger 4"]
        hull["Convex Hull Contains Origin"]
    end
    c1 --> hull
    c2 --> hull
    c3 --> hull
    c4 --> hull
    origin -.inside.-> hull
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a power grasp and a precision grasp. Give one robotic application of each.*

**Model Answer:**
A **power grasp** uses the flexed fingers and palm to clamp an object, providing high holding force with limited dexterity. Example: a robot hand gripping a hammer to drive a nail.

A **precision grasp** uses opposing finger pads (typically thumb-index) to pinch an object, offering high manipulability at lower force levels. Example: a robot hand picking up a coin from a table.

**Key difference:** contact area (palm + fingers vs. fingertips) and force-vs-dexterity trade-off.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a "slip reflex" in a behavior-based robotic hand? What sensor is used to detect it?*

**Model Answer:**
A **slip reflex** is a low-level reactive behavior that, when triggered, immediately increases grip force to prevent an object from falling. It is implemented as a *reflex layer* (Layer 0) with the highest reactivity but lowest intelligence.

**Sensor used:** A **tactile slip sensor**, typically a PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) piezoelectric film or a dynamic tactile array (e.g., SynTouch BioTac) that detects micro-vibrations or shear displacement at the contact interface. The trigger condition is the ratio of tangential to normal force exceeding the static friction coefficient: $\vert F_t / F_n \vert > \mu_s$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice-1]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **subsumption architecture** for a 3-finger robotic hand performing pick-and-place. Identify at least **four behaviors**, their sensors, and their priority order. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **force closure** and **form closure**. Derive the condition for force closure in a 2D planar grasp with four frictionless point contacts. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Subsumption architecture, proposed by Rodney Brooks (1986), organizes robot control as a vertical stack of *augmenting* layers. Each layer is a complete behavior that connects sensors to actuators. Higher layers can subsume (override or inhibit) the outputs of lower layers.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Subsumption Arbitration Diagram in Section 4.3.

**Four Behaviors with Sensors and Priority (4 marks):**

| Priority | Behavior | Sensor | Output |
|----------|----------|--------|--------|
| 0 (lowest) | Wander / Rest | None | Default motor command |
| 1 | Approach | Camera (object detection) | End-effector velocity toward object |
| 2 | Pre-shape & Curl | Vision (size) + Tactile (FSR) | Joint positions |
| 3 (highest) | Slip Reflex | Tactile slip sensor (PVDF) | Extra joint torque |

**Explanation:** When no slip is detected, Layers 1–2 drive the hand to approach, pre-shape, and close around the object. The moment Layer 3's slip sensor detects $\vert F_t \vert > \mu_s F_n$, it *inhibits* the outputs of Layers 1–2 and injects an emergency tightening torque. This guarantees safety and reactivity.

**Conclusion (0 marks but recommended):** The subsumption design avoids a central world model, making the controller robust to sensor noise and unexpected perturbations.

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Form closure:** A grasp is in form closure when the object is completely immobilized by the *geometry* of the contacts, even with **zero friction** and **no applied force**.
- **Force closure:** A grasp is in force closure when, *with friction*, the finger contact forces can generate wrenches that resist any external disturbance.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.2 for the full derivation. The 2D planar grasp with 4 frictionless contacts at $p_i$ is in force closure if and only if:

$$\text{rank}\!\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ x_1 & x_2 & x_3 & x_4 \\ y_1 & y_2 & y_3 & y_4 \end{pmatrix} = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \in \text{int}\!\left(\text{conv}\!\left(\{p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4\}\right)\right)$$

**Geometric interpretation:** the four contact points must be non-collinear, and their convex hull must contain the center of mass.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice-2]*

**(a)** Explain **impedance control** for a robotic finger. State the impedance equation and discuss how the stiffness parameter $K$ is modulated in different phases of a grasp (approach, contact, slip, lift). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **damped least-squares (DLS)** inverse of the hand Jacobian. Why is DLS preferred over the pure Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse near singularities? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Impedance Control Definition (2 marks):**
Impedance control (Hogan, 1985) regulates the *dynamic relationship* between the position error $(x - x_d)$ and the contact force $F_{\text{ext}}$ at the finger tip, rather than tracking either position or force exclusively.

**Impedance Equation (2 marks):**

$$M\,\ddot{x} + B\,\dot{x} + K\,(x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$$

Where $M$ is the desired inertia, $B$ the damping, and $K$ the stiffness of the virtual mass-spring-damper at the fingertip.

**Phase-wise Modulation of $K$ (3 marks):**

| Phase | Desired $K$ | Reason |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| Approach | Low | Soft finger avoids collision damage |
| Initial contact | High | Quickly stabilize position |
| Slip detected | Very high | Stiff grasp prevents object loss |
| Lift | Medium | Compliant enough to absorb arm motion; stiff enough to hold weight |

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.1 for the full derivation. The DLS solution is:

$$\dot{q} = J_h^{T}\!\left(J_h J_h^{T} + \lambda^{2} I\right)^{-1} v$$

**Why DLS is preferred near singularities (2 marks):**
- Near a singularity, $\sigma_{\min}(J_h) \to 0$, so $J_h^{+}$ produces $\Vert \dot{q} \Vert \to \infty$.
- DLS adds the $\lambda^{2}I$ regularizer, which guarantees $\Vert \dot{q} \Vert \leq \Vert v \Vert / \lambda$, bounded.
- This trades off a small tracking error for safe, executable joint velocities — essential when the hand is in contact with a rigid environment.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where KTU students lose 2–3 marks):**
> 1. **No units on stiffness/damping.** Always write $K$ in N/m and $B$ in Ns/m.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction cone.** Force-closure answers that omit the friction coefficient $\mu$ get partial credit only.
> 3. **Confusing form and force closure.** Form = no friction, no force. Force = with friction, forces can balance any wrench.
> 4. **No priority mention in subsumption.** State the priority order explicitly.
> 5. **Pseudo-inverse vs. DLS:** writing $J^{-1}$ instead of $J^{+}$ for non-square Jacobians loses a mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hand control in behavioral robotics** = stack of reactive, parallel behaviors with subsumption arbitration.
- **Two-layer split:** Tactical (grasp selection) + Reactive (reflexes).
- **Grasp taxonomy:** Power (Napier) → 16-grasp tree (Cutkosky).
- **Hand Jacobian** $J_h \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times nm}$ maps joint velocities to object twist.
- **Null-space motion** $N(q) = I - J_h^{+}J_h$ enables in-hand manipulation.
- **Force closure** = convex hull of friction-cone wrenches contains origin.
- **Form closure** = geometric immobilization, no friction needed.
- **Impedance control:** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x-x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$; modulate $K$ per phase.
- **DLS inverse:** $J^T(JJ^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1}$ — bounded near singularities.
- **Slip reflex:** triggered by $\vert F_t / F_n \vert > \mu_s$; uses PVDF/tactile-dynamic sensor.
- **Friction cone half-angle:** $\alpha = \arctan(\mu)$; typical $\mu = 0.3 - 0.6$ for rubber fingertips.
- **Coulomb's law:** $\vert F_t \vert \leq \mu \vert F_n \vert$.
- **Subsumption arbitration:** higher layer $\to$ *inhibits* lower; priority 3 > 2 > 1 > 0.
- **State-machine of grasp:** Approach → Pre-shape → Contact → Closed → Lift → Hold → (Regrasp or Place) → Release.
- **Grasp Quality Metric $Q$** = minimum distance from origin to boundary of wrench-cone convex hull.
- **Real-world users:** Da Vinci (precision), Amazon Picking (mixed), Robonaut 2 (power), DEKA LUKE (prosthetic).
- **Always cite units** in KTU answers: N, N/m, Ns/m, m, s, rad.
- **Always mention sensor + trigger condition** for any behavior you describe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Control in Behavioral Robotics** is the systematic mechanism by which a robot's control architecture selects, prioritizes, arbitrates, sequences, and coordinates the execution of multiple concurrent behaviors in order to produce coherent goal-directed action in a dynamic, uncertain environment. It defines the *executive layer* of the behavioral system that decides **which behavior runs, when, how strongly, and in what combination** with others.

In the context of the **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** and modern behavior-based systems, program control is the supervisory logic that manages the flow of sensor data into behaviors and the resolution of conflicting actuator commands produced by simultaneously active behaviors.

The core responsibilities of program control are:

- **Behavior Selection** — choosing an active behavior subset per cycle.
- **Behavior Coordination** — merging outputs of parallel behaviors.
- **Behavior Arbitration** — resolving conflicts when behaviors compete for the same actuator.
- **Inhibition / Suppression** — temporarily disabling lower-priority behaviors.
- **Sequencing** — chaining behaviors to accomplish composite tasks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Reflexive Brain of an Octopus**
>
> Imagine an octopus walking across a coral reef. Each arm has its own semi-autonomous "mini-brain" that handles local reactions (grip, release, avoid hot object). But the **central brain** of the octopus decides the overall program: *first crawl, then hunt, then hide*. This is exactly what program control does in a behavioral robot — it acts as the central "executive conductor" of an orchestra of independent reflex-like behaviors (the arms), deciding who plays, who pauses, and who takes the lead. The orchestra can still play jazz (reactive) when the conductor (deliberative layer) steps back.

## 1.3 Why Program Control is the Heart of Behavioral Robotics

Traditional (deliberative) AI stacks `Sense → Plan → Act`, which is brittle and slow. **Behavioral robotics** flips this to `Sense → Act`, with many small behaviors running in parallel. The *price of parallelism* is conflict — and that conflict is resolved by **program control**. Without it, a robot pushing forward (avoid-obstacle) and turning (wander) simultaneously would freeze or oscillate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Program Control is the *bridge concept* between the **reactive layer (Module 2/3 — Behaviors)** and the **deliberative/task layer (Module 4 — Architectures)**. Expect exam questions linking it to Subsumption, Motor Schemas, and FSMs.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Program Control Strategies

Program control strategies are classified along a **deliberation–reaction spectrum**:

| # | Strategy | Deliberation | Reaction | Typical Use |
|---|----------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Priority / Subsumption** | Low | Very High | Mobile robots, Roomba |
| 2 | **Finite State Machines (FSM)** | Medium | High | Industrial pick-and-place |
| 3 | **Behavior Fusion / Potential Fields** | Medium | High | Multi-objective navigation |
| 4 | **Sequencer / Scripted Control** | High | Low | Fixed missions, AUV surveys |
| 5 | **AuRA (Autonomous Robot Architecture)** | High | Medium | Hybrid indoor robots |
| 6 | ** DAMN (Distributed Architecture for Mobile Navigation)** | High | High | Multi-behavior voting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Need for Arbitration

In a behavior-based robot with $n$ behaviors $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$, each producing a control vector (e.g., translational velocity $v_i$, rotational velocity $\omega_i$), the **command fusion problem** is:

> Given a set of recommended actuator commands, produce **one** final command.

Mathematically, we have:

$$
\mathbf{u} = f(B_1(s_t), B_2(s_t), \ldots, B_n(s_t))
$$

where $s_t$ is the sensor state at time $t$, $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the final actuator vector, and $f$ is the **arbitration function** that defines the program's control policy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $f$ important?** Two behaviors may both demand a forward velocity of $1.0 \text{ m/s}$ (good), or one demands forward and another demands reverse (conflict). The choice of $f$ determines the robot's character — *aggressive*, *cautious*, *opportunistic*, or *obedient*.

### 2.1.2 The Subsumption Arbitration Policy

In Brooks' Subsumption Architecture, behaviors are organized in **strictly ordered competence levels** $L_1 \supset L_2 \supset \ldots \supset L_k$. A higher layer $L_j$ can:

- **Inhibit** (block) the output of a lower layer $L_i$ via an *Inhibitor* signal.
- **Suppress** (override) the output of a lower layer $L_i$ via a *Suppressor* signal.

Formally, the arbitration function is:

$$
\mathbf{u} = \begin{cases} \mathbf{u}_{L_j}, & \text{if } L_j \text{ is active and } L_j \text{ suppresses } L_i \\ \mathbf{u}_{L_i}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The **layer priority** is a strict total order: $L_k \succ L_{k-1} \succ \ldots \succ L_1$, where $\succ$ denotes "has priority over."

### 2.1.3 Behavior Fusion via Potential Fields

In **Motor Schema theory (Arkin, 1987)**, each behavior $B_i$ produces a **vector field** $\mathbf{V}_i(\mathbf{x})$ in the workspace. The final velocity command is the **vector sum** (or weighted sum):

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbf{V}_i(\mathbf{x})
$$

where $w_i \in [0, 1]$ is a gain weight. To avoid catastrophic cancellation, the result is normalized:

$$
\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}}{\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\|} \cdot v_{\max}
$$

### 2.1.4 Finite State Machine (FSM) Control

An FSM is a 5-tuple $(S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F)$ where:

- $S$ = finite set of states (one per behavior or behavior-group).
- $\Sigma$ = input alphabet (sensor events).
- $\delta: S \times \Sigma \to S$ = transition function.
- $s_0 \in S$ = initial state.
- $F \subseteq S$ = accepting (goal) states.

For program control, $\Sigma$ typically contains events like `OBSTACLE_NEAR`, `GOAL_REACHED`, `BUTTON_PRESSED`, `BATTERY_LOW`. Each state is associated with one active behavior or behavior-set.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---------|------------------|---------|---------------|
| Final control vector | $\mathbf{u} = f(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)$ | Arbitration result | m/s, rad/s |
| Subsumption rule | $L_k \succ L_{k-1} \succ \ldots \succ L_1$ | Strict priority order | dimensionless |
| Behavior fusion (vector sum) | $\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = \sum w_i \mathbf{V}_i$ | Weighted combination | m/s |
| Normalized command | $\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}}{\lVert \mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} \rVert} v_{\max}$ | Unit-magnitude output | m/s |
| FSM transition | $\delta(s, e) = s'$ | Next state on event $e$ | state index |
| Selection probability (probabilistic) | $P(B_i) = \frac{e^{\beta \cdot g_i}}{\sum_j e^{\beta \cdot g_j}}$ | Boltzmann selection | dimensionless |
| Activation (Action Selection) | $a_i(t+1) = a_i(t) + \alpha \cdot (\text{input}_i - \text{decay} \cdot a_i(t))$ | Continuous activation update | dimensionless |
| Inhibit signal | $\text{Inh}_{i \leftarrow j} \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary blocker | boolean |
| Suppress signal | $\text{Sup}_{i \leftarrow j} = \lambda \mathbf{u}_i, \; 0 \le \lambda \le 1$ | Scalar attenuation | dimensionless |
| Control loop frequency | $f_c = 1 / \Delta t$ | Cycle rate | Hz |
| Stability criterion (linear system) | $0 < K_p < 2 \sqrt{K_i}$ (Ziegler-Nichols) | Avoid oscillation | dimensionless |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** of a formula (e.g., "assuming normalized vectors, $w_i \in [0,1]$"). Marks are awarded for context, not just the equation.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application | Program Control Used | Why |
|-------------|----------------------|-----|
| **Autonomous Vacuum (Roomba)** | Subsumption (4 levels) | Robust, cheap, no map needed |
| **Mars Rovers (Sojourner, Spirit)** | Subsumption + FSM | Survives long communication delays |
| **Warehouse AMRs (Kiva/Amazon)** | FSM + Behavior Fusion | Deterministic, auditable |
| **Autonomous Cars (Waymo)** | Hybrid: FSM + ML + Potential Fields | Combines safety rules with planning |
| **Drone Swarms** | Distributed Voting (DAMN) | No single point of failure |
| **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)** | Strict FSM + Watchdog | Zero tolerance for errors |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Behavior Fusion with Normalization

**Problem.** A robot has two simultaneously active behaviors:

- **Avoid-Obstacle** $B_1$: produces $\mathbf{V}_1 = (0.2,\; -0.8)\; \text{m/s}$ (slow, turn left).
- **Go-to-Goal** $B_2$: produces $\mathbf{V}_2 = (0.9,\; 0.1)\; \text{m/s}$ (fast, slight right).

Weights: $w_1 = 0.7$ (high — obstacle is near), $w_2 = 0.4$.
Maximum linear speed: $v_{\max} = 1.0\; \text{m/s}$.

**Find** the final velocity command $\mathbf{u}$.

### Step 1 — Write the generic fusion equation

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbf{V}_i
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = w_1 \mathbf{V}_1 + w_2 \mathbf{V}_2
$$

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = 0.7 \cdot (0.2,\; -0.8) + 0.4 \cdot (0.9,\; 0.1)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute each component separately

**X-component (forward):**

$$
V_{\text{net},x} = 0.7 \times 0.2 + 0.4 \times 0.9 = 0.14 + 0.36 = 0.50\; \text{m/s}
$$

**Y-component (lateral):**

$$
V_{\text{net},y} = 0.7 \times (-0.8) + 0.4 \times 0.1 = -0.56 + 0.04 = -0.52\; \text{m/s}
$$

So, $\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = (0.50,\; -0.52)\; \text{m/s}$.

### Step 4 — Compute the magnitude (norm)

$$
\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = \sqrt{V_{\text{net},x}^{2} + V_{\text{net},y}^{2}}
$$

$$
\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = \sqrt{(0.50)^{2} + (-0.52)^{2}}
$$

$$
\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.2704}
$$

$$
\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = \sqrt{0.5204} \approx 0.7214\; \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Check the saturation condition

Since $\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = 0.7214 < v_{\max} = 1.0$, **no normalization is needed**. The final command is:

$$
\mathbf{u} = (0.50,\; -0.52)\; \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret physically

The robot moves forward at $0.50\; \text{m/s}$ while turning left at $-0.52\; \text{m/s}$ (lateral). The high weight of the avoid-obstacle behavior dominates, biasing the motion away from the obstacle. **This is the essence of behavior fusion** — a soft, continuous, weighted arbitration rather than a hard switch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If $w_1$ were $1.0$ and $w_2$ were $0.0$, the system reduces to pure subsumption (winner-takes-all). If all $w_i = 1.0$, it reduces to democratic vector addition. The weights are the *program control policy*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Subsumption Layer Resolution

**Problem.** A robot has three Subsumption layers:

- $L_0$ = **Wander** (lowest priority).
- $L_1$ = **Avoid-Obstacle** (middle priority).
- $L_2$ = **Explore-Room** (highest priority).

Each layer outputs a translational velocity $v_i$ and an inhibit signal $I_{i}$ to the layer immediately below.

Suppose at $t = 0$:
- $L_0$ recommends $v_0 = 0.8\; \text{m/s}$, $I_0 = 0$ (not active).
- $L_1$ recommends $v_1 = 0.2\; \text{m/s}$, $I_1 = 1$ (active, obstacle within $0.5\;\text{m}$).
- $L_2$ recommends $v_2 = 0.5\; \text{m/s}$, $I_2 = 0$ (room not yet mapped).

**Find** the final velocity $v_{\text{final}}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the highest active layer

Among $\{L_0, L_1, L_2\}$, the highest-priority active layer is $L_1$ (since $I_1 = 1$).

### Step 2 — Apply the subsumption rule

$L_1$ **inhibits** the output of $L_0$. So $v_0$ is discarded.

$L_2$ is not active, so it does not inhibit $L_1$.

### Step 3 — Output the command of the highest active layer

$$
v_{\text{final}} = v_1 = 0.2\; \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with the suppression rule

In the more general "suppress" version (where higher layer can scale lower-layer output by $\lambda$):

$$
v_{\text{final}} = \lambda \cdot v_0 + (1 - \lambda) \cdot v_2, \quad 0 \le \lambda \le 1
$$

If $L_2$ were active with $\lambda = 0.3$ and $L_0$ active:

$$
v_{\text{final}} = 0.3 \times 0.8 + 0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.24 + 0.35 = 0.59\; \text{m/s}
$$

This is **blended subsumption** — softer than binary inhibit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU Long-Answer Questions)

| Dimension | Subsumption | FSM | Motor Schema (Fusion) | DAMN Voting |
|-----------|-------------|-----|----------------------|-------------|
| **Reactivity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Deliberation** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| **Scalability** | ★★ (rigid hierarchy) | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Predictability** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Hard inhibit | State switch | Vector sum | Weighted vote |
| **Failure Recovery** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Implementation Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Best For** | Simple reactive | Sequential tasks | Multi-goal | Multi-robot |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Question (14 marks):** "Compare and contrast three program control strategies with suitable examples." — Use this matrix as your answering template.

---

## 3.4 FSM Transition Table — Worked Example

Consider a **fire-fighting robot** with states: $S = \{ \text{IDLE}, \text{SEARCH}, \text{APPROACH}, \text{EXTINGUISH}, \text{RETURN} \}$.

| Current State | Event ($\Sigma$) | Next State | Active Behavior |
|---------------|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| IDLE | BUTTON_PRESSED | SEARCH | Wander + Scan |
| SEARCH | FLAME_DETECTED | APPROACH | Go-to-Flame |
| APPROACH | WITHIN_1m | EXTINGUISH | Spray + Stop |
| EXTINGUISH | FLAME_EXTINGUISHED | RETURN | Go-to-Home |
| RETURN | AT_HOME | IDLE | Wait |

**Transition function example:**

$$
\delta(\text{SEARCH}, \text{FLAME\_DETECTED}) = \text{APPROACH}
$$

**Properties:**

- **Deterministic** — each $(s, e)$ maps to exactly one $s'$.
- **Total** — every state has a defined response to every event (else undefined behavior).
- **Reachable** — every state is reachable from $s_0 = \text{IDLE}$.

This FSM is **Moore-style** if outputs depend only on state, or **Mealy-style** if outputs depend on (state, event). Program control in robotics typically uses **Mealy** to react faster.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Activation Dynamics (Maes' Spreading Activation)

In **Action Selection theory (Maes, 1990)**, each behavior $B_i$ has an activation $a_i(t)$ updated by:

$$
a_i(t+1) = a_i(t) + \alpha \cdot \left( I_i + \sum_{j \in \text{pre}(i)} w_{ji} \cdot a_j(t) - \sum_{k \in \text{succ}(i)} w_{ik} \cdot a_i(t) \right) - \delta \cdot a_i(t)
$$

where:

- $I_i$ = external input (sensor-driven).
- $\alpha$ = learning/decay rate.
- $w_{ji}$ = weight from predecessor $j$ to $i$.
- $\delta$ = passive decay.
- $\text{pre}(i)$, $\text{succ}(i)$ = predecessor/successor sets.

**Problem:** Let $\alpha = 0.1$, $\delta = 0.05$, $I_i = 0.3$, $w_{ji} = 0.4$, $a_j = 0.6$, $\text{succ}(i)$ empty. Find $a_i(1)$ given $a_i(0) = 0.2$.

**Step 1:** Substitute:

$$
a_i(1) = 0.2 + 0.1 \cdot (0.3 + 0.4 \cdot 0.6 - 0) - 0.05 \cdot 0.2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute inner bracket:

$$
0.3 + 0.24 = 0.54
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $\alpha$:

$$
0.1 \times 0.54 = 0.054
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract decay:

$$
0.2 + 0.054 - 0.01 = 0.244
$$

So $a_i(1) = 0.244$. The behavior is **slowly activating** due to sensor input and predecessor excitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **decay term** $-\delta a_i(t)$ and lose 1 mark.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark base + 1-mark for diagram/example. Concise answers expected.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define program control in behavioral robotics. List any two program control strategies.**

**Model Answer:**

Program control is the mechanism within a robot's control architecture that selects, prioritizes, and coordinates the execution of multiple competing behaviors to produce a single coherent actuator command in real time.

Two strategies:

1. **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks)** — strict priority-based inhibition/suppression of lower layers by higher layers.
2. **Finite State Machine (FSM)** — discrete states representing behaviors, with transitions triggered by sensor events.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between inhibition and suppression in the Subsumption Architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inhibition | Suppression |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Signal type | Binary (0 or 1) | Scalar ($\lambda \in [0,1]$) |
| Effect on lower layer | Completely blocks output | Scales output by factor $\lambda$ |
| Implementation | 1-bit wire | Multi-bit bus / multiplier |
| Use case | Hard safety override | Soft blending of behaviors |

In inhibition, the lower layer's output is *replaced*. In suppression, it is *attenuated* and then blended with the higher layer's output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Subsumption Architecture** proposed by Rodney Brooks. Discuss the role of *inhibitors* and *suppressors* in program control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a three-layer subsumption controller for a **floor-cleaning robot** with layers for `WANDER → AVOID-OBSTACLE → RETURN-TO-DOCK`. Show the behavior of the robot when (i) it encounters a wall, (ii) the battery falls below 20%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Subsumption Architecture**

The **Subsumption Architecture**, introduced by **Rodney Brooks (1986)** at MIT, decomposes a robot's intelligence into **layers of task-achieving behaviors**, organized in a strict vertical hierarchy. Each layer is a **finite state machine** augmented with real-time input and output ports.

**Levels (typical for a mobile robot):**

| Level | Behavior | Purpose |
|-------|----------|---------|
| 0 | Wander | Random exploration |
| 1 | Avoid-Obstacle | Reactive safety |
| 2 | Explore-Room | Wall-following |
| 3 | Build-Map | SLAM |
| 4 | Plan-Route | A* path planning |
| 5 | Identify-Objects | Vision-based reasoning |
| 6 | Reason-About-World | High-level deliberation |

**Communication mechanisms:**

- **Inhibitor Signal** $I_{j \to i}$: A binary signal from layer $j$ to layer $i$ that, when asserted, **completely blocks** the output of $i$ (replaces it with a default or zero vector).
- **Suppressor Signal** $S_{j \to i}$: A scalar $\lambda$ that **scales** the output of $i$ before adding the higher layer's own output.

**Mathematical model:**

$$
\mathbf{u}_i^{\text{eff}} = (1 - I_{j \to i}) \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + I_{j \to i} \cdot \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\mathbf{u}_{\text{final}} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} S_{i+1 \to i} \cdot \mathbf{u}_i^{\text{eff}}
$$

**Key advantages:**

- Incremental development (add layers bottom-up).
- Robustness (lower layers continue even if higher layers fail).
- Real-time response (no central planner bottleneck).

**Key limitations:**

- Hard-coded priority (difficult to learn).
- Limited deliberation capability.
- Difficult to debug emergent behavior.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 diagram + 2 explanation of inhibit/suppress + 2 advantages + 1 limitations)**

---

**Part (b) — Three-Layer Floor-Cleaning Robot**

**Layer definitions:**

| Layer | Behavior | Inputs | Outputs | Priority |
|-------|----------|--------|---------|----------|
| 0 | Wander (random walk) | Wheel encoders, no obstacle | $v_{\text{cmd}} = 0.4\;\text{m/s}$, $\omega = \pm 0.5\;\text{rad/s}$ random | Lowest |
| 1 | Avoid-Obstacle | Sonar / IR (5 sensors) | $v_{\text{cmd}} = 0.2\;\text{m/s}$, $\omega$ away from obstacle | Middle |
| 2 | Return-to-Dock | Battery voltage, dock IR beacon | $v_{\text{cmd}} = 0.5\;\text{m/s}$, $\omega$ toward dock | Highest |

**Scenario (i) — Wall encountered at $t = 5\;\text{s}$:**

- Layer 0 (Wander) is active and outputs $\mathbf{u}_0 = (0.4, 0.5)$.
- Layer 1 detects obstacle at $0.3\;\text{m}$. It **inhibits** Layer 0: $I_{1\to 0} = 1$.
- Layer 1 outputs $\mathbf{u}_1 = (0.2, -0.7)$ (turn right).
- Layer 2 is not active (battery OK).
- **Final:** $\mathbf{u} = (0.2, -0.7)$ — robot slows and turns away.

**Scenario (ii) — Battery falls below 20% at $t = 120\;\text{s}$:**

- Layer 2 becomes active. It **inhibits** Layer 1 ($I_{2\to 1} = 1$) and Layer 0 ($I_{2\to 0} = 1$).
- Layer 2 outputs heading toward dock IR beacon: $\mathbf{u}_2 = (0.5, \theta_{\text{dock}})$.
- **Final:** $\mathbf{u} = (0.5, \theta_{\text{dock}})$ — robot immediately abandons cleaning and returns home.
- When battery reaches dock, Layer 2 is deactivated, and Layer 0 may resume after charging.

**Implementation note:** In real-time, this controller runs in a loop at $f_c = 20\;\text{Hz}$, with each layer computing its output in parallel on separate threads or hardware.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 layer table + 2 scenario (i) + 2 scenario (ii) + 1 implementation detail)**

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Motor Schema theory of behavior fusion** proposed by Arkin. Derive the final velocity command when two behaviors — `Move-to-Goal` and `Avoid-Obstacle` — are active simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Subsumption Architecture**, **Finite State Machine (FSM)**, and **Motor Schema** approaches to program control. Use a suitable application example for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987)**

A **motor schema** is a generic behavioral primitive that produces a **vector in 2D Cartesian space** representing the robot's desired instantaneous velocity. Each schema is an **independent agent** with its own perceptual trigger and its own output vector field.

**Common motor schemas (Arkin's library):**

| Schema | Equation | Behavior |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Move-to-Goal | $\mathbf{V} = v_{\max} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{x}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{robot}}}{\lVert \mathbf{x}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{robot}} \rVert}$ | Direct homing |
| Avoid-Obstacle | $\mathbf{V} = \begin{cases} \alpha \left(\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{safe}}}\right) \hat{\mathbf{r}} & d \le d_{\text{safe}} \\ 0 & d > d_{\text{safe}} \end{cases}$ | Repulsion from obstacles |
| Stay-on-Path | $\mathbf{V} = \beta \cdot d_{\text{path}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ | Path following |
| Noise / Wander | $\mathbf{V} = \gamma \cdot \mathbf{N}(0, 1)$ | Exploration |

where $d$ = distance to obstacle, $d_{\text{safe}}$ = safety threshold, $\hat{\mathbf{r}}$ = unit radial vector away from obstacle, $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ = unit normal to path, $d_{\text{path}}$ = perpendicular distance to path.

**The summation equation:**

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \mathbf{V}_i
$$

**Step-by-step derivation (similar to Section 3.1, but applied to schemas):**

Given:
- $\mathbf{V}_{\text{goal}} = (0.8, 0.0)\;\text{m/s}$ (goal is straight ahead).
- $\mathbf{V}_{\text{avoid}} = (-0.2, 0.6)\;\text{m/s}$ (obstacle to the left, push right).
- $w_{\text{goal}} = 0.6$, $w_{\text{avoid}} = 0.8$ (avoidance is urgent).

**Step 1 — Sum:**

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = 0.6 \times (0.8, 0.0) + 0.8 \times (-0.2, 0.6)
$$

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = (0.48, 0.0) + (-0.16, 0.48) = (0.32, 0.48)\;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute norm:**

$$
\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| = \sqrt{0.32^{2} + 0.48^{2}} = \sqrt{0.1024 + 0.2304} = \sqrt{0.3328} \approx 0.5769\;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to $v_{\max} = 1.0\;\text{m/s}$ (if desired):**

$$
\mathbf{u} = \frac{(0.32, 0.48)}{0.5769} \times 1.0 = (0.555, 0.832)\;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** The robot moves forward at $0.555\;\text{m/s}$ and laterally right at $0.832\;\text{m/s}$ — veering away from the obstacle while still progressing toward the goal.

**Key property of Motor Schemas:** The system is **cooperative** (behaviors blend) rather than **competitive** (one wins). This is the central advantage over Subsumption.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 schema theory + 3 derivation steps + 1 interpretation + 1 advantage statement)**

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis**

| Dimension | Subsumption | FSM | Motor Schema |
|-----------|-------------|-----|--------------|
| Originator | Brooks (1986) | Classical CS | Arkin (1987) |
| Coordination | Hard priority | Discrete state | Vector summation |
| Granularity | Behavior | State | Schema (sub-behavior) |
| Reactivity | Highest | Medium | High |
| Deliberation | Lowest | Medium (via states) | Low–Medium |
| Programming style | Layered FSMs | State diagram | Field arithmetic |
| Failure mode | Lower layer takes over | Stuck in state | Local minima |
| Application | Roomba (vacuum) | Industrial arm | Multi-goal navigation |

**Application examples:**

- **Subsumption** → **iRobot Roomba**: The original Roomba had 5 layers (cliff detect, bump, wall follow, dirt detect, docking). Layer 5 (docking) could completely override all others when battery was low.

- **FSM** → **Industrial Pick-and-Place Arm (e.g., KUKA)**: States include `HOME`, `REACH`, `GRASP`, `LIFT`, `MOVE`, `PLACE`, `RETRACT`. Transitions are triggered by sensor events (part-in-place, gripper-closed, motion-complete). Determinism and safety certification are critical.

- **Motor Schema** → **Mars Rover (Spirit/Opportunity navigation)**: When a goal (scientific target) and an obstacle (rock field) are both present, the rover blends `Go-to-Target` and `Avoid-Rock` schemas with weights tuned by terrain difficulty.

**Conclusion:** No single approach dominates. Modern robots (e.g., autonomous cars) use **hybrid architectures** — Subsumption at the safety layer, FSM for mission sequencing, and Motor Schema for low-level motion blending.

**(7 marks breakdown: 3 comparison table + 2 examples + 2 conclusion with hybrid mention)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns (Module 4 — Program Control)**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Behavior" with "Program Control"** — A behavior is a *single reaction*; program control is the *mechanism* that decides which behavior runs. Many students describe only the behavior and skip the arbitration logic. **Loss: 3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to draw a block diagram** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate **2–3 marks for diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include a Subsumption stack or FSM state diagram.
>
> 3. **Skipping the normalization step** in vector fusion. If $\|\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}}\| > v_{\max}$, the answer is incomplete without showing the clamp.
>
> 4. **Confusing Moore and Mealy machines** — In a Moore machine, outputs depend only on the state. In a Mealy machine, outputs depend on (state, input). For program control, **Mealy is preferred** because it responds faster to sensor events. Writing the wrong type costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers. $v = 0.5$ is **wrong**; $v = 0.5\;\text{m/s}$ is **right**.
>
> 6. **Missing the "inhibitor vs suppressor" distinction** — These are *not* synonyms. Inhibition is binary; suppression is a scalar blend.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Program Control**

- ✅ Program Control = the executive layer that selects, prioritizes, and coordinates behaviors.
- ✅ Three core functions: **Selection, Coordination, Arbitration**.
- ✅ **Subsumption (Brooks)** uses strict priority layers with **inhibitors** (binary block) and **suppressors** (scalar blend).
- ✅ **FSM** uses discrete states and events; transition function is $\delta(s, e) = s'$.
- ✅ **Motor Schema (Arkin)** uses vector summation with weights: $\mathbf{V}_{\text{net}} = \sum w_i \mathbf{V}_i$, then normalized to $v_{\max}$.
- ✅ **DAMN (Distributed Architecture for Mobile Navigation)** uses voting-based arbitration for multi-behavior systems.
- ✅ The control loop runs at $f_c = 1/\Delta t$ (typically 10–50 Hz).
- ✅ **Hybrid architectures** (e.g., AuRA) combine deliberative planning with reactive behaviors.
- ✅ For numerical answers, always state the **arbitration function $f$** and the **units**.
- ✅ Mention the **trade-off**: Subsumption = reactive but rigid; FSM = predictable but discrete; Schema = smooth but local-minima-prone.
- ✅ Real-world systems (Mars Rovers, Roomba, autonomous cars) use **hybrid** program control.

> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *“Program Control is the conductor; behaviors are the orchestra; the world is the audience — and the conductor must choose the music in real time.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Welding Application Using VAL Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Welding Application Using VAL Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VAL (Victor's Assembly Language)** is a high-level, real-time robot programming language originally developed by **Unimation Inc.** for the **PUMA (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly)** series of robots. It is one of the earliest commercially successful robot languages and remains a benchmark for understanding symbolic robot motion programming in **Behavioral Robotics** coursework.

In the context of **Robot Welding Applications**, VAL is used to define the welding torch trajectory, synchronize the **arc ignition/extinction** signals with motion, control **travel speed** (typically in inches/sec or mm/sec), and manage **multi-pass deposition** sequences for arc welding (MIG/MAG/TIG) and spot welding operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Music Sheet for the Robot's Hand" Analogy**
> Imagine a human welder holding a torch. Before he welds, he must: (1) walk to the workpiece, (2) position the torch at the correct standoff, (3) press the torch trigger (arc ON), (4) move steadily along the seam at a controlled pace, (5) release the trigger (arc OFF), and (6) retreat the torch. A **VAL program** is essentially this exact sequence written as structured, machine-readable English, where every command — `APPROACH`, `SPEED`, `ARC ON`, `MOVES`, `DEPART` — corresponds to a deliberate human welding action.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Weld Cell Parameters)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Travel Speed (TS)** | **5 – 50** | **mm/sec** |
| **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** | **2 – 15** | **m/min** |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | **18 – 32** | **V (DC)** |
| **Welding Current (I)** | **80 – 350** | **A** |
| **Standoff Distance (d)** | **10 – 20** | **mm** |
| **Position Accuracy** | **± 0.05 – 0.2** | **mm** |

### 1.4 Why VAL for Welding?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Reasons:**
> 1. **Tight Real-Time Synchronization** — Motion commands and I/O (arc, gas, wire feed) are interleaved at millisecond resolution.
> 2. **Path Repeatability** — Continuous path (`MOVES`, `DRAW`) commands allow precise seam tracking.
> 3. **Teach-Pendant Integration** — VAL supports on-line teaching of weld waypoints, essential for low-batch production.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a VAL Program

A VAL program is composed of three fundamental blocks:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. HEADER  → .PROGRAM <name>() │
│  2. BODY    → Motion + I/O      │
│  3. FOOTER  → END               │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Hierarchical Command Categories

**A. Motion Commands (Joint / Cartesian / Tool Frame)**

> [!NOTE]
> - `MOVE <loc>` — Point-to-point (PTP) joint-interpolated motion. Fastest path.
> - `MOVES <loc>` — Straight-line (continuous path) Cartesian motion. **Used for actual welding seam**.
> - `DRAW <dx>,<dy>,<dz>` — Incremental straight-line relative motion.
> - `APPROACH <loc>, <dist>` — PTP motion to a point located `<dist>` away from `<loc>` along the tool Z-axis.
> - `DEPART <dist>` — Retracts the tool by `<dist>` along tool Z-axis.
> - `DRIVE <joint>, <delta>, <speed>` — Single-joint move.

**B. Speed Control**

> [!NOTE]
> - `SPEED <value>` — Sets Cartesian speed in **inches/sec** (PUMA default), value range typically **0.5 to 50**.
> - `ACCEL <value>` — Acceleration ramp (range 1 to 20 in steps of ~0.2 sec).

**C. Welding / I/O Commands**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - `ARC ON` / `ARC OFF` — Energizes / de-energizes the welding power source via digital output (often DOUT 1).
> - `WELD ON` / `WELD OFF` — Some VAL dialects use this synonym.
> - `SIGNAL <port>, <state>` — General-purpose digital I/O.
> - `WAIT <condition>` — Conditional synchronization.
> - `DELAY <seconds>` — Time-based hold.

**D. Program Control**

> [!NOTE]
> - `IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... END` — Conditional branching.
> - `FOR ... END` — Loop construct.
> - `GOTO <label>` — Unconditional jump (legacy).
> - `CALL <subprog>` / `RETURN` — Subroutine mechanism.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Command Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian Travel Time | $T_t = \dfrac{\vert \vec{P}_2 - \vec{P}_1 \vert}{S_{ts}}$ | Weld pass time |
| Wire Deposition Rate | $W_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w^2 \cdot v_{wire}}{4}$ | Filler mass per sec |
| Heat Input (Arc Weld) | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel}}$ | Weld quality control |
| Speed conversion | $S_{ts} = S_{in/sec} \times 25.4$ | VAL to SI units |
| Loop-pass counter | $n_{pass} = \lceil \dfrac{t_{req}}{t_{1pass}} \rceil$ | Multi-pass welding |
| Linear distance (3D) | $\vert \vec{P} \vert = \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$ | Waypoint separation |
| Joint angle (revolute) | $\theta = \omega \cdot t$ | Servo motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Heat Input formula** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel}}$ (kJ/mm) is the single most examined relationship in KTU welding-robotics questions. Always convert units consistently: $v_{travel}$ in **mm/sec** or **mm/min**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Deployment:** VAL-derived architectures underpin welding cells used by **automotive OEMs** (e.g., PUMA 560/762 welders at Ford, GM body shops) for body-in-white assembly. Modern descendants — **VAL II, VAL 3 (Stäubli), KAREL (FANUC), RAPID (ABB)** — all retain VAL's core philosophy: declarative motion + synchronized I/O.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Time Calculation for a Linear Weld Bead

**Problem Statement:**
A PUMA robot must weld a straight seam between waypoint $P_1 = (300, 200, 150)$ mm and $P_2 = (600, 400, 150)$ mm. The VAL command `SPEED 4` (inches/sec) is given. Compute the weld pass travel time $T_t$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the displacement vector** $\vec{\Delta P}$ between $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$
\vec{\Delta P} = P_2 - P_1
$$

Substituting the numerical coordinates:

$$
\vec{\Delta P} = (600-300,\ 400-200,\ 150-150)
$$

$$
\vec{\Delta P} = (300,\ 200,\ 0)\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude (Euclidean distance)** $\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert$:

$$
\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert = \sqrt{300^2 + 200^2 + 0^2}
$$

$$
\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert = \sqrt{90000 + 40000}
$$

$$
\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert = \sqrt{130000}
$$

$$
\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert \approx 360.555\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert VAL speed from inches/sec to mm/sec.** VAL uses imperial units natively; conversion factor is **25.4 mm per inch**:

$$
S_{ts} = 4 \times 25.4 = 101.6\ \text{mm/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Travel Time formula:**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert}{S_{ts}}
$$

$$
T_t = \dfrac{360.555}{101.6}
$$

$$
T_t \approx 3.55\ \text{seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** Students commonly forget the imperial→SI conversion of VAL's `SPEED` command. The factor **25.4** appears in roughly **60% of KTU numericals**. Memorize it.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Heat Input Verification

**Problem Statement:**
A MIG welding robot passes current $I = 220$ A at voltage $V = 26$ V along the seam above. Compute the heat input $H$ in kJ/mm.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify travel speed.** From Example 1 we already determined:

$$
v_{travel} = 101.6\ \text{mm/sec} = 6096\ \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat input formula** with consistent units (mm/min for $v_{travel}$):

$$
H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel}}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{26 \times 220}{6096}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{5720}{6096}
$$

$$
H \approx 0.938\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality interpretation.** A heat input near **1 kJ/mm** is typical for thin-gauge automotive steel. Excess above **1.5 kJ/mm** risks HAZ softening.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Pass Loop Counter

**Problem Statement:**
A V-groove weld requires total filler thickness $t_{req} = 12$ mm. Each pass deposits $t_{1pass} = 3.5$ mm. Compute number of passes $n_{pass}$.

**Solution:**

$$
n_{pass} = \lceil \dfrac{t_{req}}{t_{1pass}} \rceil
$$

$$
n_{pass} = \lceil \dfrac{12}{3.5} \rceil = \lceil 3.428 \rceil = 4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures the final pass overshoots rather than undershoots — critical for full-penetration welds.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Example | Concept Tested | Final Result |
|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | VAL speed conversion + Euclidean distance | $T_t \approx 3.55$ sec |
| 3.2 | Heat input with unit consistency | $H \approx 0.938$ kJ/mm |
| 3.3 | Loop counter with ceiling function | $n_{pass} = 4$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a VAL-Controlled Welding Cell

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below depicts a **block-level functional architecture** showing the interaction between the VAL interpreter, motion controller, and welding peripherals — rather than a physical drawing, which would be ineffective in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Teach Pendant / VAL Program Source] --> B[VAL Interpreter Kernel]
    B --> C[Motion Planner PTP and CP]
    B --> D[Digital I/O Manager]
    C --> E[Servo Joint Controllers J1 J2 J3 J4 J5 J6]
    E --> F[PUMA Robot Manipulator]
    D --> G[Welding Power Source]
    D --> H[Wire Feed Unit]
    D --> I[Shielding Gas Valve]
    F --> J[Workpiece and Fixture]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    B --> K[Operator HMI Display]
    L[Speed and ACCEL Registers] --> C
    M[ARC ON OFF Flags] --> D
```
