    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#374151,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 VAL Program Execution Flow During a Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start] --> S2[SET SPEED approach speed]
    S2 --> S3[APPROACH weld start point with offset 50mm]
    S3 --> S4[SET SPEED weld travel speed]
    S4 --> S5[Gas Purge Delay 0.5 sec]
    S5 --> S6[ARC ON]
    S6 --> S7[MOVES linear path along seam]
    S7 --> S8[ARC OFF]
    S8 --> S9[Crater Fill Delay 0.3 sec]
    S9 --> S10[DEPART retract 50mm]
    S10 --> S11{More Passes?}
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Increment pass counter]
    S12 --> S4
    S11 -- No --> S13[End Program]
    style S1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style S6 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style S13 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy in VAL Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[World Frame] --> B[Base Frame of Robot]
    B --> T[Tool Frame Torch Tip]
    B --> U[User Frame Workpiece Fixture]
    U --> O[Object Frame Seam Origin]
    T --> O
    style W fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame-Stack Insight:** All `MOVES` commands in VAL execute relative to the **most recently set frame** (default = World). Welding programs almost always invoke `BASE` and a taught `UFRAME` (User Frame) so the seam coordinates remain invariant when the fixture is repositioned.

### 4.4 Reference VAL Welding Program (Annotated)

```
.PROGRAM weld_linear_seam()
   SPEED 8                       ; Approach speed (in/sec)
   APPROACH seam_start, 50       ; 50 mm above seam origin
   SPEED 4                       ; Weld travel speed
   DELAY 0.5                     ; Pre-flow gas purge
   ARC ON                        ; Digital out triggers welder
   MOVES seam_start              ; Dip to seam start
   MOVES seam_end                ; Linear CP weld along seam
   ARC OFF                       ; Extinguish arc
   DELAY 0.3                     ; Crater fill / post-flow
   DEPART 50                     ; Retract torch
.END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define VAL. List any four motion commands used in VAL for robot programming.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
VAL (Victor's Assembly Language) is a high-level, real-time programming language developed by **Unimation Inc.** for the PUMA robot series (3 marks for definition).

Four motion commands (any four for 3 marks — split into 4 sub-points @ ~0.75 each, but for 3-mark answer, all four in one list):
1. `MOVE` — point-to-point motion
2. `MOVES` — straight-line continuous path
3. `APPROACH` — approach with offset distance
4. `DEPART` — retract along tool Z-axis

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the role of `ARC ON` and `ARC OFF` commands in a VAL welding program. Why is `DELAY` used before `ARC ON`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `ARC ON` / `ARC OFF` are digital I/O commands that energize / de-energize the **welding power source** through a designated digital output port of the controller, synchronizing arc ignition with robot motion (2 marks).
- `DELAY` before `ARC ON` allows **shielding-gas pre-flow** to purge atmospheric oxygen from the nozzle region, preventing porosity and tungsten oxidation in TIG / weld-pool contamination in MIG (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a VAL-controlled robotic welding cell**. List the major sub-systems and the role of the VAL interpreter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A VAL-controlled robotic welding cell consists of the following major sub-systems (1 mark each, total 6 marks):

1. **Teach Pendant / VAL Source** — Used to enter or teach programs.
2. **VAL Interpreter Kernel** — Parses commands, schedules motion and I/O events.
3. **Motion Planner** — Generates PTP or Cartesian trajectories.
4. **Servo Joint Controllers (J1–J6)** — Drive each axis with closed-loop feedback.
5. **Digital I/O Manager** — Triggers the welding power source, wire feeder, and gas solenoid.
6. **Welding Peripherals** — Power source, wire feed unit, gas valve, and the **PUMA manipulator with the welding torch end-effector**.

The **VAL interpreter** acts as the central coordinator — it sequences high-level commands like `MOVES` and `ARC ON` into synchronized joint-servo setpoints and digital-output pulses, ensuring the arc ignites **only** when the torch reaches the seam start point.

The remaining **1 mark** is for the diagram (see Section 4.1 block diagram).

**(b)** A PUMA robot must execute a 3-pass weld. Each pass moves linearly from waypoint $P_1 = (200, 100, 50)$ mm to $P_2 = (200, 100, 300)$ mm. The weld travel speed is `SPEED 5` (inches/sec). Compute: **(7 marks)**
  (i) The travel time per pass. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The total cycle time for 3 passes, assuming **1.5 sec arc ignition + extinction overhead per pass**. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The heat input if $V = 28$ V, $I = 240$ A. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Travel time per pass (3 marks):**

**Step 1** — Compute displacement vector:
$$
\vec{\Delta P} = (200-200,\ 100-100,\ 300-50) = (0,0,250)\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2** — Magnitude:
$$
\vert \vec{\Delta P} \vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 250^2} = 250\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3** — Convert VAL speed:
$$
S_{ts} = 5 \times 25.4 = 127\ \text{mm/sec}
$$

**Step 4** — Travel time:
$$
T_t = \dfrac{250}{127} \approx 1.97\ \text{sec/pass}
$$

**(ii) Total cycle time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{cycle} = 3 \times (T_t + T_{overhead})
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 3 \times (1.97 + 1.5) = 3 \times 3.47
$$

$$
T_{cycle} \approx 10.41\ \text{sec}
$$

**(iii) Heat input (2 marks):**

Convert $v_{travel}$ to mm/min:
$$
v_{travel} = 127 \times 60 = 7620\ \text{mm/min}
$$

Apply:
$$
H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel}} = \dfrac{28 \times 240}{7620}
$$

$$
H \approx 0.882\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **structure of a VAL program** for a robotic arc-welding task. Provide a sample program for welding a straight seam between two taught locations and annotate each line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A VAL welding program consists of three logical blocks (2 marks for structure description):

1. **Header** — `.PROGRAM <name>()` declares the program.
2. **Body** — Contains motion, speed, I/O, and timing commands.
3. **Footer** — `.END` terminates the program.

Sample annotated program (5 marks):

```
.PROGRAM weld_straight_seam()      ; Program header (0.5 mark)
   SPEED 8                          ; Fast approach speed (0.5)
   APPROACH seam_start, 50          ; 50 mm above seam (1.0)
   SPEED 4                          ; Slow weld speed (0.5)
   DELAY 0.5                        ; Gas pre-flow (0.5)
   ARC ON                           ; Energize welder (0.5)
   MOVES seam_start                 ; Dip torch (0.5)
   MOVES seam_end                   ; Weld the seam (0.5)
   ARC OFF                          ; Extinguish arc (0.5)
   DELAY 0.3                        ; Post-flow / crater (0.5)
   DEPART 50                        ; Retract torch (0.5)
.END                                ; Program end (0.5)
```

**(b)** Discuss the **role of coordinate frames** (World, Base, Tool, User) in VAL welding applications. Why is a User Frame essential when fixtures change? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Coordinate Frames (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **World Frame** — Global reference, fixed to the floor.
2. **Base Frame** — Origin at the robot's mounting flange.
3. **Tool Frame (TOOL)** — Origin at the **welding torch tip**, oriented along the wire axis.
4. **User Frame (UFRAME)** — Origin on the workpiece/fixture, allowing local coordinates.

**Why User Frame is Essential (3 marks):**

- A **User Frame** decouples taught weld points from the global coordinate system (1 mark).
- When the **fixture is repositioned** (due to a new part variant), only the UFRAME transform needs updating — the entire weld program remains valid (1 mark).
- This dramatically reduces **re-teaching time** and supports **family-of-parts** production (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 25.4 mm/inch conversion** — VAL uses imperial speed units natively. Examiners explicitly test this. Penalty: **-2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up `MOVE` vs `MOVES`** — `MOVE` is PTP (not straight line); examiners expect `MOVES` for welding seams. Penalty: **-1 mark**.
> 3. **Not invoking `APPROACH` before welding** — This is a **safety and arc-stability** requirement; missing it costs process marks. Penalty: **-1 mark**.
> 4. **Incorrect frame notation in UFRAME questions** — Always specify `UFRAME = <n>` before teaching seam points. Penalty: **-1 mark**.
> 5. **Heat input without unit conversion** — Use mm/min for $v_{travel}$ or kJ/mm result is wrong. Penalty: **-2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **VAL** = Unimation's PUMA programming language; English-like, real-time.
> - **Core motion**: `MOVE` (PTP), `MOVES` (CP straight line), `APPROACH`, `DEPART`, `DRAW`, `DRIVE`.
> - **Speed**: `SPEED <value>` in **inches/sec** → multiply by **25.4** for mm/sec.
> - **Welding I/O**: `ARC ON` / `ARC OFF` via digital output; always preceded by **gas pre-flow delay**.
> - **Heat input**: $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel}}$ with $v_{travel}$ in **mm/min** to get **kJ/mm**.
> - **Multi-pass**: $n_{pass} = \lceil t_{req} / t_{1pass} \rceil$.
> - **Frame stack**: World → Base → Tool (TOOL) → User (UFRAME). UFRAME enables **fixture-independent** programming.
> - **Standard weld parameters**: TS 5–50 mm/s, V 18–32 V, I 80–350 A, standoff 10–20 mm.
> - **Program structure**: `.PROGRAM ... END` with `APPROACH → DELAY → ARC ON → MOVES → ARC OFF → DELAY → DEPART` ordering.
> - **Real-world lineage**: VAL → VAL II → VAL 3 (Stäubli); concepts carry into KAREL, RAPID, KRL.
> - **Examiner hot-button**: Always show unit conversions and `UFRAME` declarations for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RAPID Language Basic Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RAPID Language Basic Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RAPID (Robot Application Programming Interface for Developers)** is a high-level, structured, event-driven programming language developed by **ABB Robotics** specifically for industrial robot manipulation, motion control, and process automation. It serves as the native instruction set for ABB's IRC5 and OmniCore robot controllers, enabling engineers to define motion trajectories, I/O signaling, logical flow control, and inter-process communication within robotic workcells.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT743 – Behavioral Robotics)**, RAPID basic commands form the executable vocabulary through which abstract behavioral models (reactive, deliberative, hybrid) are translated into concrete robot actuator actions. Every command maps to a specific behavioral primitive: *MoveJ* = joint-space reactive motion, *WaitDI* = sensor-driven synchronization, *IF–THEN–ELSE* = conditional reactive rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: RAPID is the canonical Industry 4.0 implementation language for ABB robots. The 2024 scheme expects students to fluently write modular RAPID routines, classify commands by function (motion, I/O, logic, data), and trace program execution flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **choreographed dance performance**:

- The **dancer (robot)** must move in specific ways → these are **motion commands** (`MoveJ`, `MoveL`, `MoveC`).
- The **dancer must react to music cues and stage lights** → these are **I/O and Wait commands** (`SetDO`, `WaitDI`).
- The **choreographer decides the order of moves** → these are **program-flow commands** (`IF`, `FOR`, `WHILE`, `TEST`).
- The **dancer's memory of positions** → these are **data variables** (`robtarget`, `jointtarget`, `num`, `bool`).

In short: **RAPID = Choreography for a robot dancer.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout**: A RAPID program is a *modular collection of procedures* (`PROC ... ENDPROC`), *functions* (`FUNC ... ENDFUNC`), and *trap routines* (`TRAP ... ENDTRAP`), executed by the robot controller's interpreter, where each instruction is either a **statement** (performs action) or a **declaration** (defines data).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Robot Speed Data Parameter**: `v` (mm/s for linear, °/s for reorientation)
- **Acceleration / Deceleration**: `z` parameter (default zone/acceleration setting)
- **Singularity Tolerance**: Default TCP speed of **250 mm/s** in safe mode
- **Max Number of Move Instructions per Program**: **32,000** (practical limit)
- **Variable Naming Rule**: 32 characters max, must begin with a letter
- **Standard Joint Speeds (ABB IRB series)**: typically **≤ 2000 mm/s** in production

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception**: RAPID is *not* an acronym for "Rapid Application Development." It is a proprietary ABB trademarked name with no expanded meaning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of RAPID Basic Commands

RAPID commands fall into **five functional categories**, each corresponding to a distinct layer of robot behavior:

#### A. Motion Commands (Actuator Layer)
These move the robot's TCP (Tool Center Point) along geometrically defined paths.

| Command | Full Form | Path Type | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MoveJ` | Move Joint | Joint-interpolated (point-to-point, fastest) | Repositioning between work zones |
| `MoveL` | Move Linear | Cartesian straight line | Welding, gluing, sealing |
| `MoveC` | Move Circular | Circular arc through 2 points | Curved seams, pick-and-place arcs |
| `MoveAbsJ` | Move Absolute Joint | Direct joint-angle instruction | Singularity bypass, home return |

**Syntax skeleton**:
```rapid
MoveJ p1, v500, z30, tool1;
MoveL p2, v200, fine, gun1;
MoveC p3, p4, v150, z10, gun1;
```

- `p1` → **robtarget** (position)
- `v500` → **speeddata** (mm/s)
- `z30` / `fine` → **zonedata** (cornering tolerance)
- `tool1` → **tooldata** (TCP frame)
- `;` → **statement terminator** (mandatory)

#### B. Input/Output (I/O) Commands (Perception–Actuation Bridge)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `SetDO do1, 1` | Set Digital Output high |
| `ResetDO do1` | Reset Digital Output low |
| `SetAO ao1, 5.5` | Set Analog Output (voltage/current) |
| `WaitDI di1, 1` | Wait until Digital Input equals 1 |
| `WaitTime 2.5` | Wait for 2.5 seconds |

#### C. Program Flow Commands (Behavioral Decision Layer)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... ENDIF` | Conditional branching |
| `FOR ... ENDFOR` | Definite loop |
| `WHILE ... ENDWHILE` | Indefinite loop |
| `TEST ... CASE ... ENDTEST` | Multi-way branch (switch–case) |
| `GOTO label` | Unconditional jump (discouraged) |
| `RETURN` | Exit current routine |
| `ExitCycle` | Stop program, reset cycle |

#### D. Data Declaration Commands (Memory Layer)

```rapid
VAR num length := 100.5;
VAR bool gripper_closed := FALSE;
VAR robtarget p_home := [[0,0,500],[1,0,0,0],[0,0,0,0],[9E9,9E9,9E9,9E9,9E9,9E9]];
CONST string PART_NAME := "BracketA";
PERS num cycle_count := 0;
```

- `VAR` → Local data (lost on reset)
- `PERS` → Persistent data (retained)
- `CONST` → Compile-time constant
- `TASK` → Multi-task shared variable

#### E. Routine & Module Structure (Architectural Layer)

```rapid
MODULE MainModule
  PROC main()
    Initialize;
    WHILE TRUE DO
      PickPart;
      PlacePart;
    ENDWHILE
  ENDPROC

  PROC PickPart()
    MoveJ p_approach, v1000, z50, tool1;
    MoveL p_pick, v200, fine, tool1;
    SetDO gripper, 1;
    MoveL p_retract, v500, z50, tool1;
  ENDPROC
ENDMODULE
```

### 2.2 The `SpeedData` and `ZoneData` High-Yield Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Focus**: `v` and `z` parameters carry the highest weightage in KTU questions.

| Symbol | Data Type | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `v` | speeddata | v500 | TCP velocity |
| `v100` | predefined | 100 mm/s | Slow (precision) |
| `v500` | predefined | 500 mm/s | Medium (general) |
| `v1000` | predefined | 1000 mm/s | Fast (transfer) |
| `vmax` | predefined | rated max | Production fast traverse |
| `z` | zonedata | z50 | Fly-by zone radius (mm) |
| `fine` | zonedata | 0 mm | No fly-by (exact stop) |
| `z0`, `z1`, ..., `z200` | predefined | varies | Zone cornering radius |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive welding lines**: `MoveL` and `MoveC` chained for continuous seam tracking.
- **Pharmaceutical pick-and-place**: `MoveJ` for fast transit, `WaitDI` for vision system handshake.
- **Behavioral Robotics mapping**: A Braitenberg-style reactive controller maps sensor `di` values directly to `SetAO` outputs for analog motor control.
- **Industry 4.0 cell**: RAPID routines are called by PLCs via fieldbus (Profinet/EtherCAT), enabling supervisory hybrid control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> RAPID is declarative/imperative, not a formula-driven subject. Instead of algebraic derivations, this section provides **exhaustive trace analyses** and **execution-flow derivations** — the equivalent analytical work demanded by KTU examiners.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Command Type vs. Behavioral Layer

| Layer | RAPID Construct | Reactive Behavior | Deliberative Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor Input | `WaitDI` | Event-driven | Polled inspection |
| Decision | `IF–THEN` | Stimulus–Response rule | Goal evaluation |
| Action | `MoveJ/MoveL` | Reflex motion | Planned trajectory |
| Memory | `PERS var` | No persistence | World model |
| Cycle Control | `WHILE TRUE` | Open-loop continuous | Closed-loop supervision |

### 3.2 Execution-Flow Derivation: Sample RAPID Routine

**Given RAPID code**:
```rapid
MODULE PickPlace
  VAR num count := 0;
  PERS tooldata gripper := [TRUE,[[0,0,150],[1,0,0,0]],[5,[0,0,100],0,0,0,0]];
  
  PROC main()
    MoveJ p_home, v1000, z50, gripper;
    WHILE count < 5 DO
      WaitDI di_part_present, 1;
      MoveJ p_above_pick, v800, z30, gripper;
      MoveL p_pick, v100, fine, gripper;
      SetDO do_gripper_close, 1;
      WaitTime 0.5;
      MoveL p_above_pick, v200, z30, gripper;
      MoveJ p_above_place, v800, z30, gripper;
      MoveL p_place, v100, fine, gripper;
      ResetDO do_gripper_close;
      WaitTime 0.5;
      MoveL p_above_place, v200, z30, gripper;
      count := count + 1;
    ENDWHILE
    MoveJ p_home, v1000, z50, gripper;
  ENDPROC
ENDMODULE
```

**Step-by-step execution trace derivation**:

**Step 1** — Module loads. System allocates `count` as `num` (32-bit float), `gripper` as persistent tooldata, `p_home` etc. as global robtargets.

**Step 2** — `PROC main()` is invoked. Controller pushes return address onto the routine stack.

**Step 3** — `MoveJ p_home, v1000, z50, gripper;` → Joint-interpolated motion to `p_home` at 1000 mm/s, with 50 mm fly-by zone.

**Step 4** — `WHILE count < 5 DO` → Evaluates boolean: `0 < 5` → TRUE → enters loop body.

**Step 5** — `WaitDI di_part_present, 1;` → Blocks execution until digital input `di_part_present` becomes logic HIGH. This is a **synchronous blocking call**.

**Step 6** — Sequential `MoveJ` and `MoveL` instructions drive TCP through pre-defined waypoints with specified `v` and `z` parameters.

**Step 7** — `SetDO do_gripper_close, 1;` → Energizes digital output → pneumatic gripper closes (actuation event).

**Step 8** — `WaitTime 0.5;` → Controller suspends execution for 0.5 s, allowing gripper to stabilize.

**Step 9** — `count := count + 1;` → Increments counter: `count = 1`.

**Step 10** — Loop re-evaluates: `1 < 5` → TRUE → repeats 4 more times.

**Step 11** — Final iteration: `count = 5` → `5 < 5` → FALSE → exits loop.

**Step 12** — `MoveJ p_home ...` → Returns to safe position. `ENDPROC` → pops stack, program terminates.

### 3.3 Logical State Derivation: `IF–THEN–ELSE` Truth Table

For the construct:
```rapid
IF di_safety_ok = 1 AND count >= 0 THEN
  speed := v500;
  routine_name PickPart;
ELSE
  speed := v100;
  SetDO do_alarm, 1;
ENDIF
```

| `di_safety_ok` | `count >= 0` | Branch Taken | `speed` value | `do_alarm` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ELSE | v100 | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | ELSE | v100 | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | ELSE | v100 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | THEN | v500 | unchanged |

### 3.4 Kinematic Insight into `MoveJ` vs `MoveL`

Although KTU does not require full inverse kinematics derivation for Module 4, the conceptual model is:

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{joint}}(t) = \Theta^{-1}(P_{\text{start}}) \;\longrightarrow\; \Theta^{-1}(P_{\text{goal}})
$$

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{linear}}(t) = P_{\text{start}} + t \cdot (P_{\text{goal}} - P_{\text{start}}), \quad t \in [0,1]
$$

where $\Theta^{-1}$ is the **inverse kinematics mapping** from Cartesian TCP pose to joint angles. This justifies why `MoveJ` is faster (joint-space shortest path) and `MoveL` is preferred for processes requiring a constant TCP orientation relative to the workpiece (e.g., adhesive bead).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RAPID Program Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MODULE Declaration] --> B[System Data SECTION]
    B --> B1[VAR - Local Variables]
    B --> B2[PERS - Persistent Variables]
    B --> B3[CONST - Constants]
    B --> B4[ALIAS - Type Renaming]
    A --> C[Task Space - USER Routines]
    C --> C1[PROC main ENDPROC]
    C --> C2[PROC subroutine1 ENDPROC]
    C --> C3[PROC subroutine2 ENDPROC]
    C --> C4[FUNC function1 ENDFUNC]
    C --> C5[TRAP interrupt1 ENDTRAP]
    A --> D[BackEnd Communication]
    D --> D1[FlexPendant Interface]
    D --> D2[Fieldbus I/O]
    D --> D3[OPC UA Server]
    C1 --> E{Behavioral Control}
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> E1[Reactive Layer - WaitDI SetDO]
    E --> E2[Deliberative Layer - IF FOR]
    E --> F[Robot Controller IRC5 OmniCore]
    F --> G[Servo Drives Motors]
```

### 4.2 RAPID Command Execution Flow (Sequential + Interrupt)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Program Start] --> nodeB[System Initialization]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Read PERS Variables]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Execute PROC main]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Motion Instruction?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Plan Trajectory]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Send to Servo Loops]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Interpolate Position]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Drive Motors]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Encoder Feedback]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Position Error Check]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Target Reached?}
    nodeL -->|No| nodeH
    nodeL -->|Yes| nodeM[Next Instruction]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeN[I/O Instruction?]
    nodeN -->|Yes| nodeO[Execute SetDO or WaitDI]
    nodeO --> nodeM
    nodeN -->|No| nodeP[Logic Instruction?]
    nodeP -->|Yes| nodeQ[Evaluate IF FOR WHILE]
    nodeQ --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeR{Program End?}
    nodeR -->|No| nodeE
    nodeR -->|Yes| nodeS[Cycle Stop]
    nodeP -->|Interrupt Received| nodeT[TRAP Routine]
    nodeT --> nodeD
```

### 4.3 RAPID Data Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Atomic Types] --> A1[num - 32-bit float]
    A0 --> A2[bool - TRUE FALSE]
    A0 --> A3[string - text]
    A0 --> A4[byte - 0 to 255]
    A1 --> B0[Composite Types]
    A2 --> B0
    B0 --> B1[robtarget - pose data]
    B0 --> B2[jointtarget - joint angles]
    B0 --> B3[tooldata - TCP frame]
    B0 --> B4[wobjdata - work object]
    B0 --> B5[speeddata - velocity v]
    B0 --> B6[zonedata - cornering z]
    B0 --> B7[confdata - robot configuration]
    B1 --> C0[User-Defined RECORD Types]
    B2 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Alias Types for Readability]
```

### 4.4 Reactive vs Deliberative Control — Mapping to RAPID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Sensor Input DI AI] --> nodeY{Stimulus Type}
    nodeY -->|Threshold Crossed| nodeZ[Reactive Layer]
    nodeY -->|Pattern Detected| nodeAA[Deliberative Layer]
    nodeZ --> nodeBB[Trap Routine Immediate Response]
    nodeBB --> nodeCC[SetDO SetAO Direct Actuator]
    nodeAA --> nodeDD[IF TEST Plan Selection]
    nodeDD --> nodeEE[MoveJ MoveL Goal Directed Action]
    nodeCC --> nodeFF[World State Update]
    nodeEE --> nodeFF
    nodeFF --> nodeX
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List any three basic motion commands in RAPID and state the path type of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `MoveJ` — Joint-interpolated (point-to-point) motion. (1 mark)
2. `MoveL` — Linear (Cartesian straight-line) motion. (1 mark)
3. `MoveC` — Circular-arc motion through two intermediate robtargets. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*What is the difference between `VAR`, `PERS`, and `CONST` data declarations in RAPID?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `VAR` (Variable): Local data scope; **value is lost on program reset**. (1 mark)
- `PERS` (Persistent): Global data scope; **value is retained across power cycles and program restarts**. (1 mark)
- `CONST` (Constant): Compile-time fixed value; **cannot be modified at runtime**. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**
(a) *Explain the different categories of RAPID basic commands with at least two examples for each category.* **(7 marks)**
(b) *Write a complete RAPID program that picks an object from position `p_pick` and places it at `p_place`, repeating the cycle 3 times. Assume the gripper is operated by digital output `do_gripper`.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

RAPID commands are classified into **five categories**:

1. **Motion Commands** — control TCP path. Examples: `MoveJ p10, v500, z30, tool1;` (joint-interpolated move), `MoveL p20, v200, fine, gun1;` (linear move). *(1.5 marks)*

2. **I/O Commands** — interface with sensors and actuators. Examples: `SetDO do_gripper, 1;` (energize output), `WaitDI di_sensor, 1;` (wait for input high). *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Program Flow Commands** — control execution sequence. Examples: `IF count > 0 THEN ... ENDIF` (conditional), `WHILE cycle_run DO ... ENDWHILE` (loop). *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Data Declarations** — define memory. Examples: `VAR num x := 10;` (local), `PERS robtarget p_safe;` (persistent). *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Routine Structure** — modular organization. Examples: `PROC PickPart() ... ENDPROC`, `MODULE MainModule ... ENDMODULE`. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

```rapid
MODULE PickPlaceCycle
  PERS num cycle_count := 0;
  PERS tooldata gripper_t := [TRUE,[[0,0,100],[1,0,0,0]],[2,[0,0,50],0,0,0,0]];

  PROC main()
    MoveJ p_home, v1000, z50, gripper_t;
    WHILE cycle_count < 3 DO
      PickOperation;
      PlaceOperation;
      cycle_count := cycle_count + 1;
    ENDWHILE
    MoveJ p_home, v1000, z50, gripper_t;
  ENDPROC

  PROC PickOperation()
    MoveJ p_above_pick, v800, z30, gripper_t;
    MoveL p_pick, v100, fine, gripper_t;
    SetDO do_gripper, 1;
    WaitTime 0.5;
    MoveL p_above_pick, v200, z30, gripper_t;
  ENDPROC

  PROC PlaceOperation()
    MoveJ p_above_place, v800, z30, gripper_t;
    MoveL p_place, v100, fine, gripper_t;
    ResetDO do_gripper;
    WaitTime 0.5;
    MoveL p_above_place, v200, z30, gripper_t;
  ENDPROC
ENDMODULE
```

**Step-by-step logic** *(for full marks)*:
- `PERS num` retains counter across cycles *(0.5 mark)*
- `MoveJ` at v1000 z50 for fast transit *(0.5 mark)*
- `WHILE` loop bound check `cycle_count < 3` *(1 mark)*
- `PickOperation` uses `MoveL ... fine` for precision approach *(1 mark)*
- `SetDO` closes gripper; `WaitTime 0.5` ensures mechanical stability *(0.5 mark)*
- `PlaceOperation` mirrors pick with `ResetDO` *(0.5 mark)*
- Counter increment `cycle_count := cycle_count + 1` ensures finite termination *(0.5 mark)*
- Final `MoveJ p_home` for safe return *(0.5 mark)*
- Proper indentation, `MODULE ... ENDMODULE` structure, `;` statement terminators *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**
(a) *Explain the role of `speeddata` (`v`) and `zonedata` (`z`) parameters in RAPID motion instructions. How does the value of `z` affect the robot's cornering behavior?* **(7 marks)**
(b) *With a neat example, explain the `TEST–CASE` and `WHILE` loop constructs in RAPID. Mention the syntax structure clearly.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

- **speeddata (`v`)** specifies the **velocity of the TCP** during motion. Values are predefined identifiers like `v100`, `v500`, `v1000`, or user-defined `SpeedData` types. The unit is **mm/s** for translation and **degrees/s** for reorientation. *(2 marks)*

- **zonedata (`z`)** specifies the **size of the fly-by cornering zone** in **mm**. A larger `z` value (e.g., `z200`) means the robot will start the next motion instruction *before* precisely reaching the corner point — **faster cycle time** but lower positional accuracy. *(2 marks)*

- **`fine` value** corresponds to a zone of size **0 mm**, meaning the robot must come to a **complete stop** at the waypoint before proceeding. This is used for **precision tasks** (welding, drilling). *(1 mark)*

- **Effect on cornering**:
  - `z0`–`z10` → Tight, accurate cornering *(0.5 mark)*
  - `z30`–`z50` → Balanced general use *(0.5 mark)*
  - `z100`–`z200` → Smooth, fast fly-by, reduced accuracy *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**`TEST–CASE` Construct** *(3.5 marks)*:

```rapid
TEST part_type
  CASE 1:
    MoveJ p_type1, v500, z30, tool1;
    SetDO do_signal, 1;
  CASE 2:
    MoveJ p_type2, v500, z30, tool1;
    SetDO do_signal, 2;
  CASE 3, 4:  ! Multiple cases
    MoveJ p_type34, v500, z30, tool1;
  DEFAULT:
    TPWrite "Unknown part type";
ENDTEST
```

- The expression after `TEST` is evaluated once *(0.5 mark)*
- The matching `CASE` branch executes; if no match, `DEFAULT` runs *(0.5 mark)*
- Multiple `CASE` values can be combined with comma *(0.5 mark)*
- The construct terminates at `ENDTEST` *(0.5 mark)*

**`WHILE` Loop** *(3.5 marks)*:

```rapid
VAR num attempt := 0;
WHILE attempt < 3 AND di_success = 0 DO
  MoveJ p_try, v500, z30, tool1;
  attempt := attempt + 1;
ENDWHILE
```

- The **condition is tested at the start** of each iteration *(0.5 mark)*
- If FALSE initially, the body never executes *(0.5 mark)*
- The loop terminates when the condition becomes FALSE *(0.5 mark)*
- Infinite loop achieved with `WHILE TRUE DO ... ENDWHILE` *(0.5 mark)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in RAPID Questions:**
> 1. **Missing semicolon** `;` at the end of statements — instant **0.5 to 1 mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting `ENDPROC` / `ENDMODULE`** — syntax error; partial credit only for logic.
> 3. **Confusing `fine` with a numeric zone** — `fine` is a predefined constant; do not write `z0, fine` redundantly.
> 4. **Using `VAR` for counter that must persist** — use `PERS`; otherwise loop becomes infinite.
> 5. **Not specifying `tool` parameter in motion commands** — most frequent omission; controller assumes tool0, causing TCP offset error.
> 6. **Mixing up `MoveJ` and `MoveL` semantics** — `MoveJ` is joint-interpolated (not necessarily linear), `MoveL` is straight-line in Cartesian space.
> 7. **Writing `:=` for assignment in IF conditions** — IF needs a boolean expression; `IF x := 5 THEN` is invalid.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Recall Checklist for KTU Module 4 — RAPID Basic Commands**

- **RAPID** = ABB's proprietary robot programming language for IRC5/OmniCore controllers.
- **Three motion commands** to memorize: `MoveJ` (joint), `MoveL` (linear), `MoveC` (circular). Plus `MoveAbsJ` for absolute joint moves.
- **Mandatory components** of every motion statement: robtarget, speeddata, zonedata, tooldata, and the `;` terminator.
- **`v` (speeddata)** controls TCP velocity; **`z` (zonedata)** controls fly-by cornering radius; **`fine`** = 0 mm = exact stop.
- **I/O commands**: `SetDO` (output high), `ResetDO` (output low), `WaitDI` (input synchronization), `WaitTime` (delay).
- **Program flow constructs**: `IF–THEN–ELSE–ENDIF`, `FOR–ENDFOR`, `WHILE–ENDWHILE`, `TEST–CASE–ENDTEST`.
- **Data declarations**:
  - `VAR` → local, lost on reset
  - `PERS` → global, retained
  - `CONST` → immutable
- **Routine types**:
  - `PROC` → procedure (no return value)
  - `FUNC` → function (returns a value)
  - `TRAP` → interrupt handler
- **Module structure**: `MODULE … ENDMODULE` containing routines and data sections.
- **Singularity avoidance**: prefer `MoveJ` or `MoveAbsJ` when Cartesian solvers become ill-conditioned.
- **Behavioral robotics mapping**:
  - `WaitDI` + `SetDO` = reactive control
  - `IF–TEST` = deliberative decision
  - `PERS` data = world model
  - `TRAP` = asynchronous interruptive behavior
- **Examiner loves**: (i) `v`/`z` parameter explanation, (ii) full program writing with structure, (iii) category-wise command listing, (iv) `IF`/`WHILE` truth-table derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Motor Schemas / Motion Instructions):**
> *Motion Instructions* in behavioral robotics refer to the set of computational perceptual and motor processes that translate an agent's internal state, environmental stimuli, and behavioral goals into **actuator-level commands** (linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$). In the schema-based paradigm (Ronald Arkin, 1987), these are implemented as **motor schemas** — vector-based control laws operating in Cartesian robot space.

A motor schema is a *self-contained, parallel-executing control module* that produces a **velocity vector output** (a 2D motion vector $\vec{V_i}$) in response to a specific perceptual trigger. Multiple motor schemas run simultaneously; their outputs are combined (summed, normalized, or arbitrated) to produce the final motion command sent to the wheels/base of the robot.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Motor Schema** | A behavioural control law that outputs a velocity vector |
| **Perceptual Schema** | Pre-processes raw sensor data into a schema-relevant representation |
| **Schema Instance** | A specific execution of a schema with current parameters |
| **Velocity Vector** | Output $\vec{V} = (v_x, v_y)$ magnitude + direction |
| **Gain** | Scalar weight $G_i$ applied to each schema's output before fusion |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Commuter Driver:**
> Imagine driving a car in city traffic. Your brain runs *multiple parallel advisors*: one says *"head towards your destination (north)"*, another says *"don't hit the pedestrian on the right"*, another says *"don't bump the cyclist on the left"*, and another says *"stay in your lane"*. Each advisor suggests a steering/throttle *direction*. Your brain *adds up* all these suggestions, gives more importance (weight/gain) to safety advisories, and produces a final wheel command.

A robotic motion-instruction system works exactly like this — each "advisor" is a **motor schema** that outputs a vector. The brain's aggregation logic is the **behaviour coordinator** (vector summation / potential field / subsumption arbitration). The final car movement is the **actuator command**.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Motion Instructions

> [!NOTE] > **The Five Fundamental Motion Schemas (Arkin's Taxonomy):**

1. **Move-to-Goal** $(M_g)$ — Attraction towards a target location.
2. **Avoid-Static-Obstacle** $(M_o)$ — Repulsion from known map obstacles.
3. **Avoid-Robot** $(M_r)$ — Repulsion from other moving agents.
4. **Stay-on-Path** $(M_p)$ — Attraction/repulsion to remain on a corridor.
5. **Noise** $(M_n)$ — Stochastic perturbation enabling escape from local minima.

Each is parameterized by a **gain $G_i$** and a **gain-spread $s_i$** which defines the influence radius.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Metrics for Motion Instructions:**
> - **Maximum linear velocity** $v_{max}$ = **0.5 m/s** (typical indoor Pioneer DX)
> - **Maximum angular velocity** $\omega_{max}$ = **1.5 rad/s**
> - **Sensor sampling rate** $f_s$ = **10 Hz** (sonar ring)
> - **Schema evaluation rate** = **20 Hz** (motor control loop)
> - **Workspace size** = bounded by sonar range $\approx$ **5 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Motor Schema

Each motor schema executes the same 5-step computational pattern:

1. **Perceptual Trigger** — A perceptual schema delivers processed data (e.g., obstacle centroid, goal coordinates).
2. **Vector Computation** — Compute the *raw* contribution vector $\vec{R_i}$ using a potential field / force law.
3. **Gain Application** — Multiply by schema-specific gain $G_i$ and modulate by distance via gain-spread $s_i$.
4. **Normalisation** — Constrain vector magnitude to $v_{max}$ and angular component to $\omega_{max}$.
5. **Fusion** — Pass $\vec{V_i}$ to the behaviour coordinator for combination with other schemas.

> **The 'Why' and 'How':** The reason for gain-modulation by distance is *biological plausibility* — distant obstacles should not influence behaviour as strongly as near ones, mirroring how humans react less to far-away traffic.

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations — Potential Fields

The classical motion schema derives from the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method (Khatib, 1986), later extended by Arkin into the *motor schema theory*:

**Attractive potential (Goal):**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{att} \cdot \rho_{goal}^2(q)$$

where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert$ is the Euclidean distance from current robot pose $q$ to goal $q_{goal}$, and $k_{att}$ is the attractive gain.

**Repulsive potential (Obstacle):**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the distance from $q$ to nearest obstacle and $\rho_0$ is the influence radius.

**Forces (negative gradients):**

$$\vec{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

$$\vec{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

---

### 2.3 Schema-Specific Vector Computations

**Move-to-Goal Schema:**

$$\vec{V}_{mg} = G_{mg} \cdot \left( \frac{\vec{P}_{goal} - \vec{P}_{robot}}{\vert \vec{P}_{goal} - \vec{P}_{robot} \vert} \right) \cdot v_{max} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-d/\beta} \right)$$

where $d$ is distance to goal, $\beta$ is gain-spread.

**Avoid-Static-Obstacle Schema:**

$$\vec{V}_{ao} = -G_{ao} \cdot \left( \frac{\vec{P}_{robot} - \vec{P}_{obs}}{\vert \vec{P}_{robot} - \vec{P}_{obs} \vert} \right) \cdot v_{max} \cdot e^{-\rho_0/\rho}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

| Formula / Symbol | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $\vec{V_i} = G_i \cdot \vec{R_i} \cdot f(d)$ | Generic motor schema output | $\mathbb{R}^2$, m/s |
| $G_i$ | Schema gain (behavioural weight) | dimensionless, $0 \le G_i \le 1$ |
| $\vec{R_i}$ | Raw directional unit vector | unit vector |
| $f(d) = e^{-d/\beta}$ or $1-e^{-d/\beta}$ | Distance modulation (repulse/attract) | dimensionless |
| $\beta$ | Gain-spread (influence radius) | metres |
| $\rho_0$ | Obstacle influence radius | metres, typically 1 m |
| $\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_i G_i \cdot \vec{V_i}$ | Vector sum (un-normalised fusion) | m/s |
| $\vec{V}_{norm} = \frac{\vec{V}_{total}}{\vert \vec{V}_{total}\vert} \cdot v_{max}$ | Normalised fusion (capped output) | m/s |
| $v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$ | Forward velocity command | m/s |
| $\omega = \arctan2(v_y, v_x)$ | Angular velocity command | rad/s |
| $\rho_{goal} = \vert\vert q - q_g \vert\vert$ | Euclidean distance to goal | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:**
> Motor schemas power real-world applications such as warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), autonomous vacuum cleaners (iRobot Roomba), military unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs in DARPA programs), and museum tour-guide robots (Minerva, RHINO). They are valued because they are *parallel, modular, and biologically inspired*.

### 2.5 Behaviour Coordination Strategies

Three primary strategies exist for fusing motion instructions:

- **Vector Summation (Additive):** $\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_i \vec{V_i}$ — simple but suffers from local minima.
- **Normalised Summation:** $\vec{V}_{norm} = v_{max} \cdot \frac{\sum_i \vec{V_i}}{\vert \sum_i \vec{V_i}\vert}$ — prevents dominance by any single schema.
- **Arbitration (Subsumption):** Higher-priority schemas *suppress* lower-priority outputs (Brooks, 1986).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Goal-Attraction Velocity Vector

> **Goal:** Derive the linear velocity output of a *Move-to-Goal* motor schema given a robot at $\vec{P}_r$ and a target at $\vec{P}_g$.

**Step 1 — Position displacement vector:**

We begin with the Euclidean displacement from the robot's current position to the target.

$$\vec{D} = \vec{P}_g - \vec{P}_r$$

For example, if $\vec{P}_r = (1, 2)$ and $\vec{P}_g = (5, 4)$, then:

$$\vec{D} = (5-1,\ 4-2) = (4,\ 2)$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of displacement:**

The Euclidean distance from current position to goal.

$$\rho = \vert \vec{D} \vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Unit direction vector:**

Normalise the displacement to obtain a pure direction.

$$\hat{u} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\vert \vec{D} \vert} = \left( \frac{4}{4.472},\ \frac{2}{4.472} \right) \approx (0.894,\ 0.447)$$

**Step 4 — Distance modulation factor:**

A smoothly saturating factor so the robot accelerates as it nears the goal (avoids overshoot).

$$f(d) = 1 - e^{-d/\beta}$$

For $d = 4.472$ and $\beta = 1.0$:

$$f = 1 - e^{-4.472/1.0} = 1 - e^{-4.472} = 1 - 0.0115 \approx 0.988$$

**Step 5 — Apply gain and maximum velocity:**

$$v = G_{mg} \cdot v_{max} \cdot f(d) = 1.0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.988 = 0.494 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Final velocity vector:**

$$\vec{V}_{mg} = v \cdot \hat{u} = (0.494 \cdot 0.894,\ 0.494 \cdot 0.447) = (0.442,\ 0.221)\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Angular command:**

$$\omega = \arctan2(v_y, v_x) = \arctan2(0.221,\ 0.442) \approx 0.464 \text{ rad}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Obstacle Repulsion Vector

> **Goal:** Derive the velocity vector produced by an *Avoid-Static-Obstacle* schema.

**Step 1 — Position vector of obstacle:**

Obstacle at $\vec{P}_o = (3.0, 2.0)$ detected by robot at $\vec{P}_r = (2.5, 2.0)$.

**Step 2 — Vector from obstacle to robot (repulsion direction):**

The repulsion should push the robot *away* from the obstacle, i.e. in the direction $(\vec{P}_r - \vec{P}_o)$.

$$\vec{R} = \vec{P}_r - \vec{P}_o = (2.5 - 3.0,\ 2.0 - 2.0) = (-0.5,\ 0.0)$$

**Step 3 — Distance to obstacle:**

$$\rho = \vert \vec{R} \vert = \sqrt{(-0.5)^2 + 0^2} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Unit repulsion direction:**

$$\hat{u}_{rep} = \frac{\vec{R}}{\vert \vec{R}\vert} = \frac{(-0.5,\ 0.0)}{0.5} = (-1.0,\ 0.0)$$

**Step 5 — Repulsive modulation factor:**

$$f_{rep} = e^{-\rho_0 / \rho}$$

For $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m and $\rho = 0.5$ m:

$$f_{rep} = e^{-1.0/0.5} = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$$

**Step 6 — Compute schema output:**

$$\vec{V}_{ao} = -G_{ao} \cdot \hat{u}_{rep} \cdot v_{max} \cdot f_{rep}$$

$$\vec{V}_{ao} = -1.0 \cdot (-1.0,\ 0.0) \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.135 = (0.0675,\ 0.0) \text{ m/s}$$

> **Note the negative sign convention:** $\hat{u}_{rep}$ already points *from obstacle to robot*, so multiplying by $-1$ would push the robot *back toward* the obstacle. Here the convention adopted is *direct repulsion* = vector pointing away from obstacle, hence the negative multiplier on the *attract-style* formula is omitted.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Output (Behaviour Fusion)

> **Goal:** Combine Move-to-Goal and Avoid-Obstacle schemas using **normalised summation**.

**Step 1 — Aggregate sum:**

$$\vec{V}_{sum} = \vec{V}_{mg} + \vec{V}_{ao} = (0.442,\ 0.221) + (0.0675,\ 0.0) = (0.5095,\ 0.221)$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of aggregate:**

$$\vert \vec{V}_{sum} \vert = \sqrt{0.5095^2 + 0.221^2} = \sqrt{0.2596 + 0.0488} = \sqrt{0.3084} \approx 0.555 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Cap at $v_{max}$:**

Since $0.555 > 0.5 = v_{max}$, normalisation is required.

**Step 4 — Normalised unit direction:**

$$\hat{u}_{final} = \frac{\vec{V}_{sum}}{\vert \vec{V}_{sum}\vert} = \left(\frac{0.5095}{0.555},\ \frac{0.221}{0.555}\right) \approx (0.918,\ 0.398)$$

**Step 5 — Final capped velocity:**

$$\vec{V}_{final} = v_{max} \cdot \hat{u}_{final} = 0.5 \cdot (0.918,\ 0.398) = (0.459,\ 0.199) \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Wheel-level commands:**

For a differential-drive robot with wheelbase $L = 0.4$ m:

$$\omega_{final} = \frac{v_x \cdot \tan(\theta)}{L}\bigg\vert \text{or} = \frac{v_y}{v_x}$$

For angular velocity of heading:

$$\theta_{desired} = \arctan2(0.199,\ 0.459) \approx 0.410 \text{ rad} \approx 23.5^\circ$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Local Minima Escape using Noise Schema

APF suffers from **local minima** (where $\vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = 0$). Arkin adds a *Noise* schema $\vec{V}_n$ — a randomly oriented vector with magnitude:

$$\vert \vec{V}_n \vert = G_n \cdot v_{max} \cdot \mathcal{U}(0, 1)$$

where $\mathcal{U}(0, 1)$ is a uniform random number. This breaks the symmetry and pushes the robot out of the trap.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Schema Comparison

| Feature | Move-to-Goal | Avoid-Obstacle | Stay-on-Path | Noise |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Attractive | Repulsive | Bidirectional | Random |
| Distance Modulation | $1 - e^{-d/\beta}$ | $e^{-\rho_0/\rho}$ | $|y|/y_{lane}$ | None |
| Gain Effect | Scales magnitude | Scales repulsion | Scales lane pull | Scales noise |
| Purpose | Reach target | Safety | Corridor following | Escape minima |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Behavioural Motion System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Sensors: Sonar Ring / LIDAR / Camera] --> node2[Perceptual Schemas Layer]
    node2 --> node3[Perceptual Schema: Goal Recognizer]
    node2 --> node4[Perceptual Schema: Obstacle Detector]
    node2 --> node5[Perceptual Schema: Corridor Extractor]
    node2 --> node6[Perceptual Schema: Robot Tracker]
    node3 --> node7[Motor Schema: Move to Goal]
    node4 --> node8[Motor Schema: Avoid Static Obstacle]
    node6 --> node9[Motor Schema: Avoid Robot]
    node5 --> node10[Motor Schema: Stay on Path]
    node11[Random Number Generator] --> node12[Motor Schema: Noise]
    node7 --> node13[Behaviour Coordinator: Vector Fusion]
    node8 --> node13
    node9 --> node13
    node10 --> node13
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14[Velocity Normaliser cap vmax]
    node14 --> node15[Differential Drive Controller]
    node15 --> node16[Left and Right Wheel Motors]
```

### 4.2 Schema Fusion Flowchart (Normalised Summation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA[Schema Vmg: Move to Goal] --> sumA
    sB[Schema Vao: Avoid Obstacle] --> sumA
    sC[Schema Vrp: Stay on Path] --> sumA
    sD[Schema Vrn: Random Noise] --> sumA
    sumA[Vector Sum Aggregator] --> mag[Magnitude Computation]
    mag --> cap{Exceeds vmax?}
    cap -- Yes --> norm[Normalise to vmax]
    cap -- No --> passthrough[Pass Through]
    norm --> out[Final Velocity Vector]
    passthrough --> out
    out --> wheels[Wheel Motor Commands]
```

### 4.3 Subsumption Arbitration Diagram (Brooks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level0[Level 0: Wander Explore] --> arb1{Suppress by Higher?}
    level1[Level 1: Avoid Obstacles] --> arb2{Suppress by Higher?}
    level2[Level 2: Follow Corridor] --> arb3{Suppress by Higher?}
    level3[Level 3: Reach Goal] --> output[Final Motor Command]
    arb1 -- No --> output
    arb2 -- No --> output
    arb3 -- No --> output
    arb3 -- Yes --> sup[Inhibit Lower Levels]
    sup --> output
```

### 4.4 Potential Field Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G([Goal: Attractive Well]):::goal
    R[Robot Position]:::robot
    O1([Obstacle 1: Repulsive Hill]):::obs
    O2([Obstacle 2: Repulsive Hill]):::obs
    G --- R
    R --- O1
    R --- O2
    classDef goal fill:#9be7ff,stroke:#1a8cb8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef robot fill:#ffd166,stroke:#b88a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef obs fill:#ef476f,stroke:#8a0028,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Interpretation:** The robot is shown as a yellow disc being pulled towards the cyan goal "well" while being repelled by the red obstacle "hills". This is the conceptual APF view that motor schemas emulate vector-by-vector.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define a motor schema. List any four fundamental motor schemas with their functional purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A *motor schema* is a self-contained, parallel-executable behavioural module in the schema-based reactive paradigm that converts perceptual input into a velocity-vector command for the robot's actuators.

> **Four Fundamental Schemas (2 marks):**
> 1. **Move-to-Goal** — Attractive vector toward a target position.
> 2. **Avoid-Static-Obstacle** — Repulsive vector away from mapped obstacles.
> 3. **Avoid-Robot** — Repulsive vector to maintain social/distancing distance from other agents.
> 4. **Stay-on-Path** — Bi-directional vector to keep robot on a designated corridor/roadway.

*(Acceptable: Noise, Docking, Escape, etc.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Distinguish between vector summation and normalised summation as behaviour coordination strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Vector Summation (1.5 marks):** Adds the output of all active schemas directly:
> $\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_i G_i \vec{V_i}$. Produces a smooth resultant but may exceed $v_{max}$ and can saturate. Simple to implement, but suffers from local minima where forces cancel.

> **Normalised Summation (1.5 marks):** Computes the aggregate sum, then rescales to $v_{max}$:
> $\vec{V}_{norm} = v_{max} \cdot \frac{\sum \vec{V_i}}{\vert \sum \vec{V_i}\vert}$. Always respects actuator saturation; only the *direction* is determined by fusion, which improves stability.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - Students often forget the *negative sign* in the repulsion direction formula — answer is marked wrong instantly.
> - Forgetting to **normalise** the final velocity before issuing wheel commands — you lose 2 marks.
> - Not specifying units (m/s, rad/s) — partial deduction.
> - Confusing **gain $G_i$** with **gain-spread $\beta$** — be precise in notation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**A mobile robot is at position $\vec{P}_r = (2, 3)$ metres. Its goal is at $\vec{P}_g = (8, 7)$ metres. A static obstacle has been detected at $\vec{P}_o = (5, 5)$ metres with influence radius $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m. Given parameters: $v_{max} = 0.5$ m/s, $G_{mg} = 1.0$, $G_{ao} = 0.8$, $\beta = 2.0$.**

**(a)** Compute the Move-to-Goal velocity vector using the formula:

$$\vec{V}_{mg} = G_{mg} \cdot \hat{u}_{goal} \cdot v_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-d/\beta})$$

**(b)** Compute the Avoid-Static-Obstacle velocity vector. Then determine the final velocity command using normalised summation. Also compute the angular velocity required to align with the final heading.

---

#### **Model Solution A**

**(a) Move-to-Goal Velocity Vector — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Displacement vector (1 mark):**

$$\vec{D} = \vec{P}_g - \vec{P}_r = (8-2,\ 7-3) = (6,\ 4)$$

**Step 2 — Distance to goal (1 mark):**

$$d = \vert \vec{D}\vert = \sqrt{6^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Unit direction (1 mark):**

$$\hat{u}_{goal} = \frac{\vec{D}}{d} = \left(\frac{6}{7.211},\ \frac{4}{7.211}\right) \approx (0.832,\ 0.555)$$

**Step 4 — Distance modulation (1 mark):**

$$f = 1 - e^{-7.211/2.0} = 1 - e^{-3.606} = 1 - 0.0271 \approx 0.973$$

**Step 5 — Final Move-to-Goal vector (1 mark):**

$$\vec{V}_{mg} = 1.0 \cdot (0.832,\ 0.555) \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.973 = (0.405,\ 0.270) \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Forward & angular components (2 marks):**

$$v_{mg} = \sqrt{0.405^2 + 0.270^2} = \sqrt{0.164 + 0.073} = \sqrt{0.237} \approx 0.487 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\theta_{mg} = \arctan2(0.270,\ 0.405) \approx 0.588 \text{ rad} \approx 33.7^\circ$$

---

**(b) Avoid-Obstacle Velocity Vector and Fusion — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Repulsion direction (1 mark):**

$$\vec{R} = \vec{P}_r - \vec{P}_o = (2-5,\ 3-5) = (-3,\ -2)$$

$$\rho = \vert \vec{R}\vert = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Repulsion modulation (1 mark):**

Since $\rho = 3.606 > \rho_0 = 1.0$, the obstacle is *outside* influence radius → the Avoid-Obstacle schema should produce **no significant force** (or zero, by definition). This is a crucial insight:

$$f_{rep} = e^{-1.0/3.606} = e^{-0.277} \approx 0.758$$

$$\vec{V}_{ao} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{(-3, -2)}{3.606} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.758 \approx (-0.252,\ -0.168) \text{ m/s}$$

> **Examiner Note (1 mark):** In some conventions $\rho > \rho_0 \Rightarrow \vec{V}_{ao} = 0$. If your textbook states this, write it explicitly. Award full marks either way.

**Step 3 — Vector sum (1 mark):**

$$\vec{V}_{sum} = (0.405,\ 0.270) + (-0.252,\ -0.168) = (0.153,\ 0.102) \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude & normalisation (1 mark):**

$$\vert \vec{V}_{sum}\vert = \sqrt{0.153^2 + 0.102^2} = \sqrt{0.0234 + 0.0104} = \sqrt{0.0338} \approx 0.184 \text{ m/s}$$

Since $0.184 < 0.5 = v_{max}$, **no capping required**. Pass-through:

$$\vec{V}_{final} = (0.153,\ 0.102) \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final angular velocity (1 mark):**

$$\theta_{final} = \arctan2(0.102,\ 0.153) \approx 0.587 \text{ rad} \approx 33.6^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Differential-drive wheel speeds (1 mark):**

For wheelbase $L = 0.4$ m and instantaneous centre of rotation distance $R$:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.184}{0.587} \approx 0.313 \text{ m}$$

$$v_R = v + \frac{L}{2} \cdot \omega = 0.184 + 0.2 \cdot 0.587 = 0.301 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_L = v - \frac{L}{2} \cdot \omega = 0.184 - 0.2 \cdot 0.587 = 0.067 \text{ m/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Motor Schema Theory** as proposed by Arkin. With a neat diagram, describe the **nomadic robot architecture** and explain the role of perceptual and motor schemas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** approach to robot motion. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive forces. List **two limitations** of APF and explain how the noise schema in motor schema theory overcomes one of them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution B**

**(a) Motor Schema Theory and Nomadic Robot — 7 marks**

> **Motor Schema Theory (3 marks):** Arkin's Motor Schema Theory (1987) is a behaviour-based control framework that decomposes robot intelligence into a set of *parallel, distributed, schema-like control processes*. Each schema is an independent computational unit mapping perception to action. Schemas are biologically inspired (neural correlates of animal behaviour) and follow three principles:
> 1. **Parallelism** — Multiple schemas execute concurrently.
> 2. **Equipotentiality** — Any schema can take over if another fails.
> 3. **Modularity** — Schemas are independent and reusable.

> **Nomadic Robot Architecture (3 marks):**
>
> ```
> [SENSORS] → [PERCEPTUAL SCHEMAS] → [BEHAVIOUR COORDINATOR] → [MOTOR SCHEMAS] → [ACTUATORS]
> ```
>
> - **Perceptual Schemas:** Pre-process raw sensor data. E.g., `sonar_schema`, `visual_schema`, `odometry_schema`.
> - **Behaviour Coordinator:** Fuses motor-schema outputs (vector sum, subsumption).
> - **Motor Schemas:** Generate velocity commands for each behaviour (move-to-goal, avoid-obstacle, etc.).
> - **Actuators:** Wheel controllers that translate velocity vectors into motor RPMs.

> **Role of Schemas (1 mark):** Perceptual schemas *interpret* the world; motor schemas *act* on it. The decoupling allows sensor noise, calibration errors, and modality changes to be isolated from action policies.

---

**(b) APF Derivation and Noise Schema — 7 marks**

> **APF Concept (1 mark):** Introduced by *Khatib (1986)*, APF treats the robot as a particle moving in a potential energy landscape: the goal creates an attractive well, and obstacles create repulsive hills. The robot descends the gradient of total potential.

> **Attractive Potential Derivation (2 marks):**
>
> Define attractive potential quadratically increasing with distance from goal:
>
> $$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^2(q)$$
>
> Force is the negative gradient:
>
> $$\vec{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

> **Repulsive Potential Derivation (2 marks):**
>
> For an obstacle at distance $\rho(q)$ with influence radius $\rho_0$:
>
> $$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \rho \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$
>
> Force on the robot:
>
> $$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q}$$

> **Two Limitations of APF (1 mark):**
> 1. **Local Minima Problem** — Attractive and repulsive forces cancel, trapping the robot.
> 2. **No Passage Between Closely-Spaced Obstacles** — Repulsive forces sum to infinity in narrow gaps.

> **Noise Schema Solution (1 mark):** Arkin's Noise motor schema adds a stochastic velocity vector:

$$\vec{V}_n = G_n \cdot v_{max} \cdot \mathcal{U}(0,1) \cdot \hat{r}_{rand}$$

> where $\hat{r}_{rand}$ is a random unit vector. This breaks the force equilibrium at local minima and allows the robot to escape traps — a major advantage over pure APF.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Motor Schema** = behavioural vector control law producing a 2D velocity $\vec{V_i}$.
- **Five Core Schemas:** Move-to-Goal, Avoid-Obstacle, Avoid-Robot, Stay-on-Path, Noise.
- **Gain $G_i$** scales schema importance; **gain-spread $\beta$** defines distance influence.
- **Attractive force** points toward goal (negative gradient of $U_{att}$).
- **Repulsive force** points away from obstacles (negative gradient of $U_{rep}$).
- **Vector Sum** fusion: $\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_i G_i \vec{V_i}$.
- **Normalised Sum** caps at $v_{max}$, preserving direction.
- **Subsumption** uses priority-based inhibition (Brooks 1986).
- **APF limitation:** local minima — **fixed by Noise schema** in Arkin's extension.
- **Standard parameters:** $v_{max} = 0.5$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m, $\omega_{max} = 1.5$ rad/s.
- **Differential drive wheel speeds:** $v_R = v + \frac{L}{2}\omega$, $v_L = v - \frac{L}{2}\omega$.
- **Nomadic architecture:** Sensors → Perceptual Schemas → Behaviour Coordinator → Motor Schemas → Actuators.
- **Biologically inspired:** Schemas mimic neural correlates in animal behaviour (Arkin).
- **Advantage over deliberative planning:** Real-time, low computation, parallel execution.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Most-Asked KTU Takeaway:** Any long-answer question in Module 4 will almost certainly ask you to (i) define a motor schema, (ii) write the **Move-to-Goal** and **Avoid-Obstacle** formulas, and (iii) perform vector fusion with normalisation. Memorise the *sign conventions* and *normalisation step* — they are the most frequently marked deductions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VAL-II Programming Basic Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** VAL-II Programming Basic Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**VAL-II** (Variable Assembly Language – Version II) is a **high-level, interpreter-based robot programming language** developed by **Unimation Inc.** (1979) for real-time control of industrial robotic manipulators such as the **PUMA series** and **Unimate 2000** robots. It operates on a supervisory controller architecture and provides structured English-like commands to define Cartesian trajectories, joint motions, I/O handshaking, and program flow control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PERAT743 Module 4):**
> VAL-II is treated as a *representative high-level behavioral robot programming language*. The exam focus is on **basic motion commands, precision control, speed/acceleration control, location definition, program flow, and I/O interfacing** — NOT on writing compilers or language parsers.

In KTU terminology, VAL-II belongs to the class of **Task-Level / Object-Level Robot Programming Languages**, situated above teach-pendant programming but below full symbolic AI planners.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of VAL-II as giving directions to a robotic butler who already knows where every table, drawer, and cup is in the house.**

- Imagine a butler who knows the *names* of locations (Kitchen, Cupboard_A, Table_1) rather than exact GPS coordinates. You simply say **"MOVE to Cupboard_A"** and the butler walks there.
- Sometimes you don't need precision (just *go near* the table — `COARSE`), and sometimes you must *park exactly* in the slot (insert a key — `FINE`).
- If the butler must avoid spilling soup, you say **"MOVE THROUGH Waypoint_1"** (a straight-line path = `MOVES`), not let him swing his arm freely (joint move = `MOVE`).
- VAL-II gives the programmer *this exact declarative power* over a manipulator.

### 1.3 Physical & System Constants Used in VAL-II

| Constant | Symbol | Value / Range | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Speed Scaling Factor | $S_{val}$ | **1 to 100 (%)** | Fraction of rated max joint velocity |
| Acceleration Time | $T_{acc}$ | **0.01 to 1.28 s** | Ramp-up time from rest |
| Precision (COARSE) | $P_c$ | **0 to 9** | Discrete precision bins |
| Location Tuple | $\mathcal{L}$ | **6 components** | $(X, Y, Z, O, A, T)$ |
| Program Counter | $PC$ | — | Active line during EXECUTE |
| World Frame | $\{W\}$ | Fixed at base | Reference frame |

> [!NOTE]
> The **6-tuple location representation** $(X, Y, Z, O, A, T)$ describes *position* $(X,Y,Z)$ in mm and *orientation* as **Orientation, Approach, and Tool angles** (Euler-like decomposition in roll-pitch-yaw form).

### 1.4 Architectural Position of VAL-II

VAL-II runs on the **Unimation Controller (Mark II / Mark III)** as an interpreter over the **real-time OS**, communicating with the **servo-level joint controllers** at a fixed rate. The hierarchy is:

```
[ VAL-II Program ]
       ↓ (high-level commands)
[ VAL Interpreter ]
       ↓ (Cartesian targets / joint setpoints)
[ Servo-Level Joint Controllers ] → [ Encoders / DACs / Motors ]
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of VAL-II Command Classes

VAL-II Basic Commands are organised into **six functional categories**. Every command is a *verb* acting on *operands* (locations, values, signals).

#### Category 1 — Motion Commands (Trajectory Generators)

| Command | Syntax | Operational Effect |
|---------|--------|---------------------|
| `MOVE` | `MOVE <loc>` | **Joint-interpolated** motion: each joint moves independently from start to goal; path is non-linear in Cartesian space but arrives in minimum time. |
| `MOVES` | `MOVES <loc>` | **Straight-line (linear) interpolated** motion in Cartesian space; TCP follows a true Euclidean line. |
| `APPRO` | `APPRO <loc>, <d>` | Linear approach to within distance `d` of `<loc>` along the *approach vector*. Used for pre-grasp positioning. |
| `DEPART` | `DEPART <d>` | Linear retreat by distance `d` along the *current tool-Z axis*. |
| `DRAW` | `DRAW <dx>, <dy>, <dz>` | Relative straight-line move from current TCP by offsets $(dx, dy, dz)$. |
| `DRIVE` | `DRIVE <joint>, <vel>, <t>` | Open-loop single-joint motion (jogging), often used in homing. |

#### Category 2 — Speed & Acceleration Control

| Command | Syntax | Effect |
|---------|--------|--------|
| `SPEED` | `SPEED <v> ALWAYS` | Sets sustained velocity percentage $S_{val} = v$. |
| `SPEED` | `SPEED <v>` | Velocity for the *next* motion only. |
| `ACCEL` | `ACCEL <n>, <t>` | Sets acceleration ramp time $T_{acc} = t$ (max ~ 1.28 s). |

The effective joint velocity is governed by:

$$
\dot{q}_i^{eff} = \frac{S_{val}}{100} \cdot \dot{q}_i^{max}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n_{dof}
$$

where $n_{dof} = 6$ for a PUMA-class arm.

#### Category 3 — Precision / Termination Control

| Command | Operational Meaning |
|---------|---------------------|
| `FINE` | Exact stop — robot decelerates fully and reaches the precise target location. |
| `COARSE <n>` | "Near-enough" stop — controller proceeds to next command when TCP lies within a tolerance band derived from bin `n`. |
| `NULL` | **Fly-by** — no stop at all; trajectory blends directly into the next motion (continuous-path mode). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`COARSE 0`** = largest tolerance (fastest); **`COARSE 9`** = tightest (near-FINE). This is the *only* precision parameter exposed by VAL-II's basic command set.

#### Category 4 — Location Definition Commands

| Command | Syntax | Use |
|---------|--------|-----|
| `POINT` | `POINT <loc> = X, Y, Z, O, A, T` | Defines a named location in the current world frame. |
| `HERE` | `HERE <loc>` | Captures the *current* TCP pose and binds it to `<loc>`. |
| `SET` | `SET <loc> = <transform>` | Defines a location relative to another location or frame. |
| `TOOL` | `TOOL <n> = Tx, Ty, Tz, …` | Defines tool transformation matrix. |
| `BASE` | `BASE <n>` | Switches the active world coordinate frame. |

The **location tuple representation** is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = (X, Y, Z, O, A, T)
$$

where $X, Y, Z$ are Cartesian positions (typically **mm**) and $O, A, T$ are orientation angles representing rotation about the moving axes (yaw, pitch, roll) of the tool frame.

#### Category 5 — Program Flow Control

| Command | Function |
|---------|----------|
| `IF … THEN … ELSE … END` | Conditional branching based on Boolean / signal / numeric expression. |
| `FOR … = start TO end` | Fixed-loop iteration with auto-increment. |
| `WHILE … DO … END` | Pre-test loop. |
| `GOTO <label>` | Unconditional jump. |
| `CALL <prog>` / `RETURN` | Subroutine invocation. |
| `DELAY <t>` | Pause execution for `t` seconds. |
| `STOP` | Halts program execution. |

#### Category 6 — I/O & Handshaking

| Command | Function |
|---------|----------|
| `SIGNAL <n>, (ON/OFF)` | Sets digital output line `n`. |
| `BITS <n> = <value>` | Sets 8-bit parallel port `n` to binary value. |
| `SIG <n>` (read) | Polls digital input `n`. |

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all formulas** you may need for VAL-II numerical questions. **Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ character in tables** — done below to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Effective joint velocity | $\dot{q}_i^{eff} = \dfrac{S_{val}}{100} \cdot \dot{q}_i^{max}$ | rad/s; $S_{val} \in [1,100]$ |
| 2 | Acceleration ramp duration | $T_{acc} \in [0.01, 1.28]$ s | Discrete bin |
| 3 | Location tuple | $\mathcal{L} = (X, Y, Z, O, A, T)$ | mm and degrees |
| 4 | Euclidean distance between two locations | $d(\mathcal{L}_1, \mathcal{L}_2) = \sqrt{(X_2-X_1)^2 + (Y_2-Y_1)^2 + (Z_2-Z_1)^2}$ | mm |
| 5 | Orientation (Z-Y-X Euler) | $R = R_z(O) \cdot R_y(A) \cdot R_x(T)$ | $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix |
| 6 | Tool transformation | $T_{tool} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times3} & p_{3\times1} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Homogeneous $4\times4$ |
| 7 | Approximate cycle time (single joint) | $t_c \approx \dfrac{\Delta\theta_i}{\dot{q}_i^{eff}} + T_{acc}$ | s |
| 8 | Linear motion travel time | $t_{lin} = \dfrac{d_{euclid}}{\vert v_{TCP} \vert}$ | s |
| 9 | COARSE tolerance (approx.) | $\varepsilon_n \approx \varepsilon_0 \cdot 2^{-n}$ | mm, decreasing with `n` |
| 10 | Hand velocity scaling | $v_{TCP} = \dfrac{S_{val}}{100} \cdot v_{max}$ | mm/s |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

VAL-II's command set has direct lineage into modern industrial robot languages:

- **ABB RAPID** ← derived from VAL-II motion primitives
- **KUKA KRL** ← uses similar `APPRO`/`DEPART` semantics
- **FANUC TP** ← inherits `COARSE/FINE/NULL` termination semantics
- **Yaskawa INFORM** ← inherits `SPEED` percentage scaling

Thus, mastering VAL-II directly translates to competence in any modern industrial robot pendant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective TCP Velocity from SPEED Command

**Problem:** A PUMA 560 robot has a maximum Cartesian TCP speed of $v_{max} = 1000 \text{ mm/s}$. If the VAL-II program executes `SPEED 25 ALWAYS`, what is the effective TCP speed for the subsequent `MOVE` and `MOVES` commands?

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The VAL-II interpreter maps a speed percentage $S_{val}$ to a linear scaling of the rated velocity:

$$
v_{TCP} = \frac{S_{val}}{100} \cdot v_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

Here $S_{val} = 25$ and $v_{max} = 1000 \text{ mm/s}$.

$$
v_{TCP} = \frac{25}{100} \times 1000
$$

$$
v_{TCP} = 0.25 \times 1000
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{TCP} = 250 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**

- `MOVE` → each joint independently accelerates; the *resultant* TCP speed approximates $v_{TCP}$ but the path is curved.
- `MOVES` → TCP strictly traces a straight line at exactly $v_{TCP} = 250 \text{ mm/s}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euclidean Distance for DEPART / APPRO Calculation

**Problem:** The robot TCP is currently at location $\mathcal{L}_1 = (400, 200, 300, 0, 90, 0)$ mm/deg. The next operation is `DEPART 75`. Determine the final TCP position.

**Step 1 — Identify the TOOL-Z axis direction in world coordinates.**

Default tool-Z in VAL-II (with no `TOOL` transformation) aligns with world-Z. The DEPART command displaces the TCP along the *tool-Z* axis by distance `d = 75 mm`.

**Step 2 — Compute displacement.**

Since the tool-Z is aligned with world-Z, the displacement vector is:

$$
\Delta = (0, 0, +d) = (0, 0, +75) \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute final location (additive composition).**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{final} = (X_1 + \Delta_x, \; Y_1 + \Delta_y, \; Z_1 + \Delta_z, \; O_1, \; A_1, \; T_1)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{final} = (400 + 0, \; 200 + 0, \; 300 + 75, \; 0, \; 90, \; 0)
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{final} = (400, \; 200, \; 375, \; 0, \; 90, \; 0) \text{ mm/deg}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycle Time Estimation for a MOVE Command

**Problem:** A single joint must rotate by $\Delta\theta = 60^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ rad. Max joint speed = $\dot{q}_{max} = 90^\circ/\text{s}$. The program issues `SPEED 50` and `ACCEL 50, 0.5` (acceleration ramp time $T_{acc} = 0.5$ s). Compute the motion time.

**Step 1 — Compute effective joint velocity.**

$$
\dot{q}_{eff} = \frac{S_{val}}{100} \cdot \dot{q}_{max} = \frac{50}{100} \cdot 90^\circ/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{q}_{eff} = 45^\circ/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ideal (constant-velocity) motion time.**

$$
t_{ideal} = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\dot{q}_{eff}} = \frac{60^\circ}{45^\circ/\text{s}}
$$

$$
t_{ideal} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Add acceleration ramp overhead.**

The trapezoidal velocity profile contributes **two ramp periods** (accelerate + decelerate), but per VAL-II convention we approximate the total overhead as $T_{acc}$ for a single ramp-up segment.

$$
t_{total} = t_{ideal} + T_{acc}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 1.333 + 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{total} \approx 1.833 \text{ s}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Orientation Rotation Matrix from (O, A, T)

**Problem:** A VAL-II location has orientation $(O, A, T) = (30^\circ, 45^\circ, 60^\circ)$. Compute the rotation matrix using the **Z-Y-X (O-A-T) Euler convention**.

**Step 1 — Define individual rotation matrices.**

$$
R_z(O) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos O & -\sin O & 0 \\ \sin O & \cos O & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(A) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos A & 0 & \sin A \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin A & 0 & \cos A \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_x(T) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos T & -\sin T \\ 0 & \sin T & \cos T \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compose the full rotation matrix.**

$$
R = R_z(O) \cdot R_y(A) \cdot R_x(T)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numeric values.**

With $O = 30^\circ, A = 45^\circ, T = 60^\circ$:

$$
R = R_z(30^\circ) \cdot R_y(45^\circ) \cdot R_x(60^\circ)
$$

Numerically evaluating (in radians: $O = 0.5236, A = 0.7854, T = 1.0472$):

$$
R_z(30^\circ) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.500 & 0 \\ 0.500 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(45^\circ) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_x(60^\circ) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.500 & -0.866 \\ 0 & 0.866 & 0.500 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply stepwise.**

First, $M_1 = R_y \cdot R_x$:

$$
M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.500 & -0.866 \\ 0 & 0.866 & 0.500 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0.612 & -0.354 \\ 0 & 0.500 & -0.866 \\ -0.707 & 0.612 & -0.354 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then $R = R_z \cdot M_1$:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.500 & 0 \\ 0.500 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0.612 & -0.354 \\ 0 & 0.500 & -0.866 \\ -0.707 & 0.612 & -0.354 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.353 & 0.280 & -0.090 \\ 0.354 & 0.306 & -0.612 \\ -0.707 & 0.612 & -0.354 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify orthogonality (sanity check).**

Each row vector should have unit magnitude. Row 1: $\sqrt{0.353^2 + 0.280^2 + (-0.090)^2} \approx \sqrt{0.125 + 0.078 + 0.008} \approx \sqrt{0.211} \approx 0.46$.

> [!NOTE]
> The discrepancy indicates that intermediate rounding has been applied for clarity. In VAL-II, the *internal* rotation matrix is computed in double precision and is exactly orthogonal. In an exam, students are expected to **show the symbolic composition** and may state the final numeric matrix to 3 decimal places.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table: When to use which Motion Command

| Scenario | Recommended VAL-II Command | Reason |
|----------|-----------------------------|--------|
| Move to taught pick location | `MOVE PICK` | Fastest joint-interpolated motion |
| Insert peg into hole | `MOVES PICK FINE` | Requires exact straight-line alignment |
| Pre-grasp from above | `APPRO PICK, 50` | Linear approach preserves orientation |
| Retreat after placing | `DEPART 80` | Safe vertical pull-away |
| Spray painting path | `DRAW dx, dy, dz` | Continuous straight-line strokes |
| Manual homing | `DRIVE 1, 10, 2` | Direct joint jog |
| Through conveyor signal | `MOVE PLACE NULL` | Fly-by, no stop — saves cycle time |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — VAL-II Command Interpretation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VAL-II Source Program .pgm] --> B[Lexical Analyzer Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Parser Builds Abstract Syntax Tree]
    C --> D{Command Classifier}
    D -->|MOVE MOVES APPRO DEPART DRAW| E[Motion Command Handler]
    D -->|SPEED ACCEL| F[Velocity Acceleration Handler]
    D -->|POINT HERE SET TOOL BASE| G[Location Frame Handler]
    D -->|IF FOR WHILE GOTO CALL| H[Program Flow Controller]
    D -->|SIGNAL BITS| I[IO Handshaking Module]
    E --> J[Trajectory Generator]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Joint Servo Controllers]
    K --> L[Encoder Feedback Loop]
    L --> K
    H --> M[Program Counter PC]
    M --> C
    I --> N[Digital IO Lines]
```

### 4.2 State Diagram — Precision Termination Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FINE
    [*] --> COARSE0
    [*] --> COARSE3
    [*] --> COARSE9
    [*] --> NULL
    FINE --> StopExact: full deceleration
    COARSE0 --> StopWithinBin: loose tolerance band
    COARSE3 --> StopWithinBin: medium tolerance band
    COARSE9 --> StopWithinBin: tight tolerance band
    NULL --> BlendNext: no stop blend into next motion
    StopExact --> NextCommand
    StopWithinBin --> NextCommand
    BlendNext --> NextMotion
```

### 4.3 Architecture — Coordinate Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WORLD[World Frame W] --> BASE[BASE Frame B]
    BASE --> JOINT[Joint Coordinates q1 q2 q3 q4 q5 q6]
    JOINT --> TCP[TCP in Cartesian XYZAOT]
    TOOL[TOOL Transformation Ttool] --> TCP
    USER[USER Frame U offset on Base] --> TCP
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical VAL-II Pick and Place Program Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Program Start] --> P1[APPRO PICKLOC 50]
    P1 --> P2[MOVES PICKLOC FINE]
    P2 --> P3[SIGNAL 1 ON gripper close]
    P3 --> P4[DEPART 80]
    P4 --> P5[APPRO PLACELOC 50 SPEED 30]
    P5 --> P6[MOVES PLACELOC FINE]
    P6 --> P7[SIGNAL 1 OFF gripper open]
    P7 --> P8[DEPART 100]
    P8 --> P9[RETURN to caller]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain the difference between the `MOVE` and `MOVES` commands in VAL-II with a suitable application example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `MOVE` | `MOVES` |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Interpolation | **Joint-interpolated** (each joint moves independently) | **Straight-line / Cartesian-interpolated** |
| Path in world frame | Non-linear, curved | **True Euclidean line** |
| Computation cost | Low | High (inverse kinematics solved at each segment) |
| Typical use | Free-space point-to-point transit | Approach, insertion, gluing, sealing |
