> 5. **Skipping normalization:** Always show the saturation step $\min(1, v_{max}/\Vert v \Vert)$ in motor-schema derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Elements and Functions (Module 4)**

- ✅ A **behavior** is formally a function $B: S \rightarrow A$.
- ✅ **Four elements**: Sensors, Actuators, Behaviors, Coordinator.
- ✅ **Three coordination function types**: Subsumption (priority), Motor Schema (voting), Fuzzy (membership blend).
- ✅ **Motor schema equation**: $\vec{v}_{global} = \sum w_i \nabla U_i(\mathbf{x})$.
- ✅ **Subsumption rule**: $A = B_{i^*}$ where $i^* = \arg\max P_i$.
- ✅ **Reflexive behaviors** have reaction time **< 10 ms**; reactive **10–100 ms**; deliberative **> 1 s**.
- ✅ Always **normalize** the final command to respect actuator saturation limits.
- ✅ The **vector field** interpretation comes from $\nabla U_i(\mathbf{x})$.
- ✅ Brooks (1986) introduced subsumption; Arkin (1989) introduced motor schemas — these are **high-yield names** for KTU exams.
- ✅ Diagrams must show **closed-loop feedback** from environment back to sensors.
- ✅ State machines are used when **discrete operational modes** exist (e.g., explore → grasp).
- ✅ Fuzzy coordination provides **smooth blending** between competing behaviors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Control Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT743 - BEHAVIORAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Control Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Control Statements** in behavioral robotics are the structured programming constructs (sequential, selection, and iteration statements) used to govern the **execution flow**, **behavior arbitration**, and **decision-making logic** of autonomous robotic agents. They form the syntactic and semantic backbone for implementing reactive, deliberative, and hybrid control architectures in behavior-based robotic systems.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **PERAT743 – Behavioral Robotics**, program control statements are categorized into three fundamental families:

| Control Family | Purpose | Robotic Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Statements** | Branching logic | Behavior arbitration (subsumption) |
| **Iteration Statements** | Repetitive polling | Sensor loop, motor control loop |
| **Jump/Transfer Statements** | Altering flow | Emergency stop, mode switching |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Traffic Signal Controller:**
> Imagine a traffic policeman managing a four-way intersection. His brain continuously runs a mental program: "**IF** a car approaches from the North **AND** no car is on the East, **THEN** signal green for North; **ELSE IF** the timer exceeds 30 seconds, switch the signal." This conditional reasoning is *exactly* what a robot does with its program control statements when arbitrating between competing behaviors such as *obstacle-avoidance*, *goal-seeking*, and *wall-following*.

### 1.3 Three Pillars of Program Control

The structural foundation of any robotic control program rests on three pillars:

1. **Sequence** — The default top-to-bottom execution (e.g., `INIT → SENSE → PLAN → ACT`).
2. **Selection** — Choosing one path among several (e.g., `if obstacle_close then avoid else cruise`).
3. **Iteration** — Repeating a block while a condition holds (e.g., reading LIDAR 50 times per second).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the translation of behavioral policies (from Brooks' Subsumption to Arkin's Motor Schemas) into executable control flow using standard programming structures augmented with **state machines** and **priority arbitration**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Control Flow in Behavior-Based Robots

A behavior-based robot does not have a single monolithic program. Instead, it has **multiple concurrent behaviors**, each with its own control flow, arbitrated by a higher-level mechanism. The control flow inside each behavior block uses the standard program control statements.

#### 2.1.1 Selection (Conditional) Statements

These are the **decision-making core** of behavioral arbitration.

- **`if` Statement** — Executes a behavior only if its preconditions are satisfied.
- **`if-else` Statement** — Implements a competitive selection between two mutually exclusive behaviors.
- **`Nested if-else` / `else-if ladder`** — Used in multi-criteria behavior selection (e.g., battery + obstacle + goal).
- **`switch-case` Statement** — Used in **finite state machines (FSM)** where the robot can be in one of $N$ discrete states (e.g., `WANDER`, `CHASE`, `AVOID`, `IDLE`).

The general selection logic is mathematically equivalent to a multiplexer:

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{cases} B_1 & \text{if } C_1 \text{ is TRUE} \\ B_2 & \text{if } C_2 \text{ is TRUE} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ B_n & \text{if } C_n \text{ is TRUE} \\ B_{\text{default}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $B_i$ denotes the $i^{th}$ behavior module and $C_i$ is its corresponding activation condition.

#### 2.1.2 Iteration (Looping) Statements

Iteration statements are used for **continuous sensor polling** and **actuator refresh**, which is the heartbeat of any reactive robot.

- **`while` loop** — Condition-checked entry; used for "run while battery is above threshold."
- **`do-while` loop** — Condition-checked exit; guarantees at least one execution (useful for sensor warm-up).
- **`for` loop** — Counter-controlled; used for fixed iteration over arrays (e.g., scanning 360 LIDAR points).
- **`for-each` loop** — Traverses sensor vectors or motor command arrays.

#### 2.1.3 Jump / Transfer Statements

- **`break`** — Exits a loop or switch prematurely (e.g., emergency stop condition met).
- **`continue`** — Skips the current iteration and proceeds to the next.
- **`return`** — Exits a behavior function, returning a control vector.
- **`goto`** *(deprecated, rarely used in modern robotics frameworks)* — Unconditional transfer.

### 2.2 Why These Statements Matter in Behavioral Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The intelligence of a behavior-based robot is not in any single line of code — it emerges from the **interplay** between control statements. The `while` loop makes the robot *persistent*; the `if-else` makes it *selective*; the `switch-case` makes it *stateful*; and the nested logic gives it *hierarchy*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct | Pseudocode Template | Robotic Use Case | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `if` | `if (sensor < threshold) then actuate();` | Single behavior trigger | $O(1)$ |
| 2 | `if-else` | `if (obstacle) avoid(); else cruise();` | Competing behaviors | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | `switch-case` | `switch(state) { case WANDER: ...; break; }` | FSM state transitions | $O(1)$ average |
| 4 | `while` | `while (running) { read_sensors(); }` | Main control loop | $O(n)$ per $n$ cycles |
| 5 | `do-while` | `do { init_sensor(); } while(!ready);` | Sensor initialization | $O(1)$ min |
| 6 | `for` | `for(i=0; i<360; i++) read_lidar(i);` | Array iteration | $O(n)$ |
| 7 | `break` | `if (battery < 10%) break;` | Emergency exit | $O(1)$ |
| 8 | `continue` | `if (invalid_data) continue;` | Filter iteration | $O(1)$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial robotics (e.g., KUKA, ABB), program control statements orchestrate the **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** ladder logic. In service robots (e.g., Pepper, Boston Dynamics Spot), the high-level behavior tree nodes map directly to nested `if-else` and `switch-case` constructs. In **ROS (Robot Operating System)**, the callback functions and `while(ros::ok())` loop are direct manifestations of these control structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Behavior Arbitration as a Boolean Expression

Consider a robot with three behaviors: $B_{\text{avoid}}$ (obstacle avoidance), $B_{\text{seek}}$ (goal seeking), and $B_{\text{idle}}$ (standby). The arbitration logic using nested `if-else` can be derived formally.

**Step 1 — Define the activation conditions:**

Let $d$ be the distance to the nearest obstacle (in meters), $g$ be the distance to the goal, and $b$ be the battery percentage.

$$
C_{\text{avoid}} = (d < d_{\text{safe}}) \quad \text{where } d_{\text{safe}} = 0.5\,\text{m}
$$

$$
C_{\text{seek}} = (d \geq d_{\text{safe}}) \land (g > 0) \land (b > b_{\text{low}}) \quad \text{where } b_{\text{low}} = 15\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{idle}} = (b \leq b_{\text{low}}) \lor (g \leq 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Order the conditions by priority (Subsumption ordering):**

$$
\text{Priority} = B_{\text{idle}} > B_{\text{avoid}} > B_{\text{seek}}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to a nested `if-else` ladder:**

$$
\text{Active} = \begin{cases} B_{\text{idle}} & \text{if } C_{\text{idle}} \text{ is TRUE} \\ B_{\text{avoid}} & \text{else if } C_{\text{avoid}} \text{ is TRUE} \\ B_{\text{seek}} & \text{else if } C_{\text{seek}} \text{ is TRUE} \\ B_{\text{idle}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Embed into an infinite `while` loop with bounded cycle time:**

$$
\text{loop rate} = f_{\text{ctrl}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \quad \text{typically } T_{\text{cycle}} = 20\,\text{ms} \Rightarrow f_{\text{ctrl}} = 50\,\text{Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** Each cycle, the robot reads sensors (step 1), evaluates Boolean conditions (step 2), selects the highest-priority active behavior (step 3), and repeats at a fixed rate (step 4). This is the canonical "Sense → Think → Act" loop formalized through program control statements.

### 3.2 Derivation: Converting FSM to `switch-case`

A robot's operational states form a finite set $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$. The transition function is:

$$
\delta : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{I} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}
$$

where $\mathcal{I}$ is the set of input events (sensor triggers, timers, user commands).

For a four-state robot (WANDER, AVOID, CHASE, IDLE):

| Current State | Input Event | Next State |
|---|---|---|
| WANDER | Obstacle detected | AVOID |
| WANDER | Target spotted | CHASE |
| AVOID | Path clear | WANDER |
| CHASE | Target lost | WANDER |
| ANY | Battery low | IDLE |

This transition table is implemented directly as a `switch-case` with a nested `if` for event evaluation:

$$
\text{next\_state}(S, e) = \text{lookup}(\delta, S, e)
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Control Statement Selection Guide

| Situation in Robot | Recommended Statement | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Two behaviors compete | `if-else` | Binary choice, clear mutual exclusion |
| Multiple discrete states | `switch-case` | Scalable, readable, deterministic |
| Continuous sensor polling | `while(true)` loop | Indefinite repetition until shutdown |
| Fixed-size LIDAR scan | `for` loop | Known iteration count (e.g., 360 points) |
| At least one sensor reading | `do-while` | Guarantees one execution |
| Pre-empt dangerous action | `break` | Immediate exit from control loop |
| Skip faulty data | `continue` | Filter inside iteration |
| Modular behavior code | `return` | Encapsulation and clean exit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Behavior Arbitration Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: while true] --> B[Read Sensors LIDAR / IMU / Camera]
    B --> C{Idle Condition b less than 15 percent?}
    C -- YES --> D[Activate Idle Behavior Return to Charger]
    C -- NO --> E{Obstacle within 0.5 m?}
    E -- YES --> F[Activate Avoid Behavior Turn Left or Right]
    E -- NO --> G{Goal within range?}
    G -- YES --> H[Activate Seek Behavior Move Toward Goal]
    G -- NO --> I[Activate Wander Behavior Random Walk]
    D --> J[Actuate Motors]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Cycle Delay 20 ms]
    K --> A
    K -- Emergency Stop Button --> L[Break Loop]
    L --> M[Shutdown]
```

### 4.2 Finite State Machine (FSM) for Mobile Robot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> WANDER : start command
    WANDER --> AVOID : obstacle detected
    AVOID --> WANDER : path clear
    WANDER --> CHASE : target acquired
    CHASE --> WANDER : target lost
    AVOID --> IDLE : battery low
    CHASE --> IDLE : battery low
    WANDER --> IDLE : battery low
    IDLE --> [*] : power off
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Program Control Layer

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        IN1[LIDAR Sensor]
        IN2[IMU Sensor]
        IN3[Camera]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Program Control Layer]
        S1[Switch Case State Selector]
        S2[If Else Condition Checker]
        S3[While Main Loop Controller]
        S4[For Loop Sensor Iterator]
    end

    subgraph BH[Behavior Modules]
        B1[Avoid Behavior]
        B2[Seek Behavior]
        B3[Wander Behavior]
        B4[Idle Behavior]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Motor Driver Left]
        O2[Motor Driver Right]
    end

    IN1 --> S4
    IN2 --> S4
    IN3 --> S4
    S4 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> B1
    S1 --> B2
    S1 --> B3
    S1 --> B4
    S3 --> S2
    B1 --> O1
    B1 --> O2
    B2 --> O1
    B2 --> O2
    B3 --> O1
    B3 --> O2
    B4 --> O1
    B4 --> O2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The flowcharts above depict the canonical mapping between behavioral robotics logic and standard program control statements. The `switch-case` is the state selector, the `if-else` is the condition checker, the `while` is the master loop, and the `for` is the array iterator over sensor data.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT743]`

**Q: Differentiate between `while` and `do-while` loops in the context of a robot's sensor initialization routine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `while` loop | `do-while` loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition check** | Entry-controlled (checked *before* execution) | Exit-controlled (checked *after* execution) |
| **Minimum executions** | Zero (may never execute) | One (always executes at least once) |
| **Robotic use case** | "Run ultrasonic sensor **while** robot is moving" | "Power up the IMU and **then** check if calibration is valid" |
| **Risk** | May skip initialization entirely | Always performs one cycle, safer for hardware warm-up |

In a robot's sensor initialization routine, the `do-while` loop is preferred when the sensor must be powered and read at least once before its validity is confirmed, whereas the `while` loop is preferred for continuous background polling during operation.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT743]`

**Q: Explain the role of the `switch-case` statement in implementing a finite state machine (FSM) for a behavior-based robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `switch-case` statement provides a clean, scalable, and deterministic implementation of the FSM's transition function $\delta : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{I} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$. Each `case` label corresponds to a discrete robot state (e.g., `WANDER`, `AVOID`, `CHASE`, `IDLE`), and within each case, nested `if` conditions evaluate the input events to determine the next state. Compared to a long `if-else-if` ladder, the `switch-case` offers:

1. **Readability** — Each state is visually isolated.
2. **Maintainability** — Adding a new state requires only one new `case` block.
3. **Performance** — Compilers often optimize `switch-case` into a jump table, giving $O(1)$ average lookup versus $O(n)$ for `if-else`.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT743]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how the **subsumption architecture** arbitrates between multiple robot behaviors. Show how this arbitration is implemented using **nested `if-else`** control statements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The subsumption architecture, proposed by Rodney Brooks in 1986, organizes robot control as a layered hierarchy of behavior modules. Each layer is a finite-state machine that operates asynchronously and can "subsume" (inhibit or suppress) the output of lower-priority layers.

**Layers Example (2 marks):** For a mobile robot:
- Layer 0 (lowest): `Avoid-Obstacles`
- Layer 1: `Wander`
- Layer 2: `Explore`
- Layer 3 (highest): `Go-Home`

**Nested `if-else` Implementation (3 marks):**

The arbitration is encoded as a priority-ordered `else-if` ladder:

The highest-priority behavior whose preconditions are TRUE gets to write to the actuators, effectively subsuming all lower layers.

**(b)** Design a `while` loop-based **main control program** for a line-following robot with emergency stop capability. Clearly state the cycle time, sensor inputs, and actuator outputs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Specifications (2 marks):**
- 3 IR line sensors (left, center, right): `S_L`, `S_C`, `S_R`
- 2 DC motors: `M_Left`, `M_Right`
- Emergency stop button: `E_Stop`
- Cycle time: $T_{\text{cycle}} = 10\,\text{ms} \Rightarrow f_{\text{ctrl}} = 100\,\text{Hz}$

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

The `while(ros::ok())` pattern (or equivalent) wraps the main loop. The nested `if-else` decides motor speeds based on which sensor sees the line. The `break` statement is triggered by the emergency stop.

**Truth Table for Motor Control (2 marks):**

| S_L | S_C | S_R | Action | M_Left | M_Right |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Move forward | HIGH | HIGH |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Turn left | LOW | HIGH |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Turn right | HIGH | LOW |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Sharp left | OFF | HIGH |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Sharp right | HIGH | OFF |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Lost line → stop | OFF | OFF |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT743]`

**(a)** Discuss the **finite state machine (FSM)** approach to robot control. Convert the following behavioral specification into a complete state transition table and write the corresponding **`switch-case`** implementation: "A surveillance robot has four states — `PATROL`, `ALARM`, `INVESTIGATE`, and `RETURN`. It transitions to ALARM if motion is detected, to INVESTIGATE if the alarm persists for more than 5 seconds, and to RETURN if battery falls below 20%." **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**FSM Theory (2 marks):** An FSM is defined as a 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{I}, \delta, s_0, \mathcal{F})$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of states, $\mathcal{I}$ the input alphabet, $\delta$ the transition function, $s_0$ the initial state, and $\mathcal{F}$ the set of final/accepting states. FSMs map naturally to `switch-case` constructs.

**State Transition Table (2 marks):**

| Current State | Event | Next State |
|---|---|---|
| PATROL | motion detected | ALARM |
| PATROL | battery < 20% | RETURN |
| ALARM | timer > 5 s | INVESTIGATE |
| ALARM | battery < 20% | RETURN |
| INVESTIGATE | motion lost | PATROL |
| INVESTIGATE | battery < 20% | RETURN |
| RETURN | docked | PATROL |

**`switch-case` Implementation (3 marks):**

The outer `switch(current_state)` dispatches to the active state's logic, and the inner `if-else` checks the events to determine `next_state`. A `while(true)` wrapper runs the FSM indefinitely.

**(b)** Compare **iteration** and **selection** control statements in terms of their computational purpose, execution semantics, and applicability to a **reactive behavior-based robot**. Provide one robotic example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Selection Statements | Iteration Statements |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Choose *one* path among alternatives | *Repeat* a block multiple times |
| **Semantics** | Conditional branching on a Boolean expression | Looping until a condition is false or count is reached |
| **Complexity** | $O(1)$ per branch (constant time) | $O(n)$ for $n$ iterations |
| **Robotic example 1** | `if (front_sensor < 0.3m) avoid(); else cruise();` | `while (battery > 0) { sense(); act(); }` |
| **Robotic example 2** | `switch(state) { case PATROL: ...; }` | `for (int i = 0; i < 360; i++) lidar[i] = read();` |
| **Applicability** | Behavior arbitration, state transitions | Sensor polling, actuator refresh, data fusion loops |

In a reactive behavior-based robot, **selection** statements are used at the *behavioral level* (which behavior wins?), while **iteration** statements are used at the *operational level* (how often do we re-evaluate?). Both are essential and complementary — a well-designed robot program interleaves them hierarchically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `while` with `do-while`:** Students frequently claim both are "loops that repeat" without noting the critical entry-vs-exit condition difference. Always mention **at least one execution guarantee** for `do-while`.
> 2. **Forgetting `break` in `switch-case`:** Missing `break` causes *fall-through*, a classic KTU deduction point. State explicitly that omitting `break` leads to unintended sequential execution of the next case.
> 3. **Mixing up priority with condition order:** In subsumption arbitration, students write the highest-priority behavior at the *bottom* of the `if-else` ladder. Remember: the **first** matching condition in an `else-if` chain wins, so the **highest-priority** behavior must be the **first** `if`.
> 4. **No cycle time justification:** For a main control loop, you must specify the cycle time $T_{\text{cycle}}$ and justify the control frequency $f_{\text{ctrl}}$. Marks are lost if this is omitted.
> 5. **Confusing "state" with "behavior":** A state in an FSM is a *mode* of operation; a behavior is a *competency*. Multiple behaviors can be active within one state.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three pillars:** Sequence, Selection, Iteration — every robot program is built on these.
- **Selection statements** (`if`, `if-else`, `switch-case`) are used for **behavior arbitration** and **FSM state transitions**.
- **Iteration statements** (`while`, `do-while`, `for`, `for-each`) drive the **sense–think–act loop** at a fixed control frequency (typically **50 Hz** or **100 Hz**).
- **Jump statements** (`break`, `continue`, `return`) provide **safety overrides** like emergency stops.
- **Priority ordering** in subsumption: highest-priority behavior is the **first** `if` in an `else-if` ladder.
- **`switch-case` vs `if-else`:** Use `switch-case` for 3+ discrete states; use `if-else` for 2-way or condition-based decisions.
- **`do-while`** guarantees one execution — ideal for sensor warm-up and hardware initialization.
- **`break`** in a `switch-case` is **mandatory** to prevent fall-through (a frequent KTU pitfall).
- **Cycle time** $T_{\text{cycle}}$ must always be specified for any main robot control loop.
- **FSM** transitions map directly to `switch-case` blocks with nested event-checking `if` statements.
- **Concurrency** in behavioral robotics is achieved by *rapid time-slicing* of a single-threaded `while` loop, not by true parallelism at the control-statement level.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
